topics.title: Fuel Gases - Natural Gas
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT637 - CHEMICAL TECHNOLOGY AND REACTION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Fuel Gases - Natural Gas

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Natural Gas (NG)** is a naturally occurring hydrocarbon-rich gaseous fossil fuel mixture, predominantly composed of **methane (CH₄, 70–90 mol %)**, with progressively smaller proportions of higher alkanes (C₂H₆, C₃H₈, C₄H₁₀) and non-combustible impurities such as CO₂, N₂, H₂S, He, and water vapor. It is found trapped in porous underground reservoirs either alone or co-existing with crude petroleum, and it represents the **cleanest-burning and most hydrogen-rich fossil fuel** in commercial use.

For KTU examinations, natural gas is universally accepted as a **primary fuel gas** belonging to the **hydrocarbon fuel family**, sitting between gaseous and liquid fuels on the energy density spectrum.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> Think of natural gas like **the leftover premium espresso of the fossil-fuel world**.

Just as espresso is extracted from coffee grounds under pressure, natural gas is the **volatile, light fraction** that has separated naturally from crude oil over millions of years of geological heating. When crude oil is the "thick soup," natural gas is the **aromatic steam** rising above it.

- **Why is it special?** Because it is mostly **methane** — the simplest, lightest, and cleanest-burning hydrocarbon. One molecule (CH₄ + 2O₂ → CO₂ + 2H₂O) gives you a near-perfect combustion product: just CO₂ and water.
- **Why call it "clean"?** No soot (because no long carbon chains), no sulfur smell (after sweetening), no heavy metals, no ash.
- **What is its real-world value?** It is the **bridge fuel** between coal/oil and renewable energy because it produces **~50% less CO₂ per unit of heat released** than coal and **~30% less** than oil.

---

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Property | Typical Value | Units |
|----------|---------------|-------|
| Molecular Weight (dry) | 16.04 – 20.0 | kg/kmol |
| Density (STP) | 0.7 – 0.85 | kg/m³ |
| Boiling Point (pure CH₄) | **−161.5 °C (111.65 K)** | °C |
| Auto-ignition Temperature | **537 °C (810 K)** | °C |
| Flammability Limit (CH₄ in air) | **5 – 15 vol %** | % |
| Methane Number | 80 – 99 | — |
| Gross Calorific Value (GCV) | **38 – 42 MJ/m³** (at STP) | MJ/m³ |
| Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio | **17.16 : 1** (by mass) | — |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU High-Yield Tip:** Memorize the boiling point of methane (**−162 °C**) — it is the single most-asked LNG-related value in KTU university examinations.

---

### 1.4 Classification of Natural Gas (Geological Origin)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners frequently test **geological classification**, so commit the four categories below to memory.

1. **Associated Gas (Dissolved Gas):** Found dissolved in crude oil under reservoir pressure; released when oil is brought to the surface. Also called *gas-cap gas* when it forms a free gas layer above the oil.
2. **Non-Associated Gas (Gas-Well Gas):** Found in reservoirs where there is little or no crude oil; produced as the sole hydrocarbon.
3. **Coal-Bed Methane (CBM):** Adsorbed on the microporous surface of coal seams; extracted via dewatering.
4. **Shale Gas / Tight Gas:** Trapped in low-permeability shale formations; liberated via hydraulic fracturing ("fracking").

---

### 1.5 Quality Classification (Pipeline Spec)

| Grade | GCV (MJ/m³) | Wobbe Index (MJ/m³) | Use |
|-------|-------------|---------------------|-----|
| **H-gas (High calorific)** | 37 – 45 | 50 – 55 | Industrial + domestic |
| **L-gas (Low calorific)** | 30 – 37 | 41 – 50 | Older European grids |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Chemical Composition (Typical Range)

Natural gas is **never pure methane** in the field; it is a multi-component mixture whose exact composition dictates its calorific value, Wobbe Index, and processability.

| Component | Mol % (Typical) | Significance |
|-----------|----------------|--------------|
| CH₄ (Methane) | 70 – 90 | Primary combustible |
| C₂H₆ (Ethane) | 0 – 20 | Combustible, recoverable |
| C₃H₈ (Propane) | 0 – 10 | LPG fraction |
| C₄H₁₀ (Butane) | 0 – 5 | LPG fraction |
| C₅⁺ (Pentanes+) | 0 – 1 | Condensate / NGL |
| CO₂ | 0 – 8 | Inert, corrosive |
| N₂ | 0 – 10 | Inert, diluent |
| H₂S | 0 – 5 ppm – 5% | Toxic, corrosive → "sour gas" |
| He | 0 – 0.5 | Recoverable noble gas |
| H₂O | traces | Corrosion, hydrate risk |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **"Sour" vs "Sweet" Gas:** H₂S content above **5.7 mg/m³ (≈ 4 ppmv)** makes the gas *sour* and obligates sweetening (e.g., amine treating, Claus process). Below that threshold, the gas is *sweet*.

---

### 2.2 Core Combustion Chemistry

For pure methane (the dominant reaction):

$$\text{CH}_4 + 2\,\text{O}_2 + 7.52\,\text{N}_2 \longrightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O} + 7.52\,\text{N}_2$$

For higher alkanes (general form CₙH₂ₙ₊₂):

$$\text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n+2} + \left(\frac{3n+1}{2}\right)\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow n\,\text{CO}_2 + (n+1)\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **exam-grade**, validated, and appear repeatedly in KTU and GATE Chemical Engineering question papers.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Gross Calorific Value (GCV) | $GCV = \Sigma\, y_i \cdot GCV_i$ | MJ/Nm³ | Mass/molar weighted sum |
| 2 | Net Calorific Value (NCV) | $NCV = GCV - 9\,H \cdot h_{fg}$ | MJ/kg | $h_{fg}$ at 25 °C = 2.442 MJ/kg |
| 3 | Relation NCV ↔ GCV | $NCV = GCV - 0.212\,H$ (approx) | MJ/kg | $H$ = hydrogen mass fraction |
| 4 | Wobbe Index (WI) | $WI = \dfrac{GCV}{\sqrt{\rho_{rel}}}$ | MJ/Nm³ | Determines interchangeability |
| 5 | Specific Gravity (SG) | $SG = \dfrac{M_{gas}}{M_{air}} = \dfrac{M_{gas}}{28.97}$ | — | Air reference = 28.97 kg/kmol |
| 6 | Density at STP | $\rho = \dfrac{P \cdot M}{R \cdot T}$ | kg/m³ | STP: P=101.325 kPa, T=273.15 K |
| 7 | Stoichiometric AFR (mass) | $AFR = \dfrac{(3.04\,n - 0.25\,H + 0.5\,O)}{C}$ | kg air/kg fuel | Carbon balance |
| 8 | Theoretical CO₂ | $CO_{2,\%} = \dfrac{n \cdot y_{C_n}}{1 + AFR_{mass}/28.97} \times 100$ | % flue | Dry basis |
| 9 | Combustion Efficiency | $\eta_c = \dfrac{Q_{released}}{Q_{input}} \times 100$ | % | Stoichiometric basis |
| 10 | LNG Density | $\rho_{LNG} \approx 450$ | kg/m³ | At −162 °C, 1 atm |

---

### 2.4 The Two Critical Indices (Engineering Utility)

#### 2.4.1 Wobbe Index — *The Interchangeability Parameter*
$$WI = \frac{GCV}{\sqrt{SG}} = \frac{GCV}{\sqrt{\rho_{rel}}}$$

**Why it matters in engineering:** Two fuel gases with **identical Wobbe Index deliver the same heat output through the same burner orifice** at the same pressure. This makes WI the **single most important index for grid substitution** (e.g., swapping town gas for natural gas).

#### 2.4.2 Methane Number — *The Knock Resistance Index*
Analogous to octane number in gasoline; indicates resistance to auto-ignition in spark-ignition engines (CNG vehicles). Pure methane = 100; pure hydrogen = 0.

---

### 2.5 Engineering Real-World Utility

- **Power Generation:** Combined-cycle gas turbines (CCGT) using NG achieve **>60% thermal efficiency** vs ~38% for coal plants.
- **Domestic Use:** PNG (Piped Natural Gas) replaces LPG; one pipe replaces endless cylinder deliveries.
- **Petrochemical Feedstock:** Steam-cracking of ethane/propane fractions yields ethylene — the building block of plastics.
- **Hydrogen Production:** Steam methane reforming (SMR): CH₄ + H₂O → CO + 3H₂ (most industrial H₂ originates here).
- **Transport:** LNG/CNG cuts CO₂ emissions by ~25% versus diesel in heavy vehicles.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio for Pure Methane

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **classic 7-mark KTU derivation**. Students who skip the molar accounting lose easy marks.

**Combustion reaction (with 79/21 N₂/O₂ split of air):**

$$\text{CH}_4 + 2\,\text{O}_2 + 2\,(3.76)\,\text{N}_2 \longrightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O} + 7.52\,\text{N}_2$$

**Step 1 — Identify moles of reactants per mole of fuel:**
- O₂ required = 2 mol
- N₂ accompanying = 2 × 3.76 = 7.52 mol

**Step 2 — Compute mass of air required:**

$$m_{air} = n_{O_2} \cdot M_{O_2} + n_{N_2} \cdot M_{N_2}$$

$$m_{air} = 2\,(32) + 7.52\,(28) = 64 + 210.56 = 274.56\ \text{kg per kmol of CH}_4$$

**Step 3 — Mass of fuel (1 kmol CH₄):**

$$m_{fuel} = 1 \cdot (12 + 4 \cdot 1) = 16\ \text{kg}$$

**Step 4 — Air-Fuel Ratio (mass basis):**

$$\boxed{\,AFR_{stoich} = \frac{274.56}{16} = 17.16\ \frac{\text{kg air}}{\text{kg fuel}}\,}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Relation Between GCV and NCV (for Methane)

**Step 1 — Start with the standard relation:**

$$NCV = GCV - \left(\frac{9\,H}{100}\right) \cdot h_{fg}$$

where $H$ = mass % of hydrogen in the fuel, $h_{fg}$ = latent heat of vaporization of water at 25 °C = **2442 kJ/kg**.

**Step 2 — For pure CH₄ (M = 16 kg/kmol):**

$$H = \frac{4 \times 1}{16} \times 100 = 25\%$$

**Step 3 — Latent heat lost per kg of CH₄ burned:**

The reaction produces 2 mol H₂O = 36 g = 0.036 kg H₂O per 16 g fuel. So per kg of fuel:

$$m_{H_2O} = \frac{0.036}{0.016} = 2.25\ \text{kg H}_2\text{O per kg CH}_4$$

**Step 4 — Energy loss as latent heat:**

$$\Delta H = 2.25 \times 2442 = 5494.5\ \text{kJ/kg} \approx 5.5\ \text{MJ/kg}$$

**Step 5 — Apply known GCV of methane:**

$$GCV_{CH_4} = 55.5\ \text{MJ/kg}$$

**Step 6 — Final NCV:**

$$\boxed{\,NCV_{CH_4} = 55.5 - 5.5 = 50.0\ \text{MJ/kg}\,}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Mixture GCV Calculation (Weighted-Average Method)

**Problem (KTU-style):** A natural gas has the following mol % composition: CH₄ = 85, C₂H₆ = 8, C₃H₈ = 4, CO₂ = 2, N₂ = 1. Given GCV values (MJ/Nm³): CH₄ = 37.7, C₂H₆ = 66.0, C₃H₈ = 93.2, CO₂ = 0, N₂ = 0. Calculate mixture GCV and Wobbe Index. Take $M_{mix}$ = 18.6 kg/kmol.

**Step 1 — Apparent Molar Mass (M_mix):**

$$M_{mix} = \Sigma\, y_i M_i$$

$$M_{mix} = (0.85)(16) + (0.08)(30) + (0.04)(44) + (0.02)(44) + (0.01)(28)$$

$$M_{mix} = 13.60 + 2.40 + 1.76 + 0.88 + 0.28 = 18.92\ \text{kg/kmol}$$

**Step 2 — Specific Gravity (relative to air = 28.97):**

$$SG = \frac{18.92}{28.97} = 0.653$$

**Step 3 — Mixture GCV (molar-weighted, ideal-gas):**

$$GCV_{mix} = \Sigma\, y_i \cdot GCV_i$$

$$GCV_{mix} = (0.85)(37.7) + (0.08)(66.0) + (0.04)(93.2) + 0 + 0$$

$$GCV_{mix} = 32.045 + 5.280 + 3.728 = 41.05\ \text{MJ/Nm}^3$$

**Step 4 — Wobbe Index:**

$$WI = \frac{GCV_{mix}}{\sqrt{SG}} = \frac{41.05}{\sqrt{0.653}} = \frac{41.05}{0.808}$$

$$\boxed{\,WI \approx 50.8\ \text{MJ/Nm}^3\,}$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — LNG Volume Reduction Factor

**Step 1 — Ideal gas volume at STP per kmol:**

$$V_{gas} = \frac{RT}{P} = \frac{(8.314)(273.15)}{101.325} = 22.41\ \text{m}^3/\text{kmol}$$

**Step 2 — LNG volume per kmol of methane:**

$$V_{LNG} = \frac{M_{CH_4}}{\rho_{LNG}} = \frac{16}{450} = 0.0356\ \text{m}^3/\text{kmol}$$

**Step 3 — Volume Reduction Ratio:**

$$\boxed{\,R_{vol} = \frac{22.41}{0.0356} \approx 630\,}$$

**Engineering Insight:** Liquefying natural gas shrinks its volume by **a factor of ~630**, making transoceanic shipping economically viable (otherwise pipeline lengths of 10,000 km would be impossible).

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Property Comparison

| Fuel | GCV (MJ/kg) | H/C Ratio | CO₂/kg fuel (kg) | Stoichiometric AFR | Soot/SOx |
|------|-------------|-----------|-------------------|---------------------|----------|
| **Methane (NG)** | 55.5 | 4.0 | 2.75 | 17.16 | None/None |
| Propane (LPG) | 50.4 | 2.67 | 3.00 | 15.67 | None/None |
| Gasoline | 47.3 | ~1.95 | 3.10 | 14.7 | Possible/Slight |
| Diesel | 45.0 | ~1.85 | 3.20 | 14.5 | Yes/Yes |
| Coal (Bituminous) | 30.0 | 0.6 | 2.40 | 11.0 | Yes/Yes |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Insight:** Methane's **H/C ratio of 4** is the theoretical maximum — it is literally the **hydrogen-richest hydrocarbon possible**, which is why it produces the least CO₂ per joule of any fossil fuel.

---

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Natural Gas Industry Value Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    well[Wellhead Gas] --> sep[Field Separator]
    sep --> sweettreat[Acid Gas Removal Amine Treating]
    sweettreat --> dehyd[Dehydration Glycol Unit]
    dehyd --> ngl[NGL Recovery Cryogenic Turboexpander]
    ngl --> frac[Fractionation Train]
    frac --> c2[C2 Ethylene Feedstock]
    frac --> c3c4[C3 C4 LPG Storage]
    frac --> methane[Pure Methane Pipeline Spec]
    methane --> pipeline[PNG Transmission Pipeline]
    methane --> liquefy[LNG Liquefaction Cascade]
    methane --> compress[CNG Compression Stations]
    liquefy --> lngShip[LNG Tanker]
    compress --> cngVeh[CNG Vehicles]
    pipeline --> pwr[Combined Cycle Power Plant]
    pipeline --> dom[Domestic PNG Households]
    pipeline --> petchem[Petrochemical Steam Cracker]
```

---

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Natural Gas Liquefaction Plant

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    feed[Inlet PNG Gas 30 to 60 bar] --> comp1[Inlet Compressor Stage]
    comp1 --> amine[Amine Scrubber H2S and CO2 Removal]
    amine --> mol[Molecular Sieve Dehydrator]
    mol --> heavy[Heavy Hydrocarbon Removal Scrubber]
    heavy --> lpsep[LP Propane Precool Loop]
    lpsep --> mpsep[MP Ethylene / Mixed Refrigerant]
    mpsep --> hpsep[HP Nitrogen / Mixed Refrigerant]
    hpsep --> lh2[Linde Cold Box Final Liquefaction]
    lh2 --> stor1[Double-Walled LNG Storage Tank]
    stor1 --> loadarm[LNG Loading Arms]
    loadarm --> vessel[LNG Carrier Vessel]
    vapour[Vapour Return Compressor] --> boiloff[Boil-Off Gas BOG Recovery]
    boiloff --> vapour
    vapour --> comp1
```

**Process Notes Embedded in Flowchart:**
- **Acid-gas removal** uses MDEA or DEA solvent; target H₂S < 4 ppm.
- **Dehydration** target: water content < 1 ppm (prevents hydrate formation).
- **Liquefaction trains** are sized for **0.5 – 7.5 MTPA** (million tonnes per annum).
- **BOG recovery** is critical — a 270,000 m³ LNG carrier loses ~0.1%/day to boil-off.

---

### 4.3 Block Diagram — CNG vs LNG vs PNG Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start{Natural Gas Application}
    start -->|Long distance Cross border| pipe[PNG via Pipeline]
    start -->|Overseas Bulk transport| lng[LNG Cryogenic Ship]
    start -->|Urban Vehicle Fleet| cng[CNG 200 to 250 bar]
    start -->|Domestic Heating| pipe
    start -->|Peak shaving Storage| lng
    start -->|Industrial Burner| pipe
    start -->|Heavy Truck Highway| lng[As LCNG]

    pipe --> p1[Operating P 40 to 80 bar T Ambient]
    lng --> p2[Operating T minus 162 C P Near 1 atm]
    cng --> p3[Operating P 200 to 250 bar T Ambient]
```

---

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

> Define **Wobbe Index**. Why is it considered the most important property for fuel gas interchangeability? (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

The **Wobbe Index (WI)** of a fuel gas is defined as the ratio of its gross calorific value to the square root of its specific gravity relative to air.

$$WI = \frac{GCV}{\sqrt{SG}}$$

It is expressed in **MJ/Nm³** (or MJ/m³ at STP). WI is considered the most important property for fuel gas interchangeability because **two fuel gases with identical WI will deliver the same thermal energy through the same burner orifice at the same supply pressure**, ensuring drop-in substitution without re-tuning burners. When NG replaced town gas in European cities, WI was the compatibility metric used to redesign burner nozzles.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

> Distinguish between **LNG** and **CNG** with respect to storage conditions, energy density, and typical applications. (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

| Property | LNG (Liquefied NG) | CNG (Compressed NG) |
|----------|---------------------|----------------------|
| State | Cryogenic liquid | High-pressure gas |
| Temperature | **−162 °C** | Ambient (~25 °C) |
| Pressure | ~1 atm (in tank) | **200 – 250 bar** |
| Energy Density | ~25,000 MJ/m³ | ~9,000 MJ/m³ |
| Volume Reduction | ~630× | ~200× |
| Storage Vessel | Double-walled cryogenic tank | Heavy-walled steel/CGF cylinder |
| Application | Trans-oceanic bulk transport | Urban vehicle fuel |

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]**

**(a) Discuss the composition, classification, and key physico-chemical properties of natural gas. Why is natural gas considered the cleanest fossil fuel?** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Composition (3 marks):**
Natural gas is a multi-component hydrocarbon gas whose typical mol % composition is:

- **Methane (CH₄):** 70 – 90 % (primary combustible)
- **Ethane (C₂H₆):** 0 – 20 %
- **Propane & Butane:** 0 – 10 %
- **Pentanes+:** trace (forms condensate)
- **Inerts:** CO₂ (0 – 8 %), N₂ (0 – 10 %)
- **Impurities:** H₂S (0 – 5 %), He (0 – 0.5 %), H₂O (saturated)

**2. Classification (2 marks):**
Based on geological origin: *associated (dissolved) gas, non-associated (gas-well) gas, coal-bed methane (CBM), shale gas.* Based on H₂S content: *sweet (< 4 ppm H₂S)* and *sour (> 4 ppm H₂S)*.

**3. Why the Cleanest Fossil Fuel (2 marks):**
- **High H/C ratio = 4** (theoretical maximum) → least carbon per unit of hydrogen.
- **No soot formation** (short carbon chains vaporize completely).
- **No SOₓ emissions** when sweetened; negligible particulate matter.
- **Lowest CO₂ per unit of energy released** among all fossil fuels (~50 g CO₂/MJ vs ~95 for coal).
- **No ash or heavy-metal residues** after combustion.

---

**(b) Explain with a flow diagram the **acid-gas removal** and **natural-gas liquid (NGL) recovery** sections of a natural gas processing plant. State typical operating targets. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Acid Gas Removal (Amine Treating) (3 marks):**
Sour gas (containing H₂S, CO₂) is contacted counter-currently with an aqueous amine solution (typically **MDEA, DEA, or MEA**) in a packed/tray absorption column at **35 – 50 °C and 50 – 70 bar**.

$$\text{H}_2\text{S} + \text{R}_2\text{NH} \rightleftharpoons \text{R}_2\text{NH}_2^+ + \text{HS}^-$$

$$\text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{R}_2\text{NH} \rightleftharpoons \text{R}_2\text{NH}_2^+ + \text{HCO}_3^-$$

The rich amine is then sent to a regenerator stripper at **110 – 130 °C**, releasing concentrated H₂S + CO₂ (acid gas) overhead. The H₂S-rich stream is routed to a **Claus process** for elemental sulfur recovery.

**2. Dehydration (1 mark):**
Glycol (TEG) contactor removes water to **< 50 ppm** (often < 1 ppm for cryogenic NGL) to prevent hydrate formation.

**3. NGL Recovery (3 marks):**
The dry, sweetened gas is chilled in a **cryogenic turboexpander** to **−40 to −100 °C**. Joule–Thomson expansion drops the temperature; heavier hydrocarbons condense out. The condensed liquid is sent to a **fractionation train (deethanizer, depropanizer, debutanizer)** to produce pure C₂, C₃, C₄ streams.

**4. Operating Targets:**

| Stream | Specification |
|--------|---------------|
| Sales gas H₂S | < 4 ppm |
| Sales gas CO₂ | < 2 mol % |
| Sales gas H₂O | < 7 lb / MMSCF |
| Sales gas GCV | > 37 MJ/Nm³ |
| Ethane recovery | 85 – 95 % |
| Propane recovery | > 99 % |

---

#### **Question B [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]**

**(a) With relevant equations, derive the stoichiometric air-fuel ratio and the net calorific value (NCV) of pure methane. State the key assumptions. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Combustion Equation:**
$$\text{CH}_4 + 2\,\text{O}_2 + 7.52\,\text{N}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O} + 7.52\,\text{N}_2$$

**Step 2 — Stoichiometric AFR (3 marks):**

$$AFR_{stoich} = \frac{m_{air}}{m_{fuel}} = \frac{(2)(32) + (7.52)(28)}{1 \times 16}$$

$$AFR_{stoich} = \frac{64 + 210.56}{16} = \frac{274.56}{16}$$

$$\boxed{\,AFR_{stoich} = 17.16\ \frac{\text{kg air}}{\text{kg CH}_4}\,}$$

**Step 3 — NCV Derivation (3 marks):**
For methane, the combustion produces 2 mol H₂O. Per kg of CH₄ burned, water mass is:

$$m_{H_2O} = \frac{2 \times 18}{16} = 2.25\ \text{kg H}_2\text{O per kg fuel}$$

Latent heat loss at 25 °C:

$$\Delta H = 2.25 \times 2442 = 5494.5\ \text{kJ/kg} \approx 5.5\ \text{MJ/kg}$$

Given $GCV_{CH_4} = 55.5\ \text{MJ/kg}$:

$$NCV_{CH_4} = 55.5 - 5.5 = 50.0\ \text{MJ/kg}$$

**Step 4 — Assumptions (1 mark):**
1. Complete combustion (100 % conversion to CO₂ + H₂O).
2. Air composition = 79 % N₂ + 21 % O₂ (molar).
3. Water leaves as vapor (justifies subtracting latent heat).
4. Reference state: 25 °C, 1 atm.

---

**(b) Explain the **LNG production process** using a mixed-refluid cascade. Compare LNG with CNG and PNG as delivery modes. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Liquefaction Process (Mixed Refrigerant Cascade — APCI C3MR) (4 marks):**

Natural gas is cooled in stages using three different refrigerants:

- **Pre-cooling stage:** **Propane (C₃H₈)** at progressively lower pressures cools the gas from +30 °C to about **−35 °C**.
- **Liquefaction stage:** **Mixed Refrigerant (MR)** — a blend of N₂, CH₄, C₂H₆, C₃H₈, i-C₄H₁₀ — cools the gas from −35 °C to **−110 °C**.
- **Sub-cooling stage:** A second MR loop cools the partially liquefied gas to the final storage temperature of **−162 °C**.

The refrigerant cycles through **compression → condensation → expansion → vaporization**, absorbing heat at each evaporation step.

**2. Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Feature | PNG | CNG | LNG |
|---------|-----|-----|-----|
| Storage state | Gas | Gas | Liquid |
| Pressure | 40 – 80 bar | 200 – 250 bar | 1 atm |
| Temperature | Ambient | Ambient | −162 °C |
| Volume reduction | 1× | ~200× | ~630× |
| Energy density | 37 MJ/m³ | ~9,000 MJ/m³ | ~25,000 MJ/m³ |
| Capital cost | Pipeline (high) | Compression (medium) | Liquefaction + tankers (very high) |
| Best suited for | Continental grids | Urban fleets, small islands | Trans-oceanic bulk |

---

### 5.1 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in Fuel-Gas Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting air composition:** When computing AFR, students often write $CH_4 + 2O_2 \rightarrow CO_2 + 2H_2O$ and use $AFR = 64/16 = 4$. This is the **oxygen-only** ratio; you must include N₂ ($7.52 \times 28$ kg) for true AFR = **17.16**.
> 2. **GCV vs NCV confusion:** Always state **which calorific value** the question requires; mixing them up costs 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Wobbe Index unit mismatch:** WI is volumetric (MJ/Nm³), but if you mix NCV/GCV *and* volumetric/mass, you get **zero credit**.
> 4. **No mention of N₂ in combustion:** Examiners strictly look for the 79/21 split of air in stoichiometric calculations.
> 5. **Sweet vs Sour threshold:** Always quote **4 ppm H₂S** (not "trace" or "low") when classifying.
> 6. **LNG temperature:** Use **−162 °C (111 K)**, not −161 °C or −160 °C — precision matters.
> 7. **Flow diagrams:** In the 7-mark processing question, examiners deduct up to **3 marks** if no flow diagram/scheme is drawn. Always include one even if crude.

---

### 5.2 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist — Natural Gas**

- ✅ **Definition:** Naturally occurring hydrocarbon gas, predominantly **CH₄ (70–90 %)**.
- ✅ **Boiling point of methane:** **−162 °C** — the temperature of LNG.
- ✅ **Stoichiometric AFR (CH₄):** **17.16 kg air / kg fuel**.
- ✅ **GCV (CH₄):** **55.5 MJ/kg** | **NCV:** **50.0 MJ/kg**.
- ✅ **Volumetric GCV of pipeline NG:** **~38–42 MJ/Nm³**.
- ✅ **Wobbe Index:** $WI = GCV/\sqrt{SG}$ — defines burner interchangeability.
- ✅ **Sweet / Sour threshold:** **4 ppm H₂S**.
- ✅ **LNG volume reduction:** **~630×** vs STP gas.
- ✅ **CNG pressure:** **200 – 250 bar** at ambient temperature.
- ✅ **H/C ratio of CH₄ = 4** — highest among fossil fuels → cleanest combustion.
- ✅ **H₂S removal:** Amine treating (MDEA); **S recovery:** Claus process.
- ✅ **Dehydration target:** < 1 ppm H₂O for cryogenic NGL units.
- ✅ **NGL recovery:** Turboexpander + fractionation (deethanizer → depropanizer → debutanizer).
- ✅ **LNG cascade refrigerants:** C₃H₈ (precool) → MR (liquefaction) → MR (subcool).
- ✅ **Typical uses:** Power (CCGT > 60 % efficient), domestic PNG, CNG vehicles, petrochemical feedstock (ethylene), SMR for H₂.

> **End of Module 1 Notes — Natural Gas | KTU 2024 Scheme | PESFT637**

topics.title: LPG
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT637 - CHEMICAL TECHNOLOGY AND REACTION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** LPG

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of LPG

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Liquefied Petroleum Gas (LPG)** is a flammable mixture of hydrocarbon gases, principally **propane (C₃H₈)** and **butane (C₄H₁₀)**, which is liquefied under modest pressure (approximately 0.7 – 1.4 MPa gauge) at ambient temperature for ease of storage, transport, and handling. In the liquefied state, its volume is reduced by a factor of approximately **260** compared to its gaseous form at standard conditions (1 atm, 15 °C).

According to the **Indian Standard IS 4576** and **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS)** specifications, commercial LPG in India predominantly consists of **butane-isomer mixtures** derived from secondary processing of crude oil (cracking, reforming) and natural gas processing.

### Composition Profile (Typical Indian/International Commercial LPG)

| Component | Chemical Formula | Typical Mole % | Boiling Point (°C) @ 1 atm |
|---|---|---|---|
| Propane | C₃H₈ | 0 – 50 | −42.1 |
| Propylene | C₃H₆ | < 5 | −47.7 |
| n-Butane | n-C₄H₁₀ | 10 – 80 | −0.5 |
| Iso-Butane | i-C₄H₁₀ | 10 – 80 | −11.7 |
| n-Butylene | C₄H₈ | < 5 | −6.3 |
| Pentanes and heavier | C₅⁺ | < 2 | > 27 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Indian Specification (IS 4576):** The hydrocarbon composition must be predominantly C₃ and C₄ (≥ 95 % by mass). The residue (C₅⁺) must be **less than 2 %** by liquid volume at 38 °C. Total sulfur must be **≤ 50 mg/kg** to ensure odorless combustion safety compatibility with mercaptan-based odorants.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Squeezed Balloon" Concept

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:**
> Imagine a large, empty plastic balloon filled with propane vapor at room temperature. The balloon is huge because gas molecules are spread far apart. Now, if you gently squeeze the balloon (apply a modest pressure of about 7–8 bar), the gas condenses into a clear, colorless liquid that occupies just **1/260th** of the original volume — small enough to fit in a domestic cylinder. When the valve is opened and pressure drops, the liquid immediately vaporizes back to gas, ready to burn. **LPG is essentially "energy stored under modest pressure as a liquid."**

This unique dual-phase behavior — **liquid inside the cylinder, gas at the burner** — is the engineering foundation of LPG's portability. Because energy density is high and phase change occurs at convenient ambient temperatures, LPG became the world's most distributed hydrocarbon fuel.

## 1.3 Physical State at a Glance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key State Properties (must memorize for KTU):**
> - **Boiling point of pure propane:** −42.1 °C
> - **Boiling point of pure n-butane:** −0.5 °C
> - **Auto-ignition temperature:** **410 °C – 550 °C** (depends on composition)
> - **Flammability limits in air:** **2.1 % to 9.6 %** by volume (propane), **1.8 % to 8.4 %** (butane)
> - **Calorific value (Gross, higher):** approximately **46 – 50 MJ/kg**
> - **Density of liquid LPG:** approximately **0.51 – 0.58 g/cm³** at 15 °C
> - **Specific gravity (liquid, water = 1):** approximately **0.51 – 0.58**

## 1.4 Why LPG Matters in Chemical Technology

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industrial Significance:**
> LPG occupies a unique niche — it is **cleaner than gasoline**, **portable like diesel**, and **cheaper than natural gas** in regions without pipeline infrastructure. It is simultaneously:
> - A **domestic fuel** (cooking, water heating)
> - An **industrial fuel** (metal cutting, ceramic firing, food processing)
> - A **petrochemical feedstock** (steam cracking → ethylene, propylene)
> - An **automotive fuel** (autogas, per IS 14861)
> - A **refrigerant** (propane, R-290, with low GWP)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Thermodynamic & Physical Principles Behind LPG

The existence of LPG as a stable liquid at ambient temperature depends on three coupled physical phenomena:

1. **Critical Temperature Constraint:** The mixture's critical temperature must be higher than ambient (so that bulk liquefaction is feasible by pressure alone). For propane, $T_c = 96.7 \, °C$; for n-butane, $T_c = 152.0 \, °C$. Both are well above typical ambient (25 – 45 °C in India), which is why LPG stays liquid under modest cylinder pressures.

2. **Vapor Pressure Equilibrium:** Inside a sealed cylinder, liquid and vapor co-exist. The cylinder pressure equals the **saturation (bubble-point) pressure** of the mixture at storage temperature. As ambient temperature rises, vapor pressure rises non-linearly (per Antoine or Cox charts), which dictates the **safe filling ratio**.

3. **Latent Heat of Vaporization:** When LPG leaves the cylinder, it draws its latent heat of vaporization (≈ 425 kJ/kg for propane, ≈ 385 kJ/kg for butane) from the surroundings and from the liquid itself, causing cylinder surface frost. This **auto-refrigeration effect** explains why continuous high draw rates cause cylinder cooling.

## 2.2 Cox Chart — The Engineering Tool for LPG Vapor Pressure

The **Cox Chart** is a graphical plot of $\log_{10}(P)$ versus $(1/T)$ giving an approximately straight line for each hydrocarbon. It is the KTU-preferred tool for:
- Determining bubble-point and dew-point pressures of LPG mixtures
- Estimating composition from dew-point data
- Designing relief valves and flare systems

For a multi-component LPG mixture, **Dalton's Law** and **Raoult's Law** are combined to obtain bubble-point pressure:

$$P_{bubble} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i \, P_i^{sat}(T)$$

where $x_i$ is the mole fraction of component $i$ in the liquid phase and $P_i^{sat}(T)$ is the pure-component saturation pressure at temperature $T$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **all essential formulas** for KTU problems on LPG. Note that absolute value bars use $\vert \cdots \vert$ notation (LaTeX) to avoid markdown table syntax conflicts.

| # | Quantity / Concept | Formula | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Bubble-point pressure (ideal) | $P_{bubble} = \displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i \, P_i^{sat}(T)$ | $x_i$ = mole fraction (liquid); $P_i^{sat}$ in kPa or bar |
| 2 | Dew-point pressure (ideal) | $P_{dew} = \left[\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{y_i}{P_i^{sat}(T)}\right]^{-1}$ | $y_i$ = mole fraction (vapor) |
| 3 | Antoine equation | $\log_{10} P^{sat} = A - \dfrac{B}{C + T}$ | $T$ in °C, $P^{sat}$ in mmHg (constants A, B, C from data table) |
| 4 | Density of LPG liquid (Rackett eqn.) | $\rho_L = \rho_c \cdot Z_c^{-\left(1-T_r\right)^{2/7}}$ | $T_r = T/T_c$, $Z_c$ = compressibility at critical point |
| 5 | Specific gravity of mixture | $SG_{mix} = \displaystyle\sum_{i} w_i \cdot SG_i$ | $w_i$ = mass fraction; $SG_i$ = specific gravity of pure component |
| 6 | Gross calorific value (GCV) of mixture | $GCV_{mix} = \displaystyle\sum_{i} w_i \cdot GCV_i$ | $GCV_i$ in MJ/kg or kcal/kg |
| 7 | Lower flammability limit (Le Chatelier) | $LFL_{mix} = \left[\displaystyle\sum_{i} \dfrac{y_i}{LFL_i}\right]^{-1}$ | $y_i$ = mol fraction of fuel component in fuel mixture |
| 8 | Volume reduction ratio | $VR = \dfrac{V_{gas}(T,P)}{V_{liq}(T,P)}$ | $VR \approx 260$ for propane at 15 °C, 1 atm gas vs. sat. liquid |
| 9 | Vapor mass fraction in cylinder (filling) | $f_v = \dfrac{V_{vapor}}{V_{total}}$ | Typically $f_v \leq 0.20$ (80 % max fill by volume) |
| 10 | Air required for combustion (stoichiometric) | $Air_{st} = \dfrac{100}{21}\left(C + \dfrac{H}{4} - \dfrac{O}{2}\right)$ | Mass of air per 100 kg fuel; C, H, O are mass percentages |
| 11 | Ideal gas for vapor phase | $PV = nRT$ | $R = 8.314 \, J/(mol \cdot K)$ |
| 12 | Filling density (mass limit) | $FD = 0.42 \, kg/L$ (typical) for 50 °C service | FD = mass of LPG / volume of cylinder |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Refinery design:** Bubble-point calculations are used to set **relief valve set pressures** for LPG bullets and Horton spheres.
- **Safety engineering:** LFL (lower flammable limit) computations determine **safe-vent sizing** for indoor storage.
- **Logistics:** Filling density (FD) ensures that even at **50 °C** (highest expected storage temperature), the liquid expansion cannot completely fill the cylinder, eliminating hydraulic rupture risk.
- **Combustion engineering:** Stoichiometric air calculations are needed for **burner nozzle sizing** and **excess air optimization** in LPG-fired furnaces.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Bubble-Point Pressure for a Binary LPG Mixture (Propane + n-Butane)

### Given Data
A two-component LPG mixture has the liquid composition:
- $x_{C_3} = 0.40$ (mole fraction propane)
- $x_{nC_4} = 0.60$ (mole fraction n-butane)
- Temperature $T = 25 \, °C$

Saturation pressures at 25 °C (from standard tables):
- $P^{sat}_{C_3} = 9.39 \, bar$
- $P^{sat}_{nC_4} = 2.43 \, bar$

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — State the governing equation.**
For an ideal multi-component mixture at its bubble-point temperature, the total pressure is the sum of partial pressures (Dalton's Law + Raoult's Law for ideal behavior):

$$P_{total} = \sum_{i} P_i = \sum_{i} x_i \, P_i^{sat}(T)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the two components.**

$$P_{total} = x_{C_3} \cdot P^{sat}_{C_3} + x_{nC_4} \cdot P^{sat}_{nC_4}$$

**Step 3 — Insert numerical values.**

$$P_{total} = (0.40)(9.39) + (0.60)(2.43)$$

**Step 4 — Compute each term.**

$$P_{total} = 3.756 + 1.458$$

**Step 5 — Final result.**

$$\boxed{P_{total} = 5.214 \, bar \approx 5.21 \, bar}$$

### Physical Interpretation
This pressure, **5.21 bar**, is the cylinder pressure you would observe on a pressure gauge if this mixture were stored in a sealed LPG cylinder at 25 °C. The propane component contributes ~72 % of the total pressure because of its higher volatility.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Gross Calorific Value (GCV) of an LPG Mixture

### Given Data
An LPG mixture composition (by mass):
- Propane ($C_3H_8$): $w_{C_3} = 30 \, \%$
- n-Butane ($nC_4H_{10}$): $w_{nC_4} = 50 \, \%$
- Iso-butane ($iC_4H_{10}$): $w_{iC_4} = 20 \, \%$

Standard gross calorific values:
- $GCV_{C_3} = 46.35 \, MJ/kg$
- $GCV_{nC_4} = 45.72 \, MJ/kg$
- $GCV_{iC_4} = 45.55 \, MJ/kg$

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — State the mass-weighted mixing rule.**

$$GCV_{mix} = \sum_{i} w_i \cdot GCV_i$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the three components.**

$$GCV_{mix} = (0.30)(46.35) + (0.50)(45.72) + (0.20)(45.55)$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate each product.**

$$GCV_{mix} = 13.905 + 22.860 + 9.110$$

**Step 4 — Sum the contributions.**

$$GCV_{mix} = 45.875 \, MJ/kg$$

### Final Answer

$$\boxed{GCV_{mix} \approx 45.88 \, MJ/kg}$$

This value is consistent with the **BIS specification of GCV ≥ 41.0 MJ/kg** for Indian commercial LPG.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Volume Reduction Ratio for Propane

### Objective
Show that 1 liter of liquid propane yields approximately 261 liters of gaseous propane at 15 °C and 1 atm.

### Given
- Liquid propane density at 15 °C: $\rho_L = 0.507 \, g/cm^3 = 507 \, kg/m^3$
- Gaseous propane behaves as an ideal gas at 15 °C, 1 atm

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Mass of 1 liter of liquid propane.**

$$m = \rho_L \cdot V_L = 507 \times 10^{-3} = 0.507 \, kg$$

**Step 2 — Convert to moles.**

$$n = \frac{m}{M} = \frac{507}{44.097} = 11.50 \, mol$$

where $M_{C_3H_8} = 44.097 \, g/mol$.

**Step 3 — Apply ideal gas law at $T = 288.15 \, K$, $P = 101.325 \, kPa$.**

$$V_g = \frac{nRT}{P} = \frac{(11.50)(8.314)(288.15)}{101.325}$$

**Step 4 — Numerator evaluation.**

$$V_g = \frac{11.50 \times 8.314 \times 288.15}{101.325} = \frac{27553.6}{101.325}$$

**Step 5 — Final gas volume.**

$$V_g \approx 271.9 \, L$$

**Step 6 — Volume reduction ratio.**

$$\boxed{VR = \frac{V_g}{V_L} = \frac{271.9 \, L}{1.0 \, L} \approx 272}$$

This matches the standard textbook value of **260 – 280** depending on exact conditions. The engineering takeaway: **a 14.2 kg domestic LPG cylinder** (which fits in your kitchen) contains the equivalent of about **3,600 liters** of gaseous propane.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Lower Flammability Limit (Le Chatelier's Rule) for LPG Mixture

### Given
An LPG fuel mixture (mol fractions in fuel):
- Propane: $y_{C_3} = 0.50$
- n-Butane: $y_{nC_4} = 0.50$

Pure-component LFLs in air (vol %):
- $LFL_{C_3} = 2.1 \, \%$
- $LFL_{nC_4} = 1.8 \, \%$

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Le Chatelier's empirical formula:**

$$LFL_{mix} = \left[\sum_{i} \frac{y_i}{LFL_i}\right]^{-1}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute.**

$$LFL_{mix} = \left[\frac{0.50}{2.1} + \frac{0.50}{1.8}\right]^{-1}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate each term.**

$$\frac{0.50}{2.1} = 0.2381 \quad ; \quad \frac{0.50}{1.8} = 0.2778$$

**Step 4 — Sum.**

$$LFL_{mix} = \left[0.2381 + 0.2778\right]^{-1} = (0.5159)^{-1}$$

**Step 5 — Final result.**

$$\boxed{LFL_{mix} \approx 1.94 \, \% \, vol \, in \, air}$$

### Engineering Significance
For a confined storage room to be intrinsically safe (below the lower flammable limit even with 100 % fuel release), the room's free air volume must be sufficient to dilute any LPG leak to below **1.94 %** by volume. This drives ventilation design in LPG godowns.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Propane vs. n-Butane vs. Iso-Butane

| Property | Propane (C₃H₈) | n-Butane (n-C₄H₁₀) | Iso-Butane (i-C₄H₁₀) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Molecular weight (g/mol) | 44.097 | 58.124 | 58.124 |
| Boiling point (°C) @ 1 atm | −42.1 | −0.5 | −11.7 |
| Critical temperature (°C) | 96.7 | 152.0 | 134.9 |
| Critical pressure (bar) | 41.9 | 37.9 | 36.5 |
| Vapor pressure @ 25 °C (bar) | 9.39 | 2.43 | 3.51 |
| GCV (MJ/kg) | 46.35 | 45.72 | 45.55 |
| Density liquid @ 15 °C (kg/m³) | 507 | 584 | 563 |
| LFL in air (vol %) | 2.1 | 1.8 | 1.8 |
| Auto-ignition temp (°C) | 470 | 365 | 460 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics — LPG Process Flow

## 4.1 LPG Recovery & Bottling Block Diagram

Below is the comprehensive block-level functional architecture for **LPG recovery from refinery off-gases** and **cylinder bottling**. Each node represents a process unit; arrows denote material/energy flows.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["CRUDE OIL"] --> B["ATMOSPHERIC DISTILLATION UNIT"]
    B --> C["NAPHTHA STREAM"]
    B --> D["VACUUM RESIDUE"]
    C --> E["CATALYTIC CRACKER FCC"]
    E --> F["FCC OFF GAS C3 C4 RICH"]
    F --> G["AMINE SCRUBBER H2S REMOVAL"]
    G --> H["DEETHANIZER C1 C2 SEPARATION"]
    H --> I["DEPROPANIZER C3 C4 SPLIT"]
    I --> J["C3 STREAM PROPANE"]
    I --> K["C4 STREAM N BUTANE I BUTANE"]
    J --> L["PROPANE STORAGE BULLET"]
    K --> M["BUTANE STORAGE BULLET"]
    L --> N["LPG BLENDING MIXER"]
    M --> N
    N --> O["ETHYL MERCAPTAN ODORANT INJECTION"]
    O --> P["QUALITY LAB IS4576 TEST"]
    P --> Q["LPG STORAGE SPHERES"]
    Q --> R["CYLINDER FILLING CAROUSEL"]
    R --> S["WEIGHT CHECK LEAK TEST"]
    S --> T["DOMESTIC CYLINDER 14.2 KG"]
    S --> U["COMMERCIAL CYLINDER 19 KG 47.5 KG"]
```

### Node-by-Node Functional Description

| Node | Process Unit | Function |
|---|---|---|
| A | Crude Oil Feed | Atmospheric & vacuum distillation feed |
| B | Atmospheric Distillation | Splits crude into fractions by boiling point |
| C | Naphtha Stream | Light distillate sent to FCC |
| E | Catalytic Cracker (FCC) | Cracks heavy hydrocarbons to gasoline + LPG-range gases |
| F | FCC Off-gas | C3 + C4 rich stream containing acid gases |
| G | Amine Scrubber | Removes H₂S to < 5 mg/kg (BIS sulfur limit) |
| H | Deethanizer | Strips methane & ethane (C1, C2) overhead |
| I | Depropanizer | Splits C3 (overhead) from C4 (bottoms) |
| J, K | Pure C3 / C4 Streams | Propane product and butane product |
| L, M | Storage Bullets | Pressure vessels at ~10 bar |
| N | LPG Blender | Mixes propane + butane to meet winter/summer grade |
| O | Mercaptan Injection | Adds ethyl mercaptan (5–10 mg/kg) for leak detection |
| P | Quality Lab | Tests per IS 4576: composition, vapor pressure, residue |
| Q | Horton Spheres | Large-volume storage (up to 5000 tons) |
| R | Carousel Filler | Automated filling of 14.2 kg cylinders |
| S | Weight & Leak Test | Each cylinder verified before dispatch |

## 4.2 LPG Phase Behavior Schematic (P-T Diagram for Storage)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["PROPANE LIQUID INSIDE CYLINDER"] -->|Heat from ambient| B["PROPANE LIQUID PLUS VAPOR COEXIST"]
    B -->|Higher T| C["SUPERCRITICAL FLUID"]
    B -->|Valve opened| D["PROPANE VAPOR TO BURNER"]
    D -->|Air mix| E["COMBUSTION FLAME"]
    C -->|Cool| B
```

## 4.3 Cylinder Anatomy Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["LPG CYLINDER"] --> B["SHELL COLLAR"]
    A --> C["FOOT RING"]
    A --> D["VALVE WITH PRD PRESSURE RELIEF"]
    D --> E["BURST DISC 26 BAR"]
    D --> F["SPRING RELIEF 26 BAR"]
    A --> G["DIP TUBE LIQUID OUTLET"]
    A --> H["VAPOR SPACE"]
    H --> I["REGULATOR CONNECTION"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Define LPG. What are the main constituents of commercial LPG?**

**Model Answer:**
Liquefied Petroleum Gas (LPG) is a mixture of hydrocarbon gases — primarily **propane (C₃H₈)** and **butane (C₄H₁₀)** — stored under modest pressure (≈ 7–10 bar) in liquid form at ambient temperature. As per **IS 4576**, commercial LPG must contain at least **95 % C₃ and C₄ hydrocarbons** by mass, with the residue (C₅⁺) limited to **< 2 % by liquid volume at 38 °C**. Minor components include propylene, butylenes, and trace amounts of sulfur (≤ 50 mg/kg). The gas is odorized with **ethyl mercaptan** for leak detection since the parent hydrocarbons are naturally odorless.

### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]**
**State any three important physical properties of LPG and explain why LPG is stored as a liquid.**

**Model Answer:**
Three important physical properties of LPG:
1. **Low boiling point:** Propane boils at −42.1 °C; n-butane at −0.5 °C. These are below ambient temperature, so the substances exist as vapors at atmospheric pressure but condense to liquid under modest pressure.
2. **High calorific value:** Gross calorific value is **46 – 50 MJ/kg**, more than double that of natural gas per unit mass.
3. **Narrow flammability range:** 2.1 – 9.6 % (propane) and 1.8 – 8.4 % (butane) by volume in air, requiring controlled ventilation for safe handling.

**Why liquid?** Because in liquid form, LPG occupies **only 1/260th** of its gaseous volume at ambient conditions, enabling compact storage in portable cylinders. A 14.2 kg domestic cylinder would otherwise require a balloon of over 3,000 liters capacity as a gas.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Main Paper — Module 1]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** A LPG mixture has the following composition by mole: 35 % propane, 60 % n-butane, 5 % iso-butane. Using the vapor pressure data at 40 °C (Propane: 13.7 bar, n-Butane: 3.7 bar, iso-Butane: 5.3 bar), calculate the **(i)** bubble-point pressure and **(ii)** composition of the first bubble of vapor formed. Also determine the **(iii)** dew-point pressure of this mixture if it were all vapor at 40 °C.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the BIS specification IS 4576 for LPG in detail. List at least six quality parameters and their permissible limits.

---

### QUESTION B **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 — Module 1 Alternative]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** A refinery produces LPG by blending propane and n-butane. The blend composition by mass is: 25 % propane (GCV = 46.35 MJ/kg) and 75 % n-butane (GCV = 45.72 MJ/kg). Calculate: **(i)** the gross calorific value of the blend per kg, **(ii)** the calorific value per liter of liquid blend (using densities ρ_propane = 507 kg/m³, ρ_n-butane = 584 kg/m³), and **(iii)** the volume of stoichiometric air required at NTP per kg of blend, given that propane and butane combust as:

$$C_3H_8 + 5O_2 \rightarrow 3CO_2 + 4H_2O$$
$$C_4H_{10} + 6.5O_2 \rightarrow 4CO_2 + 5H_2O$$

Air contains 21 % O₂ by volume at NTP (22.4 L/mol).

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, describe the **LPG recovery process from a catalytic cracker (FCC)**. Mention the role of the deethanizer, depropanizer, and amine scrubber in the process.

---

## COMPLETE MODEL SOLUTIONS

### Solution for Question A

**(a) (i) Bubble-point pressure at 40 °C**

Using Raoult-Dalton's Law for an ideal solution:

$$P_{bubble} = \sum_{i} x_i \cdot P_i^{sat}$$

Substituting:
- $x_{C_3} = 0.35$, $P^{sat}_{C_3} = 13.7 \, bar$
- $x_{nC_4} = 0.60$, $P^{sat}_{nC_4} = 3.7 \, bar$
- $x_{iC_4} = 0.05$, $P^{sat}_{iC_4} = 5.3 \, bar$

$$P_{bubble} = (0.35)(13.7) + (0.60)(3.7) + (0.05)(5.3)$$
$$P_{bubble} = 4.795 + 2.220 + 0.265 = 7.28 \, bar$$

**(a) (ii) Composition of first bubble of vapor**

Using modified Raoult's Law: $y_i = \dfrac{x_i \cdot P_i^{sat}}{P_{bubble}}$

For propane: $y_{C_3} = \dfrac{(0.35)(13.7)}{7.28} = \dfrac{4.795}{7.28} = 0.659$
For n-butane: $y_{nC_4} = \dfrac{(0.60)(3.7)}{7.28} = \dfrac{2.220}{7.28} = 0.305$
For iso-butane: $y_{iC_4} = \dfrac{(0.05)(5.3)}{7.28} = \dfrac{0.265}{7.28} = 0.036$

**Check:** $0.659 + 0.305 + 0.036 = 1.000$ ✓

The first vapor bubble is enriched in the more volatile propane (66 %) compared to the original liquid (35 %).

**(a) (iii) Dew-point pressure (assuming original composition is now vapor $y_i$)**

$$P_{dew} = \left[\sum_i \frac{y_i}{P_i^{sat}}\right]^{-1}$$

$$P_{dew} = \left[\frac{0.35}{13.7} + \frac{0.60}{3.7} + \frac{0.05}{5.3}\right]^{-1}$$

$$P_{dew} = \left[0.02555 + 0.16216 + 0.00943\right]^{-1} = \left[0.19714\right]^{-1}$$

$$P_{dew} \approx 5.07 \, bar$$

**(b) BIS Specification IS 4576 — Six Key Quality Parameters**

| Parameter | Permissible Limit |
|---|---|
| 1. Total C₃ + C₄ hydrocarbons | ≥ 95 % by mass |
| 2. Residue (C₅ and heavier) | ≤ 2 % by liquid volume at 38 °C |
| 3. Total sulfur content | ≤ 50 mg/kg |
| 4. Hydrogen sulfide (H₂S) | Pass negative test (lead acetate paper test) |
| 5. Vapor pressure at 40 °C | ≤ 16 bar (Gauge) |
| 6. Corrosion (copper strip, 1 hr at 38 °C) | Not more than No. 1 ASTM classification |
| 7. Odor | Distinctive and disagreeable (mercaptan) |
| 8. Density at 15 °C | 0.51 – 0.58 kg/L |

---

### Solution for Question B

**(a) (i) Gross calorific value of the blend per kg**

Using mass-weighted mixing rule:

$$GCV_{mix} = (w_{C_3})(GCV_{C_3}) + (w_{nC_4})(GCV_{nC_4})$$
$$GCV_{mix} = (0.25)(46.35) + (0.75)(45.72)$$
$$GCV_{mix} = 11.5875 + 34.29 = 45.88 \, MJ/kg$$

**(a) (ii) Calorific value per liter of liquid blend**

Step 1 — Density of liquid blend (mass-weighted):

$$\rho_{mix} = (0.25)(507) + (0.75)(584) = 126.75 + 438 = 564.75 \, kg/m^3$$

Step 2 — Mass per liter:

$$m/L = 0.56475 \, kg/L$$

Step 3 — GCV per liter:

$$GCV/L = 45.88 \times 0.56475 = 25.91 \, MJ/L$$

**(a) (iii) Stoichiometric air required per kg**

For propane (0.25 kg): from stoichiometry, $C_3H_8 + 5O_2$ → moles of O₂ per kg propane:
- 1 kg propane = 1000/44.097 = 22.68 mol
- O₂ needed = 5 × 22.68 = 113.4 mol
- Mass of O₂ = 113.4 × 32 = 3628.8 g = 3.629 kg
- Mass of air = 3.629 / 0.233 = 15.58 kg

For n-butane (0.75 kg): $C_4H_{10} + 6.5O_2$
- 1 kg n-butane = 1000/58.124 = 17.21 mol
- O₂ needed = 6.5 × 17.21 = 111.9 mol
- Mass of O₂ = 111.9 × 32 = 3580.8 g = 3.581 kg
- Mass of air = 3.581 / 0.233 = 15.37 kg

Total air per kg of blend:
$$m_{air} = (0.25)(15.58) + (0.75)(15.37) = 3.895 + 11.528 = 15.42 \, kg \, air/kg \, fuel$$

Volume of air at NTP: Using air density at NTP ≈ 1.293 kg/m³:
$$V_{air} = 15.42 / 1.293 = 11.93 \, m^3/kg \, fuel = 11,930 \, L/kg \, fuel$$

**(b) Process Flow Description**

(Refer to the Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.1 for the complete flow.)

- **Amine Scrubber (G):** Removes H₂S from FCC off-gas using aqueous MEA/DEA solution to prevent sulfur poisoning downstream and meet BIS sulfur spec.
- **Deethanizer (H):** Removes lighter C1 (methane) and C2 (ethane) fractions overhead, which are sent to fuel gas. This ensures the LPG stream contains only C₃ and C₄.
- **Depropanizer (I):** Separates propane (overhead) from butane (bottoms), allowing the refiner to market pure propane and pure butane or blend them to meet seasonal demand (winter grade = higher propane for cold-start).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting the 80 % filling rule:** Many students calculate filling density without accounting for the **20 % vapor space** required by law. Cylinders are filled to **only 80 % volumetric capacity** at 50 °C.
> 2. **Mixing up mole and mass fractions:** In $GCV_{mix}$ use **mass** fractions; in $P_{bubble}$ use **mole** fractions. This is the single most common error in KTU scripts.
> 3. **Units in stoichiometric air problems:** Air requirement is often computed per kg fuel; students forget the **O₂ mass fraction in air is 0.233** (not 0.21 by volume). Using the wrong basis gives 10 % error.
> 4. **Ignoring the Odorant Step:** Students frequently describe LPG production but forget the **ethyl mercaptan injection step (O)**. Examiners allocate 1–2 marks specifically for this safety step.
> 5. **Bubble-point vs. dew-point confusion:** Remember — bubble-point is for a **liquid** mixture; dew-point is for a **vapor** mixture. Reverse the role of $x_i$ and $y_i$ in the formulas.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Recap — Must Memorize for KTU Module 1**

- **Definition:** LPG = liquefied C₃ + C₄ hydrocarbons stored under 7–10 bar at ambient.
- **Volume reduction:** ≈ **1 : 260 to 1 : 280** (liquid : gas).
- **Indian standard:** **IS 4576** — 95 % C₃ + C₄ by mass, residue ≤ 2 %, sulfur ≤ 50 mg/kg.
- **Odorant:** **Ethyl mercaptan (C₂H₅SH)** — added at 5–10 mg/kg for leak detection.
- **Cylinder pressure at 25 °C:** Pure propane ≈ 9.4 bar; pure n-butane ≈ 2.4 bar; typical blend ≈ 5–7 bar.
- **Filling density:** **0.42 kg/L** (max) for 50 °C service; **80 % volumetric fill** rule.
- **Bubble-point formula:** $P_{bubble} = \sum_i x_i P_i^{sat}(T)$
- **Dew-point formula:** $P_{dew} = \left[\sum_i \dfrac{y_i}{P_i^{sat}(T)}\right]^{-1}$
- **GCV of mixture:** Mass-weighted average (≈ 46 MJ/kg for commercial LPG).
- **LFL (Le Chatelier):** $LFL_{mix} = \left[\sum_i \dfrac{y_i}{LFL_i}\right]^{-1}$
- **Auto-ignition temperature:** **410 – 550 °C** (propane = 470 °C).
- **Flammability range:** Propane **2.1 – 9.6 %**; n-butane **1.8 – 8.4 %** (vol in air).
- **Process sequence:** FCC → Amine scrub → Deethanizer → Depropanizer → Blender → Mercaptan → Lab → Spheres → Bottling.
- **Safety devices on cylinder:** Pressure Relief Device (PRD), burst disc, foot ring, dip tube, vapor space.
- **Applications:** Domestic cooking, autogas, industrial fuel, petrochemical feedstock (steam cracking), refrigerant (R-290).
- **Stoichiometric air:** ~**15.4 kg air/kg LPG** (or ~12 m³ air/kg LPG at NTP).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sulphur and Sulphuric Acid
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT637 - CHEMICAL TECHNOLOGY AND REACTION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Sulphur and Sulphuric Acid

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ⚙️ SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Sulphur?

**Sulphur (S)** is a non-metallic chemical element belonging to **Group 16 (Chalcogens)** of the periodic table, with atomic number **16** and atomic mass **32.06 g/mol**. It is a yellow, brittle, crystalline solid at standard conditions (melting point: **115.21 °C**, boiling point: **444.6 °C**, density: **2.07 g/cm³**). Sulphur is the quintessential *workhorse* element of the heavy chemical industry, with the majority of globally produced sulphur being converted into **Sulphuric Acid (H₂SO₄)** — often called the **"King of Chemicals"** because more of it is produced than any other industrial chemical on Earth (over **280 million tonnes/year** globally).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sulphuric Acid is officially called the "King of Chemicals"** because its production volume is the primary indicator of a nation's industrial strength and is a key input in over **200 industrial processes** including fertilizers, petroleum refining, metallurgy, and textiles.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 💡

Think of **sulphur** as the *raw clay* and **sulphuric acid** as the *fired ceramic pot* of the chemical world. Just as clay is abundant but useless until shaped and fired, raw sulphur is a pale yellow mineral that only becomes an industrial powerhouse after a sequence of controlled oxidation reactions (burning → catalytic conversion → absorption) that finally yield sulphuric acid — the "pot" that is then used to make fertilizers, refine petroleum, and process metals.

## 1.2 Occurrence & Sources of Sulphur

Sulphur is found in nature in three primary forms:

| Form | Occurrence | Key Reserves |
|------|------------|--------------|
| **Native (Elemental) Sulphur** | Found in underground deposits, often above salt domes | Texas & Louisiana (USA), Poland, Mexico |
| **Combined as Sulphides** | Pyrites (FeS₂), Galena (PbS), Zinc blende (ZnS) | Worldwide |
| **Combined as Sulphates** | Gypsum (CaSO₄·2H₂O), Epsom salt (MgSO₄·7H₂O) | Sedimentary basins globally |

> [!NOTE]
> **Native sulphur** is the most economically significant source. It is extracted using the **Frasch Process** — a hot-water mining technique that injects superheated water (~165 °C) to melt the underground sulphur, which is then lifted to the surface using compressed air. This is analogous to *using a giant underground kettle to brew sulphur tea and siphon it out*.

## 1.3 What is Sulphuric Acid?

**Sulphuric Acid (H₂SO₄)** is a strong diprotic mineral acid, with a molecular mass of **98.08 g/mol**, density of **1.84 g/cm³** (at 98.3% concentration), and boiling point of **337 °C**. It is highly corrosive, hygroscopic (water-absorbing), and acts as both a strong acid and a powerful dehydrating agent.

> [!NOTE]
> **Properties of Commercial Sulphuric Acid (100%):**
> - Boiling Point: **337 °C**
> - Freezing Point: **10.4 °C**
> - Viscosity: **24.5 cP** at 25 °C
> - Heat of Dilution: Highly exothermic (releases significant heat when mixed with water)
> - **Always add acid to water, never water to acid** (to prevent violent exothermic splashing — the "Add Acid" rule of thumb).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🧠 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Contact Process — The Industrial Backbone

The **Contact Process** is the modern, dominant industrial route for manufacturing sulphuric acid. It superseded the older *Lead Chamber Process* due to its ability to produce higher concentration acid (98–100%) and its use of a solid catalyst (V₂O₅) for the critical SO₂ → SO₃ oxidation step.

### The Three-Stage Logic:

**Stage 1 — Sulphur Burning (S → SO₂):**
Elemental sulphur is burnt in dry air to generate sulphur dioxide:
- **S (s) + O₂ (g) → SO₂ (g)** | ΔH = **−296.8 kJ/mol**
- The burner operates at **~1000–1100 °C** with excess air (O₂ : S ratio ≈ 2:1) to keep the reaction going and to ensure complete combustion.

**Stage 2 — Catalytic Oxidation (SO₂ → SO₃):**
The SO₂ is passed over a **Vanadium Pentoxide (V₂O₅)** catalyst bed:
- **2 SO₂ (g) + O₂ (g) ⇌ 2 SO₃ (g)** | ΔH = **−196.6 kJ/mol**
- This is a **reversible, exothermic** reaction. By **Le Chatelier's Principle**, low temperature favours SO₃, but low temperature slows the rate. The **optimum temperature is ~410–440 °C** (compromise between yield and rate). Pressure has minimal effect due to negligible volume change.

**Stage 3 — Absorption (SO₃ → H₂SO₄):**
The SO₃ is absorbed in **98% H₂SO₄** (NOT water) to form **oleum (H₂S₂O₇)**, which is then diluted with water to yield commercial 98% H₂SO₄:
- **SO₃ (g) + H₂SO₄ (l) → H₂S₂O₇ (l)** (Oleum formation)
- **H₂S₂O₇ + H₂O → 2 H₂SO₄**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why 98% H₂SO₄ and not water for SO₃ absorption?**
> Direct absorption in water creates a dense, persistent **mist of H₂SO₄** due to the highly exothermic reaction with water vapour. This mist is uncondensable and causes environmental pollution. Using 98% acid avoids mist formation because the vapour pressure of SO₃ over 98% acid is the lowest of all concentrations.

### Why 'Why' and 'How' Matter:

- **Why Vanadium Pentoxide (V₂O₅)?** It replaced the older toxic and less efficient **Platinum (Pt)** catalyst in the 1920s. V₂O₅ is cheaper, more poison-resistant (especially to arsenic), and has a longer operational life.
- **How is the multi-stage conversion achieved industrially?** In modern *Double Contact Double Absorption (DCDA)* plants, gas passes through **2 to 4 catalyst beds** with inter-stage cooling, achieving an overall **SO₂ conversion of 99.5–99.8%**.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet & High-Yield Constants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet — Sulphuric Acid Manufacture**

| Parameter / Equation | Formula / Value | Units / Conditions |
|----------------------|-----------------|---------------------|
| Atomic Mass of S | **32.06** | g/mol |
| Molecular Mass of H₂SO₄ | **98.08** | g/mol |
| Density of conc. H₂SO₄ | **1.84** | g/cm³ (at 98.3%) |
| Boiling Point (100% H₂SO₄) | **337** | °C |
| Combustion Enthalpy (S → SO₂) | **ΔH = −296.8** | kJ/mol |
| Oxidation Enthalpy (SO₂ → SO₃) | **ΔH = −196.6** | kJ/mol |
| Optimum Catalyst Temp | **410–440** | °C |
| Catalyst | **V₂O₅** with K₂O promoter | Solid porous pellets |
| Equilibrium Constant (Kp) at 700 K | **~2.5** | — |
| Conversion in Single Contact | **~70%** | per pass (equilibrium limited) |
| Conversion in DCDA Plant | **99.5–99.8%** | overall |
| Air to SO₂ ratio in feed | **~1.4:1 to 1.5:1** | molar basis |
| Heat of Dilution of H₂SO₄ | **−93 to −880** | kJ/kg acid (concentration dependent) |

### Real-World Utility:
- **~60%** of sulphuric acid is used in **phosphate fertilizer** production (superphosphate, ammonium sulphate).
- **~20%** in **petroleum refining** (alkylation, catalytic cracking regeneration).
- **~10%** in **metallurgy** (leaching of copper, zinc, uranium ores).
- **~10%** in **chemical manufacturing** (HCl, HNO₃, dyes, detergents, explosives).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📐 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Equilibrium Conversion of SO₂ to SO₃

The oxidation of SO₂ is the most analytically significant step in the Contact Process. Let us derive the equilibrium relationship for conversion.

### Step 1: Define the Reaction Stoichiometry

$$
\begin{aligned}
2\,\text{SO}_2(g) + \text{O}_2(g) &\rightleftharpoons 2\,\text{SO}_3(g) \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Initial Molar Setup (Basis: 2 moles SO₂)

Let the **initial feed** contain 2 moles SO₂ and 1 mole O₂ (stoichiometric air). Let **x** be the moles of SO₂ reacted at equilibrium.

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Initial: } & n_{\text{SO}_2} = 2, \quad n_{\text{O}_2} = 1, \quad n_{\text{SO}_3} = 0 \\
&\text{Reaction: } & -2x, \quad -x, \quad +2x \\
&\text{Equilibrium: } & n_{\text{SO}_2} = 2-2x, \quad n_{\text{O}_2} = 1-x, \quad n_{\text{SO}_3} = 2x \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Total Moles at Equilibrium

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{\text{total}} &= (2-2x) + (1-x) + 2x \\
&= 3 - x
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Equilibrium Constant Expression in Terms of Mole Fraction

Let the total pressure be **P**. Using partial pressures $p_i = y_i \cdot P$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_p &= \frac{p_{\text{SO}_3}^2}{p_{\text{SO}_2}^2 \cdot p_{\text{O}_2}} \\
&= \frac{\left(\frac{2x}{3-x}\,P\right)^2}{\left(\frac{2-2x}{3-x}\,P\right)^2 \cdot \left(\frac{1-x}{3-x}\,P\right)} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Simplify the Kp Expression

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_p &= \frac{4x^2 \,(3-x)^2 \,P^2}{(3-x)^2 \cdot 4(1-x)^2 \cdot (1-x)\,P} \\
&= \frac{x^2}{(1-x)^3 \cdot P}
\end{aligned}
$$

Thus, the **fundamental KTU relation** for SO₂ conversion is:

$$
\boxed{K_p = \frac{x^2}{(1-x)^3 \, P}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation for KTU Board Exams:** Since the reaction is exothermic, **Kₚ decreases with temperature**. So a higher equilibrium constant favours a smaller x (i.e., lower conversion). Conversely, **lowering temperature increases conversion** but reduces the catalytic reaction rate — hence the industrial use of intermediate temperature (410–440 °C).

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Process Design Decisions

| Process Variable | Effect on Conversion | Effect on Rate | Industrial Choice |
|------------------|---------------------|----------------|-------------------|
| **Temperature ↑** | ↓ (exothermic) | ↑ (Arrhenius) | Optimum at **410–440 °C** |
| **Pressure ↑** | ↑ (negligible as Δn = −1) | ↑ (slight) | Operate at **1–2 atm** (cost saving) |
| **Excess Air ↑** | ↑ (shifts equilibrium) | ↑ | Use **1.4:1 O₂:SO₂** |
| **Catalyst Activity ↑** | No effect (eqm only) | ↑↑↑ | V₂O₅ with K₂O on SiO₂ |
| **Number of Beds ↑** | ↑ (Le Chatelier) | ↑ | Use **3–4 beds** + intercooling |

### Step 6: Worked Numerical Illustration

A gas mixture with composition: **SO₂ = 7%, O₂ = 11%, N₂ = 82%** enters the converter at 700 K. Find the equilibrium conversion of SO₂ if $K_p = 0.5 \text{ atm}^{-1}$ at 700 K, P = 1 atm.

Let basis = 100 mol feed. Then SO₂ = 7, O₂ = 11, N₂ = 82.

Let $x$ = moles of SO₂ reacted. Stoichiometry: 2 SO₂ + O₂ → 2 SO₃

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{\text{SO}_2} &= 7 - 2x \\
n_{\text{O}_2} &= 11 - x \\
n_{\text{SO}_3} &= 2x \\
n_{\text{N}_2} &= 82 \\
n_{\text{total}} &= 100 - x
\end{aligned}
$$

Applying the Kp relation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_p &= \frac{(2x)^2 \cdot (100-x)}{(7-2x)^2 \cdot (11-x) \cdot P} \\
0.5 &= \frac{4x^2 (100-x)}{(7-2x)^2 (11-x) \cdot 1}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Solving numerically** (iterative trial) gives $x \approx 3.05$, so SO₂ conversion = $2x/7 = 6.10/7 \approx \mathbf{87.1\%}$. This validates the high-efficiency claim of DCDA plants.

## 3.3 Material of Construction (MOC) — The Corrosion Problem

Sulphuric acid is one of the most aggressive industrial chemicals. The choice of MOC varies with concentration and temperature:

- **Dilute H₂SO₄ (< 70%):** Use **lead** (Pb) or **fibre-reinforced plastic (FRP)** tanks. Steel corrodes rapidly.
- **Concentrated H₂SO₄ (> 90%):** Use **carbon steel** — concentrated acid passivates the steel surface by forming a protective iron sulphate layer.
- **Hot, hot oleum:** Use **cast iron** or **stainless steel (SS-316)**.
- **Drying tower & absorber internals:** Use **chemical lead** with acid-proof brick lining.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often write "steel tanks for all sulphuric acid storage." This is **wrong** for dilute acid. Always specify the **concentration range** before naming the MOC.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🏗️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Contact Process

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **unit-to-unit interactions** in a modern **Double Contact Double Absorption (DCDA) Plant**. It traces the path of gas (red arrows indicate gas flow, blue arrows indicate acid flow) and identifies each major unit operation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Sulphur Feed] --> B[Sulphur Melter and Filter]
    B --> C[Sulphur Furnace / Burner at 1100C]
    C --> D[Waste Heat Boiler WHB at 450C]
    D --> E[Drying Tower with 98% H2SO4]
    E --> F[Main Blower Compressor]
    F --> G[1st Catalyst Converter Bed 600C]
    G --> H[Interstage Cooler 1]
    H --> I[2nd Catalyst Converter Bed 470C]
    I --> J[Interstage Cooler 2]
    J --> K[3rd Catalyst Converter Bed 430C]
    K --> L[1st Absorption Tower with 98.3% H2SO4]
    L --> M[Interstage Acid Cooler]
    M --> N[4th Catalyst Converter Bed 410C]
    N --> O[2nd Final Absorption Tower]
    O --> P[Oleum Product Storage]
    L --> Q[Circulating Acid Cooler]
    Q --> E
    O --> R[98% Product Acid Storage Tank]
```

### Functional Explanation of Each Block:

| Block ID | Unit Operation | Function |
|----------|----------------|----------|
| **B** | Sulphur Melter & Filter | Melts solid sulphur at ~135 °C; removes ash, dirt |
| **C** | Sulphur Furnace | Burns sulphur with dry air to produce SO₂ at ~1100 °C |
| **D** | Waste Heat Boiler (WHB) | Recovers waste heat to generate steam; cools gas to ~450 °C |
| **E** | Drying Tower | Scrubs feed air with 98% H₂SO₄ to remove moisture (critical — moisture forms acid mist) |
| **F** | Main Blower | Provides pressure head to push gas through catalyst beds |
| **G, I, K, N** | Catalyst Converter Beds | V₂O₅ catalyst beds for SO₂ → SO₃ oxidation |
| **H, J** | Interstage Coolers | Reheat tubes / heat exchangers maintain optimum temperature |
| **L, O** | Absorption Towers | Pack columns absorbing SO₃ in 98% H₂SO₄ → oleum |
| **M, Q** | Acid Coolers | Remove heat of absorption & heat of dilution |
| **P, R** | Product Storage | Oleum & 98% product acid storage |

## 4.2 Simplified Single-Line Flow of the Contact Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Sulphur + Air] -->|Combustion| S2[SO2 Gas Mixture]
    S2 -->|Catalytic Oxidation V2O5| S3[SO3 Gas]
    S3 -->|Absorption in 98% H2SO4| S4[Oleum H2S2O7]
    S4 -->|Dilution with H2O| S5[Commercial H2SO4 98%]
```

## 4.3 Process Conditions Summary Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T1[Furnace: 1000-1100 C] --> T2[WHB Outlet: 450 C]
    T2 --> T3[Bed 1 Inlet: 600 C]
    T3 --> T4[Bed 1 Outlet: 440 C]
    T4 --> T5[Bed 2 Inlet: 460 C]
    T5 --> T6[Bed 2 Outlet: 430 C]
    T6 --> T7[Bed 3 Inlet: 440 C]
    T7 --> T8[Bed 3 Outlet: 415 C]
    T8 --> T9[1st Absorber: 80-100 C]
    T9 --> T10[Bed 4 Inlet: 420 C]
    T10 --> T11[Bed 4 Outlet: 410 C]
    T11 --> T12[2nd Absorber: 70-90 C]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why multiple beds with cooling?**
> A single catalyst bed at 600 °C would reach only ~70% conversion because equilibrium limits it. By dividing the catalyst into 3–4 beds with intercooling, the temperature profile zig-zags down the curve, and each subsequent bed operates closer to optimum. **DCDA removes unreacted SO₂ between beds, pushing final conversion to 99.7%.**

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] List the major industrial uses of sulphuric acid. Why is it called the "King of Chemicals"?** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**
Sulphuric acid (H₂SO₄) is called the **"King of Chemicals"** because its annual production volume is the highest of any industrial chemical (over 280 million tonnes/year worldwide), serving as a key input to over 200 industries. **Major uses:**

- (i) **Fertilizer Industry (~60%):** Manufacturing of superphosphate, ammonium sulphate, and phosphate fertilizers.
- (ii) **Petroleum Refining (~20%):** Used in alkylation, catalytic cracking, and treatment of crude oil.
- (iii) **Chemical Manufacturing (~10%):** Production of HCl, HNO₃, detergents, dyes, and explosives.
- (iv) **Metallurgy (~10%):** Leaching of copper, zinc, and uranium ores, pickling of steel, and electro-refining.

*(3 marks — 1 for title, 2 for industrial uses.)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024] State the role of V₂O₅ catalyst in the Contact Process. Why is it preferred over platinum?** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**
**Role of V₂O₅ catalyst:** V₂O₅ (vanadium pentoxide) catalyses the oxidation of SO₂ to SO₃ in the Contact Process. The reaction is reversible and exothermic, and V₂O₅ provides an active surface that lowers the activation energy without shifting the equilibrium.

**Why preferred over platinum:**
- (i) **Cost:** V₂O₅ is far cheaper than platinum.
- (ii) **Poison resistance:** V₂O₅ is much more resistant to catalyst poisoning by arsenic and other impurities present in feed gases.
- (iii) **Operating range:** V₂O₅ is effective over a wide temperature range (400–600 °C), while Pt loses activity at high temperatures.
- (iv) **Longevity:** V₂O₅ has a longer operational life and is easier to regenerate.

*(3 marks — 1.5 for role, 1.5 for Pt comparison.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Question A (14 Marks)**
>
> **(a)** Describe with a neat flow diagram the manufacturing of sulphuric acid by the **Contact Process**, clearly listing the operating conditions, catalyst, and key reactions. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A feed gas mixture containing 7% SO₂, 11% O₂, and 82% N₂ enters the converter at 700 K. The equilibrium constant $K_p = 0.5 \text{ atm}^{-1}$. Calculate the equilibrium conversion of SO₂ at 1 atm total pressure. **(7 Marks)**

### **Model Solution for Q1(a):**

**Flow Diagram (Block Schematic):**
The **Contact Process** consists of three main stages:
1. **Sulphur Burning:** Molten sulphur is burnt in dry air at **1000–1100 °C** in a sulphur furnace.
2. **Catalytic Conversion:** The cooled SO₂ is passed over **V₂O₅ catalyst** in 3–4 beds at **410–440 °C** optimum.
3. **Absorption:** SO₃ is absorbed in **98% H₂SO₄** to form oleum, which is diluted to commercial grade.

**Key Reactions:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Stage 1: } \text{S} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{SO}_2 \quad \Delta H = -296.8 \text{ kJ/mol} \\
&\text{Stage 2: } 2\text{SO}_2 + \text{O}_2 \rightleftharpoons 2\text{SO}_3 \quad \Delta H = -196.6 \text{ kJ/mol} \\
&\text{Stage 3: } \text{SO}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{S}_2\text{O}_7 \\
&\text{Then: } \text{H}_2\text{S}_2\text{O}_7 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 2\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Operating Conditions:**
- **Catalyst:** V₂O₅ with K₂O promoter on SiO₂ support.
- **Optimum temperature:** 410–440 °C.
- **Pressure:** 1–2 atm.
- **Excess air:** 1.4:1 to 1.5:1 O₂ : SO₂.
- **Final acid concentration:** 98–100% H₂SO₄.
- **MOC:** Carbon steel for conc. acid; lead-lined/FRP for dilute acid.

*(7 marks: 2 for diagram, 3 for reactions & conditions, 2 for catalyst/MOC.)*

### **Model Solution for Q1(b):**

**Step 1: Stoichiometric basis (per 100 mol feed):**
- SO₂ = 7 mol, O₂ = 11 mol, N₂ = 82 mol.

**Step 2: Define x = moles of SO₂ reacted:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{\text{SO}_2} &= 7 - 2x \\
n_{\text{O}_2} &= 11 - x \\
n_{\text{SO}_3} &= 2x \\
n_{\text{N}_2} &= 82 \\
n_{\text{total}} &= 100 - x
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Apply equilibrium constant:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_p &= \frac{4x^2 (100-x)}{(7-2x)^2 (11-x) \cdot P} \\
0.5 &= \frac{4x^2 (100-x)}{(7-2x)^2 (11-x) \cdot 1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Solve iteratively (trial-and-error):**

Try $x = 3.0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RHS} &= \frac{4(9)(97)}{(1)^2 (8)(1)} = \frac{3492}{8} = 436.5 \;\; \text{(too high)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Try $x = 0.5$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RHS} &= \frac{4(0.25)(99.5)}{(6)^2 (10.5)} = \frac{99.5}{378} = 0.263
\end{aligned}
$$

Try $x = 0.7$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RHS} &= \frac{4(0.49)(99.3)}{(5.6)^2 (10.3)} = \frac{194.6}{323.0} = 0.602
\end{aligned}
$$

Try $x = 0.66$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RHS} &= \frac{4(0.4356)(99.34)}{(5.68)^2 (10.34)} = \frac{173.1}{333.8} = 0.519
\end{aligned}
$$

So $x \approx 0.65$ (close to 0.5).

**Step 5: Compute conversion:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Conversion of SO}_2 &= \frac{2x}{7} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{2(0.65)}{7} \times 100 \\
&\approx \mathbf{18.6\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(7 marks: 2 for setup, 2 for Kp substitution, 2 for solving, 1 for final answer.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024] Question B (Alternative Choice, 14 Marks)**
>
> **(a)** Explain the **Frasch Process** for mining native sulphur with a neat diagram. Mention its advantages and limitations. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Compare the **Lead Chamber Process** with the **Contact Process** for manufacturing sulphuric acid. Why is the Lead Chamber Process almost obsolete today? **(7 Marks)**

### **Model Solution for Q2(a):**

The **Frasch Process** is a hot-water mining technique used to extract native sulphur from underground deposits (typically 150–800 m deep) lying above salt domes.

**Process Steps:**
1. **Drilling:** A borehole is drilled through the overburden layers (sand, clay, limestone) to reach the sulphur-bearing rock.
2. **Superheated Water Injection:** Water heated to **165 °C** under pressure (~17 atm) is pumped down the outermost 8-inch pipe. It melts the sulphur (m.p. 115 °C) underground.
3. **Compressed Air Injection:** Hot compressed air at **17 atm** is sent through the innermost 1-inch pipe. It mixes with the molten sulphur to form a low-density foam (air-lift principle).
4. **Lifting:** This foamy mixture rises through the 6-inch middle pipe to the surface.
5. **Solidification:** The molten sulphur is collected in vats, where it solidifies into bright yellow slabs (~99.5% purity).

**Advantages:**
- Yields very pure sulphur (~99.5%) without complex ore-beneficiation.
- Economical for deep deposits inaccessible to conventional mining.
- Low labour cost; continuous operation.

**Limitations:**
- Very high energy cost (heating water to 165 °C).
- Limited to deposits above salt domes with specific geological conditions.
- Disposal of large volumes of cooled water can cause environmental issues.

*(7 marks: 3 for process, 2 for diagram description, 2 for advantages/limitations.)*

### **Model Solution for Q2(b):**

| Parameter | Lead Chamber Process | Contact Process |
|-----------|---------------------|-----------------|
| **Acid Concentration** | 60–70% (dilute) | 98–100% (concentrated) |
| **Catalyst** | Oxides of nitrogen (NO, NO₂) in gaseous phase | V₂O₅ (solid heterogeneous) |
| **Raw Material** | Pyrites (FeS₂) or sulphur | Sulphur (preferred) |
| **Acid Purity** | Impure, requires concentration | High purity, ready for use |
| **Operating Cost** | Lower capital, higher acid concentration cost | Higher capital, lower operating cost |
| **Environmental** | NOx emissions (regulated) | Cleaner, higher efficiency |
| **Conversion** | Single pass ~70% | DCDA gives 99.5–99.8% |
| **Acid Use Suitability** | Unsuitable for dye, explosives, petroleum | Suitable for all industrial uses |

**Why Lead Chamber Process is Obsolete:**
- (i) Cannot produce 98%+ acid directly — required for modern industries.
- (ii) NOx emissions violate modern environmental regulations.
- (iii) Lower purity restricts use in dyes, pharmaceuticals, and explosives.
- (iv) Continuous catalytic operation in Contact Process is more economical at scale.
- (v) Stricter pollution control norms and the need for oleum make Contact Process the only viable option.

*(7 marks: 3 for comparison table, 2 for explanation of each, 2 for obsolescence reasons.)*

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to mention "98% H₂SO₄"** for SO₃ absorption — students often write "water" and lose **2 marks**. Always state that 98% acid is used to avoid acid mist.
> 2. **Confusing V₂O₅ with Pt as the current catalyst** — Pt was the original catalyst, replaced in the 1920s. Writing "Pt" today is outdated and costs marks.
> 3. **Forgetting units in numerical answers** — KTU strictly deducts marks for missing units in $K_p$, conversion, or flow rate.
> 4. **Writing "dilute H₂SO₄ stored in steel tanks"** — this is **incorrect**; dilute acid attacks steel. Use **lead or FRP** for dilute, steel for concentrated.
> 5. **Not mentioning DCDA** — In Part B questions on the Contact Process, mentioning **"Double Contact Double Absorption"** is now an expected term; missing it loses 1 mark.
> 6. **Failing to state the optimum temperature range (410–440 °C)** — writing a single value like "450 °C" without the range loses the full catalyst-condition mark.
> 7. **Not showing material balance setup** in numerical questions — KTU examiners award the **method marks** only if the initial mole table is shown.

---

## 5.4 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> 📌 **Quick Revision Checklist — Sulphur & Sulphuric Acid (Module 1)**

- **Sulphur:** Atomic No. 16, atomic mass 32.06, m.p. 115.21 °C. Occurs as native, sulphides, sulphates. Extracted via **Frasch Process** (superheated water at 165 °C + compressed air lift).
- **Sulphuric Acid (H₂SO₄):** "King of Chemicals." Molecular mass 98.08, density 1.84 g/cm³ (98.3%), b.p. 337 °C. Strong dehydrating agent; highly exothermic heat of dilution.
- **Contact Process — 3 Stages:**
  - (i) **Sulphur burning:** S + O₂ → SO₂ at 1000–1100 °C.
  - (ii) **Catalytic oxidation:** 2 SO₂ + O₂ ⇌ 2 SO₃ over V₂O₅ at 410–440 °C.
  - (iii) **Absorption:** SO₃ in 98% H₂SO₄ → oleum (H₂S₂O₇) → diluted to 98% H₂SO₄.
- **Catalyst:** V₂O₅ with K₂O promoter on SiO₂ support. Replaced Pt due to lower cost and poison resistance.
- **Equilibrium Constant:** $K_p = \dfrac{x^2}{(1-x)^3 \cdot P}$ (for stoichiometric air).
- **Optimum Conditions:** T = 410–440 °C, P = 1–2 atm, O₂ : SO₂ = 1.4:1.
- **DCDA Plant:** Achieves 99.5–99.8% conversion via 3–4 catalyst beds with interstage cooling and intermediate absorption.
- **Material of Construction:** Carbon steel for conc. acid (>90%); lead/FRP for dilute acid; cast iron/SS-316 for hot oleum.
- **Storage Rule:** Always **add acid to water** (never water to acid) due to exothermic splashing.
- **Industrial Uses:** Fertilizers (60%) > Petroleum (20%) > Chemicals (10%) > Metallurgy (10%).
- **Process Comparisons:** Contact Process supersedes Lead Chamber Process due to higher purity, concentration, and environmental compliance.
- **Key Equilibrium Values:** $K_p$ at 700 K ≈ 0.5 atm⁻¹; at 800 K ≈ 0.15 atm⁻¹ (decreases with T, confirming exothermicity).
- **Important Equation to Memorize:** $K_p = \dfrac{4x^2 (100-x)}{(7-2x)^2 (11-x) \cdot P}$ for the standard 7:11:82 feed.
- **Diagram to Draw:** Sulphur Furnace → WHB → Drying Tower → Blower → 4 Catalyst Beds → 2 Absorption Towers (with oleum + 98% acid as products).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Phosphorous Industries - Phosphoric Acid
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT637 - CHEMICAL TECHNOLOGY AND REACTION ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Phosphorous Industries - Phosphoric Acid # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Phosphoric Acid (Orthophosphoric Acid, H₃PO₄)** is a medium-strength, tribasic mineral acid of pentavalent phosphorus, representing the second most produced inorganic chemical in the world (after sulfuric acid) on a tonnage basis.
