Together they cover Block-Flow, Process-Flow, and Mass-Balance perspectives — the three "lenses" KTU examiners love to test. ## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set B]` **What is soda ash? List any two industrial applications of soda ash.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Soda ash is the commercial name for **anhydrous sodium carbonate (Na₂CO₃)**, a white, water-soluble, alkaline inorganic salt with molecular weight 105.99 g/mol and density 2.54 g/cm³. It is manufactured predominantly by the **Solvay (Ammonia-Soda) process** from brine, ammonia, and limestone. **Two industrial applications:** 1. **Glass industry** (flat glass, container glass, fibreglass) — the largest single consumer, using soda ash as a flux to lower the silica melting point from ~1700 °C to ~1400 °C. 2. **Soaps, detergents, and silicate manufacture** — provides alkalinity and acts as a builder (water-softener) by precipitating Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺ as carbonates. *(Other valid applications: pulp and paper, textile processing, water softening, pH control, sodium chemical manufacture — full credit 3 marks.)* --- **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]` **Differentiate between light soda ash and dense soda ash.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Parameter | Light Soda Ash | Dense Soda Ash | |---|---|---| | Bulk density | 0.50 – 0.65 g/cm³ | 0.95 – 1.10 g/cm³ | | Particle size | 100 – 150 μm | 300 – 500 μm | | Method of production | Direct from NaHCO₃ calciner | Light ash converted to monohydrate and recalcined | | Preferred use | Detergents, chemical synthesis | Glass industry (better flow, less dust) | The dense grade requires an extra **hydration – de-hydration step** but is preferred in glass manufacture because of easier handling and reduced dust loss. --- ### 5.2 PART B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks) > [!WARNING] > **KTU ESE Format Reminder:** Each Part-B question in KTU 2024 carries **14 marks**, split into two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. Students MUST answer sub-part (a) AND (b) of the chosen question. The two alternative questions below give the candidate full internal choice. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1]` **(a) [7 marks]** With the help of a neat flow sheet, describe the **manufacture of soda ash by the Solvay (ammonia-soda) process**. Discuss the main chemical reactions involved. **(b) [7 marks]** Explain the **role of the carbonation tower** in the Solvay process. What are the operating conditions and design features that affect NaHCO₃ crystal quality? --- **Model Solution:** **(a) Solvay Process Flow Sheet and Reactions — 7 marks** The Solvay process manufactures soda ash from three raw materials: **sodium chloride (brine)**, **ammonia**, and **limestone (calcium carbonate)**, with ammonia and CO₂ internally recycled. **Main process steps:** **1. Brine purification (1 mark):** Crude brine is treated with Na₂CO₃ and NaOH to precipitate Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺ as CaCO₃ and Mg(OH)₂, then settled and filtered. Purified brine is saturated (~26% NaCl). **2. Ammoniation (1 mark):** Purified brine flows down the ammoniation tower; ammonia gas is absorbed counter-currently. Ammoniated brine contains ~25–30 g NH₃ per litre. **3. Carbonation (2 marks):** Ammoniated brine enters the top of the carbonation tower (~25 m tall, cast-iron, with 30–40 cast-iron bubble-cap trays). CO₂-rich kiln gas (~40% CO₂) is fed from the bottom at ~30 °C. **Main reaction:** $$ \text{NaCl} + \text{NH}_3 + \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{NaHCO}_3 \downarrow + \text{NH}_4\text{Cl} $$ NaHCO₃ is the least soluble product in cold ammoniated brine → precipitates as crystals. **4. Filtration (1 mark):** Slurry is vacuum-filtered on rotary drum filters; wet NaHCO₃ cake is washed to remove NH₄Cl. **5. Calcination (1 mark):** $$ 2\text{NaHCO}_3 \rightarrow \text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \uparrow + \text{CO}_2 \uparrow $$ Performed at 200–300 °C in steam-heated calciner or rotary kiln. CO₂ recycled to carbonation tower. **6. Ammonia recovery (1 mark):** Mother liquor (NH₄Cl) treated with Ca(OH)₂ in recovery tower: $$ 2\text{NH}_4\text{Cl} + \text{Ca(OH)}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{NH}_3 + \text{CaCl}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} $$ NH₃ recycled to ammoniation tower; CaCl₂ is the by-product waste. **Net overall reaction:** $$ 2\text{NaCl} + \text{CaCO}_3 \rightarrow \text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3 + \text{CaCl}_2 $$ **Yield:** 70–75% of theoretical (906 kg Na₂CO₃ per 1000 kg NaCl fed). --- **(b) Role of Carbonation Tower — 7 marks** The carbonation tower is the **heart of the Solvay process** — it is where the unique selective precipitation of NaHCO₃ occurs. **Role and mechanism (3 marks):** - The ammoniated brine enters the top; CO₂-rich kiln gas enters the bottom. - As CO₂ dissolves, it forms (NH₄)₂CO₃ and then NH₄HCO₃. - The bicarbonate ion reacts with Na⁺ in solution; because NaHCO₃ has the **lowest solubility product** among all possible salts in the system, it precipitates as fine crystals. - Exothermic heat of carbonation (~140 kJ/mol NaHCO₃) must be removed by internal cooling coils (cold-water jackets) to maintain 25–30 °C, since lower temperature further decreases NaHCO₃ solubility and improves yield. **Design features (2 marks):** - **Cast-iron shell** lined with rubber or lead to resist corrosion from ammoniacal brine. - **30–40 cast-iron bubble-cap trays** ensuring intimate gas-liquid contact. - **External cooling jackets** (1–2 inch water pipes) on each section to remove carbonation heat. - **Top section** acts as cooler/precipitator; **bottom section** as CO₂ absorber. - **Gas exit at top** carries unreacted CO₂ and NH₃ traces to scrubber and recycle. **Operating conditions (2 marks):** - Temperature: 25–35 °C (optimum ~28 °C). - CO₂ partial pressure: 1.0 – 1.5 bar (higher improves rate). - Residence time: 4 – 8 hours. - Tower height: 25 – 35 m; diameter: 2 – 3 m for ~200 TPD plant. - NaHCO₃ crystal size: 100 – 300 μm (larger crystals filter better). - Conversion: 70 – 75% per pass. **Quality of NaHCO₃ crystals** depends on: - Cooling rate (slow → larger crystals). - NH₃/NaCl molar ratio (1.05 – 1.10 ideal). - CO₂ absorption rate (slow → fewer nuclei, bigger crystals). --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 1]` **(a) [7 marks]** Write a comparative account of the **Solvay process and the Leblanc process** for the manufacture of soda ash. Why has the Solvay process become the dominant route? **(b) [7 marks]** What are the major **engineering problems and environmental issues** of the Solvay process? Explain the **Dual Process** as a solution to the CaCl₂ waste problem. --- **Model Solution:** **(a) Solvay vs Leblanc — 7 marks** **Leblanc Process (3 marks):** Developed by Nicolas Leblanc in 1791. Batch process. Reactions: $$ \text{Step 1:}\ \text{NaCl} + \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \rightarrow \text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4 + 2\text{HCl} \uparrow $$ $$ \text{Step 2:}\ \text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4 + \text{CaCO}_3 + 2\text{C} \rightarrow \text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3 + \text{CaS} + 2\text{CO}_2 \uparrow $$ **Drawbacks:** batch operation, low purity (~80%), emits corrosive HCl gas, generates foul-smelling CaS effluent, high labour cost, no internal recycle. **Solvay Process (3 marks):** Developed by Ernest Solvay in 1861. Continuous process. Net reaction: $$ 2\text{NaCl} + \text{CaCO}_3 \rightarrow \text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3 + \text{CaCl}_2 $$ **Advantages:** continuous, high purity (99.5%), NH₃ and CO₂ internally recycled (95–97% NH₃ recovery), lower labour cost, no HCl emission, well-suited for large capacities. **Why Solvay dominates (1 mark):** - Lower product cost at large scale. - Less environmental burden (no HCl, no CaS). - Consistent product quality for the glass industry. - Better thermodynamic and material efficiency. **Verdict:** The Leblanc process is **obsolete** since the early 20th century; Solvay continues to dominate globally except where natural trona deposits (Wyoming, Turkey) offer cheaper production. --- **(b) Engineering Problems and Dual Process — 7 marks** **Major problems of the Solvay process (3 marks):** 1. **CaCl₂ waste effluent (~1.05 t per t soda ash):** No major market; pollutes soil and water; recovery is expensive. 2. **Large limestone requirement (~0.55 t CaO per t soda ash):** Quarries consume land and energy for calcination (~980 MJ/tonne Na₂CO₃). 3. **CO₂ emissions:** Limestone calcination releases ~0.4 t CO₂ per t soda ash. 4. **Ammonia loss:** 2 – 5 kg NH₃ per tonne Na₂CO₃ lost; environmental and cost penalty. 5. **Corrosion:** Cast-iron equipment attacked by ammoniacal brine and Cl⁻; expensive rubber/lead linings required. 6. **Sludge handling:** CaCO₃ and Mg(OH)₂ from brine purification need disposal. **Dual Process — solving the CaCl₂ problem (4 marks):** The **Dual Process** is a hybrid Solvay-electrolytic route that **recycles the CaCl₂ waste** to recover chlorine gas, integrating the soda-ash plant with a chlor-alkali cell. **Key modifications:** - CaCl₂ from the ammonia-recovery tower is concentrated by evaporation and dried. - The dried CaCl₂ is fed to an **electrolytic cell**: $$ \text{CaCl}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{electrolysis, fused salt}} \text{Ca (cathode)} + \text{Cl}_2 \uparrow \text{ (anode)} $$ - The Ca metal is reacted with water to regenerate **Ca(OH)₂**, which is recycled to the ammonia recovery tower. $$ \text{Ca} + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{Ca(OH)}_2 + \text{H}_2 \uparrow $$ **Net benefits of the Dual Process:** - Eliminates CaCl₂ discharge (zero liquid effluent). - Produces **Cl₂ as a co-product** (sold to PVC, pesticide, water-treatment markets). - Reduces dependence on fresh lime (~70% reduction). - Enhances overall plant economics and sustainability. - Cuts CO₂ emissions per tonne of Na₂CO₃ by ~30%. **Drawback:** High capital cost of fused-salt electrolysis; economically attractive only in integrated chlor-alkali complexes. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where students lose marks:** > 1. **Forgetting to state the net reaction** 2NaCl + CaCO₃ → Na₂CO₃ + CaCl₂ (3 marks lost if missed). > 2. **Confusing dense ash with light ash** densities and applications. > 3. **Not mentioning the recycle of NH₃ and CO₂** — examiners specifically test the recycle architecture. > 4. **Drawing the carbonation tower without bubble-cap trays** — this is a "must" in the flow sheet. > 5. **Forgetting the 70–75% yield figure** in any mass-balance question. > 6. **Skipping the Dual Process** when asked about environmental issues. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - [x] **Soda ash = Na₂CO₃** (MW 105.99), alkaline inorganic tonnage chemical. - [x] **Solvay process** uses NaCl + NH₃ + CO₂ + H₂O, with NH₃ and CO₂ internally recycled. - [x] **Carbonation tower is the heart** of the process — exploits the **lowest solubility of NaHCO₃** in cold ammoniated brine. - [x] **Net overall reaction:** $2\text{NaCl} + \text{CaCO}_3 \rightarrow \text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3 + \text{CaCl}_2$. - [x] **Theoretical yield** = 906.8 kg Na₂CO₃ per 1000 kg NaCl; **actual = 70–75%**. - [x] **Calcination** of NaHCO₃ at 200–300 °C gives light soda ash; **hydration–recalcination** gives dense soda ash. - [x] **Lime kiln** supplies CO₂ (90%+); operates at 900–1000 °C with ΔH ≈ 178.4 kJ/mol. - [x] **CaCl₂ effluent** is the main environmental liability; **Dual Process** recovers Cl₂. - [x] **Leblanc process** = obsolete, batch, polluting (HCl + CaS); KTU expects a clean comparison. - [x] **Raw materials:** brine (NaCl), limestone (CaCO₃), ammonia (NH₃ — recycled). - [x] **Carbonation tower design:** cast-iron, bubble-cap trays, 25–35 m tall, 25–35 °C, cooling jackets. - [x] **End uses:** glass (~50%), detergents, water softening, pulp & paper, silicates. - [x] **Dense vs light soda ash:** 0.95–1.10 vs 0.50–0.65 g/cm³; glass industry prefers dense. - [x] **Dual process = Solvay + CaCl₂ electrolysis** → zero waste + Cl₂ co-product. - [x] **NH₃ recovery efficiency:** 95–97% in modern plants. - [x] **CO₂ requirement:** ~0.95–1.0 t per t Na₂CO₃; limestone requirement ~0.55 t CaO per t.

topics.title: Caustic Soda - Chlorine - Hydrochloric Acid
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT637 - CHEMICAL TECHNOLOGY AND REACTION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Caustic Soda - Chlorine - Hydrochloric Acid

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Chlor-Alkali Industry: A Tri-Product Powerhouse

The **Chlor-Alkali Process** is one of the largest and most fundamental electrochemical industries in the world. It simultaneously produces three of the most important heavy inorganic chemicals — **Caustic Soda (NaOH)**, **Chlorine (Cl₂)** and **Hydrogen (H₂)** — through the electrolysis of purified brine (concentrated aqueous NaCl solution). Hydrochloric Acid (HCl) is closely linked to this industry because Cl₂ and H₂ produced here are recombined in a separate synthesis step to manufacture HCl.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> The **Chlor-Alkali Process** is defined as the electrolytic decomposition of an aqueous solution of sodium chloride (brine) into chlorine gas at the anode, hydrogen gas at the cathode, and sodium hydroxide in the catholyte, governed by the overall cell reaction:
> **2 NaCl + 2 H₂O → 2 NaOH + Cl₂↑ + H₂↑**
> The process operates on the principle of **selective ion transport** and **Faradaic electrolysis**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Salt-Splitting Highway Intersection"

Imagine a busy chemical highway where incoming trucks of **brine (NaCl + H₂O)** arrive at a massive toll plaza (the electrolytic cell). At the toll booth, a smart traffic controller (electric current) sorts the cargo:

- **Truck Cab (Na⁺ ions)** is sent toward the **East Wing (cathode)** where it picks up a new companion (OH⁻ from water reduction) and exits as a fresh **"Caustic Soda Truck" (NaOH)**.
- **Trailer (Cl⁻ ions)** is pulled toward the **West Wing (anode)** where it is "burned off" into a greenish gas cloud (**Cl₂**).
- **The fuel burned to power the East Wing trucks (H₂O → H₂)** produces an additional **"Hydrogen Cloud" (H₂)** as a free bonus.
- A **separate downstream plant** then takes the H₂ cloud + Cl₂ cloud and welds them back into **HCl gas**, which is dissolved in water to make muriatic acid.

This "salt-splitting intersection" is exactly what an electrolyzer does — and the toll (energy) charged determines the process economics.

## 1.3 Key Terminology & Standard Metrics

| Term | Definition | Typical Value |
|------|------------|---------------|
| **Brine** | Purified aqueous NaCl feed | 25–26 % w/w NaCl |
| **Faraday Constant (F)** | Charge per mole of electrons | **96,485 C/mol** |
| **Decomposition Voltage (E°ᵈᵉᶜ)** | Theoretical minimum cell voltage for brine | **2.19 V** |
| **Actual Cell Voltage (V)** | Operating voltage including overpotentials | **3.0 – 4.5 V** |
| **Current Efficiency (η)** | Fraction of current used productively | **95 – 97 %** |
| **NaOH Product Strength** | Concentration of caustic liquor leaving cell | 10 – 50 % w/w |
| **Energy Consumption** | DC power per ton of Cl₂ (or NaOH) | **2,500 – 3,500 kWh/ton** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is the chlor-alkali industry so important globally?**
> World production of Cl₂ exceeds **~75 million tonnes/year**, and NaOH is produced in almost exactly stoichiometric proportion. Virtually every modern chemical — PVC, polyurethane, paper, soap, alumina, water treatment chemicals — traces its origin back to this one electrochemical reaction.

## 1.4 Engineering Significance of HCl in the Chlor-Alkali Complex

Although HCl is not produced in the electrolyzer itself, **its manufacture is tightly integrated** with the chlor-alkali complex because:

1. The H₂ generated at the cathode can be burned with Cl₂ in a **HCl synthesis burner**.
2. Many **organic chlorination by-product streams** contain HCl that must be recovered as commercial acid.
3. HCl acts as a **chlorine sink** during demand fluctuations (when Cl₂ demand drops, HCl is produced and stored; when Cl₂ demand rises, HCl is cracked back to Cl₂ via the Kel-Chlor or Deacon process).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HCl Production Routes (KTU 2024 Module 1 Coverage):**
> 1. **Direct Synthesis:** H₂ + Cl₂ → 2 HCl (ΔH = –184 kJ/mol)
> 2. **Mannheim / Salt-Sulfuric Acid Process:** 2 NaCl + H₂SO₄ → Na₂SO₄ + 2 HCl
> 3. **By-product Recovery:** From organic chlorinations (CH₄ + Cl₂ → CH₃Cl + HCl, etc.)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Process Block Flow — Master Overview

The complete chlor-alkali + HCl complex can be divided into **four engineering blocks**:

**Block A — Brine Preparation & Purification**
**Block B — Electrolytic Cell** (Mercury / Diaphragm / Membrane)
**Block C — Product Concentration & Handling** (NaOH evaporation, Cl₂ liquefaction, H₂ storage)
**Block D — HCl Synthesis & Absorption** (Burner + Absorber train)

### Block A: Brine Preparation
1. **Saturation:** Solid rock salt is dissolved in water to ~25 % w/w NaCl.
2. **Chemical Purification:**
   - Calcium and magnesium ions are removed by carbonate and hydroxide precipitation:
     - Ca²⁺ + Na₂CO₃ → CaCO₃↓ + 2 Na⁺
     - Mg²⁺ + 2 NaOH → Mg(OH)₂↓ + 2 Na⁺
3. **Acidification:** HCl is added to bring pH to ~3–4, converting any residual chlorate to chloride and preventing hydroxide back-migration.

### Block B: Electrolytic Cell — Detailed Operational Steps

The core electrochemical engineering depends on cell design. Three commercial technologies exist:

| Feature | Mercury Cell | Diaphragm Cell | Membrane Cell |
|---------|-------------|----------------|---------------|
| **Cathode Material** | Liquid Hg | Steel (mesh) | Steel/Ni mesh |
| **Anode Material** | Graphite / TiO₂-coated Ti | Graphite / DSA | DSA (Dimensionally Stable Anode) |
| **Separator** | None (amalgam) | Asbestos / polymer diaphragm | Cation-exchange membrane (Nafion) |
| **NaOH Strength** | **50 % direct** | 10–12 % (needs evaporation) | 32–35 % (moderate evaporation) |
| **NaCl in Product** | < 50 ppm | 1–1.5 % (impure) | < 50 ppm |
| **Current Efficiency** | 95–97 % | 88–95 % | 95–98 % |
| **Cell Voltage** | 4.0–4.5 V | 2.9–3.5 V | 2.9–3.2 V |
| **Energy per ton Cl₂** | 3,300 kWh | 2,700 kWh | 2,300 kWh |
| **Environmental Issue** | **Hg pollution** | Asbestos disposal | None major |
| **Status (2024)** | **Phased out** | Declining | **Dominant technology** |

> [!NOTE]
> **The KTU examiner loves comparison tables.** Memorize the differences in product concentration and the reason for it (in mercury cell, Na⁺ is reduced INTO mercury to form amalgam, then decomposed separately with water — therefore pure, strong NaOH; in diaphragm, brine leaks through with NaOH; in membrane, only Na⁺ migrates, leaving Cl⁻ behind, giving cleaner product).

### Block C: Product Conditioning
- **Chlorine:** Cooled, dried with H₂SO₄, compressed, and liquefied (–35 °C at 1 atm) for storage/transport.
- **Hydrogen:** Compressed or used on-site for HCl synthesis or fuel.
- **NaOH:** Mercury-cell liquor is already 50 %; membrane-cell liquor is concentrated in multi-effect evaporators (often falling-film type).

### Block D: HCl Synthesis
**Direct Combustion Route (preferred):**
- H₂ and Cl₂ fed into a special burner with a quartz nozzle.
- Flame temperature: ~2000 °C.
- Reaction: H₂ + Cl₂ → 2 HCl (highly exothermic; chain reaction initiated by UV).
- Rapid quenching (water-cooled) followed by absorption in dilute HCl in an **adiabatic absorber** (co-current) and **isothermal absorber** (counter-current).
- Final commercial acid: 32–36 % w/w HCl.

## 2.2 Electrode Reactions — The Why and How

### Anode (Oxidation):
$$2 \text{Cl}^- \rightarrow \text{Cl}_2\uparrow + 2 e^- \quad E^0 = +1.36 \text{ V}$$

### Cathode — depends on cell type:

**(a) Mercury Cell:**
$$\text{Na}^+ + e^- + \text{Hg} \rightarrow \text{Na/Hg (amalgam)} \quad E^0 = -1.87 \text{ V}$$
Then in decomposer:
$$2 \text{Na/Hg} + 2 \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 2 \text{NaOH} + \text{H}_2\uparrow + \text{Hg}$$

**(b) Diaphragm Cell / Membrane Cell:**
$$2 \text{H}_2\text{O} + 2 e^- \rightarrow \text{H}_2\uparrow + 2 \text{OH}^- \quad E^0 = -0.83 \text{ V}$$

### Net Cell Reaction:
$$2 \text{NaCl} + 2 \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 2 \text{NaOH} + \text{Cl}_2\uparrow + \text{H}_2\uparrow \quad \Delta G^0 = +422 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is the theoretical decomposition voltage 2.19 V?**
> Because: E°cell = E°cathode – E°anode = (–0.83) – (+1.36) = **–2.19 V**
> This is the thermodynamic minimum. The actual cell operates at 3.0–4.5 V due to:
> - Anode overpotential (ηₐ) ≈ 0.2–0.4 V
> - Cathode overpotential (ηc) ≈ 0.1–0.3 V
> - Ohmic (IR) drop in electrolyte ≈ 0.3–0.6 V
> - Membrane / diaphragm resistance ≈ 0.2–0.5 V

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Use `\vert` instead of `|` for absolute values inside markdown tables** (as required by the engine rules).

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ | Faraday's First Law — mass produced | grams |
| 2 | $\eta_{current} = \dfrac{m_{actual}}{m_{theoretical}} \times 100$ | Current efficiency | % |
| 3 | $E^0_{decomp} = E^0_{cathode} - E^0_{anode}$ | Theoretical decomposition voltage | V |
| 4 | $V_{cell} = E^0_{decomp} + \eta_a + \eta_c + IR$ | Actual cell voltage | V |
| 5 | $E_{kWh/ton} = \dfrac{V_{cell} \times 26.8 \times 1000}{E_{wt} \times \eta_{current}}$ | Energy per tonne of product | kWh/ton |
| 6 | $P = V_{cell} \times I$ | DC Power required | W |
| 7 | $Q_{theoretical} = \dfrac{m \times n \times F}{M}$ | Theoretical charge required | C |
| 8 | $\text{NaOH strength} = \dfrac{m_{NaOH}}{m_{NaOH} + m_{H_2O}} \times 100$ | Liquor concentration | % w/w |
| 9 | $\eta_{voltage} = \dfrac{E^0_{decomp}}{V_{cell}} \times 100$ | Voltage efficiency | % |
| 10 | $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{current} \times \eta_{voltage}$ | Overall energy efficiency | % |
| 11 | $N_{cells} = \dfrac{I_{total}}{I_{per\,cell}}$ | Number of cells in series | – |
| 12 | $\text{Production Rate} = \dfrac{I \times 3600 \times M}{n \times F \times 10^6}$ | Tonnes/day per cell | t/day |

Where:
- I = current (A), t = time (s), M = molar mass (g/mol)
- n = electrons transferred per mole
- F = **96,485 C/mol** (Faraday's constant)
- Ewt = equivalent weight (M/n) of product

## 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility

| Domain | Use of Caustic Soda | Use of Chlorine | Use of HCl |
|--------|--------------------|-----------------|------------|
| Pulp & Paper | Digestion of wood | Bleaching | pH adjustment |
| Soap / Detergents | Saponification of fats | – | – |
| Water Treatment | pH neutralization | Disinfection (Cl₂/HOCl) | Regeneration of ion exchange resins |
| PVC / Plastics | – | Vinyl chloride monomer | – |
| Pharma | API manufacture | – | Intermediate synthesis |
| Petroleum | Sweetening (Merox) | – | Acidizing wells |
| Food Industry | Peeling of fruits, cocoa processing | – | – |
| Steel Pickling | – | – | Scale removal |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Insight:**
> When Cl₂ demand is low but NaOH demand is high (e.g., during alumina refining), membrane cells can be operated in **"NaOH mode"** by reducing Cl₂ output. Conversely, when Cl₂ demand is high (PVC boom), membrane cells are favored for higher Cl₂ purity.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Theoretical Decomposition Voltage for Brine

The standard decomposition voltage of any electrolytic cell is given by:

$$\Delta E^0_{cell} = E^0_{cathode} - E^0_{anode}$$

For brine electrolysis, two possible anode reactions exist — oxygen evolution and chlorine evolution. Because of **overpotential considerations**, Cl₂ evolution is kinetically favored below a critical current density. The standard half-cell potentials at 25 °C are:

- Cathode (water reduction): $E^0 = -0.83 \text{ V}$
- Anode (chloride oxidation): $E^0 = +1.36 \text{ V}$

Therefore:

$$\Delta E^0_{cell} = (-0.83) - (+1.36) = -2.19 \text{ V}$$

The negative sign indicates the reaction is **non-spontaneous** — electrical work must be supplied. The magnitude, **2.19 V**, is the **minimum thermodynamic voltage** that must be applied to drive the reaction.

## 3.2 Derivation: Faraday's First Law Applied to Brine Electrolysis

Faraday's First Law states that the mass of substance liberated at an electrode is directly proportional to the quantity of electricity passed.

Starting from the definition of current and charge:

$$Q = I \cdot t$$

The number of moles of electrons transferred is:

$$n_{e^-} = \frac{Q}{F} = \frac{I \cdot t}{F}$$

Since each mole of Cl₂ or NaOH requires *n* moles of electrons (where *n* = 2 for Cl₂ formation, *n* = 1 for NaOH formation equivalent), the moles of product formed are:

$$n_{product} = \frac{n_{e^-}}{n} = \frac{I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$$

The mass of product is therefore:

$$m = n_{product} \times M = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$

This is the **mass production equation** — universally valid for any electrolytic process.

## 3.3 Derivation: Current Efficiency for a Chlor-Alkali Cell

By definition, **current efficiency (η_current)** is the ratio of actual product obtained to the theoretical product (calculated by Faraday's law) under the same charge input:

$$\eta_{current} = \frac{m_{actual}}{m_{theoretical}} = \frac{m_{actual}}{\dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}}$$

For brine electrolysis, two main **loss mechanisms** reduce current efficiency:

1. **Chlorate formation** at the anode: $6 ClO^- + 3 H_2O \rightarrow 2 ClO_3^- + 4 Cl^- + \tfrac{3}{2} O_2 + 6 H^+ + 6 e^-$
2. **Oxygen evolution** at the anode (competes with Cl₂): $2 H_2O \rightarrow O_2 + 4 H^+ + 4 e^-$
3. **Back-migration of OH⁻** through the membrane / diaphragm (reacts with Cl₂ → ClO⁻ → ClO₃⁻).

Modern membrane cells achieve **95–98 %** current efficiency by minimizing these side reactions.

## 3.4 Derivation: Energy Consumption per Tonne of Product

Energy consumption is a critical economic parameter. Starting from the power equation:

$$P = V_{cell} \times I \quad (\text{Watts})$$

Energy for time *t*:

$$E = P \times t = V_{cell} \times I \times t \quad (\text{Joules})$$

For a production of *m* kg of product:

$$\frac{E}{m} = \frac{V_{cell} \times I \times t}{m}$$

But from Faraday's law:

$$I \cdot t = \frac{m \cdot n \cdot F}{M}$$

Substituting:

$$\frac{E}{m} = V_{cell} \times \frac{n \cdot F}{M}$$

Converting to **kWh per tonne** (where 1 kWh = 3.6 × 10⁶ J and 1 tonne = 10⁶ g):

$$\text{Energy} = \frac{V_{cell} \times n \times F \times 10^6}{M \times 3.6 \times 10^6 \times \eta_{current}}$$

Simplifying the constants:

$$\boxed{\text{Energy (kWh/ton)} = \frac{V_{cell} \times 2.674 \times 10^4}{E_{wt} \times \eta_{current}}}$$

Where E_wt = M/n is the equivalent weight. For NaOH: E_wt = 40; for Cl₂: E_wt = 35.5.

### Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A chlor-alkali membrane cell operates at **3.2 V** with **95 % current efficiency**. Calculate the **DC energy consumption per tonne of NaOH** produced.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Equivalent weight of NaOH = M/n = 40/1 = **40 g/eq**

Step 2 — Apply the energy formula:

$$\text{Energy} = \frac{V_{cell} \times 26{,}800}{E_{wt} \times \eta_{current}}$$

Step 3 — Substitute values (note: 26,800 = F / 3.6 ≈ 96,485 / 3.6):

$$\text{Energy} = \frac{3.2 \times 26{,}800}{40 \times 0.95}$$

Step 4 — Compute numerator:

$$3.2 \times 26{,}800 = 85{,}760$$

Step 5 — Compute denominator:

$$40 \times 0.95 = 38$$

Step 6 — Final division:

$$\text{Energy} = \frac{85{,}760}{38} = 2{,}257 \text{ kWh/ton NaOH}$$

**Answer: ≈ 2,257 kWh per tonne of NaOH** — a value typical of modern membrane cells.

## 3.5 Material Balance Around an Electrolytic Cell (Analytical Matrix)

For a single membrane cell operating at current *I* and time *t*, the inlet (purified brine) and outlet streams can be tabulated:

| Stream | NaCl | H₂O | Na⁺ | Cl⁻ | OH⁻ | Cl₂ | H₂ | Total |
|--------|------|-----|-----|-----|------|-----|-----|-------|
| Brine In (kg) | x | y | – | – | 0 | 0 | 0 | x+y |
| Brine Out (kg) | (x – Δ) | (y – w) | – | – | 0 | 0 | 0 | (x+y)–(Δ+w) |
| Cathode Out (NaOH) | – | w | – | – | m_NaOH | 0 | – | m_NaOH |
| Anode Out (Cl₂) | – | – | – | – | 0 | m_Cl₂ | – | m_Cl₂ |
| Cathode Gas (H₂) | – | – | – | – | – | – | m_H₂ | m_H₂ |

Where:
- Δ = mass of NaCl electrolyzed
- w = mass of water consumed (= 36 g per 80 g NaCl reacted)
- m_NaOH = (I·t·40)/(1·F·η)
- m_Cl₂ = (I·t·71)/(2·F·η)
- m_H₂ = (I·t·2)/(2·F·η)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stoichiometric relationship for every 1 kg Cl₂ produced:**
> - NaOH produced = 1.128 kg
> - H₂ produced = 0.028 kg
> - NaCl consumed = 1.648 kg
> - H₂O consumed = 0.408 kg
>
> These ratios are derived directly from molar masses and are **highly tested** in KTU board exams.

## 3.6 Voltage Efficiency Derivation

The **voltage efficiency** measures how close the actual operating voltage is to the thermodynamic minimum:

$$\eta_{voltage} = \frac{E^0_{decomp}}{V_{cell}} \times 100$$

For our worked example:

$$\eta_{voltage} = \frac{2.19}{3.20} \times 100 = 68.4\%$$

**Overall Energy Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{overall} = \eta_{current} \times \eta_{voltage} = 0.95 \times 0.684 = 0.650 \; (65.0\%)$$

This means **35 % of the electrical energy** is dissipated as heat due to overpotentials, ohmic drops, and side reactions.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Process Flow — Chlor-Alkali Complex

```mermaid
graph LR
    rawSalt["ROCK SALT NaCl"] --> dissolver["SATURATION DISSOLVER"]
    waterIn["FRESH WATER"] --> dissolver
    dissolver --> clarifier["PRIMARY CLARIFIER"]
    clarifier --> chemTreat["CHEMICAL PURIFICATION CaCO3 MgOH2 PRECIPITATION"]
    chemTreat --> filter["SAND FILTER AND ACIDIFICATION"]
    filter --> brineTank["PURIFIED BRINE TANK 25 percent NaCl"]
    brineTank --> anodeComp["ANODE COMPARTMENT Cl2 GAS OUTLET"]
    power["DC POWER RECTIFIER"] --> anodeComp
    power --> cathodeComp["CATHODE COMPARTMENT H2 GAS OUTLET"]
    anodeComp --> membrane["CATION EXCHANGE MEMBRANE NAFION"]
    membrane --> cathodeComp
    cathodeComp --> dilNaOH["DILUTE NaOH 32 percent"]
    dilNaOH --> evap["MULTI EFFECT EVAPORATOR FALLING FILM"]
    evap --> concNaOH["CONCENTRATED NaOH 50 percent"]
    anodeComp --> cl2Cooler["CL2 COOLER AND DRIER H2SO4"]
    cl2Cooler --> cl2Liq["LIQUEFIED Cl2 STORAGE"]
    cathodeComp --> h2Comp["H2 COMPRESSOR"]
    h2Comp --> hclBurner["HCl SYNTHESIS BURNER 2000 C"]
    cl2Liq --> hclBurner
    hclBurner --> absorber["HCl ABSORBER TRAIN ADIABATIC ISOTHERMAL"]
    absorber --> hclProd["COMMERCIAL HCl 32 percent"]
    wasteBrine["DEPLETED BRINE 18 percent NaCl"] --> brineTank
```

## 4.2 Comparative Cell Architecture — Block-Level Functional Diagram

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph mercuryCell["MERCURY CELL CASTNER KELLNER"]
        a1["BRINE INLET TOP"] --> b1["ANODE GRAPHITE TITANIUM"]
        b1 --> c1["LIQUID MERCURY CATHODE FLOWS HORIZONTALLY"]
        c1 --> d1["SODIUM AMALGAM 0.4 percent Na"]
        d1 --> e1["DECOMPOSER WATER INLET"]
        e1 --> f1["PURE NaOH 50 percent OUTLET"]
        e1 --> g1["MERCURY RECYCLE PUMP"]
        b1 --> h1["Cl2 GAS OUTLET"]
        c1 --> i1["NO H2 AT CATHODE AMALGAM FORMS INSTEAD"]
    end

    subgraph diaphragmCell["DIAPHRAGM CELL"]
        a2["BRINE INLET ANODE"] --> b2["ANODE DSA"]
        b2 --> c2["ASBESTOS DIAPHRAGM"]
        c2 --> d2["STEEL CATHODE MESH"]
        d2 --> e2["NaOH 10 percent WITH NaCl OUTLET"]
        b2 --> f2["Cl2 GAS OUTLET"]
        d2 --> g2["H2 GAS OUTLET"]
    end

    subgraph membraneCell["MEMBRANE CELL MODERN STANDARD"]
        a3["BRINE INLET ANODE SIDE"] --> b3["DSA ANODE TITANIUM"]
        b3 --> c3["CATION MEMBRANE NAFION ONLY Na PLUS PASSES"]
        c3 --> d3["STEEL CATHODE MESH"]
        d3 --> e3["WATER INLET CATHODE SIDE"]
        e3 --> f3["NaOH 32 percent PURE LOW NaCl OUTLET"]
        b3 --> g3["Cl2 GAS OUTLET"]
        d3 --> h3["H2 GAS OUTLET"]
    end
```

## 4.3 HCl Synthesis Burner & Absorber Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    h2Supply["H2 FROM CHLOR ALKALI PLANT"] --> burner
    cl2Supply["Cl2 FROM LIQUEFIED STORAGE"] --> burner
    burner["HCl SYNTHESIS BURNER QUARTZ NOZZLE 2000 C FLAME"] --> quencher
    quencher["WATER COOLED QUENCHER 100 C"] --> absorber1
    absorber1["ADIABATIC ABSORBER CO CURRENT DILUTE HCl RECYCLE"] --> absorber2
    absorber2["ISOTHERMAL ABSORBER COUNTER CURRENT FINAL ACID"] --> hclStorage
    absorber2 --> tailGas["TAIL GAS SCRUBBER NaOH WASH"]
    tailGas --> vent["VENT TO ATMOSPHERE"]
    absorber1 --> weakAcid["WEAK HCl RETURN TO ABSORBER 1"]
    hclStorage["COMMERCIAL HCl 32 percent 36 percent"]
```

## 4.4 Mass & Energy Flow Block — Per Tonne Cl₂

```mermaid
graph LR
    feed["FEED STOCKS NaCl 1648 kg H2O 408 kg"]
    feed --> cell["ELECTROLYTIC CELL"]
    cell --> prodCl2["PRODUCTS Cl2 1000 kg H2 28 kg NaOH 1128 kg"]
    cell --> byproduct["BYPRODUCT HEAT AND O2 TRACES"]

```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Modeled after KTU University Exam pattern — concise, definition-based, 50–80 word answers expected.**

---

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**"Define the term 'current efficiency' as applied to a chlor-alkali cell. Mention two causes for its reduction in a diaphragm cell."**

**Model Answer:**
Current efficiency is defined as the ratio of actual mass of product obtained to the theoretical mass predicted by Faraday's law, expressed as a percentage:

$$\eta_{current} = \frac{m_{actual}}{m_{theoretical}} \times 100$$

In a diaphragm cell, current efficiency is reduced primarily by:
1. **Back-migration of OH⁻ ions** through the diaphragm into the anode compartment, where they react with Cl₂ to form hypochlorite and chlorate (consuming current without producing useful Cl₂).
2. **Oxygen evolution** at the anode competing with chlorine evolution (especially at high pH or low Cl⁻ concentration).

Typical diaphragm cell current efficiency: **88–95 %**.

*(3 marks: 1.5 for definition + 1.5 for two causes)*

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**"Differentiate between 'membrane cell' and 'mercury cell' processes for caustic soda manufacture with respect to: (i) product purity, (ii) environmental impact, and (iii) energy consumption."**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Membrane Cell | Mercury Cell |
|-----------|---------------|--------------|
| (i) NaOH Purity | **High** (NaCl < 50 ppm); 32–35 % strength directly | **Very High** (NaCl < 50 ppm); 50 % strength directly |
| (ii) Environmental Impact | **Negligible** — no toxic substances | **Severe Hg pollution** — chronic toxicity, bioaccumulation |
| (iii) Energy Consumption | **Lower** (~2,300 kWh/ton Cl₂) | **Higher** (~3,300 kWh/ton Cl₂) |

Membrane cells are the **modern, dominant technology**; mercury cells are being **phased out globally** under the Minamata Convention.

*(3 marks: 1 mark per parameter)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **Each Part B question is split into two 7-mark sub-parts: (a) descriptive/comparative and (b) numerical/derivation.**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks):** [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 1]

**(a)** Describe with a neat flow diagram the **manufacture of caustic soda and chlorine by the membrane cell process**. Discuss the role of the cation-exchange membrane and explain why 32 % NaOH is obtained directly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A membrane cell operates at a load of **300 kA** with a **cell voltage of 3.2 V** and a **current efficiency of 96 %**. Calculate:
   (i) Daily production of Cl₂ in tonnes.
   (ii) Energy consumption in **kWh per tonne of Cl₂**.
   (iii) Overall energy efficiency of the cell.
  **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to A(a):

**Step 1 — Brine Preparation:** Rock salt dissolved in water, purified by carbonate/hydroxide precipitation, acidified with HCl to pH ~3.

**Step 2 — Electrolysis:** Purified brine enters the **anode compartment** of a membrane cell fitted with **DSA (Dimensionally Stable Anodes — TiO₂-coated titanium)**. The cathode compartment receives deionized water. A **cation-exchange membrane (Nafion)** separates the two compartments and allows **only Na⁺ ions** to migrate from anode to cathode.

**Step 3 — Electrode Reactions:**
- Anode: $2 Cl^- \rightarrow Cl_2 + 2 e^-$
- Cathode: $2 H_2O + 2 e^- \rightarrow H_2 + 2 OH^-$
- Na⁺ migrates through membrane → combines with OH⁻ to form NaOH.

**Step 4 — Product Withdrawal:**
- Cl₂ gas leaves anode compartment (purity ~99 %).
- H₂ gas leaves cathode compartment.
- Dilute NaOH (32 % w/w) leaves cathode compartment.

**Role of membrane:** Selectively passes Na⁺ (not Cl⁻, not OH⁻). This prevents:
- Cl₂ from mixing with H₂ (safety).
- OH⁻ from migrating back and reacting with Cl₂ (efficiency loss).

**Why 32 % NaOH directly?** Because the membrane is highly selective — almost no Cl⁻ passes through — so the catholyte contains only NaOH + H₂O; equilibrium is reached at ~32 % w/w strength with reasonable water feed rate. No need for the complex amalgam-decomposer arrangement of mercury cells.

*(7 marks: 2 for diagram, 2 for reactions, 2 for membrane role, 1 for "why 32 %")*

#### Model Solution to A(b):

**Given:**
- I = 300,000 A
- V = 3.2 V
- η = 0.96
- t = 24 h = 86,400 s

**Step 1 — Theoretical mass of Cl₂ per day (Faraday's law):**

$$m_{theoretical} = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$

Substituting (M for Cl₂ = 71, n = 2):

$$m_{theoretical} = \frac{300{,}000 \times 86{,}400 \times 71}{2 \times 96{,}485}$$

$$= \frac{1.84032 \times 10^{12}}{192{,}970} = 9{,}538{,}464 \text{ g/day} \approx 9.54 \text{ tonnes/day}$$

**Step 2 — Actual production (with current efficiency):**

$$m_{actual} = m_{theoretical} \times \eta_{current} = 9.54 \times 0.96 = 9.16 \text{ tonnes/day}$$

**Step 3 — Energy consumption per tonne of Cl₂:**

$$E = V \times I \times t / m_{actual}$$

$$E = \frac{3.2 \times 300{,}000 \times 86{,}400}{9.16 \times 10^6}$$

Numerator: $3.2 \times 300{,}000 \times 86{,}400 = 8.2944 \times 10^{10}$ J

Convert to kWh: $\div 3.6 \times 10^6$ → $23{,}040$ kWh

Energy per tonne = $23{,}040 / 9.16 = 2{,}515$ kWh/ton Cl₂

**Alternatively, using the formula directly:**

$$E = \frac{V \times 26{,}800}{E_{wt} \times \eta} = \frac{3.2 \times 26{,}800}{35.5 \times 0.96} = \frac{85{,}760}{34.08} = 2{,}517 \text{ kWh/ton Cl₂}$$

**Step 4 — Overall energy efficiency:**

$$\eta_{voltage} = \frac{2.19}{3.20} \times 100 = 68.4\%$$

$$\eta_{overall} = 0.96 \times 0.684 = 0.657 \; (65.7\%)$$

**Final Answers:**
- (i) **9.16 tonnes/day** of Cl₂
- (ii) **2,517 kWh/ton Cl₂**
- (iii) **65.7 %** overall energy efficiency

*(7 marks: 2 for Faraday setup, 2 for energy calculation, 1.5 for each efficiency, 0.5 for unit handling)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks):** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1 — Alternative Choice]

**(a)** With the help of a flow diagram, describe the **manufacture of hydrochloric acid by the direct synthesis (combustion) process** starting from H₂ and Cl₂ obtained from a chlor-alkali plant. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In the HCl absorber, **10,000 kg/h** of HCl gas is absorbed in water to produce **32 % w/w commercial hydrochloric acid**. The feed water is at 25 °C and the absorber operates adiabatically with an absorption efficiency of **98 %**. Calculate:
   (i) Mass of water required per hour.
   (ii) Mass of weak acid produced if the gas entering is only 96 % pure (rest is unabsorbed N₂ from purging).
   **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to B(a):

**Step 1 — Raw Material Source:** H₂ from chlor-alkali cathode compartment; Cl₂ from liquefied Cl₂ storage or directly from anode.

**Step 2 — Pre-mixing & Filtration:** Gases are filtered, dried, and metered in stoichiometric ratio (H₂ : Cl₂ = 1 : 1 molar).

**Step 3 — Synthesis Burner:** The gases enter a refractory-lined burner through a concentric quartz nozzle. H₂ issues from the inner jet; Cl₂ from the annular jet. Ignition is initiated by a spark or pilot flame.

**Reaction:** $H_2 + Cl_2 \rightarrow 2 HCl \quad \Delta H = -184 \text{ kJ/mol}$

The flame temperature reaches ~2000 °C in the burner zone.

**Step 4 — Quenching:** Hot HCl gas is rapidly cooled in a water-cooled shell-and-tube quencher to ~100 °C to prevent corrosion and back-reaction.

**Step 5 — Absorption:**
- **Adiabatic Absorber (1st stage, co-current):** Hot HCl gas contacts dilute HCl (recycled from 2nd stage) in a falling-film or packed column. Heat of absorption is removed by adiabatic water evaporation.
- **Isothermal Absorber (2nd stage, counter-current):** Final acid (32–36 % w/w) is collected by contacting cooled HCl gas with water on graphite or PVDF-packed trays.

**Step 6 — Tail Gas Treatment:** Unabsorbed HCl is scrubbed with dilute NaOH to meet emission norms.

**Step 7 — Storage:** Commercial HCl stored in rubber-lined or FRP tanks.

**Advantages:**
- High purity acid (no sulfate impurity unlike Mannheim process).
- Continuous operation, easily scalable.
- Direct integration with chlor-alkali complex.

*(7 marks: 1.5 for raw materials, 2 for burner design, 1.5 for absorption stages, 1 for tail gas, 1 for advantages)*

#### Model Solution to B(b):

**Given:**
- HCl absorbed = 10,000 kg/h
- Product acid strength = 32 % w/w HCl
- Absorption efficiency = 98 %

**Step 1 — Mass of water required (stoichiometric, ignoring absorber equilibrium for simplicity):**

For 100 kg of 32 % acid: 32 kg HCl + 68 kg H₂O.

So for 10,000 kg HCl absorbed:

$$\text{Mass of water} = 10{,}000 \times \frac{68}{32} = 21{,}250 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Step 2 — Verification with overall material balance:**
Total product = 10,000 + 21,250 = 31,250 kg/h of 32 % acid.

HCl fraction = 10,000 / 31,250 = 0.32 ✓

**Step 3 — Adjusting for absorption efficiency:**
Since only 98 % is absorbed, the **HCl entering** the absorber must be:

$$m_{in} = \frac{10{,}000}{0.98} = 10{,}204.08 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Step 4 — Weak acid mass calculation (assuming weak acid is the 20 % recirculating stream — a typical design choice):**

The 2 % HCl that is **not absorbed** = 10,204.08 – 10,000 = **204.08 kg/h** leaves with the inert N₂ as tail gas and is scrubbed.

The water requirement stays the same (21,250 kg/h) since water is needed to dissolve the absorbed HCl.

**Step 5 — Recomputed product acid flow:**

$$\text{Product acid} = 10{,}000 \text{ (HCl)} + 21{,}250 \text{ (H}_2\text{O)} = 31{,}250 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Step 6 — Weak acid stream (if recirculated at 20 % w/w HCl):**
Typically 3–5× the product flow is recirculated. Assuming recycle ratio = 4:

$$\text{Weak acid} = 4 \times 31{,}250 = 125{,}000 \text{ kg/h at 20% w/w HCl}$$

This weak acid contains: $125{,}000 \times 0.20 = 25{,}000 \text{ kg HCl}$ and $100{,}000 \text{ kg H}_2\text{O}$, of which $25{,}000 - 10{,}000 = 15{,}000 \text{ kg HCl}$ came from the recycle back-mixing (steady state internal recirculation).

**Final Answers:**
- (i) Water required ≈ **21,250 kg/h**
- (ii) Weak acid ≈ **125,000 kg/h at 20 % w/w** (depending on chosen recycle ratio)

*(7 marks: 2 for setup, 2 for water calculation, 2 for weak acid balance, 1 for absorption efficiency adjustment)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing M with M/n:** Students often use **molar mass (71 for Cl₂)** instead of **equivalent weight (35.5)** in the energy formula. The energy equation requires E_wt = M/n.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the current efficiency factor:** The energy formula $\dfrac{V \times 26{,}800}{E_{wt}}$ gives **theoretical minimum** energy. You **must divide by η_current** for the actual energy consumption.
>
> 3. **Wrong sign in decomposition voltage:** $E^0_{cell} = E^0_{cathode} - E^0_{anode}$, NOT the reverse. Cathode potential is negative (reduction), anode is positive (oxidation).
>
> 4. **Mis-stoichiometry of brine reaction:** The reaction is $2 NaCl + 2 H_2O \rightarrow 2 NaOH + Cl_2 + H_2$. For every **2 moles of NaCl** consumed, only **1 mole of Cl₂** is produced — students often write "1 NaCl gives 1 Cl₂" (incorrect).
>
> 5. **Mixing up cell types' NaOH strength:** Mercury cell = 50 %; Membrane cell = 32 %; Diaphragm cell = 10 %. Examiner **deducts 1 mark** if this is wrong.
>
> 6. **Unit mistakes:** kWh vs Wh, kg vs tonnes, A vs kA — read the question carefully!
>
> 7. **Missing flow diagram:** For 14-mark descriptive questions, **a labeled block diagram is mandatory**. Without it, **2 marks are deducted**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-density, rapid-revision checklist (read this 30 minutes before exam):**

- ⚡ **Overall Reaction:** $2 NaCl + 2 H_2O \rightarrow 2 NaOH + Cl_2 + H_2$
- ⚡ **Theoretical Decomposition Voltage = 2.19 V** (cathode –0.83 V; anode +1.36 V)
- ⚡ **Actual Cell Voltage:** 3.0–4.5 V (Mercury highest, Membrane lowest)
- ⚡ **Faraday's Constant = 96,485 C/mol** = 26.8 Ah/mol
- ⚡ **Energy Formula:** $\dfrac{V \times 26{,}800}{E_{wt} \times \eta}$ kWh/ton
- ⚡ **Equivalent Weights:** NaOH = 40; Cl₂ = 35.5; H₂ = 1; HCl = 36.5
- ⚡ **Mercury Cell:** 50 % NaOH direct, but Hg pollution → **phased out**
- ⚡ **Diaphragm Cell:** 10–12 % NaOH + 1 % NaCl impurity → needs evaporation
- ⚡ **Membrane Cell:** 32 % NaOH, 95–98 % η, **modern standard**
- ⚡ **Nafion Membrane:** Permits only Na⁺; blocks Cl⁻ and OH⁻
- ⚡ **DSA Anode:** Ti substrate coated with TiO₂/RuO₂ — non-consumable
- ⚡ **Current Efficiency Losses:** OH⁻ back-migration, O₂ evolution, chlorate formation
- ⚡ **HCl Direct Synthesis:** $H_2 + Cl_2 \rightarrow 2 HCl$; ΔH = –184 kJ/mol; flame ~2000 °C
- ⚡ **HCl Absorber Stages:** Adiabatic (1st, co-current) + Isothermal (2nd, counter-current)
- ⚡ **Mannheim Process:** $2 NaCl + H_2SO_4 \rightarrow Na_2SO_4 + 2 HCl$ (declining, sulfate market dependent)
- ⚡ **Per tonne Cl₂:** Need 1.648 t NaCl, 0.408 t H₂O; produce 1.128 t NaOH, 0.028 t H₂
- ⚡ **Minamata Convention (2013):** Drives global phase-out of mercury cells
- ⚡ **Voltage Efficiency:** $\eta_V = \dfrac{E^0_{decomp}}{V_{cell}} \times 100$
- ⚡ **Overall Efficiency:** $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{current} \times \eta_{voltage}$
- ⚡ **Always draw a labeled block diagram** for 14-mark descriptive answers
- ⚡ **Units, units, units** — kA, kWh/ton, % w/w — examiner is strict

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Nitrogen Industries - Ammonia
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT637 - CHEMICAL TECHNOLOGY AND REACTION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Nitrogen Industries - Ammonia

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Ammonia (NH₃)** is the most important nitrogen-containing inorganic chemical, manufactured on a tonnage scale worldwide. Industrially, it is produced almost exclusively by the **Haber-Bosch Process**, in which a synthesis gas mixture of **hydrogen (H₂)** and **nitrogen (N₂)** in stoichiometric ratio (3:1 by volume) is reacted at high pressure (150–300 atm) and moderately high temperature (400–500 °C) over a promoted iron-based heterogeneous catalyst.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PESFT637 – Module 1):**
> Nitrogen Industries → **Ammonia**: Manufacture from natural gas / naphtha, partial oxidation, raw materials, reactions, catalysts, process flow diagram, operating conditions, and major engineering challenges.

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview & Real-World Analogy

Think of ammonia as **"the molecular bridge between the atmosphere and life."** Roughly **78%** of Earth's atmosphere is nitrogen gas (N₂), but N₂ is extraordinarily unreactive because of its triple bond (bond dissociation energy ≈ **945 kJ/mol**). Plants cannot use atmospheric nitrogen directly — they need **"fixed" nitrogen** like ammonia, nitrates, or urea. Without industrial ammonia synthesis, modern agriculture (feeding ~8 billion people) would collapse.

**Real-World Analogy — The "Pressure Cooker for Molecules":**
Imagine trying to force two magnets together when they repel each other. The Haber-Bosch process is like a massive **pressure cooker** that:
1. **Compresses** the gases (pressure does the "pushing"),
2. **Heats** them to give molecules enough energy to overcome repulsion,
3. Uses a **catalyst** as a "matchmaker surface" to help N≡N break apart and bond with H atoms.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Industrial Relevance:**
> - ~**85%** of global ammonia → **fertilizers** (urea, ammonium nitrate, ammonium sulfate).
> - Remaining → explosives (TNT, nitroglycerin), refrigerants, nitric acid, nylon precursors, pharmaceuticals, and wastewater treatment.

## 1.3 Key Physical & Thermodynamic Constants

| Property | Value (Standard) |
|---|---|
| Molecular weight of NH₃ | **17.03 g/mol** |
| Boiling point (1 atm) | **−33.34 °C** |
| Critical temperature | **132.4 °C** |
| Critical pressure | **111.3 atm** |
| Heat of formation (ΔH°f, 298 K) | **−46.11 kJ/mol** (exothermic) |
| Bond dissociation energy (N≡N) | **945 kJ/mol** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Haber-Bosch Reaction — Core Chemistry

The primary synthesis reaction is:

$$N_2(g) + 3H_2(g) \rightleftharpoons 2NH_3(g), \quad \Delta H_{298}^{\circ} = -92.4 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

This reaction is:
- **Exothermic** (releases heat) → favors **low temperature** at equilibrium.
- **Decrease in moles** (4 moles → 2 moles) → favors **high pressure** at equilibrium.
- **Kinetically slow** at low T → requires a **catalyst** and elevated T.

## 2.2 Thermodynamic & Kinetic Trade-off

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The central design challenge of ammonia synthesis is the **thermodynamic vs. kinetic conflict**:
> - **Low T** (e.g., 200 °C) → high equilibrium conversion but reaction is too slow.
> - **High T** (e.g., 700 °C) → fast kinetics but equilibrium shifts *backward*, giving low conversion.
> - **Compromise:** 400–500 °C + 150–300 atm + excellent catalyst.

## 2.3 Catalyst — The Iron "Matchmaker"

The **doubly-promoted fused iron catalyst** is the industrial workhorse:
- **Active phase:** Reduced α-Fe (metallic iron with body-centered cubic lattice).
- **Structural promoter:** Al₂O₃ → prevents iron crystallite sintering.
- **Electronic promoter:** K₂O (potassium oxide) → weakens N≡N bond on Fe surface, increases activity.
- Other promoters: **CaO, MgO** (resist acid poisoning, extend life).
- Operating life: **5–15 years** before re-catalysis.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why iron?** Fritz Haber tested **>20,000 catalysts** in 1909. Osmium and uranium were more active but expensive/poisonous. Iron won on cost-activity-toxicity balance. Ru (ruthenium) is more active but used only in modern Kellogg Advanced Ammonia Process (KAAP).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Equilibrium constant expression | $K_p = \dfrac{P_{NH_3}^2}{P_{N_2}\cdot P_{H_2}^3}$ | Dimensionless (or atm⁻²) |
| Single-pass conversion | $X = \dfrac{2 \times \text{moles NH}_3 \text{ produced}}{\text{moles N}_2 \text{ fed}}$ | Fraction (0–1) |
| Stoichiometric feed ratio | $\dfrac{H_2}{N_2} = 3:1$ (molar) | Industrial optimum: 2.8–3.2 |
| Van't Hoff equation (T effect) | $\dfrac{d(\ln K_p)}{dT} = \dfrac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{RT^2}$ | Predicts Kp drop with T |
| Le Chatelier (P effect) | $K_y = K_p \cdot (P)^{\Delta n}$, $\Delta n = -2$ | High P → higher NH₃ |
| Space velocity (GHSV) | $GHSV = \dfrac{\text{Vol gas feed (std)}}{\text{Vol catalyst} \cdot h}$ | h⁻¹; industrial: 5,000–15,000 h⁻¹ |
| Catalyst activity | $a = \dfrac{k_{eff}}{k_{fresh}}$ | Drops to 0.5–0.6 over life |
| Per-pass yield (typical) | 15–25% (single converter) | Improves with recycle |
| Overall plant efficiency (modern) | ~**33.5 GJ/ton NH₃** (best) | Theoretical min: 20.9 GJ/ton |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In table rows, never use the bare vertical pipe `|`. Use `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking the table parser — e.g., write $\dfrac{H_2}{N_2} \mid 3:1$ if needed.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Optimizing H₂/N₂ ratio:** Pure stoichiometric feed (3:1) gives highest single-pass conversion, but **inert gases (Ar, CH₄)** accumulate in the recycle loop. A slight excess H₂ (ratio ≈ 3.0–3.2) compensates for losses.
- **Reactor design dictates everything:** Fixed-bed converters dominate (≈ **70%** of world capacity), with axial-flow, radial-flow, and horizontal designs.
- **Energy integration:** Modern plants recover waste heat from the converter to generate **100–130 bar steam**, driving the syngas compressor → < **33.5 GJ/ton** thermal efficiency.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Equilibrium Constant & Temperature Dependence

**Starting point:** For the reaction $N_2 + 3H_2 \rightleftharpoons 2NH_3$, the standard free energy change is:

$$\Delta G^{\circ} = -RT \ln K_p$$

Using standard thermodynamic data (ΔH°ₓ, ΔS°ₓ at 298 K), integrate to find $K_p(T)$:

$$\ln \frac{K_p(T_2)}{K_p(T_1)} = -\frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{R} \left( \frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1} \right)$$

### Step-by-Step Numerical Example (KTU-style problem)

**Problem:** At 500 °C (773 K), the experimental value is $K_p = 6.5 \times 10^{-5}$ atm⁻². At 700 °C (973 K), what is $K_p$? Use $\Delta H^{\circ} = -92.4$ kJ/mol. Comment on the result.

**Step 1 — Set up Van't Hoff equation:**

$$\ln K_p(T_2) - \ln K_p(T_1) = -\frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute known values (R = 8.314 J/mol·K, ΔH° = −92,400 J/mol):**

$$\ln K_p(973) = \ln(6.5 \times 10^{-5}) - \left(\frac{-92400}{8.314}\right)\left(\frac{1}{973} - \frac{1}{773}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the temperature bracket:**

$$\frac{1}{973} - \frac{1}{773} = \frac{773 - 973}{973 \times 773} = \frac{-200}{752129} = -2.659 \times 10^{-4} \text{ K}^{-1}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the enthalpy term:**

$$-\frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{R} = -\frac{-92400}{8.314} = +11113.5 \text{ K}$$

**Step 5 — Multiply:**

$$(+11113.5) \times (-2.659 \times 10^{-4}) = -2.955$$

**Step 6 — Compute log term:**

$$\ln(6.5 \times 10^{-5}) = \ln(6.5) + \ln(10^{-5}) = 1.8718 - 11.5129 = -9.6411$$

**Step 7 — Final result:**

$$\ln K_p(973) = -9.6411 - 2.955 = -12.596$$

$$K_p(973) = e^{-12.596} = 3.36 \times 10^{-6} \text{ atm}^{-2}$$

**Step 8 — Engineering interpretation:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> $K_p$ drops by **~19×** when temperature rises from 500 °C → 700 °C. This confirms Le Chatelier: **higher T = lower equilibrium NH₃**, justifying why industrial reactors stay at 400–500 °C.

## 3.2 Derivation: Single-Pass Conversion Expression

**Feed basis:** Let $F_{N_2,0} = 1$ mol/s. Then $F_{H_2,0} = 3$ mol/s (stoichiometric), $F_{NH_3,0} = 0$.

Let $X$ = fractional conversion of N₂. Then by stoichiometry:

$$F_{N_2} = 1 - X, \quad F_{H_2} = 3(1 - X), \quad F_{NH_3} = 2X$$

Total moles at equilibrium:

$$F_T = (1 - X) + 3(1 - X) + 2X = 4 - 2X$$

Partial pressures (total pressure P, assuming ideal gas, mole fraction = yᵢ = Fᵢ/F_T):

$$P_{NH_3} = \frac{2X}{4 - 2X} P, \quad P_{N_2} = \frac{1 - X}{4 - 2X} P, \quad P_{H_2} = \frac{3(1 - X)}{4 - 2X} P$$

Substituting into equilibrium expression:

$$K_p = \frac{(2X \cdot P)^2 / (4 - 2X)^2}{\left[(1 - X)P / (4 - 2X)\right] \cdot \left[3(1 - X)P / (4 - 2X)\right]^3}$$

**Simplify step-by-step:**

$$K_p = \frac{4X^2 P^2 (4 - 2X)^4}{(4 - 2X)^2 (1 - X)^4 \cdot 27 P^4}$$

**Cancel and reduce:**

$$K_p = \frac{4X^2 (4 - 2X)^2}{27 P^2 (1 - X)^4}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Equation for Stoichiometric Feed at Pressure P:**
> $$K_p = \frac{4X^2 (2 - X)^2}{27 P^2 (1 - X)^4} \cdot \frac{1}{1} = \frac{4X^2(2 - X)^2}{27 P^2 (1 - X)^4}$$
> Solving this quartic iteratively gives the single-pass conversion $X$ at any $(T, P)$.

**Numerical check at T = 773 K, P = 200 atm:**

$K_p = 6.5 \times 10^{-5}$ atm⁻². Plugging in and solving iteratively (try $X = 0.20$):

$$\frac{4(0.04)(1.8)^2}{27(40000)(0.8)^4} = \frac{4 \times 0.04 \times 3.24}{27 \times 40000 \times 0.4096} = \frac{0.5184}{442368} \approx 1.17 \times 10^{-6}$$

This is far below $K_p$, so conversion is far from equilibrium. **Industrial reality:** even at 200 atm, 500 °C, single-pass conversion is only **15–25%** → recycle loop essential.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Operating Variables

| Variable | Effect on Equilibrium Conversion | Effect on Rate | Industrial Choice |
|---|---|---|---|
| ↑ Temperature (T) | ↓↓ (sharply) | ↑↑ (Arrhenius) | Compromise 400–500 °C |
| ↑ Pressure (P) | ↑ (mole decrease) | ↑ (concentration) | 150–300 atm |
| ↑ H₂/N₂ ratio | ↑ up to 3.0, then ↓ | Modest ↑ | 3.0–3.2 |
| Better catalyst | No effect on K | ↑↑ | Fe-K-Al₂O₃ |
| ↑ Space velocity | ↓ per-pass | ↑ productivity | 8,000–15,000 h⁻¹ |
| Inerts (Ar, CH₄) | ↓ | ↓ | Bleed stream to purge |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Overall Haber-Bosch Process Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

The Haber-Bosch plant is a **multi-section integrated flowsheet**. Below is the **Block-Level Functional Architecture** mapping the syngas preparation, ammonia synthesis, and product recovery sections.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    NGIN[Natural Gas Feed]:::feed --> DESULF[Desulfurization ZnO Guard Bed]:::prep
    DESULF --> PRIMREF[Primary Reformer CH4 + H2O Ni Catalyst 800C]:::reformer
    PRIMREF --> SECREF[Secondary Reformer Air Addition N2 Source]:::reformer
    SECREF --> HTS[High Temperature Shift CO + H2O to CO2 + H2 Fe3O4 400C]:::shift
    HTS --> LTS[Low Temperature Shift Cu-ZnO 200C]:::shift
    LTS --> CO2ABS[CO2 Absorption MEA / Benfield]:::purify
    CO2ABS --> METH[Methanation CO CO2 to CH4 Ni 300C]:::purify
    METH --> COMP[Syngas Compressor Centrifugal 200-300 atm]:::compress
    COMP --> MIX[MIX with Recycle Stream 3 to 1 H2 N2]:::mix
    MIX --> CONV[Ammonia Converter Fixed Bed Fe Catalyst 450C 200 atm]:::reactor
    CONV --> WHEX[Waste Heat Boiler Steam Generation 100 bar]:::recover
    WHEX --> COOL[Process Gas Cooling Heat Exchange]:::recover
    COOL --> AMMABS[Ammonia Condensation and Separation -33C]:::product
    AMMABS --> AMMSTOR[Liquid Ammonia Storage Atmospheric or Pressurized]:::product
    AMMABS --> RECYCLE[Purge and Recycle Loop Back to MIX]:::recycle
    RECYCLE --> MIX

    classDef feed fill:#fde68a,stroke:#b45309,color:#1f2937
    classDef prep fill:#bae6fd,stroke:#0369a1,color:#1f2937
    classDef reformer fill:#fecaca,stroke:#b91c1c,color:#1f2937
    classDef shift fill:#fed7aa,stroke:#c2410c,color:#1f2937
    classDef purify fill:#d9f99d,stroke:#4d7c0f,color:#1f2937
    classDef compress fill:#e9d5ff,stroke:#6b21a8,color:#1f2937
    classDef mix fill:#fbcfe8,stroke:#9d174d,color:#1f2937
    classDef reactor fill:#fcd34d,stroke:#92400e,color:#1f2937,stroke-width:3px
    classDef recover fill:#a7f3d0,stroke:#047857,color:#1f2937
    classDef product fill:#93c5fd,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#1f2937
    classDef recycle fill:#cbd5e1,stroke:#475569,color:#1f2937
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the diagram:**
> - **Yellow blocks** = Raw material input.
> - **Red blocks** = Reforming section (syngas generation).
> - **Orange blocks** = Water-gas shift converters.
> - **Green blocks** = Acid gas removal & final purification.
> - **Purple block** = Syngas compression.
> - **Gold (thick border)** = **Ammonia synthesis converter** (the heart of the plant).
> - **Blue blocks** = Product recovery & storage.
> - **Gray block** = Recycle loop (essential because single-pass conversion is only 15–25%).

## 4.2 Schematic of the Ammonia Synthesis Converter

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    FEEDGAS[Inlet Synthesis Gas 3H2 N2 200 atm 400C]:::in --> TOPDIST[Top Distribution Head]:::equip
    TOPDIST --> CAT1[First Catalyst Bed Fe K Al2O3]:::cat
    CAT1 --> HEATEX1[Inter-bed Heat Exchanger Cold Shot Cooling]:::equip
    HEATEX1 --> CAT2[Second Catalyst Bed]:::cat
    CATEX2[Continue]:::ghost --> CAT3[Third Catalyst Bed]:::cat
    CAT3 --> BOTDIST[Bottom Collection Plenum]:::equip
    BOTDIST --> OUTLET[Outlet Gas 15-20 percent NH3]:::out

    classDef in fill:#fde68a,stroke:#b45309
    classDef equip fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#3730a3
    classDef cat fill:#fcd34d,stroke:#92400e,stroke-width:2px
    classDef out fill:#86efac,stroke:#166534
    classDef ghost fill:#ffffff,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Quench-Type Converter Operation:** The reaction is exothermic, so the temperature rises as gas flows through each catalyst bed. **Cold shot (cold bypass) injection** between beds controls the temperature profile, keeping it close to the optimal **Temkin-Pyzhev optimum curve** (rising then falling temperature yields highest net rate).

## 4.3 Simplified Process Steps (KTU Exam Summary)

1. **Feedstock preparation:** Natural gas → desulfurization.
2. **Steam reforming:** CH₄ + H₂O → CO + 3H₂ (primary reformer, Ni catalyst, 800 °C, 30 atm).
3. **Secondary reforming:** Air injection → provides N₂, completes CH₄ conversion.
4. **Water-gas shift:** CO + H₂O → CO₂ + H₂ (HTS at 400 °C + LTS at 200 °C).
5. **CO₂ removal:** MEA, hot potash, or Rectisol process.
6. **Methanation:** Residual CO/CO₂ + H₂ → CH₄ (to avoid catalyst poisoning).
7. **Compression:** Multi-stage centrifugal to 150–300 atm.
8. **Synthesis loop:** Converter → heat recovery → NH₃ condensation → recycle.
9. **Storage:** Refrigerated atmospheric (−33 °C) or pressurized (16–20 atm).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*State the principle of ammonia synthesis. Why is an optimum temperature maintained in the Haber-Bosch converter?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Ammonia is synthesized from hydrogen and nitrogen in a 3:1 molar ratio over a promoted iron catalyst at 400–500 °C and 150–300 atm:

$$N_2 + 3H_2 \rightleftharpoons 2NH_3, \quad \Delta H = -92.4 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

Although **thermodynamically** lower temperature favors higher NH₃ equilibrium yield, the **kinetic** rate is unacceptably slow below ~400 °C. Hence an optimum temperature (~450 °C) is maintained along the **Temkin-Pyzhev optimum curve** to balance rate and conversion. **(1 + 1 + 1 = 3 marks)**

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List any three promoters used in the iron catalyst for ammonia synthesis. What is the role of each?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Al₂O₃ (alumina):** Structural promoter — prevents sintering of iron crystallites, increases surface area. **(1 mark)**
2. **K₂O (potassium oxide):** Electronic promoter — donates electrons to Fe, weakens the N≡N bond on adsorption, increasing catalytic activity. **(1 mark)**
3. **CaO / MgO:** Acid-resistant promoter — neutralizes acidic poisons (e.g., H₂S traces), extends catalyst life. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> **Examiner's Note (KTU Pattern):** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, typically split into two sub-parts: (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Either choice (A or B) is offered; students answer only one.

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**

**(a)** Draw a neat **flow diagram of the Haber-Bosch process for ammonia manufacture from natural gas** and explain the function of **any four major units**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The flow diagram is as given in **Section 4.1 (Mermaid Block Diagram)** of these notes. Functions of four key units:

| Unit | Function |
|---|---|
| **Primary Reformer (Ni, 800 °C)** | Endothermic reaction $CH_4 + H_2O \to CO + 3H_2$ in catalyst tubes heated externally; produces ~70% of total H₂. |
| **Secondary Reformer (with air)** | Air introduces N₂ for synthesis; completes CH₄ conversion via combustion + reforming. |
| **Shift Converters (HTS + LTS)** | Convert CO to CO₂ and produce additional H₂: $CO + H_2O \to CO_2 + H_2$; LTS achieves >99% conversion. |
| **CO₂ Absorption (MEA / Benfield)** | Removes CO₂ using chemical/physical solvent to prevent downstream catalyst poisoning. |
| **Ammonia Converter (Fe cat., 450 °C)** | The heart: $N_2 + 3H_2 \to 2NH_3$ at 200 atm; multi-bed with inter-cooling. |

(Marks: 3 for diagram + 4 × 1 = 7 marks)

**(b)** With relevant equations, explain the **effect of temperature and pressure on ammonia synthesis equilibrium**. A feed containing 25% N₂, 75% H₂ (by volume) enters the converter at 200 atm and 450 °C. Calculate the **equilibrium conversion of nitrogen** given $K_p = 6.5 \times 10^{-5}$ atm⁻² at 450 °C. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Effect of T and P:**
- **Temperature:** The reaction is exothermic ($\Delta H < 0$). By Le Chatelier, low T favors NH₃. By Van't Hoff, $K_p$ decreases as T increases. *(1 mark)*
- **Pressure:** 4 moles → 2 moles, i.e., $\Delta n = -2$. By Le Chatelier, higher P shifts equilibrium to NH₃. Mathematically, $K_y = K_p \cdot P^{\Delta n} = K_p / P^2$, so higher P → higher $y_{NH_3}$ at equilibrium. *(1 mark)*

**Equilibrium conversion calculation:**

Let initial moles: $N_2 = 1$, $H_2 = 3$ (per 4 mol feed; 25% N₂, 75% H₂ gives ratio 1:3). At conversion $X$:

$$N_2 = 1 - X, \quad H_2 = 3(1 - X), \quad NH_3 = 2X$$
$$F_T = 1 - X + 3(1 - X) + 2X = 4 - 2X$$

Partial pressures (P = 200 atm):

$$P_{N_2} = \frac{1 - X}{4 - 2X} \cdot 200, \quad P_{H_2} = \frac{3(1 - X)}{4 - 2X} \cdot 200, \quad P_{NH_3} = \frac{2X}{4 - 2X} \cdot 200$$

Substitute in $K_p$:

$$K_p = \frac{(2X)^2 (4 - 2X)^2}{(1 - X)(3(1 - X))^3} \cdot \frac{1}{200^2}$$

**Simplify:**

$$6.5 \times 10^{-5} = \frac{4X^2 (4 - 2X)^2}{27 (1 - X)^4 \cdot 40000}$$

**Iteration (try X = 0.20):**

$$RHS = \frac{4(0.04)(3.6)^2}{27(0.8)^4 \cdot 40000} = \frac{0.5184}{442368} = 1.17 \times 10^{-6}$$

Try X = 0.15:

$$RHS = \frac{4(0.0225)(3.7)^2}{27(0.85)^4 \cdot 40000} = \frac{1.2321}{564622} = 2.18 \times 10^{-6}$$

Try X = 0.12:

$$RHS = \frac{4(0.0144)(3.76)^2}{27(0.88)^4 \cdot 40000} = \frac{0.8145}{647940} = 1.26 \times 10^{-6}$$

Try X = 0.115:

$$RHS \approx \frac{4(0.0132)(3.77)^2}{27(0.885)^4 \cdot 40000} \approx 1.16 \times 10^{-6}$$

By interpolation, the correct X lies between 0.115 and 0.12. Closer to X ≈ **0.117 (≈ 11.7% single-pass conversion)**.

(1 mark for setup, 1 mark for substitution, 3 marks for iteration, 1 mark for final answer) = **7 marks**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]** *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a)** Discuss the **various catalysts used in ammonia synthesis**. Why is iron preferred over osmium and ruthenium even though the latter are more active? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Catalyst history and types:**

1. **Iron (Fe) catalyst** — Doubly promoted (Al₂O₃ + K₂O + CaO/MgO). Active phase is reduced α-Fe. Industrial workhorse since 1910. Cost-effective, abundant, regenerable. *(1.5 marks)*
2. **Osmium (Os)** — Haber's original choice (1909). Higher activity than Fe but extremely rare, expensive, and toxic. Not commercially viable. *(1 mark)*
3. **Ruthenium (Ru) on graphite** — Used in the **Kellogg Advanced Ammonia Process (KAAP)** since 1992. ~10–20× more active than Fe, allows lower pressure (90–120 atm) operation. However, Ru is expensive and sensitive to poisoning. *(1.5 marks)*
4. **Cobalt molybdenum (Co-Mo)** — Used in some pre-reformers for sulfur-tolerant operation. *(1 mark)*

**Why iron is preferred over Ru/Os:**
- **Cost:** Fe is ~1000× cheaper than Ru.
- **Abundance:** Fe is one of Earth's most abundant metals.
- **Robustness:** Tolerates trace poisons, has long life (5–15 years).
- **Regenerability:** Easily reduced/re-activated.
- **Performance adequacy:** With proper promoters, modern Fe catalysts achieve acceptable activity.

Net: *Best cost-to-performance ratio* → iron remains dominant (~70%+ of world capacity). *(2 marks)*

**(b)** A fresh ammonia plant processes **1000 kmol/h** of stoichiometric syngas (3:1 H₂:N₂) at **250 atm** and **450 °C** with single-pass conversion of **22%**. Calculate: **(i)** moles of NH₃ produced per hour, **(ii)** moles of unreacted N₂ and H₂ in the exit stream, (iii) overall plant production rate if **98% of unreacted gases are recycled** and the purge contains the remaining 2%. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Basis: $F_{N_2,0} = 250$ kmol/h, $F_{H_2,0} = 750$ kmol/h (3:1 stoichiometric).

**(i) Ammonia produced per pass:**
$$F_{NH_3} = 2 \times X \times F_{N_2,0} = 2 \times 0.22 \times 250 = 110 \text{ kmol/h}$$

**(ii) Unreacted gases at exit (after one pass):**
$$F_{N_2, exit} = 250(1 - 0.22) = 195 \text{ kmol/h}$$
$$F_{H_2, exit} = 750(1 - 0.22) = 585 \text{ kmol/h}$$
$$F_{NH_3, exit} = 110 \text{ kmol/h (after separation, this is the product)}$$

After NH₃ condensation, unreacted gases available for recycle:
$$F_{N_2, recycle} = 195 \text{ kmol/h}, \quad F_{H_2, recycle} = 585 \text{ kmol/h}$$

**(iii) Overall plant production with recycle:**

Recycle stream (98% of unreacted gases):
$$F_{N_2, rec} = 0.98 \times 195 = 191.1 \text{ kmol/h}$$
$$F_{H_2, rec} = 0.98 \times 585 = 573.3 \text{ kmol/h}$$

Purge (2% of unreacted gases):
$$F_{N_2, purge} = 3.9 \text{ kmol/h}, \quad F_{H_2, purge} = 11.7 \text{ kmol/h}$$

Fresh feed needed to maintain steady state = purge loss in syngas equivalent:
- To keep ratio 3:1 in feed, fresh feed per pass must add 3.9 kmol/h N₂ and 11.7 kmol/h H₂.
- This means at steady state, the **net conversion of fresh feed** ≈ 100% (within rounding).

**Total ammonia produced = feed H₂ converted / (3/2) per pass equivalent:**

Over many passes, the total NH₃ produced per hour equals the rate at which fresh syngas is fed (since the system runs at steady state with only purge loss):

Inlet (steady state) = 1000 kmol/h fresh syngas (250 N₂ + 750 H₂).
Net converted (lost to purge): 3.9 + 11.7 = 15.6 kmol/h syngas.
Thus converted to NH₃: $1000 - 15.6 = 984.4$ kmol/h syngas.
Stoichiometry: 2 kmol NH₃ per 2 kmol syngas (N₂ + 3H₂ → 2NH₃; total 4 mol syngas → 2 mol NH₃).
So $F_{NH_3, plant} = (984.4 / 4) \times 2 = 492.2$ kmol/h.

(1 + 2 + 4 = 7 marks; 1 mark for approach, 2 marks for (i)+(ii), 4 marks for recycle calculation)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where students lose marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting NH₃ condensation step** — unreacted gases must be separated from product *before* recycle; if not shown, deduct 1 mark.
> 2. **Wrong stoichiometry** — confusion between "per-pass conversion" and "overall conversion" leads to errors in recycle problems.
> 3. **Not justifying catalyst choice** — listing promoters without explaining *electronic vs. structural* role loses 1–2 marks.
> 4. **No units in final answer** — KTU deducts 0.5 mark for missing units in numerical answers.
> 5. **Skipping Van't Hoff / Le Chatelier discussion** in equilibrium problems = -2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Ammonia is synthesized** by the reaction $N_2 + 3H_2 \rightleftharpoons 2NH_3$, $\Delta H = -92.4$ kJ/mol.
- **Industrial operating window:** 400–500 °C, 150–300 atm, Fe-K-Al₂O₃ catalyst, 3:1 H₂:N₂ ratio.
- **Thermodynamic vs. kinetic trade-off** is the central design challenge.
- **Single-pass conversion = 15–25%** → recycle loop with NH₃ separation is mandatory.
- **Catalyst promoters:** Al₂O₃ (structural), K₂O (electronic), CaO/MgO (acid resistance).
- **Process steps:** Desulfurization → Primary reforming (Ni) → Secondary reforming (air) → HTS + LTS shift → CO₂ removal → Methanation → Compression → Synthesis → Condensation → Recycle.
- **Equilibrium expression:** $K_p = P_{NH_3}^2 / (P_{N_2} P_{H_2}^3)$ and master equation for stoichiometric feed:
  $$K_p = \frac{4X^2(2 - X)^2}{27 P^2 (1 - X)^4}$$
- **Van't Hoff equation:** $\ln(K_{p,2}/K_{p,1}) = -(\Delta H^{\circ}/R)(1/T_2 - 1/T_1)$.
- **Energy benchmark:** Modern plants ~ **33.5 GJ/ton NH₃**; theoretical minimum **20.9 GJ/ton**.
- **Application dominance:** ~85% of NH₃ → fertilizers (urea, ammonium nitrate, ammonium sulfate).
- **Modern alternatives:** Ru-based KAAP catalyst, electrocatalytic Haber-Bosch, "green ammonia" from H₂ via water electrolysis (renewable route) — emerging frontier research area.
- **Syngas sources:** Natural gas (dominant), naphtha, coal (China), heavy residues (partial oxidation), biomass gasification (future).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Nitric Acid
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT637 - CHEMICAL TECHNOLOGY AND REACTION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Nitric Acid

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition
**Nitric Acid (HNO₃)** is a highly corrosive, strongly oxidizing mineral acid (also called *aqua fortis* or "spirit of niter"), manufactured industrially almost exclusively via the **Ostwald Process** — a heterogeneous catalytic oxidation of ammonia. It is one of the **top ten commodity chemicals** in the world and is the most important industrial nitrogen acid (excluding sulfuric acid for general use).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** Nitric acid is manufactured by the catalytic oxidation of ammonia (Ostwald process). The process involves three sequential steps: **(1) Ammonia oxidation to nitric oxide (NO)**, **(2) Oxidation of NO to nitrogen dioxide (NO₂)**, and **(3) Absorption of NO₂ in water** to form aqueous HNO₃ of commercial strength (50–70 wt%).

### 1.2 Industrial Significance
- **Key downstream product:** Ammonium Nitrate (NH₄NO₃) — used in fertilizers and explosives.
- **Use in explosives:** Nitroglycerin, TNT, RDX, and gunpowder.
- **Use in polymers:** Manufacture of **adipic acid** (for Nylon 6,6) and **Toluene Diisocyanate (TDI)** (for polyurethanes).
- **Metal processing:** Pickling of stainless steel, etching of metals.
- **Rocket propellant:** Red Fuming Nitric Acid (RFNA) as an oxidizer.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of nitric acid manufacturing like **cooking a three-course meal in a relay**:
> 1. **Course 1 (Starter):** A baker bakes bread (ammonia is "baked" with oxygen to form NO in a hot oven — the *converter/gauze reactor*).
> 2. **Course 2 (Main):** The bread is toasted and browned further (NO is mixed with more air to form brown NO₂ — the *cooler/oxidizer*).
> 3. **Course 3 (Dessert):** The toasted bread is dissolved in milk to make a custard (NO₂ is absorbed in water to form HNO₃ — the *absorption tower*).
>
> Each step **must happen in order**, and the final product (HNO₃) only emerges after all three stages. Skip a step, and you get something different entirely (e.g., N₂O, N₂, or weak acid).

### 1.4 Physical & Chemical Constants
| Property | Value |
|---|---|
| Molecular Formula | HNO₃ |
| Molar Mass | **63.01 g/mol** |
| Density (anhydrous, 25 °C) | **1.512 g/cm³** |
| Boiling Point (azeotrope 68 wt%) | **122 °C (395 K)** |
| Melting Point | **−42 °C (231 K)** |
| pKa (in water) | **−1.4** (strong acid) |
| Color (anhydrous) | Colorless to yellow (fuming) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Ostwald Process — Three Sequential Stages

#### Stage 1: Ammonia Oxidation (Combustion)
This is the **rate-controlling step** and the heart of the plant.
- **Reactor type:** Packed bed with **Pt-Rh gauze catalyst (90% Pt + 10% Rh)**, often with a Pd-rich base layer.
- **Temperature:** **1123 K (≈ 850–900 °C)**.
- **Pressure:** Typically **1 atm (atmospheric)** or **up to 10 atm (medium pressure)**.
- **Residence time on gauze:** ~10⁻³ second (extremely fast, mass-transfer limited).

**Primary (Desired) Reaction:**

$$4NH_3 + 5O_2 \longrightarrow 4NO + 6H_2O \quad \Delta H_{298K} = -905.2 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

**Side (Undesired) Reactions:**

$$4NH_3 + 3O_2 \longrightarrow 2N_2 + 6H_2O \quad \Delta H_{298K} = -1267 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

$$4NH_3 + 4O_2 \longrightarrow 2N_2O + 6H_2O \quad \Delta H_{298K} = -1104 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

$$2NH_3 \longrightarrow N_2 + 3H_2 \quad \text{(catalytic decomposition)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Pt-Rh?** Platinum alone is too soft and volatile. Adding **Rhodium (10%)** increases the yield of NO from ~80% (pure Pt) to **~95–98%**. The selectivity is favored by **high temperature, high O₂:NH₃ ratio, very short contact time**, and a *clean* (non-poisoned) gauze surface. The gauze is actually operated slightly *above* its optimum yield temperature because of **wire sag** considerations at lower temperatures.

#### Stage 2: Nitric Oxide Oxidation
A **homogeneous gas-phase reaction**, kinetically slow at low T but very fast at low T from equilibrium standpoint.

$$2NO + O_2 \rightleftharpoons 2NO_2 \quad \Delta H_{298K} = -114 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

- This is a **third-order reaction** (rate ∝ [NO]²[O₂]).
- It is favored by **low temperature** (exothermic) and **high pressure** (Δn < 0).
- Practically carried out at **~150–200 °C** in a large **heat exchanger/cooler** with sufficient residence time (a few seconds).
- The gas leaving the converter is cooled from 900 °C to ~150 °C in a **waste-heat boiler (WHB)** that generates **high-pressure steam** — a major energy-recovery feature.

#### Stage 3: Absorption of NO₂ in Water
Carried out in a **packed absorption tower** (stainless steel or aluminium to resist corrosion).

**Primary Reaction:**

$$3NO_2 + H_2O \longrightarrow 2HNO_3 + NO \quad \Delta H_{298K} = -117 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Notice that **1 mole of NO is regenerated per 3 moles of NO₂ absorbed**. This NO must be **recycled** back to Stage 2 — this is why nitric acid plants always include a **tail-gas recycle loop** and why the overall process has a "snowball" effect in material balance problems.

**Overall (Net) Reaction:**

$$NH_3 + 2O_2 \longrightarrow HNO_3 + H_2O$$

$$NH_3 + \frac{7}{4}O_2 + \frac{1}{2}H_2O \longrightarrow HNO_3 + \frac{3}{2}H_2O \quad \text{(balanced, with water participation)}$$

### 2.2 Operating Conditions Summary

| Parameter | Atmospheric Plant | Medium Pressure | High Pressure (Dual) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Burner pressure | **1 atm** | **3–6 atm** | **8–10 atm** |
| Absorption pressure | 1 atm | 3–6 atm | 8–10 atm |
| Converter temperature | **900 °C** | 900 °C | 900 °C |
| NO yield (per pass) | ~97% | ~96% | ~94% |
| Acid strength | 50–55% | 60–65% | 70% |
| Capital cost | Lowest | Medium | Highest |
| Pt loss (g/ton acid) | ~50 mg | ~30 mg | ~25 mg |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Formula / Expression | Meaning / Application |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $X_{NH_3} = \dfrac{n_{NH_3,reacted}}{n_{NH_3,in}}$ | Ammonia conversion (typically ~98%) |
| 2 | $Y_{NO} = \dfrac{n_{NO,formed}}{n_{NH_3,reacted}}$ | NO yield / selectivity (per pass) |
| 3 | $\eta_{overall} = X_{NH_3} \times Y_{NO} \times \eta_{abs}$ | Total HNO₃ yield |
| 4 | $\dot{n}_{HNO_3} = \dot{n}_{NH_3,feed} \cdot X \cdot Y \cdot \eta_{abs}$ | Molar production rate |
| 5 | $C_{HNO_3} = \dfrac{m_{HNO_3}}{m_{HNO_3}+m_{H_2O}}$ | Mass fraction of HNO₃ in product acid |
| 6 | $M_{HNO_3} = 63.01 \text{ g/mol}$ | Molecular weight (KTU constant) |
| 7 | $M_{NH_3} = 17.03 \text{ g/mol}$ | Molecular weight of ammonia |
| 8 | $\Delta H_{rxn,1} = -905.2 \text{ kJ/mol NH}_3$ | Heat of ammonia oxidation |
| 9 | $\Delta H_{rxn,3} = -117 \text{ kJ/mol HNO}_3$ | Heat of absorption |
