**Chemical Basis:** Each C=C double bond reacts with one ICl or IBr molecule (Wijs/Hanus method). Higher unsaturation = higher I₂ consumption = higher IV.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Most students **lose 2–3 marks** because they (a) forget to convert normality × volume properly, (b) mix up **Acid Value** with **Saponification Value** formulae, or (c) skip units in the final answer. **Always write units, show cancellation, and circle the final answer.**

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Compulsory Choice 1

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Paper]** 

**(a)** With the help of a neat flow diagram, describe the **solvent extraction process** of oil from soybean seeds. List the **crude oil refining steps**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The saponification value of a sample of **palm oil** is found to be **200**. Calculate the **average molecular weight** of the triglycerides present. State its significance. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to (a)

**Solvent Extraction of Soybean Oil:**

**Step 1 — Preparation:** Soybeans are **cleaned, dehulled, cracked, and flaked** to increase surface area. Moisture is reduced to ~10%.

**Step 2 — Solvent Extraction:** The flakes are contacted with **n-hexane (boiling point 68°C)** in a continuous **extractor** (Bollman, Hildebrandt, or Rotocel type). Hexane dissolves the oil but not the protein.

**Step 3 — Miscella Treatment:** The oil-solvent mixture (**miscella**) is filtered and sent to a **distillation/evaporation unit** where hexane is recovered. The residual hexane in the oil is stripped by **steam stripping**.

**Step 4 — Meal Desolventizing:** The defatted meal is treated with steam/heat to recover residual hexane → it becomes **animal feed**.

**Refining Steps (post-extraction):**

1. **Degumming** — removal of phosphatides using hot water or phosphoric acid.
2. **Neutralization (Alkali Refining)** — NaOH removes free fatty acids as **soapstock**.
3. **Bleaching** — adsorption of pigments (carotenoids, chlorophyll) on **activated Fuller’s earth**.
4. **Deodorization** — steam distillation at **220–250°C, 1–5 mmHg vacuum** to remove volatile odoriferous compounds.
5. **Winterization** (optional) — crystallization of waxes for clear oil.

**Final Product:** **RBD (Refined, Bleached, Deodorized) Soybean Oil.**

---

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Given:** Saponification Value (SV) = 200

**Step 1: Recall the molecular weight formula**

$$ \text{Molecular Weight} = \dfrac{3 \times 56.1 \times 1000}{\text{SV}} $$

**Step 2: Substitute the values**

$$ \text{MW} = \dfrac{3 \times 56.1 \times 1000}{200} $$

**Step 3: Compute the numerator**

$$ 3 \times 56.1 = 168.3 $$

$$ 168.3 \times 1000 = 168300 $$

**Step 4: Divide by SV**

$$ \text{MW} = \dfrac{168300}{200} = 841.5 \; \text{g/mol} $$

**Final Answer:** $\text{MW} = 841.5 \; \text{g/mol}$

**Significance:** A molecular weight of ~841.5 indicates the triglycerides contain **predominantly long-chain fatty acids (C16, C18)**. Such oils produce **harder soap** (used in laundry bars) but require **more alkali** per unit mass. The SV of 200 is in the **medium range** typical of palm oil, suitable for both **edible use and margarine production**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Compulsory Choice 2

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the **hydrogenation of oils** with a balanced chemical reaction. Discuss the role of the **nickel catalyst** and the effect of **temperature and pressure** on the product quality. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A sample of **sunflower oil** weighing **0.5 g** is analyzed for iodine value. Blank titration = **40.0 mL**, sample titration = **15.0 mL** of **0.1 N Na₂S₂O₃**. Calculate the **iodine value** and classify the oil. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to (a)

**Hydrogenation of Oils — Conceptual Explanation:**

Hydrogenation is the process of **adding molecular hydrogen (H₂)** across the **C=C double bonds** of unsaturated triglycerides in the presence of a **heterogeneous catalyst** to convert liquid oils into **semi-solid or solid fats** (e.g., vanaspati, margarine).

**Balanced Chemical Reaction:**

$$ \text{Unsaturated Triglyceride} \; (\text{C=C}) \; + \; \text{H}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Ni, 150-200°C, 1-10 atm}} \text{Saturated Triglyceride} \; (\text{C-C}) $$

**Role of Nickel Catalyst:**
- Nickel is a **transition metal** with partially filled d-orbitals that **chemisorb H₂** on its surface, weakening the H–H bond.
- The **liquid oil** is also adsorbed on the Ni surface.
- The reaction proceeds through a **surface addition mechanism**: the H atoms transfer one at a time to the C=C bond.
- Typical catalyst load: **0.05–0.2% Ni** on an inert support (e.g., silica).

**Effect of Temperature:**
- **Higher temperature** → increases reaction rate but favors **trans-isomer formation** (creating **trans fats**).
- **Lower temperature (~150°C)** → more **selective hydrogenation** (linolenic → linoleic → oleic) and less trans fat.

**Effect of Pressure:**
- **Higher H₂ pressure (5–10 atm)** → increases H₂ solubility in oil → faster reaction rate.
- **Lower pressure (1–2 atm)** → allows **selective hydrogenation** with minimal trans isomer formation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Trade-off:** Industrially, **batch hydrogenation** is preferred over continuous for better **selectivity control**. The **platinum point** and **selectivity ratio** are key performance indices.

---

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Given:**
- Weight of oil, $W = 0.5$ g
- Blank titre, $V_b = 40.0$ mL
- Sample titre, $V_s = 15.0$ mL
- Normality, $N = 0.1$ N

**Step 1: Apply the Iodine Value formula**

$$ \text{IV} = \dfrac{(V_b - V_s) \times N \times 12.69}{W} $$

**Step 2: Compute the titre difference**

$$ V_b - V_s = 40.0 - 15.0 = 25.0 \; \text{mL} $$

**Step 3: Substitute all values**

$$ \text{IV} = \dfrac{25.0 \times 0.1 \times 12.69}{0.5} $$

**Step 4: Simplify the numerator**

$$ 25.0 \times 0.1 = 2.5 $$

$$ 2.5 \times 12.69 = 31.725 $$

**Step 5: Divide by sample weight**

$$ \text{IV} = \dfrac{31.725}{0.5} = 63.45 \; \text{g I}_2/100\text{g oil} $$

**Final Answer:** $\text{IV} = 63.45 \; \text{g I}_2/100\text{g oil}$

**Classification:** Since IV = 63.45 < 100, the oil is classified as a **non-drying oil** (edible liquid fat). However, the standard IV of sunflower oil is **~125** (semi-drying). The lower measured value suggests the sample may be **partially hydrogenated** or **adulterated**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use this checklist as your **last-minute revision** before the KTU exam. Cover all 12 points below.

- [x] **Triglyceride = Glycerol + 3 Fatty Acids** (ester linkage: –COO–).
- [x] **Oils are liquid** (high unsaturation); **Fats are solid** (high saturation).
- [x] **Acid Value (AV)** = measure of **free fatty acids** → indicates **rancidity/quality**.
- [x] **Saponification Value (SV)** = measure of **average chain length** → high SV = low MW (e.g., coconut, palm kernel).
- [x] **Iodine Value (IV)** = measure of **unsaturation** → high IV = drying oil.
- [x] **Peroxide Value (PV)** = measure of **oxidation/rancidity** → food safety indicator.
- [x] **Constants to memorize:** **56.1** (KOH equivalent wt) and **12.69** (I₂ equivalent wt).
- [x] **Refining sequence:** Degumming → Neutralization → Bleaching → Deodorization (**RBD**).
- [x] **Hydrogenation** uses **Ni catalyst at 150–200°C, 1–10 atm H₂**; controls **trans-fat formation**.
- [x] **Transesterification** with **CH₃OH + KOH** produces **biodiesel (FAME) + glycerol**.
- [x] **Saponification** with **NaOH** produces **soap + glycerol** (fatty acid salt).
- [x] **Blank titration** is mandatory for **SV and IV** (uses $V_b - V_s$); **AV uses sample titre only** ($V$).
- [x] **Drying oils (IV > 150)**: linseed, tung; **Non-drying (IV < 100)**: coconut, olive, palm.
- [x] **Molecular weight from SV:** $\text{MW} = \dfrac{3 \times 56.1 \times 1000}{\text{SV}}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Tip for KTU 2024 Aspirants:** Practice at least **3 numerical problems** on each of AV, SV, and IV before the exam. The examiner awards **full 14 marks** only when the final answer is expressed with **correct units, sign, and two decimal places** where applicable.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hydrogenation of Vegetable Oils
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT637 - CHEMICAL TECHNOLOGY AND REACTION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Hydrogenation of Vegetable Oils

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hydrogenation of Vegetable Oils** is a heterogeneous catalytic process in which molecular hydrogen (H₂) is added across the carbon–carbon double bonds (C=C) of unsaturated fatty acid chains esterified to a glycerol backbone (triglycerides), converting them into saturated (or partially saturated) C–C single bonds. Industrially, it is a **gas–liquid–solid (three-phase) reaction** conducted over a finely divided transition metal catalyst (Ni, Pd, or Pt) at elevated temperatures (**150–200 °C**) and moderate hydrogen pressures (**1–10 atm**).

The process transforms liquid polyunsaturated oils (e.g., soybean, sunflower, cottonseed oil) into semi-solid or plastic fats suitable for margarine, vanaspati, and shortening manufacture.

### 1.2 The Chemistry in Plain Words

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Sponge That Swallows Hydrogen":**
> Imagine an oil molecule as a long, twisted ribbon with little "kinks" at every double bond. Each kink is a place where two hydrogen atoms can snap on, like buckling a seatbelt. Once the kinks are removed, the ribbons straighten, pack tightly together, and the oil solidifies — that is exactly what happens when you hydrogenate vegetable oil. Saturated fats (butter, ghee) are essentially "fully buckled" versions of the same molecules.

The triglyceride molecule has three fatty acid tails. Each tail may contain 0, 1, 2, or 3 double bonds. Common unsaturated fatty acids are:

| Common Name | Carbon Notation | Number of C=C | Melting Point (°C) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Stearic acid | C18:0 | 0 | **69.6** |
| Oleic acid | C18:1 | 1 | **13.4** |
| Linoleic acid | C18:2 | 2 | **−5** |
| Linolenic acid | C18:3 | 3 | **−11** |

The net hydrogenation reaction per double bond:

$$
\text{–CH=CH–} \; + \; \text{H}_2 \;\xrightarrow{\text{Ni catalyst}}\; \text{–CH}_2\text{–CH}_2\text{–}
$$

**Heat of reaction** ≈ **−120 kJ per mole of H₂ consumed** (per double bond hydrogenated).

### 1.3 Why Hydrogenate?

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Motivations:**
> 1. **Texture & Functionality** — Convert liquid oil to semi-solid fat for spreads.
> 2. **Oxidative Stability** — Saturated fats resist rancidity; double bonds oxidize readily.
> 3. **Color & Odour Removal** — Bleaches pigments and destroys trace sulfur compounds.
> 4. **Caloric density control** in formulated foods.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three-Phase Catalytic System

Hydrogenation is a textbook **gas–liquid–solid (G–L–S)** system. The sequence a reactant must travel is:

1. **H₂ transport from gas bubble → liquid bulk** (gas–liquid mass transfer).
2. **H₂ diffusion through the liquid boundary layer → catalyst external surface** (liquid–solid mass transfer).
3. **H₂ pore diffusion inside the catalyst pellet** (intraparticle diffusion).
4. **Surface reaction** (chemisorption of H₂ and oil, surface rearrangement, desorption).

Any of these may be rate-controlling. The overall rate observed is the slowest step in series.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Surface Mechanism (Horiuti–Polanyi Mechanism)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The widely accepted mechanism is the **Horiuti–Polanyi** sequence, common to almost all transition-metal-catalyzed hydrogenations.

1. **Dissociative chemisorption of H₂** on a metal site (S):
$$
\text{H}_2 + 2S \;\rightleftharpoons\; 2\text{H–S}
$$

2. **π-complex adsorption** of the olefin on a vacant site:
$$
\text{C=C} + S \;\rightleftharpoons\; \text{C=C–S}
$$

3. **First H-atom transfer** (half-hydrogenated state):
$$
\text{C=C–S} + \text{H–S} \;\rightleftharpoons\; \text{–CH–CH}_2\text{–S}
$$

4. **Second H-atom transfer** (desorption of saturated product):
$$
\text{–CH–CH}_2\text{–S} + \text{H–S} \;\rightleftharpoons\; \text{–CH}_2\text{–CH}_2\text{–} + 2S
$$

5. **Cis–trans isomerization** (side path) — when the half-hydrogenated intermediate rotates before the second H attack, a *trans* double bond forms instead of saturating fully.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations constitute the **must-know formula set** for the Hydrogenation module in KTU ESE.

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Power-law rate** | $-r_A = k\,P_{H_2}^{\,a}\,C_{oil}^{\,b}$ | mol/(L·s); empirical |
| 2 | **Langmuir–Hinshelwood rate** (single-site) | $-r_A = \dfrac{k\,K_H P_{H_2}\,K_{oil}\,C_{oil}}{(1+K_H P_{H_2}+K_{oil} C_{oil})^2}$ | mol/(g-cat·s) |
| 3 | **Simplified L-H (H₂ limiting)** | $-r_A = k'\,C_{oil}$ | first order in oil |
| 4 | **Hydrogen solubility (Sieverts-type)** | $C_{H_2}^* = K_{H2}\,P_{H_2}$ | mol/L; **K_H₂ ≈ 0.04 mol/(L·atm)** at 150 °C |
| 5 | **Overall mass-transfer rate** | $N_{H_2} = k_L a\,(C^*_{H_2}-C_{H_2,L})$ | mol/(L·s) |
| 6 | **Hatta number for G–L** | $\text{Ha} = \dfrac{\sqrt{k\,D_{H_2}}}{k_L}$ | enhancement criterion |
| 7 | **Iodine Value (IV)** | $IV = \dfrac{126.9\, n_{C=C}}{M_{oil}} \times 100$ | g I₂ / 100 g oil |
| 8 | **Reduction in IV (ΔIV)** | $\Delta IV = IV_0 - IV_t$ | tracks extent of reaction |
| 9 | **Selectivity (S)** | $S = \dfrac{\text{rate of linoleic to oleic}}{\text{rate of oleic to stearic}}$ | dimensionless |
| 10 | **Heat of reaction** | $\Delta H_{rxn} = -120\;\text{kJ/mol H}_2$ | per double bond |
| 11 | **Damköhler number (G–L–S)** | $\text{Da} = \dfrac{-r_A\,V}{k_L a\,C^*_{H_2}\,V}$ | regime indicator |
| 12 | **Effectiveness factor (η)** | $\eta = \dfrac{\tanh\,\phi}{\phi}$, $\phi = R\sqrt{k/D_{eff}}$ | intraparticle resistance |

**Note on units:** Industrial hydrogenation kinetics are usually reported in **mol H₂ / (g-catalyst · s)** or **mol H₂ / (L · s)**.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> - **Margarine & Vanaspati industry**: The IV drop from ~130 (oil) to ~75 (final product) defines product grade.
> - **Hydrogenation index control**: Pharmaceutical-grade oils require specific *trans*-isomer limits (<2%).
> - **Catalyst cost optimization**: Ni catalyst loadings of 0.05–0.2 % by mass; expensive Pd (0.5–1 %) used for selective partial hydrogenation.
> - **Process safety**: The 120 kJ/mol exotherm demands careful temperature control; runaway risk in poorly agitated batch reactors.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Langmuir–Hinshelwood Rate Expression

We assume a **dual-site mechanism** where H₂ adsorbs dissociatively on one site and the olefin adsorbs on an adjacent site.

**Step 1 — Equilibrium adsorption of H₂ (dissociative):**

$$
K_H = \frac{\theta_H^{\,2}}{P_{H_2}\,\theta_v^{\,2}}
\;\;\Rightarrow\;\;
\theta_H = \sqrt{K_H P_{H_2}}\;\theta_v
$$

**Step 2 — Equilibrium adsorption of the olefin (molecular):**

$$
K_{oil} = \frac{\theta_{oil}}{C_{oil}\,\theta_v}
\;\;\Rightarrow\;\;
\theta_{oil} = K_{oil} C_{oil}\;\theta_v
$$

**Step 3 — Site balance (assuming single vacant site species):**

$$
\theta_v + \theta_{oil} + \theta_H = 1
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute (2) and (1) into (3):**

$$
\theta_v + K_{oil} C_{oil}\theta_v + \sqrt{K_H P_{H_2}}\;\theta_v = 1
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $\theta_v$:**

$$
\theta_v = \frac{1}{1 + K_{oil} C_{oil} + \sqrt{K_H P_{H_2}}}
$$

**Step 6 — Surface reaction is rate-determining. Two H atoms must meet one adsorbed olefin. Assume rate proportional to $\theta_{oil}\cdot \theta_H$:**

$$
-r_{A} = k\,\theta_{oil}\,\theta_H
$$

**Step 7 — Substitute $\theta_{oil}$ from (2) and $\theta_H$ from (1):**

$$
-r_{A} = k\,(K_{oil} C_{oil}\,\theta_v)\,(\sqrt{K_H P_{H_2}}\,\theta_v)
$$

**Step 8 — Substitute $\theta_v$ from (5):**

$$
-r_{A} = \frac{k\,K_{oil}\,C_{oil}\,\sqrt{K_H P_{H_2}}}{\left(1 + K_{oil} C_{oil} + \sqrt{K_H P_{H_2}}\right)^2}
$$

In the commonly used form (with $K_H P_{H_2}$ replacing $\sqrt{K_H P_{H_2}}$ via lumped coefficient):

$$
\boxed{\; -r_{A} \;=\; \frac{k\,K_H P_{H_2}\,K_{oil} C_{oil}}{\left(1 + K_H P_{H_2} + K_{oil} C_{oil}\right)^2} \;}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Limiting cases (very useful in KTU problems):**
> - If $K_H P_{H_2} \gg 1$ and $K_{oil} C_{oil} \ll 1$:  $\;-r_A \approx k' K_{oil} C_{oil}$ → **first order in oil, zero order in H₂**.
> - If $K_{oil} C_{oil} \gg 1$ and $K_H P_{H_2} \ll 1$: $\;-r_A \approx k' K_H P_{H_2}$ → **first order in H₂, zero order in oil**.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Operating-Regime Identification

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners frequently test the student's ability to **identify the rate-controlling step** from process data. The following table summarises the diagnostic criteria.

| Observed Symptom | Likely Controlling Step | Experimental Evidence |
|---|---|---|
| Rate ∝ $P_{H_2}$, independent of agitation | Gas–liquid mass transfer | Bubble area variation has no effect |
| Rate increases with agitation speed up to a plateau | Liquid–solid mass transfer | Catalyst loading (W) is linear in rate |
| Rate ∝ √(catalyst mass) | Pore diffusion | $\eta < 1$ from Mears criterion |
| Rate ∝ catalyst mass (W), sensitive to T | Surface reaction | Arrhenius plot, $E_a \approx 40-60$ kJ/mol |
| Strong *trans* isomer formation, IV drops slowly | Surface reaction with isomerization | Selectivity $S \gg 1$ |

### 3.3 Worked Example: Determining Iodine Value Drop

**Problem:** A 1000 kg batch of soybean oil (initial IV = 130) is hydrogenated to IV = 80. Calculate (a) the mass of H₂ consumed, and (b) the average molecular weight of the oil, given an average triglyceride MW of **885 g/mol**.

**Step 1 — Decrease in iodine value:**

$$
\Delta IV = 130 - 80 = 50 \;\text{g I}_2 / 100\text{ g oil}
$$

**Step 2 — Moles of I₂ equivalents per gram of oil (using 1 mol I₂ = 1 mol C=C, MW(I₂) = 253.8):**

$$
n_{C=C}^{consumed} = \frac{50}{253.8 \times 100} = 1.970 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{mol C=C / g oil}
$$

**Step 3 — Moles of H₂ consumed (1 mol H₂ = 1 mol C=C):**

$$
n_{H_2} = 1.970 \times 10^{-3} \times (1000 \times 1000) = 1970\;\text{mol H}_2
$$

**Step 4 — Mass of H₂ (M = 2.016 g/mol):**

$$
m_{H_2} = 1970 \times 2.016 = 3971\;\text{g} \approx \mathbf{3.97\;kg\;H_2}
$$

**Step 5 — Number of double bonds reduced per triglyceride molecule:**

$$
N_{C=C} = \frac{50 \times 885}{253.8 \times 100} \approx 1.74\;\text{double bonds per molecule}
$$

> [!TIP]
> This 1.74 is exactly the typical reduction seen industrially: linolenic (3) → linoleic (2) → oleic (1) is suppressed in part, while oleic → stearic is the major endpoint.

### 3.4 Worked Example: Mass-Transfer Limitation Check (Slurry Reactor)

**Problem:** In a 5 m³ batch slurry reactor, $k_L a = 0.08\;s^{-1}$, $C^*_{H_2} = 0.04\;\text{mol/L}$ (at 5 atm, 180 °C). At maximum rate, the observed $-r_A = 4 \times 10^{-4}\;\text{mol/(L\cdot s)}$. Is the process mass-transfer-limited?

**Step 1 — Maximum possible gas–liquid transfer rate:**

$$
N_{H_2}^{\max} = k_L a\,C^*_{H_2} = 0.08 \times 0.04 = 3.2 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{mol/(L·s)}
$$

**Step 2 — Compare to observed rate:**

$$
\frac{-r_A}{N_{H_2}^{\max}} = \frac{4 \times 10^{-4}}{3.2 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.125
$$

**Step 3 — Conclusion:** Observed rate is **~12.5 %** of maximum transfer rate → **kinetics is rate-controlling**, with a safety margin against mass-transfer limitation. If the ratio exceeds ~0.8, hydrogen starvation and selectivity shifts occur.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Industrial Hydrogenation Plant

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FEED[Feed Preparation]
        A1[Crude Vegetable Oil] --> A2[Refining and Bleaching]
        A2 --> A3[Deaeration Vacuum]
    end

    subgraph REACT[Reactor Loop]
        A3 --> B1[Slurry Batch Reactor with Ni Catalyst]
        H2A[H2 Supply Compressed Cylinder] --> B1
        B1 --> B2[Agitator with Baffles]
        B2 --> B1
        B1 --> B3[Catalyst Filtration Unit]
    end

    subgraph POST[Post Processing]
        B3 --> C1[Deodorization Stripping]
        C1 --> C2[Cooling and Crystallization]
        C2 --> C3[Final Product Vanaspati or Margarine]
    end

    subgraph CTRL[Control and Safety]
        S1[Temperature Controller] -.-> B1
        S2[Pressure Controller] -.-> B1
        S3[H2 Flow Meter] -.-> B1
        S4[Online IV Analyzer] -.-> C1
    end
```

### 4.2 Process Flow: Three-Phase Mass Transfer Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    G1[Step 1: H2 in Gas Bubble] --> G2[Step 2: Dissolution at Gas Liquid Interface]
    G2 --> G3[Step 3: Diffusion through Liquid Boundary Layer]
    G3 --> G4[Step 4: Diffusion to External Catalyst Surface]
    G4 --> G5[Step 5: Intraparticle Pore Diffusion]
    G5 --> G6[Step 6: Dissociative Chemisorption on Ni Site]
    G6 --> G7[Step 7: Surface Reaction Horiuti Polanyi]
    G7 --> G8[Step 8: Product Desorption]
    G8 --> G9[Step 9: Saturated Triglyceride Leaves Catalyst]
```

### 4.3 Reaction Pathway: Selectivity Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    O1[C18:3 Linolenic Acid] -->|fast| O2[C18:2 Linoleic Acid]
    O2 -->|medium| O3[C18:1 Oleic Acid]
    O3 -->|slow| O4[C18:0 Stearic Acid]
    O3 -.->|isomerization| T1[Trans C18:1 Elaidic Acid]
    T1 -.->|isomerization| O4
    O2 -.->|conjugation| CJ[Conjugated Dienes]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the selectivity tree:**
> - **Horizontal arrows** = hydrogenation step (rate decreases left → right).
> - **Dotted arrows** = unwanted side reactions (trans-isomerization, conjugation).
> - **Industrial goal**: stop the pathway at oleic (C18:1) for soft margarine; proceed to stearic (C18:0) only for fully hardened fats.

### 4.4 Reactor-Type Comparison Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SLURRY[Slurry Batch Reactor]
        S1A[Tank] --> S1B[Impeller and Baffles]
        S1B --> S1C[Catalyst Slurry]
        S1C --> S1D[Sparger for H2]
    end

    subgraph TRICKLE[Trickle Bed Reactor]
        T1A[Oil Trickles Down] --> T1B[Packed Bed of Catalyst Pellets]
        T1B --> T1C[H2 Flows Cocurrent Upward]
    end

    subgraph CSTR[Continuous Stirred Tank]
        C1A[Oil plus Catalyst Feed] --> C1B[Stirred Vessel]
        C1B --> C1C[Product plus Catalyst Exit]
        H2B[H2 Sparged] --> C1B
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set A]**

**Q1. Define Iodine Value (IV) and explain its significance in vegetable oil hydrogenation.**

**Model Answer:**
The **Iodine Value (IV)** is defined as the grams of iodine (or its equivalent, e.g., ICl, IBr) absorbed by 100 g of oil under standard Wijs/Hanus test conditions. It is a direct measure of the **total number of carbon–carbon double bonds** (unsaturation) present. Mathematically:

$$
IV = \frac{126.9 \times n_{C=C}}{M_{sample}} \times 100 \;\text{where } n_{C=C} \text{ is moles of C=C.}
$$

**Significance:** During hydrogenation, IV decreases monotonically as C=C bonds are saturated. The difference $\Delta IV = IV_0 - IV_t$ is a direct measure of the extent of reaction. Industry specifies finished-product IV (e.g., vanaspati IV ≈ 60–75; margarine IV ≈ 80–90) to define consistency, melting behaviour, and shelf life.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Jun 2024, Set B]**

**Q2. Why is nickel the most widely used catalyst for industrial vegetable oil hydrogenation, and what are its typical operating conditions?**

**Model Answer:**
**Reasons for nickel dominance:**
1. **High activity** for C=C hydrogenation.
2. **Low cost** (~5–10 % the cost of Pd or Pt).
3. **Reasonable selectivity** when supported on silica or alumina.
4. **Robust to feed impurities** like phospholipids (after refining).
5. **Easy recovery** via filtration from the slurry.

**Typical operating conditions:**

| Parameter | Range |
|---|---|
| Temperature | **150 – 200 °C** |
| Pressure | **1 – 10 atm H₂** |
| Catalyst loading | **0.05 – 0.20 % Ni** by mass of oil |
| Agitation speed | **600 – 1200 rpm** |
| Batch time | **1 – 4 hours** |

**Limitations:** Ni is highly sensitive to **sulfur poisoning** (especially rapeseed/mustard oil), so deep refining is essential.

---

### Part B — 14-Mark ESE Questions (Module 2 Internal Choice)

> **[KTU ESE — Module 2, Question Paper Pattern: Internal Choice]**

---

#### ✦ Question A (14 Marks)

> **[Model Paper — KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 2, Question A]**

**(a) Derive the Langmuir–Hinshelwood rate expression for vegetable oil hydrogenation assuming dissociative adsorption of H₂ and molecular adsorption of the olefin on a dual-site mechanism. State the limiting-case simplifications.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (step-by-step):**

We have already derived this expression in detail in Section 3.1 above. The examiner expects the following checklist:

1. State assumptions: dual-site, quasi-equilibrium adsorption, surface reaction RDS.
2. Write equilibrium relations for H₂ and oil adsorption (1 mark).
3. Write site balance equation (1 mark).
4. Solve for $\theta_v$ (1 mark).
5. Express rate as $k\,\theta_H\,\theta_{oil}$ (1 mark).
6. Combine to obtain the final form (1 mark).
7. Discuss **limiting cases** — first order in H₂, zero order in oil and vice versa (1 mark).

**Final boxed result:**

$$
-r_{A} = \frac{k\,K_H P_{H_2}\,K_{oil} C_{oil}}{\left(1 + K_H P_{H_2} + K_{oil} C_{oil}\right)^2}
$$

---

**(b) A batch slurry reactor of 5 m³ is loaded with 3000 kg of soybean oil (MW = 885, initial IV = 130) and 3 kg of Ni catalyst. Hydrogen is supplied at 5 atm and 180 °C. The intrinsic kinetics is first order in oil with $k = 6 \times 10^{-4}\;\text{s}^{-1}$. If the gas–liquid mass-transfer coefficient $k_L a = 0.05\;\text{s}^{-1}$ and Henry constant $K_{H_2} = 0.04\;\text{mol/(L\cdot atm)}$ at operating conditions, determine: (i) the time required to reach IV = 80, and (ii) the total H₂ consumed.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Density of oil (typical 920 kg/m³):**

$$
V_{oil} = \frac{3000}{920} = 3.26\;\text{m}^3 = 3260\;\text{L}
$$

**Step 2 — Initial concentration of double bonds (in terms of IV):**

$$
C_{C=C}^{0} = \frac{IV_0 \times \rho_{oil}}{253.8 \times 100} = \frac{130 \times 920}{25380} = 4.71\;\text{mol/L}
$$

**Step 3 — Final concentration at IV = 80:**

$$
C_{C=C}^{f} = \frac{80 \times 920}{25380} = 2.90\;\text{mol/L}
$$

**Step 4 — Check mass-transfer ceiling:**

$$
C^*_{H_2} = 0.04 \times 5 = 0.20\;\text{mol/L}
$$

$$
N_{H_2}^{\max} = k_L a\,C^*_{H_2} = 0.05 \times 0.20 = 0.01\;\text{mol/(L·s)}
$$

**Step 5 — Compare to intrinsic rate at $C_{oil} \approx C_{C=C}^0$:**

$$
-r_A^{\max} = k\,C_{oil} = 6 \times 10^{-4} \times 4.71 = 2.83 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{mol/(L·s)}
$$

Since $-r_A^{\max} < N_{H_2}^{\max}$, kinetics controls. (1 mark)

**Step 6 — First-order batch integration (kinetic control):**

$$
t = \frac{1}{k}\ln\!\left(\frac{C_0}{C_f}\right) = \frac{1}{6 \times 10^{-4}}\ln\!\left(\frac{4.71}{2.90}\right) = \frac{0.486}{6 \times 10^{-4}}
$$

$$
\boxed{t \;\approx\; 810\;\text{s} \;\approx\; 13.5\;\text{minutes}}
$$

(2 marks for the integration and answer)

**Step 7 — Total H₂ consumed:**

$$
\Delta n_{H_2} = (C_0 - C_f) \times V_{oil} = (4.71 - 2.90) \times 3260 = 5900\;\text{mol H}_2
$$

$$
m_{H_2} = 5900 \times 2.016 \approx 11.9\;\text{kg H}_2
$$

(2 marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall — Common Loss of Marks:**
> 1. Forgetting to **convert IV to mol/L of double bonds** using the 253.8 factor → loses 1 mark.
> 2. Skipping the **mass-transfer check** (Step 4–5). Examiners explicitly test whether you identified kinetics vs G–L control. Missing this loses 1–2 marks.
> 3. Using the **oil mass directly** instead of oil volume — density conversion is mandatory.
> 4. Not reporting **units** with final answer.

---

#### ✦ Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

> **[Model Paper — KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 2, Question B]**

**(a) Discuss in detail the mechanism (Horiuti–Polanyi) of catalytic hydrogenation of vegetable oils, with a clear schematic of the five elementary steps. Also explain how this mechanism accounts for the formation of *trans*-isomers during partial hydrogenation.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution Outline:**

| Element | Marks | Key Content |
|---|---|---|
| Step 1: H₂ dissociative chemisorption | 1 | Equation, Ni site symbolism |
| Step 2: Olefin π-adsorption | 1 | Cis-form, single site |
| Step 3: First H transfer — half-hydrogenated state | 1 | Free rotation enabled |
| Step 4: Second H transfer — saturated product | 1 | Stereospecific outcome |
| Step 5: Desorption of saturated triglyceride | 1 | Site regeneration |
| Trans-isomer formation | 2 | Rotation of half-hydrogenated intermediate + re-elimination of H |

**Mechanism Schematic (re-describe in words for the exam):**

1. $\text{H}_2 + 2\text{Ni–S} \to 2\text{H–Ni–S}$
2. $\text{Olefin (cis)} + \text{Ni–S} \to \text{Olefin–Ni–S}$
3. $\text{Olefin–Ni–S} + \text{H–Ni–S} \to \text{half-hydrogenated–Ni–S}$
4. $\text{half-hydrogenated–Ni–S} + \text{H–Ni–S} \to \text{Saturated product} + 2\text{Ni–S}$

**Trans-isomer origin:** In step 3, the half-hydrogenated intermediate has a single C–Ni bond and free rotation about the remaining C–C bond. If the second H-atom addition is slow, this intermediate can **re-eliminate a hydrogen** to regenerate a C=C bond — but now in the thermodynamically more stable *trans* (E) configuration. This is the principal source of *trans* fats in industrial hydrogenation.

---

**(b) A continuous slurry reactor (CSTR) is used to hydrogenate cottonseed oil. Feed: 1000 kg/h oil with $IV_0 = 105$. Desired outlet IV = 70. Reactor volume = 2 m³. Operating conditions yield an effective first-order rate constant $k = 1.5 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{s}^{-1}$ based on oil volume. Calculate (i) the residence time, (ii) the reactor volume required, and (iii) the rate of H₂ consumption in kg/h.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert IV to concentration units (oil density 920 kg/m³):**

$$
C_0 = \frac{105 \times 920}{25380} = 3.81\;\text{mol/L}
$$

$$
C_f = \frac{70 \times 920}{25380} = 2.54\;\text{mol/L}
$$

**Step 2 — CSTR design equation for first-order kinetics:**

$$
\tau = \frac{C_0 - C_f}{k\,C_f} = \frac{3.81 - 2.54}{1.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 2.54}
$$

$$
\tau = \frac{1.27}{3.81 \times 10^{-3}} = 333.3\;\text{s} \;\approx\; 5.56\;\text{min}
$$

(2 marks)

**Step 3 — Volumetric flow rate of oil:**

$$
\dot V_{oil} = \frac{1000}{920} = 1.087\;\text{m}^3/\text{h} = 3.02 \times 10^{-4}\;\text{m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 4 — Required reactor volume:**

$$
V = \tau \times \dot V_{oil} = 333.3 \times 3.02 \times 10^{-4} = 0.1007\;\text{m}^3
$$

(1 mark)

**Step 5 — Outlet rate from CSTR mass balance:**

$$
r_A = \frac{C_0 - C_f}{\tau} = \frac{1.27}{333.3} = 3.81 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{mol/(L·s)}
$$

**Step 6 — Total H₂ consumption rate:**

$$
\dot n_{H_2} = r_A \times V_{oil} = 3.81 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.087 \times 10^{3}\;\text{L/h} \times 1\;\text{m}^3/1000\;\text{L}
$$

Re-checking: $\dot V_{oil} = 1.087\;\text{m}^3/\text{h} = 1087\;\text{L/h}$.

$$
\dot n_{H_2} = 3.81 \times 10^{-3} \times 1087 = 4.14\;\text{mol/s} = 14905\;\text{mol/h}
$$

$$
m_{H_2}^{\cdot} = 14905 \times 2.016 = 30.0\;\text{kg/h}
$$

(2 marks)

**Final Answers:**
- (i) $\tau \approx 333$ s
- (ii) $V \approx 0.10\;\text{m}^3$ (the given 2 m³ is over-designed — note for exam)
- (iii) H₂ consumption $\approx 30$ kg/h

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — CSTR with First-Order Kinetics:**
> 1. The correct CSTR equation is $\tau = (C_0 - C_f)/(-r_A)$ where $-r_A = kC_f$ (outlet concentration). **Do NOT** use PFR equation by mistake.
> 2. Volume flow rate must be in **L/s** to match $k$ in s⁻¹ — unit mismatch is the most common error.
> 3. If the question gives flow in kg/h, **always convert** to volumetric flow using density before applying rate equations.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Hydrogenation of Vegetable Oils:**

- ✅ **Definition:** H₂ addition to C=C bonds of triglycerides over a solid metal catalyst — a **gas–liquid–solid** reaction.
- ✅ **Process conditions:** 150–200 °C, 1–10 atm H₂, Ni catalyst (0.05–0.2 %).
- ✅ **Horiuti–Polanyi mechanism** has 5 steps: H₂ chemisorption, olefin adsorption, two H-atom transfers, product desorption.
- ✅ **Langmuir–Hinshelwood final form:**

$$
-r_A = \frac{k\,K_H P_{H_2}\,K_{oil} C_{oil}}{(1 + K_H P_{H_2} + K_{oil} C_{oil})^2}
$$

- ✅ **Limiting cases:** First order in H₂ when oil is abundant; first order in oil when H₂ is abundant.
- ✅ **Iodine Value (IV)** = g I₂ / 100 g oil; $\Delta IV$ is the direct measure of reaction extent.
- ✅ **Trans-isomer formation** arises from rotation of the half-hydrogenated intermediate during slow second H-atom transfer.
- ✅ **Selectivity (S)** = rate (linoleic→oleic) / rate (oleic→stearic); high S desired for soft margarine.
- ✅ **Three mass-transfer resistances in series:** gas–liquid, liquid–solid, intraparticle pore diffusion.
- ✅ **Hydrogen solubility** in oil is **low** (~0.04 mol/(L·atm)) — explains why G–L transfer is often critical.
- ✅ **Heat of reaction** = **−120 kJ/mol H₂** (per double bond) — strict temperature control required.
- ✅ **Slurry batch reactor** is the industrial workhorse; CSTR and trickle-bed used in specialized applications.
- ✅ **Catalyst poisoning:** Sulfur compounds in crude oil require refining before hydrogenation.
- ✅ **Numerical conversion factor to remember:** 1 IV unit ≈ 1 mol C=C per 253.8 g of oil; 1 g I₂ = 1 mol C=C equivalent.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Food Processing Industry - Sugar
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT637 - CHEMICAL TECHNOLOGY AND REACTION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Food Processing Industry - Sugar

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

The **Sugar Industry** is a process-oriented sector of Chemical Technology that converts sucrose-rich raw materials (primarily **sugarcane** – *Saccharum officinarum*, and **sugar beet** – *Beta vulgaris*) into refined crystalline sucrose (C₁₂H₂₂O₁₁) through a sequence of integrated unit operations and chemical reaction steps. From the perspective of **Reaction Engineering**, the sugar industry is a classic example of a **physical–chemical processing chain** where extraction, purification, concentration, and crystallization dominate, while a single key chemical reaction – **acid hydrolysis of sucrose (inversion)** – plays a defining role in product loss and process control.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2 — Food Processing):**
> Sugar processing represents the *flagship fermentation-adjacent food industry* and is used to teach the integration of **mass transfer (juice extraction, crystallization), heat transfer (evaporation), and reaction kinetics (sucrose inversion)** in a single plant.

### 1.2 Raw Materials — The Two Pillars of World Sugar

| Source Plant | Scientific Name | Sucrose Content (wt %) | Primary Region |
|--------------|----------------|------------------------|----------------|
| Sugarcane | *Saccharum officinarum* | **10 – 15 %** | Tropical (India, Brazil, Cuba) |
| Sugar beet | *Beta vulgaris* | **15 – 20 %** | Temperate (EU, Russia, USA) |

The choice of raw material dictates the entire downstream sequence: beet sugar processing relies on **diffusion extraction** with hot water, whereas cane sugar processing relies on **mechanical milling**.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Kitchen Sugar Cube" Concept

Imagine you have a sponge (the cane stalk) soaked in sweet syrup. To get the syrup out, you:
1. **Crush the sponge** (milling) → raw juice + fibrous bagasse.
2. **Strain and clean the syrup** (clarification) → remove dirt, mud, wax.
3. **Boil away the water** (evaporation) → thick syrup.
4. **Cool it until crystals form** (crystallization) → sugar crystals + mother liquor (molasses).
5. **Separate the crystals** (centrifugation) and dry them.

The sugar factory is simply a giant, continuous, highly instrumented version of this kitchen process — but with precise **temperature (105–130 °C)**, **pH (6.8–7.2)**, and **vacuum control** to protect the sucrose from heat destruction and acid-driven **inversion**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric Conventions used throughout KTU boards:**
> - **Brix (°Bx):** Mass fraction of total soluble solids, measured with a refractometer at **20 °C**.
> - **Pol (°Z):** Apparent sucrose content measured by polarimetry.
> - **Purity (Q):** $Q = \dfrac{\text{Pol}}{\text{Brix}} \times 100$, expressed as a percentage.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Unit Operation Breakdown

The complete cane sugar manufacturing chain is partitioned into six tightly coupled stages:

**Stage A — Juice Extraction (Milling)**
- Cane passes through a **5-roller tandem mill** (or diffuser in modern plants).
- Each mill applies a pressure of **~200–250 kg/cm²** with imbibition water of ~30 % on cane weight.
- The output: **primary mixed juice** (Brix ≈ 14–18 °Bx, Purity ≈ 80–85 %).
- Bagasse leaving the last mill is used as **boiler fuel** (calorific value ≈ **1800 kcal/kg** at 50 % moisture).

**Stage B — Juice Clarification (Chemical Purification)**

The mixed juice contains suspended impurities, colloidal matter, pigments, and acids. Clarification is fundamentally a **coagulation–flocculation–sedimentation** unit operation assisted by two parallel reactions:

1. **Phosphatation route (most common in India):**
   - Phosphoric acid (H₃PO₄) is dosed at **300–500 ppm on juice** to convert soluble calcium phosphate into an insoluble floc.
2. **Carbonatation route (used in beet sugar and some Indian plants):**
   - Lime + CO₂ produce **calcium carbonate precipitate** that entraps impurities.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why lime (Ca(OH)₂) is added even before CO₂?**
> Because at pH ≈ **10.5–11.0**, lime neutralizes organic acids, precipitates phosphate, and coagulates proteins/waxes. Excess lime is then neutralized back to pH ≈ **6.8–7.2** by CO₂ — preventing sucrose inversion.

The clarified juice leaves the clarifier at **Brix ≈ 14–16 °Bx, Purity ≈ 86–90 %**, and the settled mud is filtered in a **rotary vacuum filter** to recover sugar.

**Stage C — Evaporation (Multiple-Effect Evaporator, MEE)**

The clarified juice (≈ 85 % water) must be concentrated to **55–60 °Bx syrup** before crystallization. This is achieved in a **5-effect falling-film evaporator** operated under progressively increasing vacuum (effect 1 at ~0.5 bar, effect 5 at ~0.05 bar).

- The boiling point elevation (BPE) of sucrose solution at 60 °Bx is approximately **4 °C**.
- **Steam economy** = 0.8 × n (where n = number of effects).

**Stage D — Crystallization (Vacuum Pan Boiling)**

This is the **heart of sugar technology** and the prime example of Reaction Engineering in the sugar industry. The syrup is further concentrated in a **batch vacuum pan** until **supersaturation** is achieved.

The **Webre–Hugot supersaturation equation** governs crystal growth:

$$\text{Coefficient of Supersaturation} = \frac{\text{Brix of mother liquor}}{\text{Solubility of sucrose at pan temperature}}$$

| Zone | Coefficient | Operation |
|------|-------------|-----------|
| Meta-stable | 1.00 – 1.30 | Crystal growth (preferred) |
| Intermediate | 1.30 – 1.50 | Caution |
| Labile | > 1.50 | Spontaneous nucleation (false grain) |

**Stage E — Centrifugation**
- The massecuite (crystal + molasses mixture) is spun in **batch basket centrifuges** at **1000–1200 RPM**.
- Mother liquor (molasses) is separated; crystals are washed with **hot water spray** to remove adhering molasses film.

**Stage F — Drying & Packaging**
- Wet sugar at ~1 % moisture is dried in a **rotary or fluidized bed dryer** using **hot air at 100–110 °C** to **< 0.05 % moisture**.
- Cooled, screened, and packed in 50 kg jute/HDPE bags.

### 2.2 The Key Reaction — Sucrose Inversion

Sucrose is a **non-reducing disaccharide** that hydrolyzes in the presence of acid and heat into **glucose + fructose** (an equimolar mixture called **invert sugar**). This is the **only significant chemical reaction** the KTU board expects students to model:

$$\text{C}_{12}\text{H}_{22}\text{O}_{11} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \xrightarrow{\text{H}^+, \Delta} \text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \;(\text{glucose}) + \text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \;(\text{fructose})$$

The reaction is **pseudo-first-order** with respect to sucrose concentration under constant [H⁺] and temperature:

$$-\frac{dC_s}{dt} = k \cdot C_s$$

The Arrhenius temperature dependence:

$$k = A \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{RT}\right)$$

with typical values $E_a \approx 25{,}800 \text{ cal/mol}$ and $A \approx 1.0 \times 10^{15} \text{ min}^{-1}$ (at pH ≈ 6.5).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the board emphasizes this reaction:**
> 1. It is the **main pathway of sucrose loss** in the plant.
> 2. It determines the **maximum safe residence time** in heaters and evaporators.
> 3. pH control at **6.8–7.2** minimizes the rate constant $k$.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| S.No | Formula / Quantity | Symbolic Form | Typical Value / Unit | Engineering Use |
|------|---------------------|---------------|----------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | Purity | $Q = \dfrac{\text{Pol}}{\text{Brix}} \times 100$ | 80 – 90 % | Quality control of juice, syrup, molasses |
| 2 | First-order sucrose inversion | $C_s = C_{s0}\,e^{-kt}$ | $C_s$ in g/L | Predicting sugar loss |
| 3 | Arrhenius rate constant | $k = A\,e^{-E_a/(RT)}$ | $E_a \approx 25.8$ kcal/mol | Effect of temperature on inversion |
| 4 | Steam economy of MEE | $E = 0.8\,n$ | n = number of effects (3–5) | Sizing boiler steam load |
| 5 | Crystallization yield | $Y_c = \dfrac{M_s^{\text{in}} - M_s^{\text{mol}}}{M_s^{\text{in}}} \times 100$ | % | Pans boiling efficiency |
| 6 | Pol extraction (cane) | $\text{Pol}_{\text{ext}} = \dfrac{\text{Pol}_{\text{juice}}}{\text{Pol}_{\text{juice}} + \text{Pol}_{\text{bagasse}}} \times 100$ | 95 – 98 % | Mill performance |
| 7 | Boiling point elevation | $\Delta T_b = K_b \cdot m$ | $\approx 4\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ at 60 °Bx | Evaporator design |
| 8 | Recoverable sugar | $S_{\text{rec}} = \dfrac{100\,(P - 40)}{100 - 40}$ (winter) | kg/tonne cane | Plant capacity planning |
| 9 | Bagasse energy | $Q_{\text{bagasse}} = m_{\text{bag}}\times LHV$ | $LHV \approx 1800$ kcal/kg | Boiler sizing |
| 10 | Crystal yield (Hugot) | $Y = \dfrac{100\,(Q - Q_m)}{100 - Q_m}$ | % | Crystallization efficiency |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Table Notation:**
> In the formulas above, the absolute value or ratio indicator is rendered using vertical bars via the LaTeX `\vert` macro where necessary (e.g., $\vert \Delta T \vert$) to avoid breaking the markdown table. Within the table cells above, division is shown with the fraction slash to preserve table integrity.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility

- The **steam economy of MEE** directly determines the **boiler capacity** and hence the **capital cost** of any sugar plant.
- The **inversion kinetics** are routinely used by factories to determine **acid dosing limits** during clarification.
- **Purity tracing** through every stage is the **principal diagnostic tool** in process control — a drop in purity at any stage signals upstream contamination or over-acidification.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Pseudo-First-Order Sucrose Inversion Kinetics

We begin with the acid-catalyzed hydrolysis of sucrose:

$$\text{C}_{12}\text{H}_{22}\text{O}_{11} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \xrightarrow{\text{H}^+,\Delta} \text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 + \text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6$$

**Step 1 — Rate Expression (true second-order):**

The stoichiometric rate law (rate ∝ [sucrose][H₂O][H⁺]) is normally written as:

$$-r_s = k_{\text{true}} \cdot [\text{H}^+] \cdot [\text{H}_2\text{O}] \cdot C_s$$

**Step 2 — Pseudo-First-Order Approximation:**

In dilute aqueous solution, water is in vast molar excess (≈ 55.5 mol/L) and [H⁺] is buffered by the lime/CO₂ control loop. Hence both $[\text{H}_2\text{O}]$ and $[\text{H}^+]$ are effectively constants during a single batch operation. They are absorbed into a new constant $k$:

$$-r_s = k \cdot C_s, \qquad k = k_{\text{true}}\cdot[\text{H}^+]\cdot[\text{H}_2\text{O}]$$

**Step 3 — Material Balance in a Batch Heater:**

For a perfectly mixed batch heater of volume $V$ with no inflow/outflow:

$$\frac{dC_s}{dt} = -k\,C_s$$

**Step 4 — Integration:**

Separating variables and integrating from $C_{s0}$ at $t=0$ to $C_s$ at time $t$:

$$\int_{C_{s0}}^{C_s} \frac{dC_s'}{C_s'} = -\int_{0}^{t} k\,dt'$$

This yields the **standard exponential decay**:

$$\boxed{\;\frac{C_s}{C_{s0}} = e^{-k\,t}\;}$$

**Step 5 — Fraction Inverted:**

The fraction of sucrose inverted in residence time $\tau$ is therefore:

$$f_{\text{inv}} = 1 - e^{-k\,\tau}$$

**Step 6 — Numerical Illustration (KTU Typical Value):**

Take $k = 0.0015 \;\text{min}^{-1}$ (corresponding to a clarified juice heater at 80 °C and pH 6.8) and a residence time $\tau = 30$ min:

$$f_{\text{inv}} = 1 - e^{-(0.0015)(30)} = 1 - e^{-0.045} \approx 1 - 0.9560 = 0.0440$$

Hence **≈ 4.4 % of sucrose** is inverted in the heater alone — a strong motivation for short residence times and pH buffering.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Invert sugar is hygroscopic and reduces crystallization yield** — every 1 % inversion costs approximately **0.6 kg of crystalline sugar per tonne of cane**. KTU examiners often test this calculation.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Crystallization Yield (Hugot Equation)

The Hugot formula relates **purity of massecuite**, **purity of mother liquor (molasses)**, and **crystal yield**.

**Step 1 — Define variables:**

- $M$ = total mass of massecuite
- $C$ = mass of crystals (pure sucrose)
- $L$ = mass of mother liquor (molasses)
- $Q$ = purity of massecuite (%)
- $Q_m$ = purity of mother liquor (%)

**Step 2 — Mass balance on massecuite:**

$$M = C + L$$

**Step 3 — Sucrose (Pol) balance on the same split:**

$$M \cdot Q = C \cdot 100 + L \cdot Q_m$$

(The crystals are pure sucrose → purity = 100 %.)

**Step 4 — Substitute $L = M - C$ into the Pol balance:**

$$M \cdot Q = 100\,C + (M - C)\,Q_m$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $C$:**

$$M \cdot Q = 100\,C + M\,Q_m - C\,Q_m$$

$$M(Q - Q_m) = C\,(100 - Q_m)$$

$$\boxed{\;Y_c = \frac{C}{M} \times 100 = \frac{100\,(Q - Q_m)}{100 - Q_m}\;}$$

This is the standard Hugot yield equation, valid for any vacuum pan stage.

**Step 6 — Numerical Illustration:**

A "C-massecuite" (final strike) has $Q = 70$ and final molasses purity $Q_m = 35$:

$$Y_c = \frac{100\,(70 - 35)}{100 - 35} = \frac{3500}{65} \approx 53.85\,\%$$

Hence about 53.85 % of the massecuite mass exits as crystalline sugar.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Steam Economy of a Multiple-Effect Evaporator

Let $S$ = mass of live steam fed to effect 1, $V_i$ = vapor generated in effect $i$, and $n$ = number of effects.

**Step 1 — Energy balance on each effect (steady-state, negligible sensible heat):**

For effect $i$, the latent heat of vapor leaving equals the latent heat of steam (or vapor from previous effect) condensing:

$$V_i \approx V_{i-1} \quad \text{(constant latent heat assumption)}$$

**Step 2 — Total water evaporated:**

$$V_{\text{total}} = V_1 + V_2 + \cdots + V_n \approx n\,V_1$$

**Step 3 — Steam economy definition:**

$$E = \frac{V_{\text{total}}}{S} = \frac{n\,V_1}{V_1} = n$$

The **ideal** steam economy equals the number of effects. In practice, due to boiling point elevation, sensible heat, and heat losses:

$$\boxed{\;E_{\text{actual}} = 0.8 \cdot n\;}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Illustration:**

A 5-effect evaporator therefore has an actual economy of $0.8 \times 5 = 4.0$, meaning **1 kg of live steam evaporates 4 kg of water**.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Sugar Industry Process Map

| Process Stage | Key Unit Operation | Governing Engineering Principle | Critical Control Variable | Typical KTU Question Type |
|---------------|---------------------|--------------------------------|---------------------------|----------------------------|
| Cane preparation | Shredding / cutting | Size reduction, diffusion surface | Cane length ~ 25–50 mm | 3-mark descriptive |
| Milling | 5-roller tandem | Mechanical expression | Pol % bagasse | 14-mark mass balance |
| Clarification | Phosphatation/carbonatation | Coagulation, sedimentation | pH 6.8–7.2, T = 100 °C | 7-mark reaction derivation |
| Evaporation | Multiple-effect | Heat transfer, vapor recompression | Steam economy = 0.8n | 14-mark energy balance |
| Crystallization | Vacuum pan | Phase change, supersaturation | Webre coefficient 1.0–1.3 | 7-mark Hugot yield |
| Centrifugation | Basket centrifuge | Mechanical separation | Wash water quantity | 3-mark qualitative |
| Drying | Fluidized bed | Convective heat & mass transfer | Final moisture < 0.05 % | 3-mark descriptive |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Process Flow Diagram — Complete Cane Sugar Manufacturing Plant

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Sugarcane Reception and Weighbridge] --> B[Cane Preparation - Cutter and Shredder]
    B --> C[Tandem Mill Train - 5 Rollers with Imbibition]
    C --> D[Mixed Juice Receiving Tank]
    C --> E[Bagasse to Boiler House as Fuel]
    D --> F[Heater - Juice Preheating to 70 C]
    F --> G[Clarifier - Phosphatation with Lime and H3PO4]
    G --> H[Rotary Vacuum Filter for Press Mud]
    H --> I[Clear Juice Storage Tank]
    I --> J[Multiple Effect Evaporator - 5 Effects]
    J --> K[Syrup at 60 Brix]
    K --> L[Vacuum Pan A - First Strike Crystallization]
    L --> M[Centrifuge A - A Sugar Recovery]
    M --> N[A Molasses Forwarded to B Pan]
    N --> O[Vacuum Pan B - Second Strike]
    O --> P[Centrifuge B - B Sugar Recovery]
    P --> Q[Final Molasses - Storage for Distillery]
    M --> R[Sugar Dryer and Cooler]
    P --> R
    R --> S[Screening and Grading]
    S --> T[Packaging and Storage]

    classDef raw fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#7d6608,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef process fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#1b4f72,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Reaction Engineering Lens

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FEED[Feed Section]
        F1[Cane or Beet] --> F2[Size Reduction Unit]
        F2 --> F3[Juice Extraction Unit]
    end

    subgraph REACT[Reaction and Mass Transfer Section]
        R1[Clarification Reactor - Acid Base Neutralization]
        R2[Evaporator Train - Heat Transfer]
        R3[Crystallizer - Phase Change Reactor]
    end

    subgraph SEP[Separation Section]
        S1[Centrifuge - Mechanical Separation]
        S2[Dryer - Convective Separation]
    end

    subgraph CONTROL[Process Control Layer]
        C1[pH Sensor 6.8 to 7.2]
        C2[Temperature Sensor 105 to 130 C]
        C3[Brix and Pol Sensor]
        C4[Vacuum Sensor]
    end

    F3 --> R1
    R1 --> R2
    R2 --> R3
    R3 --> S1
    S1 --> S2
    S2 --> P1[Final Crystalline Sugar]

    C1 -.-> R1
    C2 -.-> R2
    C3 -.-> R3
    C4 -.-> R3
```

### 4.3 Control Logic for Crystallization (Supervisory Loop)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ST[Start Vacuum Pan Charging] --> ME[Maseruite Reaches Target Brix 92]
    ME --> SUP{Supersaturation Coefficient in Meta-stable Zone 1.0 to 1.3?}
    SUP -- Yes --> GR[Crystal Growth Phase - Feed Syrup and Steam]
    SUP -- No --> COAR[Adjust Vacuum or Steam to Restore Meta-stable Zone]
    COAR --> SUP
    GR --> TGT{Target Crystal Size Reached 0.6 to 1.2 mm?}
    TGT -- No --> GR
    TGT -- Yes --> DR[Drop Massecuite to Centrifuge]
    DR --> END[End of Batch Cycle]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
Define the terms **Brix**, **Pol**, and **Purity** as used in sugar technology. Why is purity monitoring done at every stage?

**Model Answer:**

- **Brix (°Bx):** The total soluble solid content of a sugar-bearing liquid, measured by refractometer at 20 °C, expressed as weight percent of sucrose in pure solution.
- **Pol (°Z):** The apparent sucrose concentration measured by optical rotation (polarimetry), typically at 20 °C using a 200 mm tube.
- **Purity (Q):** $Q = \dfrac{\text{Pol}}{\text{Brix}} \times 100$, an indicator of how much of the dissolved solid is actually sucrose.

Purity is monitored stage-wise because a sudden drop indicates contamination, over-acidification (inversion), or microbial infection. It is the **single most important quality indicator** in a sugar factory.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
What is **sucrose inversion**? State the reaction and the two key process variables that control its rate.

**Model Answer:**

Sucrose inversion is the **acid-catalyzed hydrolysis of sucrose** into an equimolar mixture of glucose and fructose (invert sugar):

$$\text{C}_{12}\text{H}_{22}\text{O}_{11} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \xrightarrow{\text{H}^+, \Delta} \text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 + \text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6$$

The two key controlling variables are:
1. **pH of the juice** (kept at **6.8–7.2**; lower pH → faster inversion), and
2. **Temperature × residence time** (both exponential in the Arrhenius rate law).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Describe with a neat flow diagram the complete manufacturing process of **refined cane sugar** starting from sugarcane reception to packaged sugar. Highlight the function of each unit. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The complete process flow with unit functions:

1. **Cane Reception and Weighing** – trucks unload onto a weighbridge; representative samples are analyzed for pol % and fibre content.

2. **Cane Preparation** – knives and shredders reduce the cane to a fibrous mat; increases extraction efficiency by disrupting cells.

3. **Milling (5-roller tandem)** – three rollers with top roller applying ~250 kg/cm²; imbibition water (≈ 30 % on cane) is sprayed between mills. Produces **mixed juice (≈ 14–18 °Bx, 80–85 purity)** and **bagasse (≈ 50 % moisture)**.

4. **Mixed Juice Screening** – removes bagacillo (fine fibre) using DSM screens.

5. **Juice Pre-heating** – juice is heated to 70 °C to enhance subsequent clarification reactions.

6. **Clarification (Phosphatation)** – lime (Ca(OH)₂) raises pH to ~10.5; H₃PO₄ (300–500 ppm) forms calcium phosphate floc that entraps impurities; settled mud is filtered in a rotary vacuum filter.

7. **Evaporation** – 5-effect falling-film MEE concentrates clear juice from ~16 °Bx to **55–60 °Bx syrup**.

8. **Crystallization (Vacuum Pan)** – syrup is seeded and boiled under vacuum to produce A-, B-, and C-massecuites with progressively lower purities.

9. **Centrifugation** – basket centrifuges separate crystals from mother liquor at 1000–1200 RPM.

10. **Drying, Cooling, Screening, and Packaging** – final sugar dried to < 0.05 % moisture, graded by mesh size, packed in 50 kg bags.

**(b)** The first-order rate constant for sucrose inversion in a juice heater is $k = 2.5 \times 10^{-3}$ min⁻¹. If the juice spends 25 minutes in the heater, calculate the **fraction of sucrose inverted** and the **% loss per tonne of sucrose fed**, given initial sucrose = 200 g/L. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the kinetic model:**

$$\frac{C_s}{C_{s0}} = e^{-k\,t}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the given values:**

$$\frac{C_s}{C_{s0}} = e^{-(2.5 \times 10^{-3})(25)} = e^{-0.0625}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the exponential:**

$$e^{-0.0625} = 0.9394$$

**Step 4 — Fraction inverted:**

$$f_{\text{inv}} = 1 - 0.9394 = 0.0606 \approx 6.06\,\%$$

**Step 5 — Sucrose lost per litre:**

$$C_{s,\text{lost}} = 200 \times 0.0606 = 12.12 \text{ g/L}$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation:**

Approximately **6 % of the sucrose entering the heater is destroyed** — equivalent to **60.6 kg of sugar lost per 1000 L** of juice, confirming why pH buffering and residence-time reduction are critical.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a)** With a sketch, explain the working of a **multiple-effect evaporator (MEE)** used in sugar processing. Derive an expression for the **steam economy** of an *n*-effect evaporator and state the actual value for a 5-effect system. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Working Principle:**

A multiple-effect evaporator reuses the vapor generated in one effect (called effect 1) as the heating medium for effect 2, whose vapor in turn heats effect 3, and so on. Vacuum increases progressively from effect 1 (~0.5 bar) to effect n (~0.05 bar), allowing boiling at successively lower temperatures while preserving the sucrose.

**Derivation — Steam Economy:**

Let:
- $S$ = live steam fed to effect 1
- $V_i$ = vapor generated in effect $i$
- $L_i$ = latent heat of vaporization

**Step 1 — Heat balance on each effect (steady state, neglecting sensible heat):**

$$V_i \cdot L_i = V_{i-1} \cdot L_{i-1} \qquad i = 2, 3, \ldots, n$$

**Step 2 — For dilute sugar solutions, $L_i \approx \text{constant}$:**

$$V_i \approx V_{i-1} \approx V_1$$

**Step 3 — Total water evaporated:**

$$V_{\text{total}} = V_1 + V_2 + \cdots + V_n = n \cdot V_1 = n \cdot S$$

**Step 4 — Steam economy (definition):**

$$\boxed{\;E = \frac{V_{\text{total}}}{S} = n\;}$$

**Step 5 — Actual steam economy (with BPE and losses):**

$$\boxed{\;E_{\text{actual}} = 0.8 \cdot n\;}$$

**Step 6 — For a 5-effect system:**

$$E_{\text{actual}} = 0.8 \times 5 = 4.0 \text{ kg water evaporated per kg live steam}$$

---

**(b)** A **vacuum pan** produces a massecuite of mass 12 tonnes with purity $Q = 70$ and the resulting final molasses has purity $Q_m = 32$. Using the **Hugot equation**, determine the mass of crystalline sugar recovered. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — State the Hugot equation:**

$$Y_c = \frac{100\,(Q - Q_m)}{100 - Q_m}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the given values:**

$$Y_c = \frac{100\,(70 - 32)}{100 - 32} = \frac{100 \times 38}{68}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the numerator and denominator:**

$$Y_c = \frac{3800}{68} = 55.88\,\%$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by massecuite mass:**

$$C = Y_c \times \frac{M}{100} = 0.5588 \times 12{,}000 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 5 — Final answer:**

$$\boxed{\;C \approx 6705.9 \text{ kg} \approx 6.71 \text{ tonnes of crystalline sugar}\;}$$

The remaining ~5.29 tonnes is final molasses, which is sent to a distillery or animal feed plant.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Sugar Industry Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the pseudo-first-order assumption** when stating the inversion rate law → loses 1–2 marks (KTU examiners expect the explicit "water and H⁺ are constant" justification).
> 2. **Writing the Hugot equation with inverted signs** ($100 - Q$ instead of $100 - Q_m$) — a single sign error costs 3–4 marks.
> 3. **Not mentioning BPE (boiling point elevation)** in the steam economy derivation — examiners specifically allocate **at least 1 mark** for the actual economy factor of 0.8.
> 4. **Mixing up Brix and Pol** in the purity formula — always remember **Pol in the numerator**, Brix in the denominator.
> 5. **Skipping the pH control justification (6.8–7.2)** in inversion questions — even if the calculation is correct, the conceptual explanation of *why* pH is controlled earns the full 7 marks.
> 6. **Drawing the flow diagram without naming the equipment** (e.g., writing only "evaporation" instead of "5-effect falling-film MEE") — KTU board examiners allocate **2 marks** specifically for correctly named unit operations.
> 7. **Not stating units** (e.g., reporting yield without "%" or mass without "kg/tonnes") — at least 0.5 mark is reserved for unit consistency.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Food Processing Industry: Sugar**

- ☐ **Sucrose formula:** C₁₂H₂₂O₁₁; key reaction is **acid hydrolysis to glucose + fructose (invert sugar)**.
- ☐ **Three core quality metrics:** **Brix (°Bx), Pol (°Z), Purity (Q = Pol/Brix × 100)** — know them cold.
- ☐ **Critical pH window:** **6.8–7.2** — outside this range, sucrose loss accelerates exponentially.
- ☐ **Pseudo-first-order inversion kinetics:** $C_s = C_{s0}\,e^{-kt}$, with $E_a \approx 25.8$ kcal/mol.
- ☐ **Arrhenius form:** $k = A\,e^{-E_a/(RT)}$ — increasing T by 10 °C approximately **doubles** the rate of inversion.
- ☐ **Milling target:** Pol % bagasse < 1 %; mixed juice purity 80–85 %; clarified juice purity 86–90 %.
- ☐ **Clarification chemistry:** Lime → pH 10.5 (neutralizes acids, coagulates proteins); H₃PO₄ or CO₂ → re-acidify to pH 7 (precipitates calcium phosphate/carbonate).
- ☐ **MEE design:** Number of effects = **3 to 5**; ideal economy = n; actual economy = **0.8 n**; BPE at 60 °Bx ≈ **4 °C**.
- ☐ **Crystallization control:** Operate in the **meta-stable zone** (Webre coefficient 1.0–1.3); avoid labile zone to prevent false grain.
- ☐ **Hugot yield formula:** $Y_c = \dfrac{100\,(Q - Q_m)}{100 - Q_m}$ — valid for any vacuum pan strike.
- ☐ **By-products (don't forget for viva):** **Bagasse** (fuel), **Molasses** (ethanol/feed), **Press mud** (manure/wax), **CO₂** (carbonation).
- ☐ **Sugar losses budget (typical):** 1 % milling + 1 % clarification + 1 % inversion + 2 % molasses = **~5 %** total.
- ☐ **Final product specs:** Moisture < **0.05 %**, ICUMSA colour < **80 IU**, crystal size **0.6–1.2 mm**.
- ☐ **Typical plant capacities:** Small = 1250 TCD; Medium = 2500–5000 TCD; Large = 10,000+ TCD (TCD = Tonnes Cane per Day).
- ☐ **Reaction engineering takeaway:** The sugar plant is a beautifully integrated **mass + heat + reaction** system where every 1 % improvement in inversion control translates to **~6 kg extra sugar per tonne of cane**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Industrial Alcohol
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT637 - CHEMICAL TECHNOLOGY AND REACTION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Industrial Alcohol

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Industrial Alcohol

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Alcohol (Ethanol / Ethyl Alcohol, C₂H₅OH)**
> Industrial alcohol is ethyl alcohol (ethanol) produced on a commercial scale through fermentation of sugars/starches by yeast or via synthetic chemical routes (chiefly catalytic hydration of ethylene). In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, it is the *denatured or rectified spirit* manufactured in bulk for use as a solvent, a chemical feedstock (for acetaldehyde, ethyl acetate, butadiene, glycol ethers), an industrial fuel additive, and a pharmaceutical excipient — *not* intended for direct human consumption.

| Property | Value | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Molecular weight | **46.07 g/mol** | Basis for stoichiometric yield calculations |
| Boiling point (pure) | **78.37 °C** | Determines distillation column operating pressure |
| Density at 20 °C | **0.789 g/cm³** | Used in volume-to-mass conversions in industry |
| Azeotrope with water | **95.6 % v/v ethanol at 78.2 °C** | Upper bound for simple distillation purity |
| Flash point | **13 °C** | Classified as a **Class IB Flammable Liquid** (NFPA) |
| Latent heat of vaporization | **38.56 kJ/mol** (at bp) | Governs reboiler steam duty |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PESFT637 / Module 2):** The module treats industrial alcohol as a *case study* in combining **biochemical reaction engineering** (yeast fermentation, Monod kinetics) with **downstream separation processes** (rectification, azeotropic distillation, molecular-sieve dehydration). Questions almost always fuse the reaction step with one separation step.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Brewery Inside a Refinery"

Imagine a bakery that grows its own yeast, lets the yeast "eat" sugar in giant vats, and exhales CO₂ while producing alcohol — much like how a brewery makes beer. The catch is that the resulting "beer" is only about **10 % alcohol**, and what industry really wants is **>99.9 % pure ethanol** (absolute alcohol). So the process is essentially a **two-act industrial play**:

1. **Act I — Bioreactor (Fermenter):** Microbial reaction that *produces* ethanol in a dilute aqueous broth.
2. **Act II — Distillation Train (Beer Column → Rectifying Column → Dehydration Unit):** A sequence of physical separations that *purify* it to industrial specification.

Just as you cannot make espresso without a machine that boils water and condenses steam, you cannot make industrial alcohol without a fermentation step *followed by* a multi-column distillation train. This **reaction + separation coupling** is the central theme KTU expects you to master.

---

## 1.3 Why Industrial Alcohol? — Engineering Motivation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Industrial alcohol is the **largest-volume bioproduct** in the world, with global production exceeding **100 billion litres/year** (2024). It is a *platform chemical* that sits upstream of more than 50 downstream derivatives.

**Primary Industrial Applications:**
- **Chemical Feedstock:** → Acetaldehyde → Acetic acid / Butadiene / Ethyl acetate / Glycol ethers
- **Pharmaceutical:** Solvent for tinctures, syrups, and injectables (after denaturation removal).
- **Cosmetics & Personal Care:** Perfume carrier, antiseptic.
- **Fuel:** E10, E20, E85 gasoline blends; second-generation cellulosic ethanol.
- **Beverages:** Potable/rectified spirit (after denaturant removal).

---

## 1.4 Classification of Industrial Alcohol Grades

| Grade | Purity (v/v) | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Rectified Spirit (RS)** | ≥ 95.0 % | Solvent, intermediate |
| **Absolute Alcohol (Anhydrous)** | ≥ 99.5 % | Pharma, fuel, lab reagent |
| **Denatured Spirit (SDS)** | ~95 %, with additives | Industrial solvent, anti-brewery |
| **Power Alcohol** | ~95 % + gasoline / benzene | Motor fuel |
| **Cologne Spirit** | Very high, near-anhydrous | Cosmetics |

> [!NOTE]
> The term **"denatured"** means a small quantity of methanol, pyridine, or denatonium benzoate has been added to render the alcohol unfit for drinking, thereby avoiding the high excise duty imposed on potable spirits.

---

## 1.5 Raw Material Routes — KTU Expects You to Know All Three

### Route A — Sugarcane / Sugar Beet Molasses Fermentation (Most Common in India)
- India produces **>300 million tonnes** of sugarcane annually; molasses is the by-product after sugar crystallization.
- Molasses typically contains **45–55 % total sugars** (sucrose, glucose, fructose).

### Route B — Starch Hydrolysis (Grains, Cassava)
- Sources: corn (USA), wheat (Europe), tapioca (India, SE Asia).
- Starch (C₆H₁₀O₅)ₙ must first be hydrolysed to glucose via **mashing with enzymes** (α-amylase + glucoamylase).

### Route C — Synthetic / Petrochemical Route
- **Direct hydration of ethylene:** C₂H₄ + H₂O → C₂H₅OH (H₃PO₄/SiO₂ catalyst, ~300 °C, 60–70 atm).
- Dominant when cheap ethylene is available (USA, Middle East).
- Gives **synthetic ethanol**, chemically identical to bioethanol but fossil-derived.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU frequently asks: *"Compare fermentation and synthetic routes of ethanol production."* Always present the comparison in a **table** (yield, conditions, CAPEX, OPEX, purity, CO₂ footprint).

---

## 1.6 Production Tonnage Snapshot (Engineering Context)

> [!NOTE]
> India is one of the **top 3 ethanol producers** globally. The **Ethanol Blended Petrol (EBP) Programme** targets **E20 (20 % blending)** by 2025, driving huge demand for industrial alcohol from sugar mills and distilleries.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Complete Industrial Alcohol Process — Step-by-Step Logic

The overall process is a **five-stage train** that combines a biochemical reactor with three physical separation steps:

1. **Feed Preparation (Milling / Dilution / Sterilization)**
2. **Biochemical Reaction (Fermentation)**
3. **Primary Separation (Beer Stripping / Distillation)**
4. **Secondary Separation (Rectification to 95.6 % azeotrope)**
5. **Tertiary Separation (Azeotropic Distillation or Molecular Sieve Dehydration → Absolute Alcohol)**

Let's dissect each stage from an engineering standpoint.

---

### Stage 1 — Feed Preparation

**For Molasses Route:**
- Molasses is diluted to a sugar concentration of **15–20 % w/v**.
- pH is adjusted to **4.0–4.5** using H₂SO₄ (optimum for *Saccharomyces cerevisiae*).
- Nutrient salts added: **(NH₄)₂SO₄** as nitrogen source; **KH₂PO₄** as phosphorus source.
- Solution is sterilized at **80–90 °C for 30 min** to kill wild yeasts and competing bacteria.

**For Grain/Starch Route:**
- Grains are milled, slurried with water, and **cooked at 145–155 °C** (jet cooker).
- Liquefaction: **α-amylase** at 85–90 °C converts starch → maltodextrins.
- Saccharification: **glucoamylase** at 55–65 °C converts maltodextrins → glucose.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why sterilize?** Wild bacteria (*Lactobacillus*) consume glucose faster than yeast and produce lactic acid instead of ethanol. Even 1 % contamination can drop ethanol yield by 5–10 %.

---

### Stage 2 — Fermentation (The Heart of the Process)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Concept Anchor:** This is a *biochemical CSTR* (continuous stirred-tank reactor) operated in **fed-batch** or **continuous mode**, governed by **Monod kinetics** for yeast growth and **Luedeking–Piret kinetics** for ethanol formation.

#### 2.1.1 Governing Biochemical Reactions

**From Hexose (Glucose):**

$$
\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \xrightarrow{\text{yeast, anaerobic}} 2\,\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} + 2\,\text{CO}_2 \quad \Delta H = -92\;\text{kJ/mol}
$$

**From Sucrose (first hydrolyzed by invertase):**

$$
\text{C}_{12}\text{H}_{22}\text{O}_{11} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \xrightarrow{\text{invertase}} 2\,\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \xrightarrow{\text{zymase}} 4\,\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} + 4\,\text{CO}_2
$$

#### 2.1.2 Stoichiometric Yield — The "Why" Behind 51.1 %

**Theoretical mass yield from glucose:**

$$
Y_{\text{ethanol/glucose}} = \frac{2 \times M_{\text{EtOH}}}{1 \times M_{\text{glucose}}} = \frac{2 \times 46.07}{180.16} = 0.511\;\text{g EtOH / g glucose}
$$

**Theoretical mass yield from sucrose:**

$$
Y_{\text{ethanol/sucrose}} = \frac{4 \times 46.07}{342.30} = 0.538\;\text{g EtOH / g sucrose}
$$

**Theoretical CO₂ yield from glucose:**

$$
Y_{\text{CO}_2/\text{glucose}} = \frac{2 \times 44.01}{180.16} = 0.489\;\text{g CO}_2/\text{g glucose}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Number:** Memorize **0.511 (or 51.1 %)** as the *theoretical maximum* ethanol yield on glucose basis. Industrial yields are **85–92 %** of theoretical due to yeast cell-mass production and minor by-products (glycerol, higher alcohols / fusel oils).

#### 2.1.3 Yeast Kinetics — Monod Equation

Cell growth rate:

$$
\mu = \frac{\mu_{\max}\, S}{K_S + S}
$$

Where $\mu$ = specific growth rate (h⁻¹), $\mu_{\max}$ = maximum specific growth rate (~0.4 h⁻¹ for *S. cerevisiae*), $S$ = limiting substrate (glucose) concentration (g/L), and $K_S$ = half-saturation constant (~0.2 g/L).

**Ethanol inhibition is critical** — high ethanol concentration denatures yeast cell membranes. The *Aiba–Shimiya* model captures this:

$$
\mu = \mu_{\max}\,\frac{S}{K_S + S}\,\exp(-k_i P)
$$

Where $P$ = ethanol concentration (g/L), $k_i$ = inhibition constant (~0.02 L/g).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Forgetting the inhibition term. Above ~**100 g/L ethanol (~12.7 % v/v)**, yeast growth essentially stops — this is why fermenters are rarely run past this concentration. Vacuum fermentation or membrane recycle reactors are used to overcome this limit.

#### 2.1.4 Fermenter Operating Conditions

| Parameter | Typical Value |
|---|---|
| Temperature | **30–35 °C** |
| pH | **4.0–5.0** |
| Residence time | **36–72 h** |
| Final broth ethanol | **7–12 % v/v** |
| Sugar conversion | **85–95 %** |
| Yeast loading | **5–10 % v/v inoculum** |

---

### Stage 3 — Beer Column (CO₂ Stripping + Crude Distillation)

The "beer" leaving the fermenter contains **7–12 % ethanol, water, yeast, fusel oils, aldehydes, esters, glycerol, and dissolved CO₂**.

**Beer Column Function:**
- Strip ethanol from the broth.
- Top: ethanol-rich vapour (35–50 % ethanol) goes to rectification.
- Bottom: **slop / spent wash** (essentially water + dissolved solids).
- CO₂ is vented (often recovered for carbonation or other uses).

**Operating Parameters:**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Number of trays | 22–28 (sieve trays) |
| Reboiler steam | 0.45–0.55 kg/L of ethanol produced |
| Top pressure | Slightly above atmospheric |
| Top temperature | ~95 °C |
| Bottom temperature | ~105 °C |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Beer column is usually modelled as a **stripping section only** (no rectifying section) because feed enters at top. Tray-by-tray mass balances using **Kremser equation** or McCabe–Thiele are favourite exam topics.

---

### Stage 4 — Rectifying Column (Concentration to 95.6 % Azeotrope)

The 35–50 % overhead vapour from beer column is fed to the rectifying column, where it is concentrated up to the **ethanol–water azeotrope at 95.6 % v/v**.

**Key Design Features:**

| Feature | Specification |
|---|---|
| Number of trays | 40–60 (with feed near middle) |
| Reflux ratio | **2–3** (close to minimum) |
| Top product | **95.6 % EtOH** (azeotrope) |
| Top temperature | **78.2 °C** |
| Bottom product | Water (trace EtOH, <0.1 %) |
| Side draw | Fusel oil (higher alcohols, drawn at ~50 °C plate) |

**Why does it stop at 95.6 %?** Because ethanol and water form a **minimum-boiling homogeneous azeotrope**. No number of simple distillation plates can cross this barrier.

---

### Stage 5 — Dehydration to Absolute Alcohol (>99.5 %)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **most conceptually rich** sub-topic in KTU Module 2. The 4 % water remaining in azeotrope must be removed to obtain absolute alcohol. Three industrial methods exist:

#### Method 1 — Azeotropic Distillation (Using Benzene or Cyclohexane)
- Add **benzene** (or cyclohexane, or diethyl ether) to break the azeotrope.
- Benzene forms a **ternary heteroazeotrope** with ethanol and water that boils at **64.9 °C** (lower than either binary).
- Ternary azeotrope separates into two liquid phases; water-rich phase is decanted; benzene-rich phase is returned as reflux.
- Bottoms: **anhydrous ethanol (>99.5 %)**.

#### Method 2 — Extractive Distillation (Using Ethylene Glycol)
- Ethylene glycol has a much higher boiling point (~197 °C).
- Acts as a **solvent that alters the relative volatility** of water vs ethanol.
- Water is taken overhead; bottoms = glycol + ethanol → separate distillation column recovers both.

#### Method 3 — Molecular Sieve Adsorption (Modern, Dominant in Industry Today)
- Use **3 Å zeolite (type A) molecular sieves**.
- Pore diameter ≈ **3 Å (0.3 nm)** — admits H₂O (kinetic diameter 2.6 Å) but excludes ethanol (4.4 Å).
- Two-bed system: one adsorbs, the other is regenerated under vacuum.
- **Energy efficient**: ~**1.1 kg steam per litre** vs ~**3 kg** for azeotropic distillation.
- This is the **industry standard** for fuel-grade ethanol today.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favourite Question:** *"Discuss the methods used for breaking the ethanol–water azeotrope."* Always include all three methods in your answer with a **comparative table**.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> This is your **exam cheat sheet**. Every formula below has appeared in KTU past papers. Memorize the boxed constants.

| # | Formula / Expression | Physical Meaning | Units | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Y_{\text{EtOH/glucose}} = \dfrac{2 M_{\text{EtOH}}}{M_{\text{glucose}}} = 0.511$ | Theoretical ethanol yield from glucose | g/g | **0.511** (theoretical), **0.435–0.470** (actual) |
| 2 | $Y_{\text{EtOH/sucrose}} = \dfrac{4 \times 46.07}{342.30} = 0.538$ | Theoretical ethanol yield from sucrose | g/g | **0.538** theoretical |
| 3 | $\mu = \dfrac{\mu_{\max}\, S}{K_S + S}$ | Monod growth kinetics | h⁻¹ | $\mu_{\max}\approx 0.4\;\text{h}^{-1}$ |
| 4 | $\mu = \mu_{\max}\dfrac{S}{K_S + S}e^{-k_i P}$ | Aiba inhibited growth (with ethanol) | h⁻¹ | $k_i\approx 0.02\;\text{L/g}$ |
| 5 | $\nu_{\text{EtOH}} = \nu_{\max}\dfrac{S}{K_S^\prime + S}\dfrac{K_P}{K_P + P}$ | Luedeking–Piret ethanol formation | g/L·h | — |
| 6 | $Y_{X/S} = \dfrac{\Delta X}{\Delta S}$ | Yield of cell mass on substrate | g/g | **0.10–0.15** |
| 7 | $r_{\text{EtOH}} = \nu\, X$ | Volumetric ethanol production rate | g/L·h | **1–3** |
| 8 | $\theta_c = \dfrac{1}{\mu - k_d}$ | Critical dilution rate (washout) | h | — |
| 9 | $\text{EtOH \% v/v} = \dfrac{V_{\text{EtOH(mL)}}}{V_{\text{broth(mL)}}} \times 100$ | Volumetric ethanol concentration | % | **7–12** |
| 10 | $\rho_{\text{EtOH}} = 0.789\;\text{g/cm}^3$ at 20 °C | Density of pure ethanol | g/cm³ | Use for conversions |
| 11 | Azeotrope: **95.6 % v/v** at **78.2 °C**, 1 atm | Ethanol–water minimum azeotrope | — | — |
| 12 | $Q_{\text{reboiler}} = \dot{m}_{\text{EtOH}}\,\lambda_{\text{EtOH}}\,(R+1)$ | Reboiler heat duty | kW | $R$=reflux ratio |
| 13 | $\dot{m}_{\text{steam}} = \dfrac{Q_{\text{reboiler}}}{\lambda_{\text{steam}}}$ | Steam flow to reboiler | kg/h | — |
| 14 | $D = \dfrac{F\,x_F - W\,x_W}{x_D - x_W}$ | Distillate rate from rectifying column | kg/h | Mass balance |
| 15 | $R_{\min} = \dfrac{x_D - y_F}{y_F - x_F}$ | Minimum reflux ratio (McCabe–Thiele) | — | For design |
| 16 | $\text{CO}_2\,\text{per L EtOH} \approx 0.96\;\text{kg}$ | CO₂ by-product from fermentation | kg/L | Stoichiometric |
| 17 | $A = \dfrac{L}{D}$ | Reflux ratio (alternative form) | — | Typical = 2–3 |

> [!WARNING]
> **Care with absolute values:** When using $x_D$, $x_F$, $x_W$ in the table, write them as decimals (e.g., 0.956), not percentages, in the equations. KTU examiners routinely deduct marks for unit confusion.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Industrial alcohol manufacturing is a **flagship case study** in integrating:

- **Biochemical reaction engineering** (Monod kinetics, CSTR vs PFR modelling of fermenter).
- **Mass-transfer operations** (stripping in beer column).
- **Equilibrium-stage separations** (rectifying column, McCabe–Thiele analysis).
- **Advanced separations** (azeotropic/extractive distillation, adsorption).
- **Energy integration** (heat integration across the train, vapour recompression, mechanical vapour recompression for molecular sieve regeneration).

It is the **textbook example** in Fogler, Levenspiel, and McCabe–Smith–Harriott of a *multi-unit chemical process*.
