# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between addition and condensation polymerization with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| **Aspect** | **Addition Polymerization** | **Condensation Polymerization** |
|---|---|---|
| By-product | No by-product released | Small molecules (H₂O, HCl, NH₃) released |
| Monomer type | Usually unsaturated (vinyl, vinylidene) | Difunctional (containing –OH, –COOH, –NH₂) |
| Mechanism | Chain-growth, free-radical/ionic | Step-growth |
| Example | Polyethylene from ethylene | Nylon-6,6 from adipic acid + hexamethylenediamine |

**(1 mark each for: by-product, monomer type, example + mechanism distinction.)**

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Classify polymers based on thermal behavior. Give two examples for each class.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Thermoplastic polymers** — Soften reversibly on heating; can be remolded. Linear/branched chains. *Examples: Polyethylene (PE), Polystyrene (PS).* (1.5 marks)

2. **Thermosetting polymers** — Irreversibly harden on heating due to crosslinking; cannot be remelted. *Examples: Bakelite (Phenol-Formaldehyde), Melamine-Formaldehyde.* (1.5 marks)

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern**, each Part-B question has an **internal choice**. Solve **any one** of the two alternatives. The 14-mark split is **7 + 7** across two sub-parts.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Module 2]`

**(a)** Classify polymers based on **origin, structure, polymerization mechanism, and thermal behavior**. Discuss each category with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain **addition polymerization** in detail, covering its mechanism (initiation, propagation, termination), and derive an expression for the **degree of polymerization (DP)**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Classification (7 marks):**

**(i) Based on Origin (1.5 marks):**
- **Natural:** Polymers from plants/animals. *Examples: Natural rubber, cellulose, starch, proteins, DNA.*
- **Semi-synthetic:** Chemically modified natural polymers. *Examples: Cellulose acetate, nitrocellulose, rayon.*
- **Synthetic:** Man-made. *Examples: Polyethylene, PVC, Nylon, Bakelite.*

**(ii) Based on Structure (1.5 marks):**
- **Linear:** Straight chains, e.g., HDPE, Nylon-6,6.
- **Branched:** Side branches, e.g., LDPE.
- **Cross-linked:** Chains joined by covalent bonds, e.g., Bakelite, vulcanized rubber.
- **Network (3D):** Highly crosslinked, e.g., Melamine, Epoxy.

**(iii) Based on Polymerization (2 marks):**
- **Addition (Chain-growth):** No by-product. *Examples: PE, PP, PVC, Teflon, Polystyrene.*
- **Condensation (Step-growth):** By-product eliminated. *Examples: Nylon-6,6, Terylene (PET), Bakelite.*

**(iv) Based on Thermal Behavior (2 marks):**
- **Thermoplastic:** Reversible softening; linear/branched; recyclable. *Examples: PE, PP, Nylon, PS.*
- **Thermosetting:** Irreversible hardening; crosslinked network; not remeltable. *Examples: Bakelite, Epoxy, Melamine, Urea-formaldehyde.*

**(b) Addition Polymerization (7 marks):**

**Mechanism (5 marks):**

**Step 1 — Initiation:**
A free radical initiator (e.g., benzoyl peroxide) decomposes to form radicals, which attack the double bond of a vinyl monomer:

$$\text{Initiator} \longrightarrow \text{R}^{\bullet}$$

$$\text{R}^{\bullet} + \text{CH}_2\text{=CHX} \longrightarrow \text{R-CH}_2\text{-}\overset{\bullet}{\text{C}}\text{HX}$$

**Step 2 — Propagation:**
The active chain radical adds monomer units sequentially:

$$\text{R-CH}_2\text{-}\overset{\bullet}{\text{C}}\text{HX} + \text{CH}_2\text{=CHX} \longrightarrow \text{R-CH}_2\text{-CHX-CH}_2\text{-}\overset{\bullet}{\text{C}}\text{HX}$$

**Step 3 — Termination:**
Two growing chains combine (coupling) or disproportionate:

$$\text{Coupling: } \text{M}_n^{\bullet} + \text{M}_m^{\bullet} \longrightarrow \text{M}_{n+m}$$

$$\text{Disproportionation: } \text{M}_n^{\bullet} + \text{M}_m^{\bullet} \longrightarrow \text{M}_n + \text{M}_m$$

**Derivation of DP (2 marks):**

For a polymer with number-average molecular weight $\bar{M}_n$ and monomer molecular weight $M_0$:

$$\bar{M}_n = n \cdot M_0$$

Rearranging:

$$\boxed{\text{DP} = n = \frac{\bar{M}_n}{M_0}}$$

**Numerical example:** For PVC with $\bar{M}_n = 140{,}000$ g/mol and $M_0 = 62.5$ g/mol, $n = 2240$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Internal Choice Alternative)*

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Module 2]`

**(a)** Explain the **classification of polymers based on tacticity** with neat diagrams. Discuss how tacticity influences crystallinity and mechanical properties. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **classification of polymers based on intermolecular forces** (Billmeyer's classification). Compare the properties of elastomers, fibers, thermoplastics, and thermosets. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Tacticity Classification (7 marks):**

When a vinyl monomer $\text{CH}_2\text{=CHX}$ polymerizes, the substituent X can be arranged in three stereochemical configurations:

1. **Isotactic (2 marks):** All X groups on the **same side** of the zigzag chain. → High regularity → high crystallinity → high tensile strength, high melting point. *Example: Isotactic polypropylene (iPP).*

2. **Syndiotactic (2 marks):** X groups **alternately** placed on opposite sides. → Regular → semi-crystalline. *Example: Syndiotactic polypropylene (sPP).*

3. **Atactic (2 marks):** X groups **randomly** distributed. → Irregular → amorphous, soft, low strength. *Example: Atactic polypropylene (aPP) is rubbery; atactic PS is glassy.**

**Influence on properties (1 mark):**
- Isotactic > Syndiotactic > Atactic in crystallinity, density, rigidity, and melting point.

**(b) Billmeyer's Classification (7 marks):**

Classification based on the **magnitude of intermolecular forces**:

| **Class** | **Force Magnitude** | **Chain Feature** | **Example** | **Property** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Elastomers** (2 marks) | Weakest | Linear chains + few crosslinks | Natural rubber, Neoprene | Elastic, reversible stretching |
| **Fibers** (2 marks) | Strongest (H-bonds/dipoles) | Linear, highly oriented, polar | Nylon-6,6, Terylene | High tensile strength, low elongation |
| **Thermoplastics** (1.5 marks) | Intermediate | Linear/branched, no crosslinks | PE, PP, PVC, PS | Soften on heat, recyclable |
| **Thermosets** (1.5 marks) | Covalent crosslinks | 3D network | Bakelite, Epoxy, Melamine | Rigid, infusible, heat-resistant |

**Comparison Summary:**
- **Elastomers ↔ Thermosets:** Both have crosslinks, but elastomers have sparse crosslinks (allowing chain mobility) while thermosets have dense networks (preventing motion).
- **Fibers ↔ Thermoplastics:** Both linear, but fibers have strong dipole/H-bonding, making them strong and crystalline; thermoplastics have weaker secondary bonding.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mark-Loss Pitfalls — Polymer Classification:**
> 1. **Mixing up classifications:** A polymer can belong to multiple classes simultaneously (e.g., Nylon is synthetic + condensation + linear + thermoplastic + fiber). Examiners deduct marks if you treat these as mutually exclusive.
> 2. **Forgetting by-products in condensation:** Many students write "condensation polymerization has by-products" but **fail to name the by-product** (H₂O, HCl, NH₃) — losing 1 mark.
> 3. **Confusing thermoplastics with thermosets:** A common error is stating thermosets "soften on heating" — this is **false**. Thermosetting polymers **decompose or char** on excessive heating; they do not melt.
> 4. **Ignoring tacticity in exam answers:** When asked about polypropylene properties, students must specify **which type** (iso, syndio, atactic) — atactic PP is a sticky gum, isotactic PP is a hard plastic.
> 5. **No by-product in addition polymerization:** Students often incorrectly claim "small molecules are released" in addition polymerization. This is **wrong** — addition polymerizations conserve all atoms.
> 6. **Confusing "degree of polymerization" with "molecular weight":** DP is **dimensionless**; molecular weight has units of g/mol. Always use $\text{DP} = \bar{M}_n / M_0$.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Basic Classification of Polymers**

- **Definition:** Polymer = many monomers; $n$ = degree of polymerization.
- **Seven Classification Criteria:** Source, Structure, Polymerization, Thermal, Tacticity, Application, Intermolecular Forces.
- **Origin:** Natural (cellulose), Semi-synthetic (cellulose acetate), Synthetic (PE).
- **Structure:** Linear (HDPE), Branched (LDPE), Cross-linked (Bakelite), Network (Epoxy).
- **Polymerization:** Addition (no by-product; PE, PVC) vs. Condensation (H₂O/HCl/NH₃; Nylon, Terylene, Bakelite).
- **Thermal:** Thermoplastic (reversible; PE, PP, PS) vs. Thermosetting (irreversible; Bakelite, Melamine).
- **Tacticity:** Isotactic (high crystallinity) > Syndiotactic (semi) > Atactic (amorphous).
- **Application Classes:** Fibers (Nylon), Elastomers (Natural rubber), Plastics (PE), Resins (Epoxy).
- **Billmeyer's Force Order:** Thermosets (covalent) > Fibers (H-bonds) > Thermoplastics (dipole/VdW) > Elastomers (weakest + crosslinks).
- **Key Formulae:**
  - $\text{DP} = \bar{M}_n / M_0$
  - $\text{PDI} = \bar{M}_w / \bar{M}_n \geq 1$
  - $\bar{M}_n = \sum N_i M_i / \sum N_i$
- **Critical Distinctions for Exam:**
  - Addition → no by-product.
  - Condensation → small-molecule by-product.
  - Thermoplastic → recyclable.
  - Thermosetting → non-recyclable, rigid.
  - Cross-linked = thermoset (almost always).
- **Common Examples to Memorize:** PE, PP, PVC, PS, Nylon-6,6, Terylene (PET), Bakelite, Melamine, Epoxy, Natural rubber, Cellulose.
- **Avoid "I don't know" answers** — always classify by **multiple criteria** for full marks in 14-mark questions.

> **End of Module 2 — Basic Classification of Polymers | KTU PESFT637 2024 Scheme**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Synthesis of Polyethylene
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT637 - CHEMICAL TECHNOLOGY AND REACTION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Synthesis of Polyethylene

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of Polyethylene (PE)

**Polyethylene (PE)** is a thermoplastic polymer synthesized by the addition polymerization (chain-growth polymerization) of the ethylene monomer (ethene, $C_2H_4$, $CH_2=CH_2$). It is the most widely produced commodity plastic in the world, characterized by a long saturated hydrocarbon backbone of repeating $-CH_2-CH_2-$ units.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The word "polyethylene" strictly refers to polymers built from the ethylene repeat unit. Any modification using co-monomers (1-butene, 1-hexene) produces **copolymers** such as LLDPE, which is a frequent KTU exam distinction.

The general polymerization reaction is:

$$n \, CH_2=CH_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Catalyst / Initiator}} (-CH_2-CH_2-)_n$$

Where **n** is the degree of polymerization (DP), typically ranging from $10^3$ to $10^5$ in commercial grades.

## 1.2 Major Industrial Grades of Polyethylene

The properties and applications of PE are dictated by its molecular architecture, which is controlled entirely by the synthesis route.

| Grade | Abbreviation | Density (g/cm³) | Synthesis Route | Key Feature |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Low-Density Polyethylene | LDPE | 0.910 – 0.925 | Free-Radical Polymerization | Long & short chain branches |
| Linear Low-Density Polyethylene | LLDPE | 0.918 – 0.940 | Ziegler-Natta / Metallocene + co-monomer | Linear with short branches |
| High-Density Polyethylene | HDPE | 0.941 – 0.970 | Ziegler-Natta / Phillips / Metallocene | Highly linear, minimal branching |
| Ultra-High Molecular Weight PE | UHMWPE | 0.930 – 0.935 | Ziegler-Natta | Molecular weight 3–6 million g/mol |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Lego Brick Factory"**
> Imagine ethylene molecules as identical, two-stud Lego bricks. The synthesis of polyethylene is like snapping these bricks into a single long chain. However, *how* and *where* you snap them decides the final product:
> - If you snap them randomly under high pressure using a "free-radical hand," the bricks occasionally grow side branches — this is **LDPE**, soft and flexible.
> - If you snap them in an orderly line on a metallic "Ziegler-Natta assembly table" (catalyst surface), you get straight, tightly packed chains — this is **HDPE**, rigid and tough.
> - If you add a few "different colored bricks" (1-butene or 1-hexene co-monomers), the chains stay linear but with controlled short branches — this is **LLDPE**.

The catalyst/initiator, pressure, and temperature effectively act as the "machine settings" that determine whether the final polymer chains are straight, branched, dense, or fluffy.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in PE Characterization

- **Density (ρ):** measured in **g/cm³** (0.91 – 0.97 range)
- **Melt Flow Index (MFI/MFR):** measured in **g/10 min**
- **Degree of Polymerization (DP):** dimensionless number
- **Number Average Molecular Weight ($\overline{M_n}$)** and **Weight Average Molecular Weight ($\overline{M_w}$)**: in **g/mol**
- **Polydispersity Index (PDI = $\overline{M_w} / \overline{M_n}$):** dimensionless

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition Recall Box — "Free Radical vs. Coordination Polymerization"**
> - **Free Radical Polymerization:** Proceeds via free radical intermediates; used for LDPE. High pressure (1000–3000 atm) and high temperature (80–300 °C) required.
> - **Coordination Polymerization:** Proceeds via transition metal catalysts (Ziegler-Natta, Phillips, Metallocene); used for HDPE/LLDPE. Low pressure (1–40 atm) and low temperature (50–100 °C) required.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Polymerization Mechanisms for Polyethylene

The synthesis of polyethylene is fundamentally a **chain-growth (addition) polymerization**. The synthesis pathways are classified by the active center that propagates the chain:

### A. Free-Radical Polymerization (FRP) — LDPE Route

The active center is an **unpaired electron (free radical)**. Initiators such as **di-tert-butyl peroxide**, **benzoyl peroxide**, or **oxygen** decompose thermally to generate radicals.

**Step 1 — Initiation**

$$I \xrightarrow{k_d} 2 \, R^{\bullet}$$

$$R^{\bullet} + CH_2=CH_2 \xrightarrow{k_i} R-CH_2-CH_2^{\bullet}$$

**Step 2 — Propagation**

$$R-(CH_2-CH_2)_n^{\bullet} + CH_2=CH_2 \xrightarrow{k_p} R-(CH_2-CH_2)_{n+1}^{\bullet}$$

**Step 3 — Termination (by Combination)**

$$R-(CH_2-CH_2)_n^{\bullet} + \,^{\bullet}(CH_2-CH_2)_m-R \xrightarrow{k_{tc}} R-(CH_2-CH_2)_{n+m}-R$$

**Step 3' — Termination (by Disproportionation)**

$$R-(CH_2-CH_2)_n^{\bullet} + \,^{\bullet}(CH_2-CH_2)_m-R \xrightarrow{k_{td}} R-(CH_2-CH_2)_n-H \; + \; R-(CH_2-CH_2)_{m-1}-CH=CH_2$$

**Step 4 — Chain Transfer (to monomer, polymer, solvent, or transfer agent)**

$$R-(CH_2-CH_2)_n^{\bullet} + CH_2=CH_2 \xrightarrow{k_{tr,M}} R-(CH_2-CH_2)_n-H \; + \; CH_2=CH^{\bullet}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Significance of Chain Transfer:** The "backbiting" chain transfer to polymer in LDPE synthesis is what generates the characteristic **long chain branches (LCB)**, which lowers the density and crystallinity. The 1,3- and 1,5-intramolecular hydrogen transfers in FRP of ethylene are a classic KTU short-answer question.

### B. Coordination (Insertion) Polymerization — HDPE / LLDPE Route

The active center is a **carbon-metal bond** at the surface of a transition metal catalyst. The mechanism is called the **Cossee–Arlman mechanism** and uses heterogeneous Ziegler-Natta catalysts (e.g., $TiCl_3$ or $TiCl_4$ supported on $MgCl_2$) with a co-catalyst (organoaluminum compound such as $Al(C_2H_5)_3$).

**Catalyst Activation Step:**

$$TiCl_4 + Al(C_2H_5)_3 \rightarrow [TiCl_3 \cdot \text{active site}] + Al(C_2H_5)_2Cl + C_2H_5^{\bullet}$$

**Propagation by Migratory Insertion (Cossee-Arlman):**

1. Ethylene monomer **π-coordinates** to the vacant site of the transition metal (Ti).
2. The growing polymer chain **migrates** to the coordinated ethylene.
3. A new vacant coordination site is generated for the next monomer.

The chain growth occurs via a **cis-insertion** of the C=C double bond into the M–C bond.

### C. Phillips Catalyst System

A special case of coordination polymerization. Catalyst: **$CrO_3$ supported on $SiO_2-Al_2O_3$**, no co-catalyst required. Used in slurry or gas-phase process for HDPE. Operates at 80–110 °C, 30–40 atm. The mechanism is debated but believed to involve a $Cr(II)$–alkyl active species.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units | Applicable To |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R_p = k_p [M] [M^{\bullet}]$ | Rate of polymerization | mol/(L·s) | FRP (LDPE) |
| 2 | $R_i = 2 f k_d [I]$ | Rate of initiation | mol/(L·s) | FRP |
| 3 | $R_t = 2 k_t [M^{\bullet}]^2$ | Rate of termination | mol/(L·s) | FRP |
| 4 | $k_d = A_d \exp(-E_d / RT)$ | Arrhenius for initiator | s⁻¹ | FRP |
| 5 | $\overline{X_n} = \dfrac{R_p}{R_i + R_{tr,M}} = \dfrac{1}{C_M}$ | Number-average DP (FRP) | dimensionless | LDPE |
| 6 | $C_M = k_{tr,M} / k_p$ | Chain transfer constant to monomer | dimensionless | LDPE |
| 7 | $C_S = k_{tr,S} [S] / (k_p [M])$ | Chain transfer constant to solvent | dimensionless | LDPE |
| 8 | $\overline{X_n} = \dfrac{1}{(1-p)}$ (ideal case) | DP in living polymerization | dimensionless | Ziegler-Natta |
| 9 | $R_p = k_p [C^{\ast}] [M]$ | Rate of polymerization (coordination) | mol/(L·s) | HDPE |
| 10 | $\overline{M_n} = 28 \cdot \overline{X_n}$ | Molar mass (PE repeat unit = 28 g/mol) | g/mol | All PE |
| 11 | $PDI = \overline{M_w} / \overline{M_n}$ | Polydispersity Index | dimensionless | All PE |
| 12 | $MFI = \dfrac{K}{\eta}$ (approx.) | Inverse relation of viscosity | g/10 min | All PE |
| 13 | $C_{tr} = k_{tr}/k_p$ | General chain transfer constant | dimensionless | All PE |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units Reminder:** Repeat unit of polyethylene is $-CH_2-CH_2-$ with molar mass $2(12) + 4(1) = \mathbf{28 \; g/mol}$. This is a critical conversion factor for all KTU numerical problems on PE.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Tubular Reactors (LDPE):** Continuous stirred or plug-flow tubular reactors operating at 1000–3000 atm and 130–300 °C. Used in ICI, BASF Lupolen process.
- **Autoclave Reactors (LDPE):** Stirred high-pressure autoclaves. The stirred-tank allows better mixing of initiator streams and produces LDPE with different MWD characteristics.
- **Slurry Process (HDPE, Phillips/Hoechst):** Catalyst suspended in isobutane or hexane diluent. Polymer precipitates as slurry.
- **Gas-Phase Fluidized Bed (HDPE, Union Carbide UNIPOL):** Catalyst particles fluidized by ethylene gas. No solvent required — environmentally preferred.
- **Solution Process (HDPE, DuPont):** Polymer dissolves in hot cyclohexane; product recovered by cooling/flash.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Industry Insight — Why HDPE is "greener":** Ziegler-Natta/Phillips processes operate at low pressures (1–40 atm), unlike LDPE (1000–3000 atm). This drastically reduces compressor cost and energy — a frequently asked 3-mark question in KTU ESE.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Steady-State Rate of Polymerization (FRP — LDPE)

The "Steady-State Approximation" assumes the radical concentration is constant ($d[M^{\bullet}]/dt \approx 0$) shortly after initiation begins. This is the foundation of FRP kinetics and is **a guaranteed 7-mark KTU problem**.

**Step 1 — Apply SSA to propagating radicals:**

$$\frac{d[M^{\bullet}]}{dt} = R_i - R_t = 0$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the rate expressions:**

$$2 f k_d [I] - 2 k_t [M^{\bullet}]^2 = 0$$

This rearranges to:

$$[M^{\bullet}]^2 = \frac{f k_d [I]}{k_t}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for radical concentration:**

$$[M^{\bullet}] = \left( \frac{f k_d [I]}{k_t} \right)^{1/2}$$

**Step 4 — Write the rate of propagation (which equals rate of polymerization):**

$$R_p = k_p [M] [M^{\bullet}]$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $[M^{\bullet}]$ into $R_p$:**

$$\boxed{R_p = k_p [M] \left( \frac{f k_d [I]}{k_t} \right)^{1/2}}$$

This is the canonical "square-root dependence" equation of FRP, and forms the basis of all LDPE kinetics. KTU 14-mark problems often ask the student to:

1. Derive $R_p$ starting from SSA.
2. Show the order with respect to monomer (1) and initiator (1/2).
3. Compute the number-average degree of polymerization.

## 3.2 Derivation of $\overline{X_n}$ (Number-Average Degree of Polymerization) in FRP

**Step 1 — Define $\overline{X_n}$ as rate of chain growth divided by rate of chain termination + chain transfer:**

$$\overline{X_n} = \frac{R_p}{R_t + R_{tr,M} + R_{tr,S} + \cdots}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the rate expressions:**

$$\overline{X_n} = \frac{k_p [M] [M^{\bullet}]}{2 k_t [M^{\bullet}]^2 + k_{tr,M} [M] [M^{\bullet}] + k_{tr,S} [S] [M^{\bullet}] + \cdots}$$

**Step 3 — Factor out $[M^{\bullet}]$ from denominator:**

$$\overline{X_n} = \frac{k_p [M]}{[M^{\bullet}] \left( 2 k_t + k_{tr,M} [M] + k_{tr,S} [S] + \cdots \right)}$$

**Step 4 — In the limit where chain transfer is negligible, substitute $[M^{\bullet}] = (f k_d [I] / k_t)^{1/2}$:**

$$\overline{X_n} = \frac{k_p [M]}{2 (f k_d [I] k_t)^{1/2}}$$

**Step 5 — Invert to obtain the Mayo Equation (for systems with chain transfer):**

$$\frac{1}{\overline{X_n}} = \frac{1}{(\overline{X_n})_0} + C_M + C_S \frac{[S]}{[M]}$$

Where:
- $(\overline{X_n})_0$ = degree of polymerization in the absence of any chain transfer
- $C_M = k_{tr,M}/k_p$ = chain transfer constant to monomer
- $C_S = k_{tr,S}/k_p$ = chain transfer constant to solvent

> [!NOTE]
> **The Mayo Equation** is a centerpiece of free-radical polymerization kinetics. By plotting $1/\overline{X_n}$ vs. $[S]/[M]$, one obtains a straight line with slope $C_S$ and intercept $1/(\overline{X_n})_0 + C_M$ — this is a classic KTU numerical/plotting problem.

## 3.3 Derivation of $\overline{X_n}$ for Living Polymerization (Ziegler-Natta — HDPE)

In coordination polymerization, assuming no chain transfer and no termination, the number-average DP is given by:

**Step 1 — DP definition:**

$$\overline{X_n} = \frac{\text{Moles of monomer consumed}}{\text{Moles of polymer chains formed}} = \frac{[M]_0 - [M]}{[C^{\ast}]}$$

**Step 2 — If conversion is complete ($[M] \to 0$):**

$$\overline{X_n} = \frac{[M]_0}{[C^{\ast}]}$$

**Step 3 — In terms of molecular weight:**

$$\overline{M_n} = 28 \cdot \overline{X_n} = \frac{28 \, [M]_0}{[C^{\ast}]}$$

This shows a direct relationship between the catalyst concentration and molecular weight — a **higher catalyst loading gives lower $\overline{M_n}$** (more chains growing in parallel). This is the conceptual opposite of free-radical polymerization, where initiator concentration reduces $\overline{M_n}$ by terminating chains.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 14-Mark Pattern)

> **Problem:** A free-radical polymerization of ethylene is carried out at 130 °C with benzoyl peroxide as initiator. Given:
> - $k_d = 1.0 \times 10^{-5}$ s⁻¹
> - $f = 0.6$
> - $[I]_0 = 0.01$ mol/L
> - $k_t = 5.0 \times 10^7$ L/(mol·s)
> - $k_p = 4.0 \times 10^3$ L/(mol·s)
> - $[M] = 2.0$ mol/L (ethylene in solvent)
>
> **Calculate (a) the rate of polymerization $R_p$, (b) the rate of initiation $R_i$, and (c) the number-average degree of polymerization $\overline{X_n}$.**

**Solution (a) — $R_p$:**

**Step 1:** Compute $f k_d [I]$:

$$f k_d [I] = (0.6)(1.0 \times 10^{-5})(0.01) = 6.0 \times 10^{-8} \; \text{mol/(L·s)}$$

**Step 2:** Compute radical concentration:

$$[M^{\bullet}] = \left( \frac{6.0 \times 10^{-8}}{5.0 \times 10^7} \right)^{1/2} = (1.2 \times 10^{-15})^{1/2} = 3.46 \times 10^{-8} \; \text{mol/L}$$

**Step 3:** Compute $R_p$:

$$R_p = k_p [M] [M^{\bullet}] = (4.0 \times 10^3)(2.0)(3.46 \times 10^{-8}) = 2.77 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{mol/(L·s)}$$

$$\boxed{R_p = 2.77 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{mol/(L·s)}}$$

**Solution (b) — $R_i$:**

$$R_i = 2 f k_d [I] = 2(0.6)(1.0 \times 10^{-5})(0.01) = 1.2 \times 10^{-7} \; \text{mol/(L·s)}$$

$$\boxed{R_i = 1.2 \times 10^{-7} \; \text{mol/(L·s)}}$$

**Solution (c) — $\overline{X_n}$:**

$$\overline{X_n} = \frac{k_p [M]}{2 (f k_d [I] k_t)^{1/2}} = \frac{(4.0 \times 10^3)(2.0)}{2 \sqrt{(6.0 \times 10^{-8})(5.0 \times 10^7)}}$$

Compute the denominator:

$$2 \sqrt{3.0} = 2(1.732) = 3.464$$

$$\overline{X_n} = \frac{8000}{3.464} = 2309.5$$

$$\boxed{\overline{X_n} \approx 2310, \quad \overline{M_n} = 28 \times 2310 \approx 64{,}700 \; \text{g/mol}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** This is a typical "LDPE-like" molecular weight (around 60,000 g/mol), confirming that free-radical polymerization under these conditions gives a polymer in the commercial LDPE range.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Synthesis Routes

| Parameter | LDPE (FRP) | HDPE (Ziegler-Natta) | HDPE (Phillips) | LLDPE (ZN + co-monomer) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Catalyst/Initiator | Peroxides / O₂ | TiCl₄ + AlR₃ | CrO₃ / SiO₂-Al₂O₃ | TiCl₄ + AlR₃ + 1-butene/1-hexene |
| Pressure | 1000–3000 atm | 1–10 atm | 30–40 atm | 1–30 atm |
| Temperature | 130–300 °C | 50–100 °C | 80–110 °C | 60–100 °C |
| Reactor | Tubular / Autoclave | Slurry / Gas phase | Slurry loop | Gas phase / Solution |
| Branching | High (LCB + SCB) | Negligible | Negligible | Short chain only |
| Density (g/cm³) | 0.910–0.925 | 0.941–0.970 | 0.940–0.965 | 0.918–0.940 |
| $\overline{M_w}$ range | 10⁵ – 5×10⁵ | 5×10⁴ – 5×10⁵ | 5×10⁴ – 5×10⁵ | 5×10⁴ – 3×10⁵ |
| PDI | Broad (3–20) | Narrow–Moderate (2–10) | Broad (4–15) | Moderate (2–6) |
| Active center | Radical (C•) | Ti–C bond (heterogeneous) | Cr–C bond (heterogeneous) | Ti–C bond (heterogeneous) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Process Flow — Overall Polyethylene Synthesis Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    RawFeed["Ethylene Feed CH2=CH2"] --> PurityCheck["Purification Drying and O2 Removal"]
    PurityCheck --> RouteDecision{"Synthesis Route Selection"}

    RouteDecision --> FRPRoute["Free Radical Polymerization"]
    RouteDecision --> CoordRoute["Coordination Polymerization"]

    FRPRoute --> Initiator["Peroxide Initiator benzoyl peroxide"]
    Initiator --> HiPress["High Pressure 1000 to 3000 atm and 130 to 300 C"]
    HiPress --> LDPEOut["LDPE Product Density 0.910 to 0.925 g per cm3"]
    LDPEOut --> AppFilm["LDPE Applications Film and Packaging"]

    CoordRoute --> CatChoice{"Catalyst Type"}

    CatChoice --> ZNPath["Ziegler Natta TiCl4 plus Al triethyl"]
    ZNPath --> LowPressZN["Low Pressure 1 to 10 atm and 50 to 100 C"]
    LowPressZN --> HDPEOut1["HDPE Product Density 0.941 to 0.970 g per cm3"]
    HDPEOut1 --> AppPipe["HDPE Applications Pipes and Containers"]

    CatChoice --> PhillipsPath["Phillips CrO3 on SiO2 Al2O3"]
    PhillipsPath --> LowPressPhil["Slurry 30 to 40 atm and 80 to 110 C"]
    LowPressPhil --> HDPEOut2["HDPE Product Density 0.940 to 0.965 g per cm3"]
    HDPEOut2 --> AppPipe2["HDPE Applications Bottles and Drums"]

    CatChoice --> MetallocenePath["Metallocene Cp2ZrCl2 plus MAO"]
    MetallocenePath --> CoMono["Co monomer 1 hexene or 1 octene"]
    CoMono --> LLDPEOut["LLDPE Product Density 0.918 to 0.940 g per cm3"]
    LLDPEOut --> AppStretch["LLDPE Applications Stretch film and Liners"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Architecture — Free Radical Polymerization Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    StartFRP["Free Radical Polymerization for LDPE"] --> InitStep["Step 1 Initiation Initiator I decomposes"]

    InitStep --> Rad1["Two Free Radicals R dot generated"]
    Rad1 --> AddStep["R dot adds to Ethylene Monomer M"]
    AddStep --> Chain1["First Monomer Radical R CH2 CH2 dot formed"]

    Chain1 --> PropStep["Step 2 Propagation"]
    PropStep --> PropLoop["Repeat Addition of Ethylene M onto growing chain radical"]
    PropLoop --> ChainLong["Long Chain Radical R CH2 CH2 n dot"]

    ChainLong --> TermStep["Step 3 Termination"]
    TermStep --> TermChoice{"Termination Pathway"}

    TermChoice --> Comb["Combination Two radicals form one dead chain"]
    TermChoice --> Disprop["Disproportionation Two radicals yield one saturated and one unsaturated dead chain"]

    Comb --> DeadChain["Dead Polymer Chain"]
    Disprop --> DeadChain

    ChainLong --> TransferStep["Step 4 Chain Transfer"]
    TransferStep --> TransChoice{"Transfer to"}

    TransChoice --> TMono["Transfer to Monomer M"]
    TransChoice --> TSolv["Transfer to Solvent S"]
    TransChoice --> TPoly["Transfer to Polymer P backbiting causes long chain branching"]
    TransChoice --> TCTA["Transfer to Chain Transfer Agent CTA"]

    TMono --> NewRad["New radical initiates a new chain"]
    TSolv --> NewRad
    TPoly --> NewRad
    TCTA --> NewRad

    NewRad --> PropStep
    DeadChain --> Product1["LDPE Product Randomly Branched"]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Architecture — Ziegler-Natta Coordination Polymerization (Cossee-Arlman)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    StartZN["Ziegler Natta Polymerization for HDPE"] --> CatPrep["Catalyst Preparation TiCl3 surface"]

    CatPrep --> VacSite["Active Site has a Vacant Coordination Site"]
    VacSite --> CoordStep["Step 1 Pi Coordination of Ethylene Monomer to Ti"]

    CoordStep --> MigrateStep["Step 2 Migration of Growing Chain to coordinated Monomer"]
    MigrateStep --> InsertStep["Step 3 Cis Insertion of C=C into Ti C bond"]

    InsertStep --> NewVac["New Vacant Site generated on Ti"]
    NewVac --> CoordStep

    InsertStep --> ChainExtend["Polymer Chain Extended by Two Carbons"]
    ChainExtend --> Decision1{"Continue or Terminate"}

    Decision1 --> Continue["Insertion continues Living Polymerization"]
    Decision1 --> Termination["Beta Hydride Elimination or Chain Transfer to H2"]
    Termination --> DeadHDPE["Dead HDPE Chain Linear Minimal branching"]

    Continue --> HDPEOut["HDPE Product Linear and High Density"]
    DeadHDPE --> HDPEOut
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Reactor Architecture — Industrial LDPE Tubular Reactor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    FeedEth["Ethylene Compressor 1000 to 3000 atm"] --> Mixer1["Mixer with Initiator Injection Point 1"]
    Mixer1 --> PreHeater["Preheater 80 to 130 C"]
    PreHeater --> ReactorTube["Tubular Reactor Jacketed Length 0.5 to 2 km"]

    ReactorTube --> Inj2["Initiator Injection Point 2"]
    Inj2 --> ReactorTube2["Second Reaction Zone"]
    ReactorTube2 --> Inj3["Initiator Injection Point 3"]

    ReactorTube2 --> Cooler["Product Cooler 30 to 60 C"]
    Cooler --> Separator["High Pressure Separator Pressure Reduction"]
    Separator --> RecycleCompressor["Recycle Compressor Unreacted Ethylene"]
    RecycleCompressor --> FeedEth

    Separator --> Extruder["Extruder Granulator"]
    Extruder --> LDPEFinal["LDPE Pellets Final Product"]
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Reactor Architecture — HDPE Gas-Phase Fluidized Bed Reactor (UNIPOL Type)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    EthyleneIn["Ethylene Feed Gas 30 to 40 atm"] --> BottomDist["Plenum Distributor at Reactor Bottom"]
    ComonomerIn["Co monomer 1 butene or 1 hexene"] --> BottomDist
    NitrogenIn["Nitrogen Inert Gas"] --> BottomDist

    BottomDist --> FluidBed["Fluidized Bed of PE Particles 80 to 100 C"]

    CatalystIn["Ziegler Natta or Metallocene Catalyst Slurry Injected"] --> FluidBed

    FluidBed --> GrowthZone["Polymerization on Catalyst Particles Each Particle becomes a Microreactor"]
    GrowthZone --> BedExpansion["Bed Expansion due to Polymer Growth"]
    BedExpansion --> DischargeLine["Discharge Line to Product Tank"]

    DischargeLine --> Degasser["Degasser Residual Monomer Removal"]
    Degasser --> Pelletizer["Pelletizer and Additive Addition"]
    Pelletizer --> HDPEFinal["HDPE Pellets Product"]

    FluidBed --> TopSection["Reactor Top Freeboard"]
    TopSection --> Cyclone["Cyclone Fines Recovery"]
    Cyclone --> RecycleLine["Recycle to Fluid Bed"]
    RecycleLine --> FluidBed

    FluidBed --> CoolJacket["External Cooling Jacket Removes Reaction Heat exothermic"]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
