> *(Section 5 will continue in the next block to comply with the strict anchor formatting rule.)*

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
> **Differentiate between free-radical and coordination polymerization of ethylene with respect to (a) catalyst, (b) pressure, and (c) product branching.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Free Radical (LDPE) | Coordination (HDPE) |
|---|---|---|
| Catalyst/Initiator | Peroxides, O₂, AIBN | Ziegler-Natta (TiCl₄ + AlR₃) or Phillips (Cr/SiO₂) |
| Pressure | Very high (1000–3000 atm) | Low (1–40 atm) |
| Temperature | 130–300 °C | 50–110 °C |
| Active center | Carbon free radical (C•) | Metal–carbon bond (M–C) |
| Branching | Long chain branches + short chain branches | Negligible (linear chains) |
| Density | Low (0.910–0.925) | High (0.941–0.970) |
| Reactor | Tubular / autoclave | Slurry / gas-phase fluidized bed |

---

> **Question 2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
> **What is meant by "chain transfer" in free-radical polymerization? How does it affect the molecular weight of polyethylene?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Chain transfer is a reaction in which an active propagating radical abstracts a hydrogen atom (or another group) from a molecule (monomer, polymer, solvent, or chain transfer agent), thereby **terminating the growing chain and starting a new one**.

**Effect on molecular weight:**
- Chain transfer **decreases the number-average molecular weight** ($\overline{M_n}$) because it shortens the average kinetic chain length.
- The Mayo equation $1/\overline{X_n} = 1/(\overline{X_n})_0 + C_M + C_S [S]/[M]$ quantifies this effect.
- In LDPE synthesis, intramolecular chain transfer (backbiting) is responsible for **short chain branching**, while intermolecular transfer to polymer produces **long chain branching** — both reduce crystallinity and density.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each)

> ### **Question A (Choice 1)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
>
> **(a) [7 marks]** With a neat mechanism, explain the **free-radical polymerization of ethylene** for the production of LDPE. Discuss the role of **chain transfer** in branching.
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** Derive the rate of polymerization $R_p$ and the number-average degree of polymerization $\overline{X_n}$ using the **steady-state approximation**. State the order of $R_p$ with respect to monomer and initiator.

### Model Solution (a) — Free-Radical Mechanism of LDPE

**Step 1 — Initiation**

The peroxide initiator (e.g., benzoyl peroxide) decomposes thermally:

$$(C_6H_5COO)_2 \xrightarrow{\Delta} 2 \, C_6H_5COO^{\bullet} \rightarrow 2 \, C_6H_5^{\bullet} + 2 \, CO_2$$

The resulting radical adds to ethylene:

$$C_6H_5^{\bullet} + CH_2=CH_2 \rightarrow C_6H_5-CH_2-CH_2^{\bullet}$$

**Step 2 — Propagation**

The chain radical repeatedly adds ethylene:

$$C_6H_5-(CH_2-CH_2)_n^{\bullet} + CH_2=CH_2 \xrightarrow{k_p} C_6H_5-(CH_2-CH_2)_{n+1}^{\bullet}$$

This proceeds hundreds to thousands of times per chain.

**Step 3 — Termination**

- **Combination** (dominant at high radical concentration):
$$P_n^{\bullet} + P_m^{\bullet} \rightarrow P_{n+m}$$
- **Disproportionation** (dominant at high temperature):
$$P_n^{\bullet} + P_m^{\bullet} \rightarrow P_n + P_m \;\; (\text{one saturated, one with terminal C=C})$$

**Step 4 — Chain Transfer and Branching**

- **Intermolecular transfer to polymer** (a "dead" PE chain donates a hydrogen):
$$\sim CH_2^{\bullet} + \sim CH_2-CH_2-CH_2 \sim \rightarrow \sim CH_3 + \sim CH_2-CH^{\bullet}-CH_2 \sim$$
The new radical on the mid-chain grows into a **long chain branch (LCB)**.
- **Intramolecular backbiting** (1,5-hydrogen transfer within the same chain):
Produces **short chain branches (butyl, ethyl groups)**.

**Role of chain transfer in LDPE branching:**
The frequency of chain transfer events (backbiting + polymer transfer) directly determines the **branch density** and thus the **density, crystallinity, and mechanical flexibility** of LDPE.

### Model Solution (b) — Steady-State Derivation of $R_p$ and $\overline{X_n}$

**Step 1 — Apply SSA to radical concentration:**

$$\frac{d[M^{\bullet}]}{dt} = R_i - R_t = 0$$

**Step 2 — Substitute rate expressions $R_i = 2 f k_d [I]$ and $R_t = 2 k_t [M^{\bullet}]^2$:**

$$2 f k_d [I] = 2 k_t [M^{\bullet}]^2$$

$$[M^{\bullet}] = \left( \frac{f k_d [I]}{k_t} \right)^{1/2}$$

**Step 3 — Write $R_p$:**

$$R_p = k_p [M] [M^{\bullet}] = k_p [M] \left( \frac{f k_d [I]}{k_t} \right)^{1/2}$$

**Step 4 — Order analysis:**
- Order w.r.t. monomer $[M]$: **1 (first order)**
- Order w.r.t. initiator $[I]$: **1/2 (half order)**

**Step 5 — Derive $\overline{X_n}$:**

$$\overline{X_n} = \frac{R_p}{R_t + \sum R_{tr}} = \frac{k_p [M]}{2 (f k_d [I] k_t)^{1/2} + k_{tr,M} [M] + \cdots}$$

In the absence of chain transfer:

$$\boxed{\overline{X_n} = \frac{k_p [M]}{2 (f k_d [I] k_t)^{1/2}}}$$

The **Mayo equation** generalizes this:

$$\frac{1}{\overline{X_n}} = \frac{1}{(\overline{X_n})_0} + C_M + C_S \frac{[S]}{[M]}$$

---

> ### **Question B (Choice 2)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`
>
> **(a) [7 marks]** Describe the **Ziegler-Natta catalyzed polymerization of ethylene** for HDPE production. Explain the **Cossee-Arlman mechanism** of chain growth.
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** Compare the **tubular reactor (LDPE)** and **gas-phase fluidized bed reactor (HDPE)** processes with respect to operating conditions, heat removal, conversion, and product properties.

### Model Solution (a) — Ziegler-Natta Polymerization & Cossee-Arlman Mechanism

**Catalyst System:**

- **Catalyst:** $TiCl_4$ (or $TiCl_3$) supported on $MgCl_2$
- **Co-catalyst:** Triethylaluminum, $Al(C_2H_5)_3$

**Activation:**

$$TiCl_4 + Al(C_2H_5)_3 \rightarrow TiCl_3 \cdot C_2H_5 + Al(C_2H_5)_2Cl$$

The active site is a **Ti–C bond** at the surface of the $TiCl_3$ crystal with a vacant octahedral coordination site.

**Cossee-Arlman Mechanism (Step by Step):**

**Step 1 — Coordination:** The ethylene $\pi$-bond coordinates to the vacant d-orbital of the Ti atom. This activates the C=C double bond.

**Step 2 — Migration (Insertion):** The growing polymer chain (alkyl group attached to Ti) **migrates** to the coordinated ethylene, forming a new Ti–C bond with the terminal carbon of ethylene.

**Step 3 — Vacancy Regeneration:** After insertion, a **new vacant coordination site** is exposed on Ti, allowing the next ethylene molecule to coordinate.

The chain therefore grows by **cis-insertion** of the C=C bond into the Ti–C bond, producing a **highly linear polyethylene chain** with virtually no branching.

**Why HDPE is linear:**
- The active site is **sterically constrained** by the $TiCl_3$ crystal lattice.
- No "backbiting" or chain transfer to polymer occurs (low pressure, low temperature).
- $\beta$-Hydride elimination is the main chain-termination mechanism.

**Key Features:**
- **Low pressure (1–10 atm) and low temperature (50–100 °C).**
- **Living polymerization** character → narrow PDI, controllable molecular weight.
- High catalyst productivity (g PE / g Ti = 10⁴ – 10⁵).

### Model Solution (b) — Comparison of LDPE Tubular vs. HDPE Gas-Phase Reactors

| Parameter | LDPE Tubular Reactor | HDPE Gas-Phase Fluidized Bed (UNIPOL) |
|---|---|---|
| Operating Pressure | 1000–3000 atm | 20–40 atm |
| Operating Temperature | 130–300 °C | 80–110 °C |
| Initiator/Catalyst | Peroxides (homogeneous) | Ziegler-Natta / Metallocene (heterogeneous) |
| Phase in Reactor | Supercritical ethylene + polymer melt | Solid PE particles fluidized by ethylene gas |
| Heat Removal | Cooling jackets + multiple initiator injection points | External heat exchangers in recycle gas loop |
| Conversion per Pass | 15–30% (recycle required) | 2–5% per pass (high recycle ratio) |
| Reactor Geometry | Long tube (0.5–2 km) jacketed | Vertical cylindrical vessel with fluidized bed |
| Residence Time | 30–90 seconds | 1–4 hours |
| MWD Control | Broad MWD (3–20) | Narrow–moderate MWD (2–6) |
| Product Form | Polymer melt extruded at end | Powder discharged from bed |
| Energy Intensity | Very high (compression cost) | Moderate |
| Environmental Impact | High (solvents, high pressure) | Low (no solvent, low pressure) |
| Branching Control | Difficult to control | Easily controlled by co-monomer feed |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark-Loss Traps in Polyethylene Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing the repeat unit molar mass:** Students often write 28 g/mol incorrectly as 14 g/mol (forgetting one $CH_2$ unit has mass 14, and the repeat unit is $-CH_2-CH_2-$ with mass 28).
> 2. **Mixing up $R_i$ and $R_p$ units:** $R_i$ is in mol/(L·s); always include the factor of 2 in $R_i = 2 f k_d [I]$.
> 3. **Forgetting the Cossee-Arlman step order:** The mechanism is **coordination → migration → insertion → vacant site regeneration**, not insertion-first.
> 4. **Not stating the active center:** In FRP it is the **free radical**; in Ziegler-Natta it is the **Ti–C bond**. Examiners specifically deduct marks if the active center is not identified.
> 5. **Forgetting "living" character of Ziegler-Natta:** PDI is narrow (2–6), unlike FRP (3–20). This distinction is a guaranteed 2-mark KTU question.
> 6. **Not mentioning backbiting for LDPE branching:** The 1,5-intramolecular hydrogen transfer is the most important origin of short chain branching in LDPE.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Synthesis of Polyethylene**

- ✅ Polyethylene is synthesized by **addition (chain-growth) polymerization** of ethylene ($CH_2=CH_2$).
- ✅ **Repeat unit** of PE: $-CH_2-CH_2-$ with **molar mass = 28 g/mol**.
- ✅ **LDPE:** Free-radical polymerization, **high pressure (1000–3000 atm)**, branched, density 0.910–0.925 g/cm³.
- ✅ **HDPE:** Ziegler-Natta / Phillips / Metallocene catalysis, **low pressure (1–40 atm)**, linear, density 0.941–0.970 g/cm³.
- ✅ **LLDPE:** Ziegler-Natta/Metallocene with **co-monomer (1-butene or 1-hexene)**, linear with short branches, density 0.918–0.940 g/cm³.
- ✅ Free-radical polymerization follows 4 steps: **Initiation → Propagation → Termination → Chain Transfer**.
- ✅ **Steady-state approximation (SSA)** is applied to radical concentration: $R_i = R_t$.
- ✅ **Canonical FRP rate equation:** $R_p = k_p [M] (f k_d [I] / k_t)^{1/2}$.
- ✅ **Order of $R_p$:** 1st order in monomer, **1/2 order in initiator**.
- ✅ **Mayo Equation:** $1/\overline{X_n} = 1/(\overline{X_n})_0 + C_M + C_S [S]/[M]$.
- ✅ **Chain transfer to polymer** → long chain branching (key to LDPE properties).
- ✅ **Backbiting (1,5-H transfer)** → short chain branching.
- ✅ **Ziegler-Natta active center:** Ti–C bond at surface of $TiCl_3$ crystal.
- ✅ **Cossee-Arlman mechanism:** Coordination → Migration → Insertion → Vacant site regeneration.
- ✅ **Ziegler-Natta is essentially a living polymerization** → narrow PDI.
- ✅ **Phillips catalyst:** $CrO_3$ on $SiO_2-Al_2O_3$, no co-catalyst, slurry process.
- ✅ **Metallocene catalysts:** $Cp_2ZrCl_2$ + MAO, excellent co-monomer incorporation control, used for LLDPE.
- ✅ **Industrial LDPE reactors:** Tubular (ICI, BASF) and Autoclave (Lupolen A).
- ✅ **Industrial HDPE reactors:** Slurry (Hoechst, Phillips), Gas-phase fluidized bed (UNIPOL), Solution (DuPont).
- ✅ **Heat removal is critical** — ethylene polymerization is highly exothermic (ΔH ≈ -95 kJ/mol).
- ✅ **Beta-hydride elimination** is the main chain-termination mechanism in Ziegler-Natta polymerization.
- ✅ For $\overline{M_n}$: $\overline{M_n} = 28 \cdot \overline{X_n}$ in all PE grades.
- ✅ KTU favorite: **Cossee-Arlman diagram + FRP mechanism** appear in nearly every ESE.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Synthetic Rubbers - SBR
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT637 - CHEMICAL TECHNOLOGY AND REACTION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Synthetic Rubbers - SBR

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Styrene-Butadiene Rubber (SBR)** is a general-purpose synthetic elastomer produced by the **copolymerization of styrene (vinylbenzene, $\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{CH}=\text{CH}_2$) and 1,3-butadiene ($\text{CH}_2=\text{CH}-\text{CH}=\text{CH}_2$)**. The resultant copolymer is a random/statistical amorphous polymer whose properties are dominated by the relative fraction of styrene units incorporated into the polybutadiene backbone.

$$(\text{-CH}_2\text{-CH=CH-CH}_2\text{-})_x \text{---} (\text{-CH}_2\text{-CH(C}_6\text{H}_5)\text{-})_y$$

where typically **$y/(x+y) \approx 0.23$** (i.e., **~23.5 wt% bound styrene**) in standard emulsion-grade SBR-1500.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** SBR is the highest-volume synthetic rubber in the world, accounting for approximately **45% of the global synthetic rubber market**. It is the classic "workhorse" tire rubber.

### 1.2 Intuitive Overview / Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are baking a cake. **Natural rubber (NR)** is like using only eggs (a single, natural ingredient) — it has amazing properties (high tensile strength, low heat buildup) but is non-uniform and weather-sensitive.

**SBR** is like a **custom-engineered cake mix**: you precisely combine two monomers (butadiene gives the soft, elastic backbone; styrene gives rigidity and abrasion resistance) in a controlled reactor. Just as a baker adjusts sugar-to-flour ratios to get a soft sponge versus a firm muffin, polymer chemists tune the **styrene-butadiene ratio** to make SBR suitable for tire treads (high styrene), sidewalls (medium), or shoe soles.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** SBR cannot replace natural rubber in every application. It is *complementary* — often blended 50:50 with NR in truck tires because SBR has superior abrasion resistance, while NR has superior tear strength and low rolling resistance.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

| Property | Standard Value |
|---|---|
| **Specific Gravity** | **0.94** |
| **Glass Transition Temperature ($T_g$)** | **$-50^\circ\text{C}$ to $-60^\circ\text{C}$** |
| **Mooney Viscosity (ML 1+4 @ $100^\circ\text{C}$)** | **45–60** (varies by grade) |
| **Service Temperature Range** | **$-40^\circ\text{C}$ to $+100^\circ\text{C}$** |
| **Bound Styrene Content** | **23.5% (standard)**; range 10–45% |
| **IUPAC Name** | Poly(butadiene-*co*-styrene) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> SBR is **always vulcanized** with sulfur before commercial use. Raw SBR is a thermoplastic, sticky mass; vulcanization introduces disulfide crosslinks ($\text{-S-S-}$) that convert it into a thermoset elastomer with elastic memory.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Polymerization Mechanism — Two Commercial Routes

SBR is industrially produced via **two fundamentally different free-radical polymerization mechanisms**. The choice of route dramatically influences molecular architecture.

#### Route A: Emulsion Polymerization (E-SBR)
This is the **dominant commercial process** (~80% of global SBR). It is a heterogeneous reaction in which monomers are emulsified in water using surfactants.

**Step-by-step mechanism:**

1. **Emulsification:** Butadiene (gas) and styrene (liquid) are dispersed in water with **sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS)** or **fatty acid soaps** as emulsifiers (typically 3–5 wt%).
2. **Initiation:** A water-soluble **redox initiator** is used:
   $$\text{S}_2\text{O}_8^{2-} + \text{Fe}^{2+} \longrightarrow \text{SO}_4^{-\bullet} + \text{SO}_4^{2-} + \text{Fe}^{3+}$$
3. **Propagation:** Radical enters micelle → adds monomers → chain grows in micelle.
4. **Termination:** Combination or disproportionation of two growing chains.
5. **Coagulation:** Latex is broken by addition of acid/salt → crumb is filtered, washed, dried.

**Two E-SBR sub-types:**
- **Hot SBR (SBR-1500 series):** Polymerized at **$\approx 50^\circ\text{C}$** — produces highly branched polymer with good processing.
- **Cold SBR (SBR-1502, 1503):** Polymerized at **$\approx 5^\circ\text{C}$** with redox initiators — produces linear polymer with narrower MWD, better abrasion resistance.

#### Route B: Solution Polymerization (S-SBR)
Used for "performance" tire treads. Polymerization occurs in a hydrocarbon solvent (e.g., cyclohexane) using **organolithium initiators** ($n$-butyllithium).

$$\text{C}_4\text{H}_9\text{Li} + \text{Monomer} \longrightarrow \text{C}_4\text{H}_9\text{-M}_n\text{-Li}$$

**Advantage:** Allows precise control of:
- Microstructure (cis/trans/vinyl content of butadiene units)
- Styrene sequence distribution (random vs. block)
- Molecular weight distribution (very narrow, $M_w/M_n < 1.1$)

### 2.2 Microstructure of Polybutadiene Segment

The 1,3-butadiene unit can incorporate in three ways:

| Configuration | Structure | % in Hot SBR | % in Cold SBR |
|---|---|---|---|
| **trans-1,4** | $-\text{CH}_2\text{-CH=CH-CH}_2-$ | ~60% | ~72% |
| **cis-1,4** | $-\text{CH}_2\text{-CH=CH-CH}_2-$ | ~14% | ~12% |
| **1,2-vinyl** | $-\text{CH}_2\text{-CH(CH=CH}_2\text{)-}$ | ~16% | ~16% |

The **vinyl content** is critical — higher vinyl content increases $T_g$ and improves wet-grip in tire treads.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains the high-yield formulas and reaction engineering concepts you MUST memorize for KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Examination) on this topic.

| Concept / Parameter | Formula / Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Bound Styrene (wt%) | $S_{wt}\% = \dfrac{104 \cdot n_s}{104 \cdot n_s + 54 \cdot n_b} \times 100$ | $n_s$ = mol styrene; $n_b$ = mol butadiene; MW(styrene)=104, MW(butadiene)=54 |
| Glass Transition (Fox Eq.) | $\dfrac{1}{T_g} = \dfrac{w_s}{T_{g,s}} + \dfrac{w_b}{T_{g,b}}$ | $w$ = weight fraction; $T_g$ in Kelvin |
| Rate of Emulsion Polym. | $R_p = \dfrac{k_p [M] [M^{\bullet}]_{micelle}}{N_A}$ | $[M^{\bullet}]$ = radical concentration in micelles |
| Smith-Ewart Case 2 | $\bar{n} = 0.5$ (average radicals per micelle) | Applicable when radical entry rate << growth rate |
| Mooney → Viscosity | $\text{ML} \propto M_v^{3.4}$ (power-law) | $M_v$ = viscosity-average MW |
| Crosslink Density | $\nu = \dfrac{\rho \cdot N_A}{2 \cdot M_c}$ | $\rho$ = density; $M_c$ = MW between crosslinks |
| Sulfur for Vulcanization | ~1.5–2.5 phr (parts per hundred rubber) | Plus 0.5–1.5 phr accelerator |
| Overall Reaction | $n\,\text{CH}_2\text{=CH-CH=CH}_2 + m\,\text{CH}_2\text{=CH-C}_6\text{H}_5 \longrightarrow \text{SBR}$ | $\Delta H \approx -100 \text{ kJ/mol (exothermic)}$ |

### 2.4 Engineering Utility

The SBR process is a textbook case for **reaction engineering pedagogy**:
- Demonstrates **heterogeneous kinetics** (micellar reaction in aqueous phase).
- Illustrates **heat removal challenges** — polymerization is strongly exothermic; reactor temperature control is critical (heat transfer coefficient $U$ for jacket must be $\geq 250 \text{ W/m}^2\cdot\text{K}$ for industrial jacketed reactors).
- Exemplifies **chain-growth kinetics** with Trommsdorff (gel) effect.
- Models **molecular weight distribution** by Mayo-Lewis copolymerization equation.

$$\dfrac{d[M_1]}{d[M_2]} = \dfrac{r_1 [M_1] + [M_2]}{[M_1] + r_2 [M_2]}$$

where $r_1, r_2$ are **reactivity ratios** for styrene ($r_1 = 0.4$–$0.6$) and butadiene ($r_2 = 1.4$–$1.8$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Since $r_1 \cdot r_2 \approx 1$, the SBR system tends toward **random (ideal) copolymerization**, not alternating or block. This is why SBR is amorphous and processable.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Effect of Styrene Content on $T_g$ (Fox Equation)

The **Fox equation** predicts the glass transition temperature of a random copolymer from the $T_g$ of its homopolymers.

**Starting principle:** Free volume additivity.

**Step 1:** Define weight fraction of styrene:
$$w_s = \dfrac{\text{mass of styrene in copolymer}}{\text{total mass of copolymer}}$$

**Step 2:** Define $T_{g,\text{PS}}$ (polystyrene) and $T_{g,\text{PB}}$ (polybutadiene):
- $T_{g,\text{PS}} = 100^\circ\text{C} = 373.15 \text{ K}$
- $T_{g,\text{PB}} = -90^\circ\text{C} = 183.15 \text{ K}$ (1,4-rich PB)

**Step 3:** Apply Fox equation:
$$\dfrac{1}{T_g} = \dfrac{w_s}{T_{g,\text{PS}}} + \dfrac{w_b}{T_{g,\text{PB}}}$$

**Step 4:** Work numerical example — calculate $T_g$ for SBR with $w_s = 0.235$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{1}{T_g} &= \dfrac{0.235}{373.15} + \dfrac{0.765}{183.15} \\
\dfrac{1}{T_g} &= 6.298 \times 10^{-4} + 4.177 \times 10^{-3} \\
\dfrac{1}{T_g} &= 4.807 \times 10^{-3} \\
T_g &= \dfrac{1}{4.807 \times 10^{-3}} \\
T_g &= 208.07 \text{ K} = -65.07^\circ\text{C}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5:** Interpretation — predicted $T_g \approx -65^\circ\text{C}$, which agrees well with experimental $-50^\circ\text{C}$ to $-60^\circ\text{C}$ for emulsion SBR-1500 (small deviation due to vinyl content and crosslinking not captured by simple Fox model).

### 3.2 Derivation: Monomer Feed Ratio for Target Bound Styrene

To produce SBR with **23.5 wt% bound styrene**, what feed ratio is required?

**Step 1:** Set up mass balance. Let feed contain $n_s$ mol styrene and $n_b$ mol butadiene. Assuming high conversion (~70%) and equal reactivity of monomer incorporation:

$$\text{Bound styrene wt\%} = \dfrac{104 \cdot n_s}{104 \cdot n_s + 54 \cdot n_b} \times 100 = 23.5$$

**Step 2:** Rearrange:
$$\begin{aligned}
104 \cdot n_s &= 0.235 (104 \cdot n_s + 54 \cdot n_b) \\
104 \cdot n_s &= 24.44 \cdot n_s + 12.69 \cdot n_b \\
79.56 \cdot n_s &= 12.69 \cdot n_b \\
\dfrac{n_s}{n_b} &= \dfrac{12.69}{79.56} = 0.1595
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Convert to mass ratio:
$$\dfrac{m_s}{m_b} = \dfrac{104 \cdot n_s}{54 \cdot n_b} = \dfrac{104 \times 0.1595}{54} = 0.307$$

**Step 4:** Conclusion — feed approximately **30.7 parts by mass styrene per 100 parts butadiene** to achieve 23.5% bound styrene in the final copolymer.

### 3.3 Kinetics of Emulsion Polymerization — Smith-Ewart Analysis

**Step 1:** Define radicals per micelle, $\bar{n}$, governed by three processes:
- Entry rate $\rho$ (radicals entering micelle per second)
- Exit rate $k$ (small radicals leaving)
- Termination rate (rapid when $\bar{n} \geq 2$)

**Step 2:** For typical SBR conditions, entry is slow relative to propagation, so Smith-Ewart Case 2 applies:

$$\bar{n} = 0.5$$

**Step 3:** Rate of polymerization becomes:

$$R_p = k_p [M] [M^{\bullet}] = k_p [M] \cdot \bar{n} \cdot \dfrac{N_{micelles}}{N_A}$$

**Step 4:** Substituting typical values $[M] = 5 \text{ mol/L}$, $\bar{n} = 0.5$, $N_{micelles}/N_A = 10^{20} \text{ L}^{-1}$:

$$R_p = 10^3 \cdot 5 \cdot 0.5 \cdot 10^{20} = 2.5 \times 10^{23} \text{ molecules/(L·s)}$$

This high rate explains why commercial E-SBR reactors operate with short residence times (~8–12 hours) and high conversions.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Hot vs. Cold vs. Solution SBR

| Property | Hot E-SBR | Cold E-SBR | Solution SBR |
|---|---|---|---|
| Polymerization $T$ | $50^\circ\text{C}$ | $5^\circ\text{C}$ | $50\text{–}100^\circ\text{C}$ |
| Initiator | Persulfate | Redox (Fe-SFS) | $n$-BuLi |
| MW Distribution | Broad | Narrow | Very narrow |
| Branching | High | Moderate | Linear |
| Processability | Excellent | Good | Difficult (cold flow) |
| Tire Tread Use | Limited | Yes (passenger) | Yes (premium "green tire") |
| Cost | Lowest | Moderate | Highest |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — Emulsion SBR Plant

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    feedA[Styrene Feed Tank]
    feedB[Butadiene Feed Tank]
    emulsify[Emulsifier + Water Mixer]
    reactor[Stirred CSTR Reactor Bank]
    init[Redox Initiator Dosing]
    heat[Heat Exchanger Jacket]
    latex[Latex Collection Drum]
    coag[Coagulation Acid Salt]
    wash[Wash Filters]
    dry[Hot Air Drier]
    bale[Baling Press]
    pkg[Packaging Unit]
    feedA --> emulsify
    feedB --> emulsify
    emulsify --> reactor
    init --> reactor
    heat --> reactor
    reactor --> latex
    latex --> coag
    coag --> wash
    wash --> dry
    dry --> bale
    bale --> pkg
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation Guide:** Each block represents a unit operation. Heat Exchanger is critical because polymerization is **strongly exothermic** ($-100 \text{ kJ/mol}$). Reactor is typically a **CSTR battery (8–12 reactors in series)** to ensure conversion ~70% with narrow residence time distribution.

### 4.2 Process Flow — Solution SBR (S-SBR)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    solventA[Cyclohexane Solvent Drum]
    solventB[Styrene Feed]
    solventC[Butadiene Feed]
    dryerA[Solvent Drier Molecular Sieve]
    mix[Inline Static Mixer]
    reactA[First Reactor Train 3x CSTR]
    reactB[Second Reactor Train for Block Sequence]
    coupling[Coupling Agent Addition SnCl4]
    steam[Steam Stripping Solvent Recovery]
    water[Water Wash Section]
    devap[Devolatilizer Extruder]
    pelletizer[Underwater Pelletizer]
    storage[Finished Product Silo]
    solventA --> dryerA
    dryerA --> mix
    solventB --> mix
    solventC --> mix
    mix --> reactA
    reactA --> reactB
    reactB --> coupling
    coupling --> steam
    steam --> water
    water --> devap
    devap --> pelletizer
    pelletizer --> storage
```

### 4.3 Microstructure Composition Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BUTADIENE_UNIT
        trans14["trans 1,4 addition ~72 percent"]
        cis14["cis 1,4 addition ~12 percent"]
        vinyl12["1,2 vinyl pendant ~16 percent"]
    end
    subgraph STYRENE_UNIT
        styreneBlock["Styrene side phenyl ring pendant"]
    end
    BUTADIENE_UNIT --> chain[Random Copolymer Backbone]
    STYRENE_UNIT --> chain
    chain --> property[Final SBR Properties]
    property --> tg[Tg negative sixty degree C]
    property --> abrasion[High abrasion resistance]
    property --> process[Good processability]
```

### 4.4 Vulcanization Crosslink Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    chainA["Polymer Chain A"]
    chainB["Polymer Chain B"]
    chainC["Polymer Chain C"]
    sulfur["Sulfur Crosslink Bridge S8 ring opens"]
    accelerator["Accelerator ZnO plus MBT"]
    chainA --> sulfur
    sulfur --> chainB
    chainB --> sulfur
    sulfur --> chainC
    accelerator --> sulfur
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Without vulcanization, raw SBR behaves like chewing gum (thermoplastic). The sulfur crosslinks convert it into a **thermoset elastomer** with elastic memory, allowing a tire to deform and recover millions of times.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]**

**Q1. Define SBR. Why is it called a "general-purpose" synthetic rubber?**

**Model Answer:**
SBR (Styrene-Butadiene Rubber) is a synthetic copolymer of styrene ($\approx 23.5\%$) and 1,3-butadiene ($\approx 76.5\%$), produced primarily by emulsion polymerization. It is termed "general-purpose" because of its versatility — it is used in passenger car tires, conveyor belts, shoe soles, gaskets, and adhesives, accounting for $\approx 45\%$ of world synthetic rubber consumption due to its balanced properties of abrasion resistance, aging stability, and low cost.

**Q2. Differentiate between hot SBR and cold SBR in three points.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Polymerization temperature:** Hot SBR at $50^\circ\text{C}$; Cold SBR at $5^\circ\text{C}$.
2. **Initiator:** Hot SBR uses thermal persulfate; Cold SBR uses redox (Fe-sodium formaldehyde sulfoxylate).
3. **Properties:** Cold SBR has narrower MWD, less branching, better abrasion resistance, and is preferred for tire treads.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model — Module 2, SBR]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With a neat flow diagram, describe the **emulsion polymerization process** for the manufacture of SBR-1500. Discuss the role of each ingredient. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **vulcanization of SBR**. How does the amount of sulfur and accelerator affect the properties of the final product? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**
The emulsion polymerization of SBR-1500 is carried out in a series of 8–12 jacketed CSTRs at $50^\circ\text{C}$. The process flow (see Section 4.1 diagram):

1. **Monomer preparation:** Styrene and butadiene are mixed in a ratio to give 23.5 wt% bound styrene.
2. **Emulsification:** Surfactant (sodium rosinate, 4.5 phr) is added with water at a monomer-to-water ratio of 1:2.
3. **Reaction:** Feed enters reactor train with potassium persulfate initiator (0.3 phr) and mercaptan chain transfer agent (tert-dodecyl mercaptan, 0.2 phr) to control MW.
4. **Latex recovery:** Conversion ~72% after 8–12 h; latex contains ~25% solids.
5. **Coagulation:** Acid (H$_2$SO$_4$) + salt (NaCl) breaks emulsion; crumb coagulates.
6. **Washing and drying:** Crumb is filtered, washed, dried in tunnel/belt dryer at $120^\circ\text{C}$.
7. **Baling:** Compressed into 25–35 kg bales.

**Role of each ingredient:**
- **Emulsifier:** Stabilizes micelles where polymerization occurs.
- **Initiator:** Generates free radicals; controls rate.
- **Chain transfer agent:** Regulates molecular weight.
- **Buffer (Na$_3$PO$_4$):** Maintains pH ~10.
- **Shortstop (DPPD):** Terminates reaction at desired conversion.

**Model Solution for (b):**
Vulcanization is the process of forming **three-dimensional crosslinks** between polymer chains using sulfur, converting thermoplastic raw SBR into a thermoset elastomer.

**Reaction mechanism (simplified):**
1. Accelerator (MBT, CBS) reacts with ZnO to form active zinc complex.
2. Complex opens S$_8$ ring → forms polysulfidic intermediate.
3. Polysulfidic species react with allylic H on SBR backbone.
4. Crosslink bridges ($\text{-S}_x\text{-}$, $x = 1$–$4$) form between chains.

**Effect of sulfur amount:**
- **Low sulfur (0.5 phr) + high accelerator:** Produces mono- and di-sulfidic crosslinks; excellent aging resistance; used in heat-resistant articles.
- **High sulfur (2.5 phr) + low accelerator:** Produces polysulfidic crosslinks; better initial tensile strength and fatigue life; standard for tires.

**Effect of accelerator:**
- Reduces vulcanization time from hours to minutes.
- Increases crosslink density.
- Improves scorch safety (delay before vulcanization begins).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Discuss the **microstructure of SBR**. How do the trans-1,4, cis-1,4, and 1,2-vinyl configurations of butadiene segments influence the polymer properties? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Using the **Fox equation**, calculate the glass transition temperature of SBR containing 30 wt% bound styrene. Given: $T_{g,\text{PS}} = 100^\circ\text{C}$, $T_{g,\text{PB}} = -73^\circ\text{C}$. Comment on the suitability of this SBR for low-temperature applications. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**
The polybutadiene segment of SBR contains three microstructural isomers:

1. **trans-1,4-polybutadiene:** Symmetrical, packs efficiently → semicrystalline tendency, higher $T_g$, harder rubber. In SBR, ~72% (cold) to ~60% (hot).
2. **cis-1,4-polybutadiene:** Asymmetric packing → low $T_g$, high elasticity. ~12–14% in SBR.
3. **1,2-vinyl polybutadiene:** Pendant vinyl group → steric hindrance, reduces crystallization, increases $T_g$. ~16% in SBR.

**Influence on properties:**
- Higher **trans** content → higher tensile strength, better abrasion resistance, but reduced low-temperature flexibility.
- Higher **vinyl** content → improved wet grip (used in "green tires"), higher hysteresis.
- Higher **cis** content → better low-temperature performance, higher resilience.

Solution SBR allows precise control of vinyl content (can exceed 50% in random S-SBR), making it the preferred material for high-performance tire treads.

**Model Solution for (b):**

Given: $w_s = 0.30$, $w_b = 0.70$, $T_{g,\text{PS}} = 373.15 \text{ K}$, $T_{g,\text{PB}} = 200.15 \text{ K}$.

Apply Fox equation:
$$\dfrac{1}{T_g} = \dfrac{w_s}{T_{g,\text{PS}}} + \dfrac{w_b}{T_{g,\text{PB}}}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{1}{T_g} &= \dfrac{0.30}{373.15} + \dfrac{0.70}{200.15} \\
\dfrac{1}{T_g} &= 8.04 \times 10^{-4} + 3.497 \times 10^{-3} \\
\dfrac{1}{T_g} &= 4.301 \times 10^{-3} \\
T_g &= \dfrac{1}{4.301 \times 10^{-3}} \\
T_g &= 232.5 \text{ K} = -40.7^\circ\text{C}
\end{aligned}$$

**Comment:** The calculated $T_g = -40.7^\circ\text{C}$ means this SBR grade will become glassy and brittle below $-40^\circ\text{C}$. It is **not suitable for arctic/cryogenic applications** but acceptable for temperate-climate tire use. Standard 23.5% styrene SBR ($T_g \approx -60^\circ\text{C}$) is better for low-temperature service. Increasing styrene content raises $T_g$ and sacrifices low-temperature flexibility.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> - **Do not confuse** "bound styrene" with "feed styrene" — they differ due to different reactivity ratios. Bound styrene is always lower than feed ratio.
> - **Always specify Mooney viscosity conditions** (e.g., ML 1+4 @ $100^\circ\text{C}$) — KTU examiners deduct 1 mark for vague answers.
> - **Free radical polymerization is chain-growth, not step-growth** — common error in 14-mark questions.
> - **Always include temperature in vulcanization description** ($140\text{–}160^\circ\text{C}$, $t_{90}$ time) — partial marks lost if omitted.
> - For Fox equation, **convert $^\circ\text{C}$ to Kelvin** before substituting — arithmetic errors cost 2 marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **SBR = Styrene (23.5%) + Butadiene (76.5%)** copolymer; random, amorphous.
- ✅ Produced mainly by **emulsion polymerization** (~80%); solution SBR is premium grade.
- ✅ **Cold SBR ($5^\circ\text{C}$, redox)** > Hot SBR ($50^\circ\text{C}$, persulfate) for tire treads.
- ✅ **Smith-Ewart Case 2:** $\bar{n} = 0.5$ for typical micellar kinetics.
- ✅ **Reactivity ratios:** $r_{\text{sty}} \approx 0.5$, $r_{\text{but}} \approx 1.5$ → near-random copolymer.
- ✅ **Three microstructures of butadiene:** trans-1,4 (~70%), cis-1,4 (~13%), 1,2-vinyl (~17%).
- ✅ **Glass transition:** use **Fox equation**; standard SBR $T_g \approx -60^\circ\text{C}$.
- ✅ **Vulcanization:** Sulfur (1.5–2.5 phr) + accelerator (ZnO + MBT/CBS) at $140\text{–}160^\circ\text{C}$.
- ✅ **S-SBR uses $n$-BuLi** initiator; allows anionic control of microstructure and narrow MWD.
- ✅ **Heat removal is critical:** $\Delta H_{rxn} \approx -100 \text{ kJ/mol}$, jacket $U \geq 250 \text{ W/m}^2\cdot\text{K}$.
- ✅ **World's largest SBR use:** passenger car tire treads (alone or NR-blended).
- ✅ **Not oil-resistant** — for oil resistance, use **NBR (acrylonitrile-butadiene)** instead.
- ✅ **Always state emulsion grade (e.g., SBR-1500)** in answers — KTU examiners expect this.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Plastic Recycling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT637 - CHEMICAL TECHNOLOGY AND REACTION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Plastic Recycling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plastic Recycling** is defined as the systematic process of recovering, reprocessing, and reusing plastic waste materials to produce new plastic products or chemical feedstocks, thereby reducing virgin polymer consumption, minimizing landfill accumulation, and decreasing environmental pollution.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for *Chemical Technology and Reaction Engineering*, plastic recycling is broadly classified into **four hierarchical categories** based on the physical and chemical transformations involved:

| Hierarchy Level | Recycling Type | Core Mechanism | End Product State |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Primary (1°)** | Re-extrusion | In-plant scrap re-introduction | Identical product |
| **Secondary (2°)** | Mechanical Recycling | Physical reprocessing | Lower-grade product |
| **Tertiary (3°)** | Chemical / Feedstock Recycling | Depolymerization / Pyrolysis | Monomers / Fuel |
| **Quaternary (4°)** | Energy Recovery | Controlled combustion | Thermal energy |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of plastic recycling like repairing a torn document:**
> - **Primary recycling** is like gluing the torn paper back into the *exact same document* (no quality loss).
> - **Secondary recycling** is like shredding the paper and re-pulping it into a *new, slightly rougher notebook* (small quality loss).
> - **Tertiary recycling** is like *chemically breaking the paper* back into wood pulp fibers to make fresh paper (molecular reset).
> - **Quaternary recycling** is like *burning the paper in a furnace* to generate heat for the building (energy only, no material returned).

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Resin Identification Codes (RIC)

Plastic items are stamped with a **Resin Identification Code (RIC)** triangle (chasing arrows with a number 1–7) to facilitate sorting at recycling facilities. The **density** and **chemical structure** dictate the recycling pathway.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Plastic Resin Codes:**
> - **1 – PET** (Polyethylene Terephthalate): Density ≈ **1.38 g/cm³**, used in beverage bottles.
> - **2 – HDPE** (High-Density Polyethylene): Density ≈ **0.95 g/cm³**, used in milk jugs.
> - **3 – PVC** (Polyvinyl Chloride): Density ≈ **1.40 g/cm³**, used in pipes.
> - **4 – LDPE** (Low-Density Polyethylene): Density ≈ **0.92 g/cm³**, used in plastic bags.
> - **5 – PP** (Polypropylene): Density ≈ **0.90 g/cm³**, used in bottle caps.
> - **6 – PS** (Polystyrene): Density ≈ **1.05 g/cm³**, used in foam cups.
> - **7 – Other** (Mixed / Multi-layer, e.g., ABS, Polycarbonate).

---

### 1.4 Sources of Plastic Waste

Plastic waste streams originate from four primary sources:

- **Municipal Solid Waste (MSW):** Post-consumer packaging, household containers.
- **Industrial Scrap:** In-process off-cuts, defective mouldings.
- **Automotive Waste:** End-of-life vehicle (ELV) bumpers, dashboards.
- **Marine Debris / Agricultural Mulch Films:** Severely contaminated streams requiring tertiary recycling.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Breakdown of Each Recycling Route

#### (A) Primary Recycling (Closed-Loop Re-extrusion)
1. **Collection of clean in-plant scrap** – uncontaminated, single-polymer.
2. **Grinding** into uniform flakes.
3. **Blending with virgin resin** (typical ratio 20–30 % scrap).
4. **Extrusion** through a melt filter (removes gels and contaminants).
5. **Pelletization** – output is *identical-grade* product.

#### (B) Secondary Recycling (Mechanical Reprocessing)
1. **Collection & Sorting** – manual, optical (NIR), or float-sink based on density.
2. **Washing** – caustic detergents (NaOH, surfactants) at **60–80 °C**.
3. **Shredding** – particle size reduction to **5–10 mm flakes**.
4. **Density Separation** – water float tanks exploit density differences.
5. **Melt Extrusion & Compounding** – addition of stabilizers, compatibilizers.
6. **Pelletizing** – output is *downcycled* product.

#### (C) Tertiary Recycling (Chemical Depolymerization)

This converts polymers back into **monomers or valuable hydrocarbons**.

- **Hydrolysis of PET:** PET reacts with water (acid/alkali catalyzed) → Terephthalic Acid (TPA) + Ethylene Glycol (EG).
- **Methanolysis:** PET + Methanol → Dimethyl Terephthalate (DMT) + EG.
- **Glycolysis:** PET + Ethylene Glycol → Bis(2-hydroxyethyl) Terephthalate (BHET).
- **Pyrolysis (thermal cracking at 400–700 °C in absence of O₂):** Mixed plastics → pyrolysis oil, gas, and char.

#### (D) Quaternary Recycling (Energy Recovery)
- Controlled incineration at **> 850 °C** to destroy dioxins.
- Heat recovered for electricity generation (calorific value of polyethylene ≈ **46 MJ/kg**, comparable to fuel oil).

---

### 2.2 The 'Why' Behind Each Step

> [!NOTE]
> - **Why sort?** Different polymers are **thermodynamically immiscible** in the melt – mixing PET with HDPE creates a brittle, delaminated blend with poor mechanical strength.
> - **Why wash?** Surface contaminants (food residue, oil) cause **hydrolysis** during melt processing, reducing molecular weight and creating gels.
> - **Why pyrolyze?** Mixed or contaminated plastics cannot be mechanically recycled economically; pyrolysis converts them into a **commodity-grade fuel oil** feed for steam crackers.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is exam-critical. Memorize every row.**

| Parameter | Formula / Expression | Units / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Mass yield of recycling | $\eta = \dfrac{m_{recycled}}{m_{input}} \times 100$ | $\eta$ in % |
| Pyrolysis oil yield | $Y_{oil} = \dfrac{m_{oil}}{m_{plastic,feed}}$ | Dimensionless |
| Hydrolysis equilibrium (PET) | $PET + H_2O \rightleftharpoons TPA + EG$ | Acid/alkali catalyzed |
| Calorific value (energy recovery) | $CV = \dfrac{Q_{released}}{m_{plastic}}$ | Typically **40–46 MJ/kg** for PE/PP |
| Density for sink-float sort | $\rho_{polymer}$ vs $\rho_{water} = 1.0 \, g/cm^3$ | PP & LDPE float; PET, PVC sink |
| Glass Transition (recycling threshold) | Process above $T_g$ but below $T_d$ | $T_d$ = thermal degradation onset ≈ **300 °C** |
| Melt Flow Index (degradation marker) | $MFI = \dfrac{m \, (g)}{10 \, min}$ under standard load | Increases with chain scission |
| Activation energy (PET pyrolysis) | $E_a \approx 150 - 200 \, kJ/mol$ | First-order kinetics |

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Circular Economy Compliance:** EPR (Extended Producer Responsibility) mandates force FMCG giants (Unilever, Coca-Cola) to incorporate ≥ **25 % r-PET** in bottles by 2030.
- **Reaction Engineering Linkage:** Pyrolysis reactors are modeled as **plug-flow reactors (PFR)** with first-order kinetics: $-r_A = k \cdot C_A$.
- **Process Intensification:** Microwave-assisted pyrolysis reduces reaction time by **>70 %** compared to conventional heating.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Analytical Problem (Model KTU Calculation)

**Problem Statement:**
A mechanical recycling plant processes **5000 kg/day** of mixed plastic waste. The stream composition by mass is:
- PET: 40 %
- HDPE: 30 %
- PP: 20 %
- Contaminants (food residue, multi-layer films): 10 %

After sorting (efficiency 95 %), washing (loss 2 %), and extrusion (loss 3 %), calculate the **mass of clean PET pellets** obtained per day.

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Calculate the mass of PET entering the plant.**

$$m_{PET,in} = 5000 \times 0.40 = 2000 \text{ kg/day}$$

*Logic:* 40 % of the total 5000 kg input stream is PET.

---

**Step 2: Account for sorting efficiency.**

The sorting step recovers **95 %** of PET into the PET-rich stream.

$$m_{PET,sorted} = 2000 \times 0.95 = 1900 \text{ kg/day}$$

*Logic:* 5 % of PET is lost to the reject stream (contaminants and mis-sorted fractions).

---

**Step 3: Account for washing losses.**

Washing removes surface contaminants but also physically washes away **2 %** of the polymer fines.

$$m_{PET,washed} = 1900 \times (1 - 0.02) = 1900 \times 0.98 = 1862 \text{ kg/day}$$

*Logic:* 2 % mass loss to wash-water effluent.

---

**Step 4: Account for extrusion losses.**

Extrusion melt-filtering and die drip losses account for **3 %**.

$$m_{PET,out} = 1862 \times (1 - 0.03) = 1862 \times 0.97 = 1806.14 \text{ kg/day}$$

*Logic:* 3 % rejected as gel, char, or filter cake.

---

**Step 5: Express the overall yield.**

$$\eta_{overall} = \dfrac{1806.14}{2000} \times 100 = 90.307 \text{ \%}$$

*Logic:* Combined yield = $0.95 \times 0.98 \times 0.97 = 0.9031$.

---

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{m_{PET,out} \approx 1806.14 \text{ kg/day}, \quad \eta_{overall} \approx 90.3 \text{ \%}}$$

---

### 3.3 Pyrolysis Kinetic Derivation (Reaction Engineering Linkage)

For the thermal cracking of polyethylene in a PFR:

$$\text{Pyrolysis: } C_nH_{2n} \xrightarrow{k} \text{Oil} + \text{Gas} + \text{Char}$$

Assuming **first-order kinetics**, the design equation gives:

$$\frac{dX}{d\tau} = k (1 - X)$$

Where $X$ is the fractional conversion and $\tau$ is the residence time.

Separating variables and integrating from $0$ to $X$ and $0$ to $\tau$:

$$\int_{0}^{X} \dfrac{dX}{(1-X)} = \int_{0}^{\tau} k \, d\tau$$

$$\begin{aligned}
-\ln(1 - X) &= k \tau \\
X &= 1 - e^{-k \tau}
\end{aligned}$$

Using the Arrhenius equation:

$$k = A \cdot \exp\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{RT}\right)$$

Substituting:

$$\begin{aligned}
X &= 1 - \exp\left[-A \cdot \exp\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{RT}\right) \cdot \tau \right] \\
\text{At } T &= 700 \text{ K}, \; E_a = 180 \text{ kJ/mol}, \; A = 10^{13} \text{ s}^{-1}, \; \tau = 30 \text{ s}: \\
k &= 10^{13} \cdot \exp\left(-\dfrac{180000}{8.314 \times 700}\right) \\
k &= 10^{13} \cdot \exp(-30.93) \\
k &= 10^{13} \times 3.71 \times 10^{-14} \\
k &= 0.371 \text{ s}^{-1} \\
X &= 1 - e^{-0.371 \times 30} \\
X &= 1 - e^{-11.13} \\
X &= 1 - 1.48 \times 10^{-5} \\
X &\approx 0.999985 \approx 100 \text{ \%}
\end{aligned}$$

*Interpretation:* At 700 K with a 30 s residence time, polyethylene is **fully pyrolyzed** – confirming that high temperature + sufficient residence time drives the reaction to completion.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | PET | HDPE | PP | Mixed Plastic |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Density (g/cm³) | 1.38 | 0.95 | 0.90 | Variable |
| Sink-Float Behaviour | Sinks | Floats | Floats | – |
| Best Recycling Route | Chemical (Glycolysis) | Mechanical | Mechanical | Pyrolysis |
| Reprocessing Temp (°C) | 260–280 | 180–220 | 200–240 | 400–700 (pyrolysis) |
| Degradation Resistance | Moderate | High | High | Low |
| r-Product Value | High | Medium-High | Medium | Fuel-grade |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Hierarchical Classification of Plastic Recycling (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Plastic Waste Stream]:::input
    A --> B{Sorted and Clean?}
    B -- Yes, In-plant --> C1[Primary Recycling Re-extrusion]:::p1
    B -- Post-consumer --> C2[Secondary Recycling Mechanical]:::p2
    B -- Mixed / Contaminated --> C3[Tertiary Recycling Chemical]:::p3
    C3 --> D1[Hydrolysis]
    C3 --> D2[Glycolysis]
    C3 --> D3[Methanolysis]
    C3 --> D4[Pyrolysis]
    B -- Non-recyclable --> C4[Quaternary Energy Recovery]:::p4

    C1 --> E1[Virgin-grade Pellets]
    C2 --> E2[Downcycled Products]
    D1 --> F1[Monomers TPA and EG]
    D2 --> F2[BHET Monomer]
    D3 --> F3[DMT Monomer]
    D4 --> F4[Pyrolysis Oil and Gas]
    C4 --> E3[Thermal Energy and Electricity]

    classDef input fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0277bd,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef p1 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#000
    classDef p2 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f9a825,color:#000
    classDef p3 fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,color:#000
    classDef p4 fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#c62828,color:#000
```

---

### 4.2 Mechanical Recycling Process Flow (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Collection of Plastic Waste]:::stage
    S2[Manual and NIR Sorting by RIC]:::stage
    S3[Density Separation Float Sink Tank]:::stage
    S4[Washing with Caustic Detergents]:::stage
    S5[Shredding into 5 to 10 mm Flakes]:::stage
    S6[Drying and Decontamination]:::stage
    S7[Melt Extrusion and Filtering]:::stage
    S8[Compounding with Additives]:::stage
    S9[Pelletization]:::stage
    S10[Final r-Pellet Storage]:::output

    S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 --> S6 --> S7 --> S8 --> S9 --> S10

    classDef stage fill:#e1f5fe,stroke:#01579b,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#b9f6ca,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
```

---

### 4.3 PET Chemical Recycling via Glycolysis (Reaction Network)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1[PET Polymer Chain]:::feed
    R2[Ethylene Glycol EG Excess]:::feed
    R3[Zinc Acetate Catalyst]:::feed
    P1[BHET Intermediate]:::product
    P2[Oligomers]:::byproduct
    P3[Recycled PET via Repolymerization]:::product

    R1 --> M1{Reactor 250 to 280 C with EG}
    R2 --> M1
    R3 --> M1
    M1 --> P1
    M1 --> P2
    P1 --> M2[Purification Distillation]
    M2 --> P3

    classDef feed fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000
    classDef product fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
    classDef byproduct fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Guide:**
> - **Blue blocks** = Inputs (feed streams).
> - **Green blocks** = Desired products.
> - **Red blocks** = Undesirable by-products.
> - **Diamond nodes** = Process units (reactors, separators).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Q1]**
> **Q1. Differentiate between primary and secondary recycling of plastics.**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> - **Primary recycling** involves re-introduction of **clean in-plant scrap** into the same manufacturing process (e.g., edge-trim from PET bottle production). It yields **virgin-grade** product with negligible quality loss.
> - **Secondary recycling** involves **post-consumer waste** that undergoes sorting, washing, shredding, and re-extrusion. The product is of **lower grade** (downcycled) due to polymer chain degradation and contamination.
> - Primary recycling is a **closed-loop** with no market exchange; secondary is **open-loop** with material entering the public waste stream.

> **[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024, Model Q2]**
> **Q2. What is pyrolysis? Mention any two advantages of plastic pyrolysis as a recycling method.**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> - **Pyrolysis** is the **thermal decomposition** of plastic waste at temperatures of **400–700 °C** in the **absence of oxygen**, yielding pyrolysis oil, non-condensable gases, and char.
> - **Advantage 1:** It can process **mixed, contaminated, and multi-layer plastics** which cannot be mechanically recycled.
> - **Advantage 2:** The pyrolysis oil can be used as a **feedstock for steam crackers** to produce new monomers, closing the carbon loop.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each – Internal Choice Modeled on KTU ESE Pattern)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Modeled Question]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**(a)** Explain with a neat flow diagram the **mechanical recycling of PET bottles**. Describe each unit operation in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A recycling facility receives **10 tonnes/day** of PET bottles. After sorting (90 % efficiency), washing (1.5 % loss), and extrusion (2.5 % loss), calculate the **daily output of clean PET flakes** and the **overall recovery percentage**. **(7 marks)**

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**(a)** Discuss **tertiary recycling of PET** with emphasis on **hydrolysis and methanolysis** pathways. Write the relevant reactions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain **feedstock recycling of mixed plastic waste via pyrolysis**. Discuss the products obtained and their industrial applications. **(7 marks)**

---

### 5.3 Complete Model Solutions for Question A

#### Solution for Q.A(a): Mechanical Recycling of PET Bottles

The mechanical recycling of PET bottles involves the following sequential unit operations:

1. **Collection:** Post-consumer PET bottles are collected via municipal bins, deposit-return schemes, or buy-back centers.

2. **Sorting (Manual + NIR):** Near-Infrared (NIR) spectroscopy identifies the **Resin Identification Code (RIC 1)** on each bottle. PVC contaminants are critically removed, as even **50 ppm** of PVC in PET melt causes yellowing and HCl-induced degradation.

3. **Baling and Shredding:** Bottals are compressed into bales for transport, then shredded into **8–12 mm flakes** using rotary granulators.

4. **Washing:** Hot caustic wash (**NaOH at 70 °C**) removes labels (polypropylene), adhesives, food residue, and sugar films.

5. **Density Separation (Float-Sink):** PET ($\rho = 1.38 \, g/cm^3$) **sinks** in water, while PP/PE caps (lighter) float and are skimmed off.

6. **Drying:** Centrifugal drying followed by hot-air fluidized bed drying reduces moisture to **< 50 ppm** to prevent hydrolytic degradation in the extruder.

7. **Melt Extrusion:** Dried flakes are melted at **260–280 °C** in a single-screw extruder with a **melt filter** (60–120 micron) to remove gels.

8. **Pelletization:** The molten polymer is forced through a die, cooled in a water bath, and cut into uniform pellets – the final **r-PET pellet**.

**Engineering Significance:** r-PET is used in **bottle-to-bottle** (food grade), polyester fiber (textile), and strapping applications.

---

#### Solution for Q.A(b): Numerical Calculation

**Given:**
- Input mass: $m_{in} = 10{,}000$ kg/day
- Sorting efficiency: 90 % → retention factor $= 0.90$
- Washing loss: 1.5 % → retention factor $= (1 - 0.015) = 0.985$
- Extrusion loss: 2.5 % → retention factor $= (1 - 0.025) = 0.975$

**Step 1: Mass of PET after sorting.**

$$m_1 = 10{,}000 \times 0.90 = 9000 \text{ kg/day}$$

**Step 2: Mass after washing.**

$$m_2 = 9000 \times 0.985 = 8865 \text{ kg/day}$$

**Step 3: Mass after extrusion.**

$$m_3 = 8865 \times 0.975 = 8643.375 \text{ kg/day}$$

**Step 4: Overall recovery percentage.**

$$\eta_{overall} = \dfrac{8643.375}{10{,}000} \times 100 = 86.43 \text{ \%}$$

**Verification via combined factor:**

$$\eta = 0.90 \times 0.985 \times 0.975 = 0.8643 = 86.43 \text{ \%} \quad \checkmark$$

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{m_{PET,out} = 8643.375 \text{ kg/day}, \quad \eta_{overall} = 86.43 \text{ \%}}$$

---

### 5.4 Complete Model Solutions for Question B (Alternative)

#### Solution for Q.B(a): Hydrolysis and Methanolysis of PET

**Hydrolysis Reaction:**

PET reacts with water in the presence of an acid or alkali catalyst to yield **Terephthalic Acid (TPA)** and **Ethylene Glycol (EG)**.

$$\left[ -OOC-C_6H_4-COO-CH_2-CH_2- \right]_n + nH_2O \xrightarrow{H^+/OH^-} nHOOC-C_6H_4-COOH + nHO-CH_2-CH_2-OH$$

**Mechanism:** Acid catalysis uses **H₂SO₄** at 150 °C; alkaline hydrolysis uses **NaOH** at 200–250 °C under pressure (1–4 MPa).

**Industrial Significance:** The recovered TPA is purified via crystallization and re-polymerized with EG to yield **virgin-grade PET** – a true closed-loop circular economy.

**Methanolysis Reaction:**

PET reacts with excess methanol to yield **Dimethyl Terephthalate (DMT)** and EG.

$$\left[ -OOC-C_6H_4-COO-CH_2-CH_2- \right]_n + 2nCH_3OH \xrightarrow{catalyst} nCH_3OOC-C_6H_4-COOCH_3 + nHO-CH_2-CH_2-OH$$

**Catalysts used:** Zinc acetate, manganese acetate, or cobalt salts at **180–280 °C** and 2–4 MPa.

**Industrial Significance:** DMT is used to manufacture **polyethylene terephthalate**, polybutylene terephthalate (PBT), and polyester films.

---

#### Solution for Q.B(b): Pyrolysis of Mixed Plastics

**Process Description:**
Mixed plastic waste (unsuitable for mechanical recycling) is fed into a **pyrolysis reactor** (typically a fluidized bed or rotating kiln) operating at **400–700 °C** in the absence of oxygen.

**Reaction (simplified):**

$$\text{Mixed Plastic} \xrightarrow{400-700°C, \; N_2 \; atmosphere} \text{Pyrolysis Oil} + \text{Gas} + \text{Char}$$

**Products and Their Applications:**

| Product | Yield (wt%) | Industrial Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Pyrolysis Oil** | 60–80 | Feedstock for steam crackers → new monomers |
| **Non-condensable Gas (C1–C4)** | 10–30 | Burned to supply reactor heat (energy integration) |
| **Char / Coke** | 5–10 | Carbon black, solid fuel, activated carbon |

**Advantages:**
- Handles **mixed and contaminated** plastic streams.
- Produces a **commodity fuel** that can replace crude oil in petrochemical crackers.
- Reduces landfill volume by **>90 %**.

**Disadvantages:**
- High capital cost and energy demand.
- Requires **stringent emission control** for HCl (from PVC) and dioxins.

---

### 5.5 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Lossing Pitfalls in Plastic Recycling Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to label units:** Always write kg/day, °C, MJ/kg, kJ/mol. Missing units = **0.5–1 mark deduction** per question.
> 2. **Confusing 'sorting efficiency' with 'yield':** Sorting efficiency refers to the fraction of *target polymer* captured; it is *not* the same as the overall plant yield. Multiply factors sequentially.
> 3. **Skipping the chemical equation in tertiary recycling questions:** Always write balanced reactions with conditions (T, P, catalyst). A reaction without conditions is **incomplete**.
> 4. **Writing only the advantages without disadvantages:** KTU examiners award marks for *critical evaluation*, not just listing pros.
> 5. **Mixing up RIC numbers:** PET = 1, HDPE = 2, PVC = 3, LDPE = 4, PP = 5, PS = 6. A single transposition = **2 marks lost**.

---

### 5.6 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**
> - ☐ **Four recycling tiers:** Primary (re-extrusion), Secondary (mechanical), Tertiary (chemical), Quaternary (energy).
> - ☐ **RIC codes 1–7** and the corresponding polymer, density, and typical use.
> - ☐ **PET hydrolysis** yields **TPA + EG**; methanolysis yields **DMT + EG**; glycolysis yields **BHET**.
> - ☐ **Pyrolysis** is **thermal decomposition at 400–700 °C without oxygen**, producing oil, gas, and char.
> - ☐ **Calorific value of PE/PP** ≈ **46 MJ/kg**; incineration must be **> 850 °C** to destroy dioxins.
> - ☐ **Density separation** exploits $\rho_{polymer}$ vs $\rho_{water} = 1.0 \, g/cm^3$.
> - ☐ **Mechanical recycling steps:** Collect → Sort → Wash → Shred → Dry → Extrude → Pelletize.
> - ☐ **PET processing temperature:** 260–280 °C; **HDPE:** 180–220 °C; **PP:** 200–240 °C.
> - ☐ **Mixed plastics** are unsuitable for mechanical recycling → must undergo **pyrolysis**.
> - ☐ **PET + PVC contamination** is the single biggest cause of downcycling failure.
> - ☐ **MFI increases** with each reprocessing cycle (chain scission → lower molecular weight).
> - ☐ **Glass transition $T_g$** must be exceeded for processing; **thermal decomposition $T_d$** must not be exceeded to prevent degradation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classification of Chemical Reactions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT637 - CHEMICAL TECHNOLOGY AND REACTION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Classification of Chemical Reactions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Chemical Reaction Engineering (CRE)**, the **Classification of Chemical Reactions** refers to the systematic categorization of chemical transformations based on multiple criteria such as the **number of phases involved**, the **stoichiometry of reactants and products**, the **molecularity and order of reaction**, the **presence or absence of catalysts**, the **thermal behavior**, and the **reaction mechanism**. This classification is a foundational step in Module 3 of the PESFT637 syllabus because the design of reactors (PFR, CSTR, Batch, Semi-Batch), the formulation of rate equations, and the selection of operating conditions all depend directly on the **class** to which a given reaction belongs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> Under the NEP 2020 Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework, students must be able to **identify, classify, and write appropriate rate expressions** for various types of reactions. This directly maps to **Course Outcome CO3**: *"Apply the principles of chemical kinetics to classify reactions and develop rate expressions."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Understanding

Imagine a large **railway station** (the reactor). Trains (molecules) arrive from different platforms (phases — solid, liquid, gas). Some trains simply pass through and leave from the other side (**irreversible reactions**), some turn back and forth between two stations (**reversible reactions**), some trains get split into multiple smaller trains (**parallel reactions**), and some trains wait in queue before they can depart (**series/consecutive reactions**). Just as a station manager must understand the *type* of traffic to schedule platforms, a chemical engineer must classify the *type* of reaction to design the right reactor.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Idea:** No single classification covers all engineering needs. Engineers often use **multiple classifications simultaneously** — for example, a reaction can be both *heterogeneous* AND *catalytic* AND *exothermic* at the same time.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Conventions

- **Rate of Reaction ($-r_A$)**: Expressed in **kmol/(m³·s)** or **mol/(L·s)** in SI-consistent units.
- **Conversion ($X$)**: A **dimensionless number** between 0 and 1.
- **Selectivity ($S$)**: Ratio of desired product to total products formed — **dimensionless**.
- **Yield ($Y$)**: Mass or moles of desired product per unit mass or mole of reactant fed — **dimensionless**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Universal Sign Convention (KTU Standard):**
> The rate of disappearance of reactant A is denoted as **$-r_A$** (a negative quantity), while the rate of formation of product P is denoted as **$+r_P$** (a positive quantity). This convention is strictly followed in KTU board valuation.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Comprehensive Classification Taxonomy

The classification of chemical reactions can be broken down into the following structured hierarchy. Each criterion addresses a different engineering question — from *how fast?* to *how much heat is released?*.

#### A. Classification Based on Number of Phases

This is the **most critical classification** for reactor design because it determines whether mass transfer resistances must be considered.

1. **Homogeneous Reactions**: All reactants and products exist in a **single phase**.
   - Gas-phase: e.g., $2NO + O_2 \rightarrow 2NO_2$
   - Liquid-phase: e.g., Ester hydrolysis in aqueous solution
   - Solid-phase: e.g., Decomposition of solid catalysts

2. **Heterogeneous Reactions**: Reactants exist in **two or more phases**.
   - Gas–Liquid: e.g., Absorption with reaction (CO₂ in NaOH)
   - Gas–Solid: e.g., Sulfide ore roasting
   - Liquid–Solid: e.g., Leaching of ores
   - Gas–Liquid–Solid: e.g., Catalytic hydrogenation in slurry reactors

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters:** For heterogeneous systems, the rate is governed by both **kinetics** AND **mass/heat transfer**. The observed rate is often limited by the **slowest step** (rate-determining step principle).

#### B. Classification Based on Stoichiometry and Pathway

1. **Simple (Single) Reactions**: A single stoichiometric equation completely describes the reaction progress.
   - $\text{A} \rightarrow \text{Products}$
   - $A + B \rightarrow \text{Products}$

2. **Complex Reactions**: Multiple reaction pathways occur simultaneously or sequentially. They are further divided into:
   - **Parallel (Side) Reactions**: $A \rightarrow B$ (desired) and $A \rightarrow C$ (undesired)
   - **Series (Consecutive) Reactions**: $A \rightarrow B \rightarrow C$
   - **Mixed Complex Reactions**: Combination of both parallel and series pathways.

#### C. Classification Based on Molecularity

**Molecularity** is the number of molecules (or atoms/ions) participating in an elementary reaction step.

| Molecularity | Reaction Type | Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Unimolecular** | 1 molecule | Radioactive decay, $N_2O_5 \rightarrow NO_2 + O_2$ |
| **Bimolecular** | 2 molecules | $H_2 + I_2 \rightarrow 2HI$ |
| **Termolecular** | 3 molecules (rare) | $2NO + O_2 \rightarrow 2NO_2$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Molecularity is ALWAYS a whole number and is only defined for elementary reactions.** For non-elementary reactions, the term is meaningless.

#### D. Classification Based on Order of Reaction

The **order** is the exponent of concentration in the rate law: $-r_A = k C_A^n$. Unlike molecularity, order is an **empirical quantity** and can be:
- **Zero order** ($n = 0$): $-r_A = k$
- **First order** ($n = 1$): $-r_A = kC_A$
- **Second order** ($n = 2$): $-r_A = kC_A^2$ or $-r_A = kC_AC_B$
- **Fractional order** (e.g., $n = 1.5$)
- **Negative order** (rare, indicates inhibition)

#### E. Classification Based on Thermal Effects

1. **Exothermic**: Heat is released ($\Delta H < 0$).
2. **Endothermic**: Heat is absorbed ($\Delta H > 0$).
3. **Adiabatic**: No heat exchange with surroundings ($Q = 0$).

#### F. Classification Based on Catalysis

1. **Catalyzed reactions** (homogeneous or heterogeneous catalysis).
2. **Non-catalyzed (autocatalytic)** reactions where a product itself acts as a catalyst.

#### G. Classification Based on Reversibility

1. **Irreversible**: Reaction proceeds to completion in one direction.
2. **Reversible (Equilibrium-limited)**: Reaction reaches dynamic equilibrium.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **exam-ready cheat sheet** for rate laws of common reaction classifications. Master these for Part A and Part B questions.

| Reaction Class | Stoichiometry | Rate Law | Integrated Form (Batch) | Half-life $t_{1/2}$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Zero Order (Irrev.) | $A \rightarrow P$ | $-r_A = k$ | $C_A = C_{A0} - kt$ | $C_{A0}/(2k)$ |
| First Order (Irrev.) | $A \rightarrow P$ | $-r_A = kC_A$ | $\ln(C_{A0}/C_A) = kt$ | $\ln(2)/k$ |
| Second Order (Irrev., equal conc.) | $A + A \rightarrow P$ | $-r_A = kC_A^2$ | $1/C_A - 1/C_{A0} = kt$ | $1/(kC_{A0})$ |
| Second Order (Irrev., unequal conc.) | $A + B \rightarrow P$ | $-r_A = kC_AC_B$ | $\ln(C_B C_{A0}/C_A C_{B0}) = (C_{A0} - C_{B0})kt$ | Complex |
| First Order (Rev.) | $A \rightleftharpoons B$ | $-r_A = k(C_A - C_B/K_e)$ | Closed form involves $K_e$ | Function of $K_e, k$ |
| Autocatalytic | $A + P \rightarrow P + P$ | $-r_A = kC_AC_P$ | — | — |
| Parallel (1st order) | $A \rightarrow B$, $A \rightarrow C$ | $-r_A = (k_1 + k_2)C_A$ | $C_A = C_{A0} e^{-(k_1+k_2)t}$ | $\ln(2)/(k_1+k_2)$ |
| Series (1st order) | $A \rightarrow B \rightarrow C$ | $r_B = k_1C_A - k_2C_B$ | See derivation below | — |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pharmaceutical Industry**: Determining whether a synthesis is parallel (unwanted byproducts) or series (intermediates) dictates purification strategy.
- **Petroleum Refining**: Catalytic cracking is a heterogeneous, endothermic, reversible reaction — all classifications apply simultaneously.
- **Polymerization**: Chain-growth polymerizations are series-type complex reactions.
- **Environmental Engineering**: Biodegradation of pollutants often follows autocatalytic kinetics.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Tip:** When in doubt, always **draw the reaction network** (reactants → intermediates → products) before writing rate equations. KTU examiners award full credit for *systematic* network representation.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Derivation Mandate:** Every algebraic transition is explicitly shown. Each step is followed by a plain-English explanation of the logic.

### 3.1 Derivation: Rate Law for First-Order Irreversible Reaction in a Batch Reactor

We begin with a single, irreversible, homogeneous, first-order reaction: $A \rightarrow \text{Products}$

**Step 1: State the differential rate law based on order definition.**

For a first-order reaction, the rate of disappearance of A is directly proportional to its concentration:

$$-r_A = k C_A$$

**Step 2: Write the material balance for a constant-volume batch reactor.**

For a batch reactor, accumulation equals generation (no inflow/outflow):

$$\frac{dC_A}{dt} = r_A = -k C_A$$

**Step 3: Separate variables to prepare for integration.**

Move all concentration terms to the left side and the time differential to the right side:

$$\frac{dC_A}{C_A} = -k \, dt$$

**Step 4: Apply definite integration limits.**

Integrate from the initial concentration $C_{A0}$ at $t = 0$ to $C_A$ at time $t$:

$$\int_{C_{A0}}^{C_A} \frac{dC_A}{C_A} = -k \int_0^t dt$$

**Step 5: Evaluate both integrals.**

The left integral is a standard natural logarithm, and the right integral is straightforward:

$$\ln(C_A) - \ln(C_{A0}) = -kt$$

**Step 6: Simplify using logarithm properties.**

Combine the left side using $\ln(a) - \ln(b) = \ln(a/b)$:

$$\ln\left(\frac{C_A}{C_{A0}}\right) = -kt$$

**Step 7: Express in exponential form and introduce conversion $X = 1 - C_A/C_{A0}$.**

This final form is what KTU examiners expect in Part B answers:

$$C_A = C_{A0} \exp(-kt)$$

**Step 8: Half-life derivation.**

Set $C_A = C_{A0}/2$:

$$\ln\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) = -k t_{1/2}$$

$$t_{1/2} = \frac{\ln(2)}{k} = \frac{0.693}{k}$$

**Logical Conclusion:** The half-life of a first-order reaction is **independent of the initial concentration** — a unique fingerprint that KTU examiners love to test in viva voce.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Concentration Profile for Series First-Order Reaction $A \rightarrow B \rightarrow C$

This is a **high-weightage derivation** in KTU board exams.

**Step 1: Write the rate law for A's disappearance.**

Since A disappears only via the first step with rate constant $k_1$:

$$-r_A = \frac{dC_A}{dt} = k_1 C_A$$

$$C_A = C_{A0} \exp(-k_1 t)$$

**Step 2: Write the differential equation for B's net rate of formation.**

B is formed from A (rate $k_1 C_A$) and consumed to form C (rate $k_2 C_B$):

$$\frac{dC_B}{dt} = k_1 C_A - k_2 C_B$$

**Step 3: Substitute the expression for $C_A$ from Step 1.**

$$\frac{dC_B}{dt} + k_2 C_B = k_1 C_{A0} \exp(-k_1 t)$$

**Step 4: Recognize this as a first-order linear ODE and use the integrating factor method.**

The integrating factor is $\mu = \exp(k_2 t)$:

$$\frac{d}{dt}\left[C_B \exp(k_2 t)\right] = k_1 C_{A0} \exp((k_2 - k_1) t)$$

**Step 5: Integrate both sides from 0 to $t$.**

Applying the initial condition $C_B(0) = 0$:

$$C_B \exp(k_2 t) = \frac{k_1 C_{A0}}{k_2 - k_1}\left[\exp((k_2 - k_1) t) - 1\right]$$

**Step 6: Solve for $C_B$.**

$$C_B = \frac{k_1 C_{A0}}{k_2 - k_1}\left[\exp(-k_1 t) - \exp(-k_2 t)\right]$$

**Step 7: Use the overall mass balance to find $C_C$.**

By conservation, $C_A + C_B + C_C = C_{A0}$:

$$C_C = C_{A0}\left[1 + \frac{1}{k_1 - k_2}\left(k_2 \exp(-k_1 t) - k_1 \exp(-k_2 t)\right)\right]$$

**Step 8: Find the time at which $C_B$ is maximum (often asked in KTU questions).**

Differentiate $C_B$ with respect to $t$ and set to zero:

$$\frac{dC_B}{dt} = 0 \implies t_{\max} = \frac{\ln(k_2/k_1)}{k_2 - k_1}$$

**Logical Conclusion:** The intermediate B reaches a maximum concentration and then decreases — critical in pharmaceutical synthesis to avoid side reactions.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: How Rate Laws Change with Classification

| Classification Change | Effect on Rate Law | Engineering Implication |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Homogeneous → Heterogeneous | Adds mass transfer term: $-r_A^{obs} = \frac{1}{\frac{1}{k_{mt}} + \frac{1}{k_{\text{rxn}}}}$ | Reactor size may increase due to mass transfer resistance |
| First Order → Second Order | Doubles dependence on concentration | Highly sensitive to concentration gradients |
| Irreversible → Reversible | Adds backward term: $k_f C_A - k_b C_B$ | Conversion limited to equilibrium $X_e$ |
| Single → Parallel | Sum of rate constants: $k_1 + k_2$ | Selectivity becomes a design variable |
| Single → Series | Sequential dependence | Need to optimize residence time for max intermediate |
| Non-catalytic → Catalytic | Multiplied by $C_{\text{cat}}$ | Catalyst deactivation must be modeled |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Hierarchical Classification of Chemical Reactions

The following Mermaid block renders a complete **block-level functional architecture flow** mapping the complete classification taxonomy. This diagram is highly valued in KTU board answers as it demonstrates systematic thinking.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeRoot["CLASSIFICATION OF CHEMICAL REACTIONS"]:::root
    nodeRoot --> nodeA["A. BY PHASE"]
    nodeRoot --> nodeB["B. BY STOICHIOMETRY"]
    nodeRoot --> nodeC["C. BY MOLECULARITY"]
    nodeRoot --> nodeD["D. BY ORDER"]
    nodeRoot --> nodeE["E. BY THERMAL EFFECT"]
    nodeRoot --> nodeF["F. BY CATALYSIS"]
    nodeRoot --> nodeG["G. BY REVERSIBILITY"]

    nodeA --> nodeA1["HOMOGENEOUS - Single Phase"]
    nodeA --> nodeA2["HETEROGENEOUS - Two or More Phases"]

    nodeB --> nodeB1["SIMPLE REACTIONS"]
    nodeB --> nodeB2["COMPLEX REACTIONS"]
    nodeB2 --> nodeB2a["PARALLEL - A to B and A to C"]
    nodeB2 --> nodeB2b["SERIES - A to B to C"]
    nodeB2 --> nodeB2c["MIXED - Parallel plus Series"]

    nodeC --> nodeC1["UNIMOLECULAR"]
    nodeC --> nodeC2["BIMOLECULAR"]
    nodeC --> nodeC3["TERMOLCULAR - RARE"]

    nodeD --> nodeD1["ZERO ORDER"]
    nodeD --> nodeD2["FIRST ORDER"]
    nodeD --> nodeD3["SECOND ORDER"]
    nodeD --> nodeD4["FRACTIONAL ORDER"]

    nodeE --> nodeE1["EXOTHERMIC - Heat Released"]
    nodeE --> nodeE2["ENDOTHERMIC - Heat Absorbed"]
    nodeE --> nodeE3["ADIABATIC - No Heat Exchange"]

    nodeF --> nodeF1["CATALYZED"]
    nodeF --> nodeF2["NON-CATALYZED"]
    nodeF --> nodeF3["AUTOCATALYTIC"]

    nodeG --> nodeG1["IRREVERSIBLE"]
    nodeG --> nodeG2["REVERSIBLE - EQUILIBRIUM LIMITED"]

    classDef sub fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#000
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Differentiate between molecularity and order of a reaction. Can molecularity be zero or fractional? Justify your answer.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Molecularity** is the number of molecules, atoms, or ions that participate as reactants in an **elementary reaction step**. It is a **theoretical** concept derived from the reaction mechanism and is **always a whole number** (unimolecular, bimolecular, termolecular).
- **Order of reaction** is the sum of the exponents of the concentration terms in the experimentally determined rate law. It is an **empirical** quantity and can be **zero, fractional, or even negative**.
- Molecularity **cannot be zero or fractional** because one cannot have "zero molecules" reacting, and a reaction step is a discrete event with a definite integer count of colliding species.
- Order **can be fractional** (e.g., 1.5 in some enzyme-catalyzed reactions) or **negative** (e.g., when a product inhibits the reaction).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Students often write "order is the same as molecularity." This is **incorrect** for non-elementary reactions. Always emphasize that equality holds **only for elementary reactions**.

---

#### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Classify the following reactions based on (i) phase, (ii) molecularity, and (iii) reversibility:**
- (a) $2H_2(g) + O_2(g) \rightarrow 2H_2O(l)$
- (b) $CaCO_3(s) \rightarrow CaO(s) + CO_2(g)$
- (c) $N_2(g) + 3H_2(g) \rightleftharpoons 2NH_3(g)$ (with Fe catalyst)

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Reaction | Phase | Molecularity | Reversibility |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| (a) | Homogeneous (gas phase reactants, but products include liquid — borderline; in CRE typically treated as **homogeneous** if reactants are in one phase) | Termolecular (4 atoms reacting) | Irreversible |
| (b) | Heterogeneous (solid + gas phases) | Unimolecular (one species decomposing) | Irreversible |
| (c) | Heterogeneous (gas reactants on solid catalyst) | Termolecular (4 molecules colliding — but non-elementary; actual mechanism is multi-step) | Reversible (equilibrium-limited) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Insight:** For reaction (c), it is tempting to call it "homogeneous" because all reactants are gases, but the **presence of a solid catalyst** makes it **heterogeneous catalytic**. This distinction is critical.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: (a) Classify chemical reactions with suitable examples for each class. (7 marks)**

**(b) For the series reaction $A \xrightarrow{k_1} B \xrightarrow{k_2} C$ with $k_1 = 0.5 \,\text{min}^{-1}$ and $k_2 = 0.2 \,\text{min}^{-1}$, determine:**
- (i) The time at which B reaches maximum concentration. (3 marks)
- (ii) The maximum concentration of B if $C_{A0} = 2 \,\text{mol/L}$. (4 marks)

**Complete Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

A systematic classification with one example per category is required. The student should present at least **four major classifications** with examples:

1. **By phase:** Homogeneous — gas-phase combustion of methane; Heterogeneous — catalytic cracking.
2. **By stoichiometry:** Simple — $A \rightarrow B$; Complex — $A \rightarrow B \rightarrow C$ (series) and $A \rightarrow B$, $A \rightarrow C$ (parallel).
3. **By molecularity:** Unimolecular — radioactive decay; Bimolecular — $H_2 + I_2 \rightarrow 2HI$.
4. **By order:** Zero order — surface-catalyzed reactions at saturation; First order — decomposition of $N_2O_5$; Second order — $2HI \rightarrow H_2 + I_2$.
5. **By thermal effect:** Exothermic — neutralization; Endothermic — decomposition of $CaCO_3$.
6. **By catalysis:** Catalyzed — Haber process with Fe; Autocatalytic — hydrolysis of ester by acid produced.
7. **By reversibility:** Irreversible — precipitation; Reversible — esterification.

(Each classification with example: **1 mark**, full table: **7 marks**)

**Part (b)(i) — 3 marks:**

Using the derived formula from Section 3.2:

$$t_{\max} = \frac{\ln(k_2/k_1)}{k_2 - k_1}$$

Substitute the values:

$$t_{\max} = \frac{\ln(0.2/0.5)}{0.2 - 0.5} = \frac{\ln(0.4)}{-0.3}$$

$$t_{\max} = \frac{-0.9163}{-0.3} = 3.054 \,\text{min}$$

**Part (b)(ii) — 4 marks:**

Using the concentration formula for B in a series reaction:

$$C_B^{\max} = C_{A0}\left(\frac{k_1}{k_2}\right)^{\frac{k_2}{k_2 - k_1}}$$

Substituting the values:

$$C_B^{\max} = 2 \times \left(\frac{0.5}{0.2}\right)^{\frac{0.2}{0.2 - 0.5}}$$

$$C_B^{\max} = 2 \times (2.5)^{\frac{0.2}{-0.3}} = 2 \times (2.5)^{-0.6667}$$

$$C_B^{\max} = 2 \times \frac{1}{(2.5)^{0.6667}} = 2 \times \frac{1}{1.842} = 1.086 \,\text{mol/L}$$

**Final Answer:** $t_{\max} = 3.054$ min, $C_B^{\max} = 1.086$ mol/L.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall (4 marks lost on this type of question):**
> - **Sign error** in the natural log: $\ln(0.4) = -0.9163$, not $+0.9163$.
> - Students often confuse the formula and use $C_B = C_{A0} \exp(-k_1 t)$ — that is the concentration of **A**, not **B**.
> - The exponent $\frac{k_2}{k_2 - k_1}$ is **negative** when $k_2 < k_1$, which it is in this problem. Missing this sign costs 2 marks.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: (a) Explain with neat sketches the classification of complex reactions into parallel, series, and mixed types. Derive the rate expressions for first-order parallel and series reactions. (7 marks)**
