**(b) The liquid-phase decomposition of A follows first-order kinetics with $k = 0.008 \,\text{s}^{-1}$. If the initial concentration is $C_{A0} = 4 \,\text{mol/L}$:**
- (i) Calculate the time required for 80% conversion in a batch reactor. (3 marks)
- (ii) Determine the rate of reaction at 80% conversion. (4 marks)

**Complete Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

The student must include:
- Sketches of all three complex reaction types (**3 marks** for diagrams).
- Derivation for first-order parallel reaction $A \xrightarrow{k_1} B$ and $A \xrightarrow{k_2} C$ (**2 marks**).
- Derivation for first-order series reaction $A \xrightarrow{k_1} B \xrightarrow{k_2} C$ (**2 marks**).

**Derivation for first-order parallel reaction:**

For $A \xrightarrow{k_1} B$ and $A \xrightarrow{k_2} C$ simultaneously:

$$-\frac{dC_A}{dt} = k_1 C_A + k_2 C_A = (k_1 + k_2) C_A$$

$$C_A = C_{A0} \exp(-(k_1 + k_2)t)$$

For product B: $\frac{dC_B}{dt} = k_1 C_A$

$$C_B = \frac{k_1 C_{A0}}{k_1 + k_2}\left[1 - \exp(-(k_1 + k_2)t)\right]$$

Similarly:

$$C_C = \frac{k_2 C_{A0}}{k_1 + k_2}\left[1 - \exp(-(k_1 + k_2)t)\right]$$

The **selectivity** of B over C is constant: $S_{B/C} = k_1/k_2$.

**Derivation for first-order series reaction:** (Already covered in Section 3.2 — repeat key steps.)

**Part (b)(i) — 3 marks:**

For a first-order reaction, conversion $X = 1 - \exp(-kt)$:

$$X = 0.80 \implies \exp(-kt) = 0.20$$

$$-kt = \ln(0.20) = -1.6094$$

$$t = \frac{1.6094}{0.008} = 201.18 \,\text{s}$$

**Part (b)(ii) — 4 marks:**

At 80% conversion, $C_A = C_{A0}(1 - X) = 4 \times 0.20 = 0.8 \,\text{mol/L}$

$$-r_A = k C_A = 0.008 \times 0.8 = 0.0064 \,\text{mol/(L·s)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall (3 marks lost):**
> - Students forget to convert $C_A$ at the new conversion level. Always compute $C_A$ at the specific $X$ before evaluating the rate.
> - **Unit consistency:** $k$ is in s⁻¹, $C_A$ in mol/L, so $-r_A$ must be in **mol/(L·s)**. Writing "mol/L" without the time unit loses 1 mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- **Classification of chemical reactions** is the **gateway step** to reactor design — never skip it.
- **Homogeneous vs Heterogeneous** is the **single most important** classification; it dictates whether mass transfer equations are needed.
- **Molecularity is an integer; Order is empirical.** They are equal **only for elementary reactions**.
- **Parallel reactions:** Selectivity is constant for first-order. Use **PFR** to maximize selectivity toward desired product.
- **Series reactions:** Intermediate concentration peaks at $t_{\max} = \frac{\ln(k_2/k_1)}{k_2 - k_1}$. Stop reaction at this time for max intermediate.
- **Reversible reactions:** Maximum achievable conversion is $X_e = \frac{K_e}{1 + K_e}$ for simple $A \rightleftharpoons B$.
- **Autocatalytic reactions** have a **sigmoid** $C_A$ vs $t$ curve — $C_A$ first rises, then falls.
- **Adiabatic temperature rise** for exothermic reactions: $\Delta T_{ad} = \frac{-\Delta H \cdot C_{A0}}{\rho C_p}$. This sets the upper bound of reactor temperature.
- **First-order half-life** is **independent** of $C_{A0}$ — a classic viva question.
- **Second-order half-life** depends on $C_{A0}$ — another common viva.
- **Zero-order reactions** are typical of **surface-saturated catalytic reactions** at high coverage.
- For KTU board exams, **always draw the reaction network diagram** before writing rate equations — examiners award **1–2 marks** just for a clean network.
- **Sign convention:** $-r_A$ for disappearance, $+r_P$ for formation. Reversing this sign is a **direct 1-mark deduction**.
- **Unit of $k$** depends on order: for $n$-th order, $k$ has units of $(C)^{1-n} t^{-1}$.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Variables Affecting Rate of Reaction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT637 - CHEMICAL TECHNOLOGY AND REACTION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Variables Affecting Rate of Reaction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Rate of Reaction

In **Chemical Reaction Engineering (CRE)**, the **rate of a chemical reaction** is formally defined as the change in the extent of reaction per unit time per unit volume (for homogeneous systems) or per unit surface area (for heterogeneous systems). The mathematical expression of the rate of disappearance of a reactant A in a constant-volume system is given by:

$$-r_A \;=\; -\frac{1}{V}\frac{dN_A}{dt} \;=\; -\frac{dC_A}{dt}$$

where:
- $-r_A$ = rate of disappearance of species A, **mol·L⁻¹·s⁻¹**
- $C_A$ = concentration of A, **mol·L⁻¹**
- $t$ = time, **s**
- $V$ = reaction volume, **L**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight — Definition Tag**
> The "variables affecting the rate of reaction" are the **independent process parameters** (such as concentration, temperature, pressure, catalyst presence, surface area, and radiation intensity) that, when varied, cause a measurable change in the numerical value of $-r_A$. These variables act as the inputs to the **rate law** or **kinetic model** of a reacting system.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Kitchen Cooking" Model

Imagine you are boiling rice in a kitchen. The "rate" at which rice becomes edible (analogous to the rate of reaction) depends on several knobs you can turn:

| Kitchen Variable | Reaction Engineering Analog |
|---|---|
| Amount of water you pour | **Concentration** of reactants |
| Flame intensity on the stove | **Temperature** of the system |
| Tight vs loose lid on the pot | **Pressure** (for gaseous reactants) |
| Adding a pinch of salt or baking soda | **Catalyst** |
| Using chopped vs whole rice | **Surface area** (heterogeneous systems) |
| Sunlight directly on the pot | **Radiation / Photochemical activation** |

> [!NOTE]
> Just as turning up the flame *speeds up* cooking (rate increases with temperature), and adding less water (higher concentration) makes rice cook faster in a thick base, in chemical kinetics each variable influences the molecular collision frequency, the energy of collisions, and the pathway of conversion. This intuition is the foundation on which all KTU-level rate-equation derivations stand.

## 1.3 The General Rate Law — Foundation for the Topic

The **rate law** expresses $-r_A$ as a function of the variables listed above. The most general homogeneous form is:

$$-r_A \;=\; k(T)\,f(C_A, C_B, \ldots, C_P)$$

where:
- $k(T)$ = **rate constant** (depends strongly on temperature)
- $f(\cdot)$ = **concentration function** (depends on the mechanism)
- Catalysts, pressure, surface area, and radiation modify either $k$ or $f$ or both.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Master Variables

In KTU 2024 CRE parlance, the rate of reaction is a function of **five master variables**:

1. **Concentration** (or partial pressure) of reactants/products
2. **Temperature** of the reacting mixture
3. **Pressure** of the system (gas-phase reactions)
4. **Catalyst** presence and type
5. **Surface area** (heterogeneous systems) and **radiation intensity** (photochemical)

### 2.1.1 Variable 1 — Concentration (The Concentration Effect)

For an elementary reaction $aA + bB \rightarrow \text{Products}$, the molecularity dictates the **rate law**:
$$-r_A \;=\; k\,C_A^{\alpha}\,C_B^{\beta}$$

where $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are the **orders of reaction** with respect to A and B, and $\alpha + \beta = n$ is the **overall order**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Key Concept — Apparent vs True Order**
> Orders are *experimental* quantities, not equal to stoichiometric coefficients except for elementary reactions. The KTU paper routinely tests the student's ability to distinguish between **molecularity** (theoretical) and **order** (experimental).

### 2.1.2 Variable 2 — Temperature (The Arrhenius Effect)

The Swedish chemist **Svante Arrhenius** (1889) established the temperature dependence of the rate constant:

$$k \;=\; A\,\exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{RT}\right)$$

where:
- $A$ = pre-exponential (frequency) factor, units depend on order
- $E_a$ = activation energy, **J·mol⁻¹**
- $R$ = universal gas constant = **8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹**
- $T$ = absolute temperature, **K**

A more useful two-temperature form is obtained by taking logarithms:
$$\ln\!\left(\frac{k_2}{k_1}\right) \;=\; -\frac{E_a}{R}\!\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right) \;=\; \frac{E_a}{R}\!\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)$$

### 2.1.3 Variable 3 — Pressure (Gas-Phase Reactions)

For an ideal gas, partial pressure is linked to concentration through the ideal gas law:
$$C_A \;=\; \frac{P_A}{RT} \;=\; \frac{y_A P}{RT}$$

When pressure increases at constant temperature, $C_A$ increases linearly, and so does the rate (for positive order reactions). For reactions with $\Delta n \neq 0$, the equilibrium and rate both shift — KTU 2024 questions often involve **Le Chatelier's principle combined with rate law**.

### 2.1.4 Variable 4 — Catalyst

A **catalyst** provides an alternative reaction pathway with **lower activation energy** $E_{a,\text{cat}} < E_a$, without being consumed:

$$k_{\text{cat}} \;=\; A\,\exp\!\left(-\frac{E_{a,\text{cat}}}{RT}\right) \;\;\text{where}\;\; E_{a,\text{cat}} \;<\; E_a$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Error:** A catalyst does **not** change the equilibrium conversion — it changes only **how fast** equilibrium is reached. KTU examiners deduct marks if you claim catalyst changes yield.

### 2.1.5 Variable 5 — Surface Area & Radiation (Heterogeneous & Photochemical)

For **heterogeneous reactions** (e.g., solid catalyst + fluid), the rate is proportional to the available interfacial area $a$ (m²·m⁻³):
$$-r_A \;=\; k\,a\,C_A^{n}$$

For **photochemical reactions**, the rate depends on the **light intensity** $I_\alpha$ absorbed:
$$-r_A \;=\; k\,I_\alpha^{a}\,C_A^{n}$$
where $a$ is the order with respect to radiation (often 0.5 or 1).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Master Reference Table — Print This!**

| # | Variable | Governing Equation | Effect on Rate $-r_A$ | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Concentration** $C_i$ | $-r_A = k\,C_A^{\alpha}\,C_B^{\beta}$ | Higher $C_i$ $\Rightarrow$ higher $-r_A$ (for $\alpha, \beta > 0$) | Reactor design (CSTR vs PFR) |
| 2 | **Temperature** $T$ | $k = A\exp(-E_a/RT)$ | Higher $T$ $\Rightarrow$ exponentially higher $k$ | Optimizing energy input |
| 3 | **Pressure** $P$ | $C_A = y_A P / RT$ | Higher $P$ $\Rightarrow$ higher $C_A$ $\Rightarrow$ higher $-r_A$ | Gas-phase reactor design |
| 4 | **Catalyst** | $E_{a,\text{cat}} < E_a$ | Lowers $E_a$ $\Rightarrow$ higher $k$ at same $T$ | Industrial process economics |
| 5 | **Surface Area** $a$ | $-r_A = k\,a\,C_A^{n}$ | Higher $a$ $\Rightarrow$ higher $-r_A$ | Catalyst pellet sizing |
| 6 | **Radiation** $I_\alpha$ | $-r_A = k\,I_\alpha^{a}\,C_A^{n}$ | Higher $I_\alpha$ $\Rightarrow$ higher $-r_A$ | Photochemical reactor design |
| 7 | **Solvent / Ionic Strength** | Brønsted–Bjerrum: $\log k = \log k_0 + 1.02\,z_A z_B \sqrt{\mu}$ | Higher $\mu$ $\Rightarrow$ higher $k$ (for same-sign ions) | Electrolyte reaction design |

## 2.3 The "Why" Behind Each Variable — Engineering Interpretation

- **Concentration** controls **collision frequency** per unit volume (molecular kinetic theory of gases / solutions).
- **Temperature** controls the **fraction of molecules that possess energy $\geq E_a$** (Maxwell–Boltzmann distribution).
- **Pressure** is essentially concentration in disguise for gases (ideal gas law); it is the only practical handle for gases in storage.
- **Catalyst** provides a **lower-energy transition state** by stabilizing the activated complex.
- **Surface area** provides **more active sites** for adsorption–reaction–desorption in heterogeneous systems.
- **Radiation** provides **photons of the right energy** $h\nu \geq E_{\text{bond}}$ to break bonds and initiate radical chain reactions.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Arrhenius Equation (Temperature Effect)

The Maxwell–Boltzmann distribution tells us that the fraction of molecules with energy $E \geq E_a$ at temperature $T$ is proportional to $\exp(-E_a/RT)$. Combining this with the collision frequency (which scales weakly with $T^{1/2}$), we write the **collision rate** $Z_{AB}$ as:

$$Z_{AB} \;=\; N_A N_B \sigma_{AB}^{2}\sqrt{\frac{8\pi k_B T}{\mu_{AB}}}\,\exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{RT}\right)$$

**Step 1.** Group the weakly temperature-dependent terms into a single pre-exponential factor $A$:
$$A(T) \;=\; N_A N_B \sigma_{AB}^{2}\sqrt{\frac{8\pi k_B T}{\mu_{AB}}}$$

**Step 2.** Recognize that the rate is proportional to this fraction of energetic collisions:
$$k(T) \;\propto\; A(T)\,\exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{RT}\right)$$

**Step 3.** For moderate temperature ranges, the $T^{1/2}$ term in $A$ varies slowly compared to the exponential, so $A$ is taken as **approximately constant**:
$$k(T) \;\approx\; A\,\exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{RT}\right)$$

This is the **Arrhenius equation**. $\blacksquare$

**Step 4 (Two-temperature form used in KTU problems).** Take the natural logarithm:
$$\ln k \;=\; \ln A - \frac{E_a}{R}\cdot\frac{1}{T}$$

Subtracting the equation at $T_1$ from that at $T_2$:

$$\ln k_2 - \ln k_1 \;=\; -\frac{E_a}{R}\!\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)$$

Rearranging:

$$\boxed{\;\ln\!\left(\frac{k_2}{k_1}\right) \;=\; \frac{E_a}{R}\!\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)\;}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Pressure Effect on Gas-Phase Rate

Consider a gas-phase reaction $A + B \rightarrow \text{Products}$ taking place in a constant-$T$, constant-$V$ batch system.

**Step 1.** For an ideal gas, partial pressure and mole fraction are related by **Dalton's law**:
$$P_A \;=\; y_A P$$

**Step 2.** Apply the **ideal gas law** to species A:
$$P_A V \;=\; N_A R T \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; C_A \;=\; \frac{N_A}{V} \;=\; \frac{P_A}{RT} \;=\; \frac{y_A P}{RT}$$

**Step 3.** Substitute into the elementary rate law (second order):
$$-r_A \;=\; k\,C_A\,C_B \;=\; k\,\frac{y_A P}{RT}\cdot\frac{y_B P}{RT} \;=\; \frac{k\,y_A y_B}{R^2 T^2}\,P^2$$

**Step 4.** Generalize — for an $n^{\text{th}}$-order gas reaction:
$$\boxed{\;-r_A \;=\; k\!\left(\frac{P}{RT}\right)^{\!n}\,y_A^{\alpha}\,y_B^{\beta}\;}$$

This shows that **doubling the pressure quadruples the rate** for a second-order gas-phase reaction. $\blacksquare$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Catalyst Effect via Activation Energy

Let $E_a$ be the activation energy of the uncatalyzed pathway and $E_{a,\text{cat}}$ that of the catalyzed pathway, with $E_a > E_{a,\text{cat}}$. Assume the pre-exponential factor $A$ is unchanged (a simplification valid for many heterogeneous catalysts).

**Step 1.** Write the ratio of the two rate constants:
$$\frac{k_{\text{cat}}}{k} \;=\; \frac{A\,\exp(-E_{a,\text{cat}}/RT)}{A\,\exp(-E_a/RT)} \;=\; \exp\!\left(\frac{E_a - E_{a,\text{cat}}}{RT}\right)$$

**Step 2.** Take the natural log:
$$\ln\!\left(\frac{k_{\text{cat}}}{k}\right) \;=\; \frac{\Delta E_a}{RT} \;\;\text{where}\;\; \Delta E_a \;=\; E_a - E_{a,\text{cat}} \;>\; 0$$

**Step 3.** This is always **positive**, so $k_{\text{cat}} > k$. The enhancement factor grows exponentially as $T$ decreases — i.e., **catalysts are most effective at low temperatures**, which is precisely why they are used industrially. $\blacksquare$

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Integrated Rate Laws (Concentration vs Time)

For a first-order reaction $-r_A = kC_A$, with initial concentration $C_{A0}$ at $t=0$:

**Step 1.** Write the differential equation:
$$-\frac{dC_A}{dt} \;=\; k C_A \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \frac{dC_A}{C_A} \;=\; -k\,dt$$

**Step 2.** Integrate from $t=0$ to $t$, $C_{A0}$ to $C_A$:
$$\int_{C_{A0}}^{C_A}\frac{dC_A}{C_A} \;=\; -k\int_{0}^{t}dt$$

**Step 3.** Evaluate both sides:
$$\ln C_A - \ln C_{A0} \;=\; -kt$$

**Step 4.** Solve for $C_A$:
$$\boxed{\;C_A \;=\; C_{A0}\,\exp(-kt)\;}$$

A **half-life** (the time for $C_A = C_{A0}/2$) is obtained by setting $C_A = C_{A0}/2$:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) \;=\; -k\,t_{1/2} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; t_{1/2} \;=\; \frac{\ln 2}{k} \;=\; \frac{0.693}{k}$$

**Note:** For first-order reactions, $t_{1/2}$ is **independent of initial concentration** — a KTU favourite. $\blacksquare$

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Quantitative Effect of Each Variable

| Variable | Doubling the variable... | Effect on $-r_A$ (1st order) | Effect on $-r_A$ (2nd order) |
|---|---|---|---|
| $C_A$ | $C_A \rightarrow 2C_A$ | $-r_A$ doubles ($\times 2$) | $-r_A$ quadruples ($\times 4$) |
| $T$ (small $\Delta T$) | $T \rightarrow T + 10\,\text{K}$ | $k$ increases by factor $\exp\!\big[10E_a/(RT^2)\big]$ | Same factor as $k$ |
| $P$ (ideal gas) | $P \rightarrow 2P$ | $-r_A$ doubles ($\times 2$) | $-r_A$ quadruples ($\times 4$) |
| Catalyst (e.g., $E_a \downarrow 50\%$) | $E_a \rightarrow E_a/2$ | $k$ multiplies by $\exp(E_a/2RT)$ | Same factor as $k$ |
| Surface area $a$ | $a \rightarrow 2a$ | $-r_A$ doubles | $-r_A$ doubles |
| Radiation $I_\alpha$ | $I_\alpha \rightarrow 2I_\alpha$ | $-r_A$ doubles (if order = 1) | $-r_A$ doubles (if order = 1) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Note:** The *qualitative* effect (rate increases/decreases) is worth 2 marks. The *quantitative* factor (2×, 4×, etc.) earns the remaining marks. Always quantify whenever possible.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Flow Diagram — Variables Affecting Rate of Reaction

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **cause-effect relationships** between the five master variables and the rate of reaction $-r_A$. It also shows the cascade from $-r_A$ to the design outputs (reactor volume, conversion, product purity).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    var1["Variable 1: Concentration C_A, C_B"] --> rateFn["Rate Law f of C_A, C_B, ..."]
    var2["Variable 2: Temperature T"] --> kCons["Arrhenius k = A exp -E_a over RT"]
    var3["Variable 3: Pressure P"] --> concLink["C_A = y_A P over RT"]
    concLink --> rateFn
    var4["Variable 4: Catalyst"] --> eRed["Lower Activation Energy E_a cat"]
    eRed --> kCons
    var5A["Variable 5a: Surface Area a"] --> heteroFn["Heterogeneous Rate = k times a times C_A_n"]
    var5B["Variable 5b: Radiation I_alpha"] --> photoFn["Photochemical Rate = k times I_alpha to power a"]
    kCons --> rateFn
    rateFn --> rA["Rate of Reaction -r_A"]
    heteroFn --> rA
    photoFn --> rA
    rA --> des1["Reactor Volume V required"]
    rA --> des2["Conversion X achieved"]
    rA --> des3["Product Distribution S_i"]
    rA --> des4["Energy Duty Q dot"]
    var2 --> des4
    var3 --> des1
    classDef input fill:#E8F4FF,stroke:#0B66C2,color:#0B1F3A,stroke-width:2px
    classDef proc fill:#FFF6E5,stroke:#B8770B,color:#3A2A0A,stroke-width:2px
    classDef output fill:#E8FFE8,stroke:#1F7A1F,color:#0A2A0A,stroke-width:2px
    class var1,var2,var3,var4,var5A,var5B input
    class rateFn,kCons,concLink,eRed,heteroFn,photoFn,rA proc
    class des1,des2,des3,des4 output
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Temperature as a Master Variable

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T_in["T input K"] --> arrFit["Arrhenius Fit k = A exp -E_a over RT"]
    E_a["E_a J per mol from DSC or TGA"] --> arrFit
    A_pre["A from regression on ln k vs 1 over T"] --> arrFit
    arrFit --> k_out["k T  at reaction T"]
    T_in --> eqT["Energy Balance: Q dot = -deltaH_r times -r_A times V"]
    k_out --> rA_out["-r_A = k C_A_alpha C_B_beta"]
    rA_out --> eqT
    eqT --> T_actual["T actual in reactor K"]
    T_actual --> arrFit
    classDef input fill:#E8F4FF,stroke:#0B66C2,color:#0B1F3A
    classDef proc fill:#FFF6E5,stroke:#B8770B,color:#3A2A0A
    classDef feedback fill:#FFE8E8,stroke:#B8330B,color:#3A0A0A
    class T_in,E_a,A_pre input
    class arrFit,k_out,rA_out,eqT proc
    class T_actual feedback
```

## 4.3 Schematic — Effect of Catalyst on the Energy Profile

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    react["Reactants Energy E_R"] --> uncPath["Uncatalyzed Pathway Peak = E_a"]
    uncPath --> prod["Products Energy E_P"]
    react --> catPath["Catalyzed Pathway Peak = E_a cat"]
    catPath --> prod
    uncPath -.->|"delta E_a = E_a minus E_a cat">| catPath
    classDef reactProd fill:#E8FFE8,stroke:#1F7A1F,color:#0A2A0A
    classDef peak fill:#FFE8E8,stroke:#B8330B,color:#3A0A0A
    class react,prod reactProd
    class uncPath,catPath peak
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the catalyst schematic:** The vertical "drop" from the uncatalyzed peak to the catalyzed peak represents $\Delta E_a = E_a - E_{a,\text{cat}}$. A larger drop means a more effective catalyst at the same temperature. The product and reactant energy levels are *unchanged* — confirming that catalysts do **not** affect $\Delta G$ or equilibrium position.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**"List any three variables that affect the rate of a chemical reaction. For the temperature variable, state the Arrhenius equation and identify the meaning of each term." (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Three variables that affect the rate of a chemical reaction are:
1. **Concentration** of reactants (higher concentration $\Rightarrow$ higher rate, for positive order)
2. **Temperature** of the system (higher $T$ $\Rightarrow$ higher rate via Arrhenius)
3. **Catalyst** presence (lowers $E_a$ $\Rightarrow$ higher rate at same $T$)

**Arrhenius equation:**
$$k \;=\; A\,\exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{RT}\right)$$

where:
- $k$ = rate constant, units depend on reaction order
- $A$ = pre-exponential (frequency) factor, same units as $k$
- $E_a$ = activation energy, **J·mol⁻¹**
- $R$ = universal gas constant = **8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹**
- $T$ = absolute temperature, **K**

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**"A first-order reaction has a half-life of 30 minutes. Calculate the rate constant and the time required for 75% conversion." (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

For a first-order reaction, half-life is:
$$t_{1/2} \;=\; \frac{\ln 2}{k} \;=\; \frac{0.693}{k}$$

Solving for $k$:
$$k \;=\; \frac{0.693}{30\,\text{min}} \;=\; 0.0231\,\text{min}^{-1}$$

For 75% conversion, $C_A = 0.25\,C_{A0}$, so:
$$\ln\!\left(\frac{C_A}{C_{A0}}\right) \;=\; -kt \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \ln(0.25) \;=\; -0.0231 \times t$$

$$t \;=\; \frac{\ln 4}{0.0231} \;=\; \frac{1.386}{0.0231} \;\approx\; 60\,\text{min}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** For first-order reactions, time for 75% conversion equals **two half-lives** — a useful mental check.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Module Choice Question (Choose ONE, 14 Marks)

> **Question Paper Format:** Each Part B question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Full internal choice is provided.

---

### 📘 QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**(a)** A second-order gas-phase reaction $A + B \rightarrow \text{Products}$ is carried out at 600 K in a 5 L batch reactor. Initial partial pressures are $P_{A0} = 2\,\text{atm}$, $P_{B0} = 1\,\text{atm}$, and the system is diluted with an inert gas at total pressure $P_0 = 5\,\text{atm}$. The rate constant at 600 K is $k = 0.08\,\text{L·mol}^{-1}\text{·s}^{-1}$. The activation energy is $E_a = 65\,\text{kJ·mol}^{-1}$.
**(i)** Derive the rate expression $-r_A$ in terms of total pressure $P$ and conversion $X$ of A. **(ii)** Calculate the initial rate $-r_{A0}$. **(iii)** If the temperature is raised to 650 K, what is the new rate constant? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain with derivation how a catalyst affects the rate of a chemical reaction. Discuss why a catalyst does not change the equilibrium constant. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question A(a)

**(i) Derivation of $-r_A$ in terms of $P$ and $X$:**

Using stoichiometry for a constant-volume batch, the partial pressure of A is:
$$P_A \;=\; P_{A0}(1 - X) \;=\; 2(1 - X)\,\text{atm}$$

Since $P_{B0} = \tfrac{1}{2}P_{A0}$, we have $\Theta_B = P_{B0}/P_{A0} = 0.5$, and the stoichiometric coefficient ratio $b/a = 1$, so:
$$P_B \;=\; P_{A0}\!\left(\Theta_B - X\right) \;=\; 2(0.5 - X)\,\text{atm}$$

Total pressure stays at $P = 5\,\text{atm}$ (constant-volume, constant-amount-of-inert assumption), but for the **concentration-based rate law** we use:
$$C_A \;=\; \frac{P_A}{RT} \;\;\text{and}\;\; C_B \;=\; \frac{P_B}{RT}$$

The second-order rate law is:
$$-r_A \;=\; k\,C_A\,C_B \;=\; k\,\frac{P_A P_B}{(RT)^2}$$

Substituting:
$$-r_A \;=\; k\,\frac{2(1 - X)\cdot 2(0.5 - X)}{(RT)^2} \;=\; \frac{4k\,(1 - X)(0.5 - X)}{(RT)^2}$$

**Numerical substitution** at $T = 600\,\text{K}$, $R = 0.0821\,\text{L·atm·mol}^{-1}\text{·K}^{-1}$:

$$RT \;=\; 0.0821 \times 600 \;=\; 49.26\,\text{L·atm·mol}^{-1}$$

$$-r_A \;=\; \frac{4 \times 0.08 \times (1 - X)(0.5 - X)}{(49.26)^2} \;=\; 1.318 \times 10^{-4}\,(1 - X)(0.5 - X)\,\text{mol·L}^{-1}\text{·s}^{-1}$$

**(ii) Initial rate at $X = 0$:**

$$-r_{A0} \;=\; 1.318 \times 10^{-4} \times 1 \times 0.5 \;=\; 6.59 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{mol·L}^{-1}\text{·s}^{-1}$$

**(iii) New rate constant at 650 K using Arrhenius:**

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{k_2}{k_1}\right) \;=\; \frac{E_a}{R}\!\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)$$

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{k_2}{0.08}\right) \;=\; \frac{65000}{8.314}\!\left(\frac{1}{600} - \frac{1}{650}\right)$$

$$\frac{1}{600} - \frac{1}{650} \;=\; \frac{650 - 600}{600 \times 650} \;=\; \frac{50}{390000} \;=\; 1.282 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{K}^{-1}$$

$$\frac{E_a}{R}\cdot 1.282 \times 10^{-4} \;=\; 7818 \times 1.282 \times 10^{-4} \;=\; 1.0025$$

$$k_2 \;=\; 0.08 \times \exp(1.0025) \;=\; 0.08 \times 2.725 \;=\; 0.218\,\text{L·mol}^{-1}\text{·s}^{-1}$$

**Answer:** $-r_A = 1.318 \times 10^{-4}\,(1 - X)(0.5 - X)\,\text{mol·L}^{-1}\text{·s}^{-1}$; $-r_{A0} = 6.59 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{mol·L}^{-1}\text{·s}^{-1}$; $k_2 = 0.218\,\text{L·mol}^{-1}\text{·s}^{-1}$.

---

#### Model Solution — Question A(b)

**Catalyst Effect — Derivation:**

For an uncatalyzed reaction with activation energy $E_a$, the Arrhenius equation gives:
$$k \;=\; A\,\exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{RT}\right)$$

When a catalyst is introduced, it provides an alternative pathway with a *lower* activation energy $E_{a,\text{cat}}$, with $E_{a,\text{cat}} < E_a$:

$$k_{\text{cat}} \;=\; A\,\exp\!\left(-\frac{E_{a,\text{cat}}}{RT}\right)$$

**Step 1.** Form the ratio (assuming $A$ is approximately unchanged):
$$\frac{k_{\text{cat}}}{k} \;=\; \exp\!\left(\frac{E_a - E_{a,\text{cat}}}{RT}\right) \;>\; 1$$

Hence $k_{\text{cat}} > k$, meaning the rate is higher at the same temperature.

**Step 2.** As $T$ decreases, the exponent $\dfrac{E_a - E_{a,\text{cat}}}{RT}$ grows larger, so the catalyst's benefit is *more pronounced at lower temperatures*. This is why catalysts are critical for low-temperature industrial processes (e.g., ammonia synthesis, catalytic converters).

**Why catalyst does not change $K$:**

The **equilibrium constant** $K$ is related to the standard Gibbs free energy change:
$$\Delta G^{\circ} \;=\; -RT\ln K$$

A catalyst stabilizes the **transition state** but does **not** change the energies of reactants or products. Hence $\Delta G^{\circ}$ is unchanged, and so $K$ is unchanged. The catalyst merely accelerates the *approach* to equilibrium, not the *position* of equilibrium.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. ❌ **Confusing $E_a$ with $\Delta H$** — Activation energy is for the transition state, not the heat of reaction.
> 2. ❌ **Saying "catalyst increases the yield"** — Wrong! Catalyst changes rate, not equilibrium conversion.
> 3. ❌ **Forgetting to convert $E_a$ to J** when $R = 8.314\,\text{J·mol}^{-1}\text{·K}^{-1}$. If $E_a$ is given in kJ, multiply by 1000 first.
> 4. ❌ **Using $T$ in °C** in the Arrhenius equation — must convert to **K**.
> 5. ❌ **Dropping the $A$ factor's units** — keep $A$ in the same units as $k$.

---

### 📗 QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a)** For a first-order liquid-phase reaction $A \rightarrow \text{Products}$, the rate constant at 298 K is $k_1 = 2.5 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{s}^{-1}$ and at 318 K is $k_2 = 7.5 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{s}^{-1}$. Calculate:
**(i)** The activation energy $E_a$ using the Arrhenius equation.
**(ii)** The pre-exponential factor $A$.
**(iii)** The half-life at 350 K. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a neat schematic, explain the effect of pressure on the rate of a gas-phase reaction. Why is pressure a powerful variable for gas-phase reactions but not for liquid-phase ones? **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question B(a)

**(i) Activation energy $E_a$:**

Using the two-temperature Arrhenius form:
$$\ln\!\left(\frac{k_2}{k_1}\right) \;=\; \frac{E_a}{R}\!\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)$$

$$\frac{k_2}{k_1} \;=\; \frac{7.5 \times 10^{-4}}{2.5 \times 10^{-4}} \;=\; 3.0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \ln 3.0 \;=\; 1.0986$$

$$\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2} \;=\; \frac{1}{298} - \frac{1}{318} \;=\; \frac{318 - 298}{298 \times 318} \;=\; \frac{20}{94764} \;=\; 2.1108 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{K}^{-1}$$

$$E_a \;=\; \frac{R \cdot \ln(k_2/k_1)}{(1/T_1) - (1/T_2)} \;=\; \frac{8.314 \times 1.0986}{2.1108 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$E_a \;=\; \frac{9.134}{2.1108 \times 10^{-4}} \;=\; 43{,}277\,\text{J·mol}^{-1} \;\approx\; 43.3\,\text{kJ·mol}^{-1}$$

**(ii) Pre-exponential factor $A$:**

$$A \;=\; k_1 \exp\!\left(\frac{E_a}{RT_1}\right) \;=\; 2.5 \times 10^{-4} \times \exp\!\left(\frac{43277}{8.314 \times 298}\right)$$

$$\frac{E_a}{RT_1} \;=\; \frac{43277}{2477.6} \;=\; 17.467$$

$$A \;=\; 2.5 \times 10^{-4} \times \exp(17.467) \;=\; 2.5 \times 10^{-4} \times 3.80 \times 10^{7} \;\approx\; 9500\,\text{s}^{-1}$$

**(iii) Half-life at 350 K:**

First compute $k$ at 350 K:
$$\ln\!\left(\frac{k_3}{k_1}\right) \;=\; \frac{E_a}{R}\!\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_3}\right) \;=\; \frac{43277}{8.314}\!\left(\frac{1}{298} - \frac{1}{350}\right)$$

$$\frac{1}{298} - \frac{1}{350} \;=\; \frac{350 - 298}{298 \times 350} \;=\; \frac{52}{104300} \;=\; 4.985 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{K}^{-1}$$

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{k_3}{k_1}\right) \;=\; 5205.3 \times 4.985 \times 10^{-4} \;=\; 2.595$$

$$k_3 \;=\; 2.5 \times 10^{-4} \times \exp(2.595) \;=\; 2.5 \times 10^{-4} \times 13.40 \;=\; 3.35 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{s}^{-1}$$

Half-life:
$$t_{1/2} \;=\; \frac{0.693}{k_3} \;=\; \frac{0.693}{3.35 \times 10^{-3}} \;\approx\; 207\,\text{s}$$

**Answer:** $E_a \approx 43.3\,\text{kJ·mol}^{-1}$; $A \approx 9500\,\text{s}^{-1}$; $t_{1/2} \approx 207\,\text{s}$ at 350 K.

---

#### Model Solution — Question B(b)

**Effect of pressure on gas-phase rate — explanation:**

For an ideal gas, concentration and pressure are linearly related:
$$C_A \;=\; \frac{P_A}{RT} \;=\; \frac{y_A P}{RT}$$

For an elementary gas-phase reaction of order $n$, the rate law is:
$$-r_A \;=\; k\,C_A^{n} \;=\; k\!\left(\frac{P}{RT}\right)^{\!n}\,y_A^{n}$$

**Key observations:**
1. **Doubling $P$ at constant $T$** doubles $C_A$, hence **doubles** the rate for a first-order gas reaction, **quadruples** for a second-order, and so on.
2. The pressure effect is **isothermal** — no temperature change is needed. This is operationally attractive.
3. For reactions with $\Delta n \neq 0$, high pressure also shifts the equilibrium toward the side with fewer moles (Le Chatelier), making the effect *combined* on overall conversion.

**Why pressure is not a "powerful" variable for liquid-phase reactions:**

Liquids are nearly **incompressible**. Increasing pressure by 100 atm changes liquid density by less than a few percent, and hence $C_i$ barely changes. The rate of a liquid-phase reaction is therefore essentially pressure-insensitive in the practical operating range.

In contrast, gases have densities that scale linearly with $P$ (ideal-gas law), so a 10-fold pressure increase gives a 10-fold concentration increase. This is why industrial gas-phase processes (e.g., Haber–Bosch at 200–300 atm; polyethylene at 1000+ atm) routinely use pressure as a primary design handle.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Question B:**
> 1. ❌ **Mixing up $C_A$ with $P_A$** — these are *not* equal; they are linked by $C_A = P_A/RT$.
> 2. ❌ **Treating partial pressure as identical to total pressure** — use $P_A = y_A P$, not just $P$.
> 3. ❌ **Stating "rate is proportional to pressure"** without specifying reaction order — wrong; rate scales with $P^n$, where $n$ is the order.
> 4. ❌ **Forgetting the units of $R$** when substituting — if $C$ is in mol/L, $P$ in atm, use $R = 0.0821\,\text{L·atm·mol}^{-1}\text{·K}^{-1}$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Module 3**

- ☐ **Rate of reaction** is a *function* of concentration, temperature, pressure, catalyst, and (for heterogeneous/photochemical systems) surface area or radiation.
- ☐ The **rate law** has the form $-r_A = k(T)\cdot f(\text{concentrations})$; the Arrhenius equation gives $k(T) = A\exp(-E_a/RT)$.
- ☐ **Temperature is the most powerful handle** — exponential effect via $E_a/RT$. A 10 K rise typically doubles or triples the rate.
- ☐ **Pressure matters only for gases** — rate scales with $P^n$ for an $n^{\text{th}}$-order gas reaction. Liquids are nearly incompressible.
- ☐ **Catalysts** lower $E_a$ but **never** change $K$ (equilibrium constant). They are most effective at low temperatures.
- ☐ **For a first-order reaction**, $C_A = C_{A0}\exp(-kt)$ and $t_{1/2} = 0.693/k$ (independent of $C_{A0}$).
- ☐ **Units of $k$** depend on order: $\text{s}^{-1}$ for 1st, $\text{L·mol}^{-1}\text{·s}^{-1}$ for 2nd, $\text{L}^2\text{·mol}^{-2}\text{·s}^{-1}$ for 3rd.
- ☐ **Always use absolute temperature (K)** in Arrhenius, never °C.
- ☐ **Always use SI-consistent units** for $E_a$ and $R$ (both in J, or both in kJ with matching $R$).
- ☐ **Two-temperature Arrhenius** form is the workhorse for KTU numerical problems: $\ln(k_2/k_1) = (E_a/R)(1/T_1 - 1/T_2)$.
- ☐ **For heterogeneous reactions** with a solid catalyst, the rate includes surface area per volume, $a$ (m²·m⁻³): $-r_A = k\,a\,C_A^{n}$.
- ☐ **For photochemical reactions**, $-r_A$ depends on light intensity $I_\alpha$ and its order $a$: $-r_A = k\,I_\alpha^{a}\,C_A^{n}$.
- ☐ **Distinguish molecularity (theoretical, from mechanism)** from **order (experimental, from data)** — KTU tests this distinction explicitly.

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Variables Affecting Rate of Reaction**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Definition of Reaction Rate
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT637 - CHEMICAL TECHNOLOGY AND REACTION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Definition of Reaction Rate

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In **Chemical Reaction Engineering (CRE)**, the **rate of reaction** is formally defined as the **rate at which the number of moles of a specific chemical species changes per unit time per unit volume of the reacting system**.

Mathematically, the **rate of disappearance of reactant A** is expressed as:

$$-r_A = -\frac{1}{V}\frac{dN_A}{dt}$$

And the **rate of formation of product P** is expressed as:

$$r_P = +\frac{1}{V}\frac{dN_P}{dt}$$

Where:
- $N_A$ = number of moles of species A at time $t$
- $V$ = volume of the reactor system
- $t$ = time
- The negative sign for reactants indicates that $N_A$ decreases with time ($dN_A/dt < 0$), but $-r_A$ remains a **positive quantity**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Memorize Verbatim):**
> "The rate of reaction, $-r_A$, is defined as the moles of A reacting (disappearing) per unit time per unit volume of the reactor."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a **water tank with a drain at the bottom**:

- The **water level (concentration)** represents the **amount of reactant**.
- The **rate at which water flows out** is analogous to the **rate of reaction**.
- If you have a **larger tank (more volume)**, the same outflow rate causes a **slower drop in water level**. This is precisely why we divide by volume $V$ in the definition — to **normalize** the rate so that it is **independent of the system size**.

**Real-world industrial example:** In a refinery's catalytic cracker, hydrocarbons break down into smaller molecules every second. The reaction rate tells the engineer **how many kilograms of feedstock are being converted per cubic meter of catalyst per second** — a critical metric for sizing reactors and determining throughput.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction — Rate vs. Rate of Reaction:**
> - **Rate of reaction ($r_A$)**: Intensive property (independent of system size), measured in **mol/(m³·s)** or **mol/(L·s)**.
> - **Rate of formation/disappearance ($\frac{dN_A}{dt}$)**: Extensive property (depends on total amount), measured in **mol/s**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown of the Definition

The formal definition of reaction rate can be decomposed into the following logical steps:

1. **Identify a Reference Species**: Pick any species $i$ in the reaction mixture. Conventionally, the limiting reactant **A** is chosen as the reference.

2. **Measure Molar Change**: Calculate the change in the number of moles $dN_i$ of that species over an infinitesimal time interval $dt$.

3. **Normalize by Volume**: Divide by the reactor volume $V$ to obtain an **intensive** quantity that does not depend on how much material is present.

4. **Apply Sign Convention**:
   - For **reactants** (being consumed): add a **negative sign** so the rate is positive.
   - For **products** (being formed): keep the sign **positive** as $dN_P/dt > 0$.

5. **Result**: The reaction rate $-r_A$ is a **positive scalar** representing the speed of the chemical transformation.

### 2.2 Constant Volume Assumption (Most Common Case)

For the vast majority of **liquid-phase reactions** and **gas-phase reactions in batch reactors with constant T and P**, the volume $V$ remains essentially constant. In such cases, $V$ can be brought inside the derivative:

$$-r_A = -\frac{1}{V}\frac{dN_A}{dt} = -\frac{d(C_A \cdot V)}{V \cdot dt} = -\frac{V \, dC_A}{V \, dt} = -\frac{dC_A}{dt}$$

This simplification yields the **most commonly used textbook form**:

$$-r_A = -\frac{dC_A}{dt}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **When does this simplification fail?**
> - **Gas-phase reactions with significant mole change** (e.g., $2A \rightarrow B + C$): Volume changes as reaction proceeds.
> - **Variable-density systems** (e.g., polymerizations where density changes with conversion).
> - **Reactions in PFR/flow reactors with varying molar flow**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Reference Table

| **Formula** | **Description** | **Units** | **Applicable System** |
|---|---|---|---|
| $-r_A = -\dfrac{1}{V}\dfrac{dN_A}{dt}$ | General rate definition (reactant A) | mol/(m³·s) | Any reactor |
| $r_P = +\dfrac{1}{V}\dfrac{dN_P}{dt}$ | General rate definition (product P) | mol/(m³·s) | Any reactor |
| $-r_A = -\dfrac{dC_A}{dt}$ | Simplified form | mol/(m³·s) | **Constant volume only** |
| $\dfrac{r_A}{-a} = \dfrac{r_B}{-b} = \dfrac{r_C}{c} = \dfrac{r_D}{d}$ | Stoichiometric relation | dimensionless ratio | General reaction $aA + bB \rightarrow cC + dD$ |
| $r_i = \dfrac{1}{\nu_i}\dfrac{1}{V}\dfrac{dN_i}{dt}$ | Generalized rate with stoichiometric coefficient $\nu_i$ (negative for reactants, positive for products) | mol/(m³·s) | **Universal** |
| $\text{Rate} = k \cdot C_A^{\alpha} \cdot C_B^{\beta}$ | Power-law (n-th order) rate law | mol/(m³·s) | Homogeneous reactions |
| $C_A = C_{A0}(1 - X_A)$ | Concentration in terms of conversion $X_A$ | mol/m³ | Constant volume batch |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Reactor Sizing**: Knowing the rate determines the volume $V$ needed to achieve a target conversion.
- **Catalyst Design**: Industrial catalysts are evaluated based on how they enhance the intrinsic reaction rate.
- **Process Safety**: Highly exothermic reactions with very large rates require specialized cooling systems.
- **Environmental Engineering**: Biodegradation rates in wastewater treatment plants are computed using the same definition.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Relationship Between Rates of Different Species

**Starting Reaction:**

$$aA + bB \longrightarrow cC + dD$$

For every $a$ moles of A consumed, $b$ moles of B are consumed, and $c$ moles of C and $d$ moles of D are produced.

**Step 1**: Express the change in each species proportional to stoichiometric coefficients.

$$\frac{-dN_A}{a} = \frac{-dN_B}{b} = \frac{dN_C}{c} = \frac{dN_D}{d}$$

*Reasoning:* The stoichiometric coefficients dictate the molar ratio in which species are consumed/produced. Dividing each change by its respective coefficient gives a **common extensive reaction rate** $\frac{d\xi}{dt}$ where $\xi$ is the extent of reaction.

**Step 2**: Divide each term by the reactor volume $V$ (which is the same for all species in a well-mixed system).

$$\frac{-1}{V}\frac{dN_A}{a} = \frac{-1}{V}\frac{dN_B}{b} = \frac{1}{V}\frac{dN_C}{c} = \frac{1}{V}\frac{dN_D}{d}$$

*Reasoning:* Since $V$ is a scalar, it can be factored out. This converts the extensive quantities into intensive reaction rates.

**Step 3**: Recognize that each term is the rate of reaction with appropriate sign convention.

$$\frac{-r_A}{a} = \frac{-r_B}{b} = \frac{r_C}{c} = \frac{r_D}{d}$$

*Reasoning:* By definition, $-r_A = -\frac{1}{V}\frac{dN_A}{dt}$ (and similarly for other species). Substituting these definitions directly into Step 2 yields the canonical stoichiometric relationship.

**Step 4**: Rearrange to the commonly cited board-exam form.

$$\boxed{\frac{r_A}{-a} = \frac{r_B}{-b} = \frac{r_C}{c} = \frac{r_D}{d}}$$

*Reasoning:* The negative signs in the denominator ensure that all ratios are **positive numbers**. This is the form universally accepted in KTU board examinations.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Constant Volume Simplification

**Starting Point:**

$$-r_A = -\frac{1}{V}\frac{dN_A}{dt}$$

**Step 1**: Express moles as the product of concentration and volume: $N_A = C_A \cdot V$.

$$-r_A = -\frac{1}{V}\frac{d(C_A \cdot V)}{dt}$$

**Step 2**: Apply the product rule of differentiation: $\frac{d(C_A V)}{dt} = V\frac{dC_A}{dt} + C_A\frac{dV}{dt}$.

$$-r_A = -\frac{1}{V}\left[V\frac{dC_A}{dt} + C_A\frac{dV}{dt}\right]$$

**Step 3**: Assume **constant volume**, so $\frac{dV}{dt} = 0$.

$$-r_A = -\frac{1}{V}\left[V\frac{dC_A}{dt} + 0\right]$$

**Step 4**: Cancel $V$ from numerator and denominator.

$$\boxed{-r_A = -\frac{dC_A}{dt}}$$

*Reasoning:* This is the most frequently used form in batch reactor design and textbook problem-solving under isothermal, constant-density conditions.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Sign Convention Reference

| **Species Type** | **Change** | **Rate Symbol** | **Sign** | **Numerical Value** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Reactant A (being consumed) | $dN_A < 0$ | $-r_A$ | Positive | $-r_A > 0$ |
| Reactant B (being consumed) | $dN_B < 0$ | $-r_B$ | Positive | $-r_B > 0$ |
| Product C (being formed) | $dN_C > 0$ | $r_C$ | Positive | $r_C > 0$ |
| Product D (being formed) | $dN_D > 0$ | $r_D$ | Positive | $r_D > 0$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Hierarchical Decomposition of Reaction Rate Definition

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["REACTION RATE r"] --> node2["Reference Species Selection"]
    node2 --> node3["Reactant A -rA"]
    node2 --> node4["Product P +rP"]
    node3 --> node5["Normalize by Volume V"]
    node4 --> node5
    node5 --> node6["Constant Volume Assumption"]
    node6 --> node7["dCA/dt Form"]
    node5 --> node8["General Form dNA/dt"]
    node8 --> node9["Stoichiometric Relation"]
    node9 --> node10["rA/-a = rB/-b = rC/c = rD/d"]
    node7 --> node11["Apply to Reactor Types"]
    node10 --> node11
    node11 --> node12["Batch Reactor"]
    node11 --> node13["CSTR"]
    node11 --> node14["PFR"]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Sequence Flow: Rate Evaluation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stepA["Identify Reaction Stoichiometry"] --> stepB["Choose Reference Species"]
    stepB --> stepC["Measure dNi over dt"]
    stepC --> stepD["Divide by Reactor Volume V"]
    stepD --> stepE{"Is V constant?"}
    stepE -- Yes --> stepF["Use -rA = dCA/dt"]
    stepE -- No --> stepG["Use General Form -rA = 1/V dNA/dt"]
    stepF --> stepH["Compute Numerical Value with Units"]
    stepG --> stepH
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: From Definition to Application

| **Block Layer** | **Function** | **Output** |
|---|---|---|
| **Layer 1 — Stoichiometric Layer** | Defines molar ratios of consumption and formation | Stoichiometric coefficients $a, b, c, d$ |
| **Layer 2 — Differential Layer** | Captures instantaneous change in moles | $\frac{dN_i}{dt}$ |
| **Layer 3 — Normalization Layer** | Converts to intensive property via $V$ | $\frac{1}{V}\frac{dN_i}{dt}$ |
| **Layer 4 — Sign Convention Layer** | Applies negative sign for reactants | $-r_A$ (positive) for reactants |
| **Layer 5 — Stoichiometric Coupling** | Links rates of all species via $\nu_i$ | Unified rate $r$ for the reaction |
| **Layer 6 — Reactor Integration Layer** | Used in mass balances for design | Reactor sizing and conversion prediction |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

Define the rate of reaction $-r_A$ with respect to reactant A. State its SI units.

**Model Answer:**

The rate of reaction $-r_A$ is defined as the **number of moles of reactant A that disappear (react) per unit time per unit volume of the reacting system**.

$$-r_A = -\frac{1}{V}\frac{dN_A}{dt}$$

**SI Units:** $\text{mol}/(\text{m}^3 \cdot \text{s})$

The negative sign ensures that $-r_A$ is a **positive quantity**, since $dN_A/dt < 0$ for a reactant being consumed.

*(3 marks: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for the equation, 1 mark for units with correct sign convention)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

For the reaction $2A + B \rightarrow 3C + D$, establish the relationship between the rates $-r_A$, $-r_B$, $r_C$, and $r_D$.

**Model Answer:**

Applying the stoichiometric relation:

$$\frac{r_A}{-2} = \frac{r_B}{-1} = \frac{r_C}{3} = \frac{r_D}{1}$$

Equivalently:

$$\frac{-r_A}{2} = \frac{-r_B}{1} = \frac{r_C}{3} = \frac{r_D}{1}$$

This relationship arises because the **stoichiometric coefficients dictate the molar ratios** in which the species are consumed and produced. For every 2 moles of A reacting, 1 mole of B reacts, 3 moles of C form, and 1 mole of D forms.

*(3 marks: 2 marks for the correct relationship, 1 mark for the justification)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Define the rate of reaction. Explain the significance of the negative sign in $-r_A$ and the factor $1/V$ in the definition. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the elementary reaction $\text{N}_2 + 3\text{H}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{NH}_3$, the rate of formation of $\text{NH}_3$ is measured as $r_{\text{NH}_3} = 0.04 \text{ mol}/(\text{L} \cdot \text{s})$. Calculate the rates of disappearance of $\text{N}_2$ and $\text{H}_2$, and verify the stoichiometric consistency. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Definition and Significance (7 marks):**

**Definition:** The rate of reaction with respect to a species $i$ is the change in the number of moles of $i$ per unit time per unit volume of the reacting mixture:

$$-r_A = -\frac{1}{V}\frac{dN_A}{dt}$$

**Significance of the negative sign (3 marks):**
- For reactants, $N_A$ **decreases** with time, so $\frac{dN_A}{dt} < 0$.
- The negative sign converts this negative derivative into a **positive rate value**.
- This ensures that all reported reaction rates are **positive numbers**, which is the engineering convention for rates.

**Significance of $1/V$ (3 marks):**
- $V$ in the denominator normalizes the rate to an **intensive property** (independent of system size).
- Without this normalization, two reactors with different volumes producing the same moles of product would have different "rates," which is not useful for comparison.
- It allows the rate to be expressed per unit volume (e.g., $\text{mol}/(\text{L} \cdot \text{s})$), enabling **scale-up** calculations.

**Constant volume simplification (1 mark):** If $V$ is constant, $-r_A = -\frac{dC_A}{dt}$.

---

**(b) Numerical Calculation (7 marks):**

**Given:** $r_{\text{NH}_3} = 0.04 \text{ mol}/(\text{L} \cdot \text{s})$

**Step 1: Apply the stoichiometric relation.**

$$\frac{r_{\text{N}_2}}{-1} = \frac{r_{\text{H}_2}}{-3} = \frac{r_{\text{NH}_3}}{2}$$

**Step 2: Calculate $-r_{\text{N}_2}$.**

From the relation:
$$\frac{-r_{\text{N}_2}}{1} = \frac{r_{\text{NH}_3}}{2}$$

$$-r_{\text{N}_2} = \frac{r_{\text{NH}_3}}{2} = \frac{0.04}{2} = 0.02 \text{ mol}/(\text{L} \cdot \text{s})$$

**Step 3: Calculate $-r_{\text{H}_2}$.**

$$\frac{-r_{\text{H}_2}}{3} = \frac{r_{\text{NH}_3}}{2}$$

$$-r_{\text{H}_2} = \frac{3 \times r_{\text{NH}_3}}{2} = \frac{3 \times 0.04}{2} = 0.06 \text{ mol}/(\text{L} \cdot \text{s})$$

**Step 4: Verify stoichiometric consistency.**

Check that the ratio of rates equals the ratio of stoichiometric coefficients:

$$\frac{-r_{\text{N}_2}}{1} : \frac{-r_{\text{H}_2}}{3} : \frac{r_{\text{NH}_3}}{2} = 0.02 : 0.02 : 0.02 = 1 : 1 : 1 \checkmark$$

The stoichiometric relation is satisfied.

**Final Answers:**
- $-r_{\text{N}_2} = 0.02 \text{ mol}/(\text{L} \cdot \text{s})$
- $-r_{\text{H}_2} = 0.06 \text{ mol}/(\text{L} \cdot \text{s})$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Supplementary, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Derive the general expression relating the rates of all species in the reaction $aA + bB \rightarrow cC + dD$ starting from the definition of the rate of reaction. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A liquid-phase reaction $A \rightarrow B$ is carried out in a constant-volume batch reactor. The initial concentration of A is $C_{A0} = 2.0 \text{ mol/L}$. After 10 minutes, the concentration of A drops to $C_A = 0.5 \text{ mol/L}$. Calculate the average rate of reaction over this interval. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Write the reaction: $aA + bB \rightarrow cC + dD$.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Apply the principle that the molar changes are proportional to stoichiometric coefficients:

$$\frac{-dN_A}{a} = \frac{-dN_B}{b} = \frac{dN_C}{c} = \frac{dN_D}{d}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Divide throughout by $V$:

$$\frac{-1}{V}\frac{dN_A}{a} = \frac{-1}{V}\frac{dN_B}{b} = \frac{1}{V}\frac{dN_C}{c} = \frac{1}{V}\frac{dN_D}{d}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Substitute the rate definitions:

$$\boxed{\frac{r_A}{-a} = \frac{r_B}{-b} = \frac{r_C}{c} = \frac{r_D}{d}}$$

---

**(b) Numerical Calculation (7 marks):**

**Given:** $C_{A0} = 2.0 \text{ mol/L}$, $C_A = 0.5 \text{ mol/L}$, $\Delta t = 10 \text{ min}$.

**Step 1 (2 marks):** For a constant-volume system, the average rate is:

$$-r_A = -\frac{\Delta C_A}{\Delta t} = -\frac{C_A - C_{A0}}{t - t_0}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Substitute the values:

$$-r_A = -\frac{0.5 - 2.0}{10 - 0} = -\frac{-1.5}{10} = 0.15 \text{ mol}/(\text{L} \cdot \text{min})$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Convert to standard SI units (optional but recommended):

$$-r_A = \frac{0.15}{60} = 0.0025 \text{ mol}/(\text{L} \cdot \text{s}) = 2.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mol}/(\text{L} \cdot \text{s})$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Final answer:

$$-r_A = 0.15 \text{ mol}/(\text{L} \cdot \text{min}) \quad \text{or} \quad 2.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mol}/(\text{L} \cdot \text{s})$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Loss Areas:**
> 1. **Sign Convention Error (–2 marks):** Writing $r_A = \frac{1}{V}\frac{dN_A}{dt}$ without the negative sign for a reactant. Always add the negative sign for **reactants**.
> 2. **Forgetting to Divide by V (–2 marks):** Writing $-r_A = -\frac{dN_A}{dt}$ without the volume term. The $V$ factor is **mandatory** for intensive rate.
> 3. **Stoichiometric Ratio Reversal (–2 marks):** Writing $\frac{r_A}{a} = \frac{r_B}{b}$ instead of $\frac{r_A}{-a} = \frac{r_B}{-b}$. Reactants must carry negative signs in the denominator.
> 4. **Wrong Units (–1 mark):** Writing $\text{mol/s}$ instead of $\text{mol}/(\text{m}^3 \cdot \text{s})$. The volume normalization must appear in units.
> 5. **Confusing Rate with Conversion (–1 mark):** $-r_A$ is a rate (intensive), while $X_A$ is a dimensionless conversion. They are **not** interchangeable.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- ✅ **Reaction Rate Definition:** $-r_A = -\frac{1}{V}\frac{dN_A}{dt}$ — moles of A reacting per unit time per unit volume.
- ✅ **SI Units:** $\text{mol}/(\text{m}^3 \cdot \text{s})$; CGS variant: $\text{mol}/(\text{L} \cdot \text{s})$.
- ✅ **Sign Convention:** Negative sign is added for reactants so that the reported rate is always positive.
- ✅ **$1/V$ Normalization:** Converts the rate from an extensive to an intensive property, enabling scale-independent comparison.
- ✅ **Constant Volume Simplification:** When $V$ = constant, $-r_A = -\frac{dC_A}{dt}$.
- ✅ **Stoichiometric Relation:** $\frac{r_A}{-a} = \frac{r_B}{-b} = \frac{r_C}{c} = \frac{r_D}{d}$ — links rates of all species in the reaction.
- ✅ **Reference Species Choice:** Any species can be chosen; the rates of all others follow from stoichiometry.
- ✅ **Intensive vs. Extensive:** $\frac{dN_A}{dt}$ is extensive; $-r_A = \frac{1}{V}\frac{dN_A}{dt}$ is intensive.
- ✅ **Industrial Relevance:** Reactor sizing, catalyst screening, process safety, and environmental engineering all rely on this definition.
- ✅ **Board Exam Formula:** Always write the boxed relation $\frac{r_A}{-a} = \frac{r_B}{-b} = \frac{r_C}{c} = \frac{r_D}{d}$ with negative signs on the reactant denominators.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Kinetics of Homogeneous Reactions - Concentration Dependent Term of a Rate Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT637 - CHEMICAL TECHNOLOGY AND REACTION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Kinetics of Homogeneous Reactions - Concentration Dependent Term of a Rate Equation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

For a **single homogeneous reaction** occurring in a single phase, the **rate of reaction** ($-r_A$) is expressed as the product of two fundamentally distinct terms:

$$-r_A = \left(\text{rate constant term}\right) \times \left(\text{concentration dependent term}\right)$$

Mathematically,

$$-r_A = k \cdot f(C_A, C_B, C_C, \ldots)$$

where:
- $-r_A$ = rate of disappearance of reactant **A**, expressed in **(kmol · m⁻³ · s⁻¹)** or equivalently **(mol · L⁻¹ · s⁻¹)**.
- $k$ = **reaction rate constant** (or specific reaction rate), whose units are **NOT fixed** but depend on the order of reaction.
- $f(C_A, C_B, \ldots)$ = the **concentration dependent term** — the central object of study in this topic. It is a function ONLY of the concentrations of the reacting species (and, for reversible reactions, sometimes products).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The concentration dependent term $f(C_A)$ is the heart of reaction kinetics.** The rate constant $k$ only captures the *temperature dependence* (via Arrhenius law), while $f(C_A)$ captures the *composition dependence* of the reaction rate. KTU examiners routinely isolate this term for direct examination.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Traffic Highway" Model

Imagine a multi-lane highway where vehicles (molecules) flow toward a destination (the reaction products). The **speed at which vehicles clear the highway** (reaction rate) depends on TWO independent factors:

| Real-World Analogy | Chemical Kinetics Counterpart |
|---|---|
| Vehicle density per lane | Concentration of reactant $C_A$ |
| Posted speed limit (weather-dependent) | Rate constant $k$ (temperature-dependent) |
| Total cars passing a checkpoint per hour | Reaction rate $-r_A$ |

If the posted speed limit is fixed ($k$ = constant), the rate of cars passing depends purely on **how many cars are on the road** — that is the concentration dependent term $f(C_A)$. A nearly empty road means fewer collisions (low $f(C_A)$); a packed road means many collisions per unit time (high $f(C_A)$).

### 1.3 Why Isolate the Concentration Dependent Term?

The concentration dependent term $f(C_A)$ is treated as **separable** from $k$ because:

1. It depends **only on composition** (which the engineer can manipulate via flow rates, recycle, etc.).
2. It is **independent of temperature** at the level of form (only $k$ changes with $T$).
3. It encapsulates the **reaction order** and **molecularity** information.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The course outcome (CO) mapped to this module emphasizes that students must be able to *identify, write, and manipulate* the concentration dependent term for orders 0, 1, 2, 3, and $n$, plus elementary reversible and autocatalytic forms.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of the Rate Equation

The generalized rate equation is decomposed as:

$$-r_A = k \cdot f(C_A, C_B, C_C, C_D)$$

The functional form of $f(\cdot)$ is **empirically determined** (or derived from mechanism for elementary reactions). The most commonly encountered forms in the KTU syllabus are categorized below.

### 2.2 Order of Reaction — The Critical Parameter

The **order** of a reaction with respect to a species is defined as the exponent of the concentration of that species in the rate law.

For a general rate law:

$$-r_A = k \, C_A^{\alpha} \, C_B^{\beta} \, C_C^{\gamma} \cdots$$

- $\alpha$ = order w.r.t. A
- $\beta$ = order w.r.t. B
- $n = \alpha + \beta + \gamma + \cdots$ = **overall order of reaction**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Order ≠ Molecularity.** *Molecularity* is a theoretical concept referring to the number of molecules colliding in an elementary step (always an integer: 1, 2, 3). *Order* is an empirical quantity and CAN be fractional, zero, or negative.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The table below is the **exam-ready cheat sheet** for the concentration dependent term across standard reaction orders.

**Irreversible Reactions:**

| Order $n$ | Reaction Type | Rate Equation $-r_A$ | Concentration Term $f(C_A)$ | Units of $k$ |
|:---:|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **0** | $A \rightarrow \text{Products}$ | $-r_A = k$ | $f(C_A) = 1$ | $\text{kmol} \cdot \text{m}^{-3} \cdot \text{s}^{-1}$ |
| **1** | $A \rightarrow \text{Products}$ | $-r_A = k \, C_A$ | $f(C_A) = C_A$ | $\text{s}^{-1}$ |
| **2** | $A + A \rightarrow \text{Products}$ | $-r_A = k \, C_A^{2}$ | $f(C_A) = C_A^{2}$ | $\text{m}^{3} \cdot \text{kmol}^{-1} \cdot \text{s}^{-1}$ |
| **2** | $A + B \rightarrow \text{Products}$ | $-r_A = k \, C_A \, C_B$ | $f(C_A, C_B) = C_A \, C_B$ | $\text{m}^{3} \cdot \text{kmol}^{-1} \cdot \text{s}^{-1}$ |
| **3** | $A + B + C \rightarrow \text{Products}$ | $-r_A = k \, C_A \, C_B \, C_C$ | $f(C_A, C_B, C_C) = C_A C_B C_C$ | $\text{m}^{6} \cdot \text{kmol}^{-2} \cdot \text{s}^{-1}$ |
| **n** | $A \rightarrow \text{Products}$ | $-r_A = k \, C_A^{n}$ | $f(C_A) = C_A^{n}$ | $(\text{m}^{3})^{n-1} \, (\text{kmol})^{1-n} \, \text{s}^{-1}$ |

**Reversible Elementary Reactions:**

| Reaction Type | Rate Equation $-r_A$ | Concentration Term $f(C_A, C_B)$ |
|:---|:---|:---|
| $A \rightleftharpoons B$ | $-r_A = k \left(C_A - \dfrac{C_B}{K_C}\right)$ | $f(C_A, C_B) = C_A - \dfrac{C_B}{K_C}$ |
| $A + B \rightleftharpoons C + D$ | $-r_A = k \left(C_A C_B - \dfrac{C_C C_D}{K_C}\right)$ | $f(C_A, C_B, C_C, C_D) = C_A C_B - \dfrac{C_C C_D}{K_C}$ |

**Autocatalytic Reactions:**

| Reaction Type | Rate Equation $-r_A$ | Concentration Term |
|:---|:---|:---|
| $A + R \rightarrow R + R$ (autocatalytic) | $-r_A = k \, C_A \, C_R$ | $f(C_A, C_R) = C_A \, C_R$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Reverse the pipe escape rule:** Whenever you see $\vert x \vert$ in textbook notation, write it as $\lvert x \rvert$ or $\vert x \vert$ in LaTeX within prose. In markdown table cells, prefer writing $\lvert x \rvert$ to avoid table-breaking.

### 2.4 Engineering Utility — Where This Matters in Practice

1. **Reactor Design (CSTR & PFR):** The concentration dependent term is integrated across the reactor volume in a PFR, or solved directly via material balance in a CSTR. Without $f(C_A)$, reactor sizing is impossible.
2. **Process Control & Optimization:** Knowing $f(C_A)$ allows engineers to predict conversion at different residence times.
3. **Catalyst Selection:** For autocatalytic reactions, $f(C_A, C_R)$ reveals that the reaction is self-accelerating — crucial in biochemical and polymerization reactors.
4. **Safety Analysis:** Highly nonlinear $f(C_A)$ (e.g., third-order) implies a runaway risk — used in thermal hazard assessment.

### 2.5 How to Determine $f(C_A)$ — The Logic Chain

Step 1 → Write the **stoichiometric equation** carefully, noting stoichiometric coefficients.
Step 2 → For **elementary reactions**, write the rate law directly using molecularity as order.
Step 3 → For **non-elementary reactions**, use experimental concentration-vs-time data.
Step 4 → Apply **linearization** by taking logarithms: $\ln(-r_A) = \ln k + \alpha \ln C_A + \beta \ln C_B + \cdots$ — a plot of $\ln(-r_A)$ vs $\ln C_A$ yields slope $\alpha$.
Step 5 → Verify via **integral method** by comparing $-r_A = -dC_A/dt$ data against integrated forms.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Rate Constant Units as a Function of Reaction Order

This derivation is **frequently asked in KTU Part A** for 3 marks.

**Starting Premise:** A general rate law for reaction of order $n$ in a single species:

$$-r_A = k \, C_A^{n}$$

**Step 1 — Identify the units of each quantity.**

$$[-r_A] = \frac{\text{kmol}}{\text{m}^{3} \cdot \text{s}}, \quad [C_A] = \frac{\text{kmol}}{\text{m}^{3}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute units into the rate law.**

$$\frac{\text{kmol}}{\text{m}^{3} \cdot \text{s}} = [k] \cdot \left(\frac{\text{kmol}}{\text{m}^{3}}\right)^{n}$$

**Step 3 — Solve algebraically for $[k]$.**

$$\begin{aligned}
[k] &= \frac{\text{kmol}}{\text{m}^{3} \cdot \text{s}} \cdot \left(\frac{\text{m}^{3}}{\text{kmol}}\right)^{n} \\
[k] &= \frac{\text{kmol}}{\text{m}^{3} \cdot \text{s}} \cdot \frac{\text{m}^{3n}}{\text{kmol}^{n}} \\
[k] &= \text{kmol}^{1-n} \cdot \text{m}^{3n-3} \cdot \text{s}^{-1}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Verification for special cases.**
- For $n = 0$: $[k] = \text{kmol} \cdot \text{m}^{-3} \cdot \text{s}^{-1}$ ✓ (matches row 1 of the table).
- For $n = 1$: $[k] = \text{s}^{-1}$ ✓ (matches row 2).
- For $n = 2$: $[k] = \text{kmol}^{-1} \cdot \text{m}^{3} \cdot \text{s}^{-1}$ ✓ (matches row 3).

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for unit deduction:** *General unit rule:* $[k] = (\text{conc})^{1-n} \cdot (\text{time})^{-1}$. Always the same form regardless of units chosen.

### 3.2 Derivation: Concentration Dependent Term for a Second-Order Reaction with Two Reactants

Consider the elementary reaction $A + B \rightarrow \text{Products}$.

**Step 1 — Molecularity = 2**, so by the elementary reaction rule, order = molecularity. Thus order is 1 with respect to A AND 1 with respect to B.

**Step 2 — Write the rate law directly from stoichiometry.**

$$-r_A = k \, C_A^{1} \, C_B^{1} = k \, C_A \, C_B$$

**Step 3 — The concentration dependent term is therefore:**

$$f(C_A, C_B) = C_A \, C_B$$

**Step 4 — Express in terms of conversion.** Let $\nu_A = \nu_B = 1$ and the initial concentrations be $C_{A0}$ and $C_{B0}$ with stoichiometric ratio $\Theta_B = C_{B0}/C_{A0}$.

The concentration of A and B at conversion $X_A$ becomes:

$$C_A = C_{A0}(1 - X_A), \quad C_B = C_{B0} - \frac{\nu_B}{\nu_A} C_{A0} X_A = C_{A0}(\Theta_B - X_A)$$

**Step 5 — Substitute into the concentration dependent term.**

$$\begin{aligned}
f(C_A, C_B) &= C_A \, C_B \\
&= C_{A0}(1 - X_A) \cdot C_{A0}(\Theta_B - X_A) \\
&= C_{A0}^{2}(1 - X_A)(\Theta_B - X_A)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — The complete rate equation in terms of conversion becomes:**

$$-r_A = k \, C_{A0}^{2} \, (1 - X_A)(\Theta_B - X_A)$$

This is the form used to **size a PFR** by integration of $dV/F_{A0} = dX_A / (-r_A)$.

### 3.3 Derivation: Concentration Dependent Term for a Reversible Elementary Reaction

Consider $A \rightleftharpoons B$ (elementary, single-step both directions).

**Step 1 — Write the forward and reverse elementary rate laws.**

$$\text{Forward rate: } r_{\text{fwd}} = k_f \, C_A, \quad \text{Reverse rate: } r_{\text{rev}} = k_r \, C_B$$

**Step 2 — Net rate of disappearance of A is the difference:**

$$-r_A = r_{\text{fwd}} - r_{\text{rev}} = k_f \, C_A - k_r \, C_B$$

**Step 3 — At equilibrium, $-r_A = 0$, so $k_f C_{Ae} = k_r C_{Be}$. Define the equilibrium constant:**

$$K_C = \frac{k_f}{k_r} = \frac{C_{Be}}{C_{Ae}}$$

**Step 4 — Eliminate $k_r$ in favor of $k_f$ and $K_C$:**

$$k_r = \frac{k_f}{K_C}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute back into the rate law:**

$$\begin{aligned}
-r_A &= k_f \, C_A - \frac{k_f}{K_C} C_B \\
-r_A &= k_f \left(C_A - \frac{C_B}{K_C}\right)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Identify the concentration dependent term:**

$$\boxed{f(C_A, C_B) = C_A - \frac{C_B}{K_C}}$$

This is the form used to **predict equilibrium approach** and **reactor yield** for reversible systems.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Order to Functional Behavior

| Order $n$ | $f(C_A)$ form | Behavior at low $C_A$ | Behavior at high $C_A$ | Common Real-World Examples |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| 0 | $1$ | Constant rate | Constant rate | Surface-catalyzed (saturated) reactions |
| 1 | $C_A$ | Linear in $C_A$ | Linear in $C_A$ | Radioactive decay, many first-order decompositions |
| 2 | $C_A^{2}$ | Very slow | Quadratically fast | Dimerization reactions |
| $n$ | $C_A^{n}$ | Vanishing | Strongly accelerating | Polymerization, complex chain reactions |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of the Rate Equation

The diagram below illustrates the **modular decomposition** of the rate equation and shows how the concentration dependent term interfaces with the rate constant, reactor type, and design equation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Reaction System Input]:::input --> B0[Identify Reaction Stoichiometry]
    B0 --> C0{Elementary or Non-Elementary?}
    C0 -->|Elementary| D0[Apply Molecularity Rule]
    C0 -->|Non-Elementary| E0[Use Experimental Data and Linearization]
    D0 --> F0[Write Concentration Dependent Term fCA]
    E0 --> F0
    F0 --> G0[Combine with Rate Constant k]
    G0 --> H0[Assemble Full Rate Law -rA]
    H0 --> I0{Choose Reactor Type}
    I0 -->|PFR| J0[PFR Design Equation dV FA0 dX A -rA]
    I0 -->|CSTR| K0[CSTR Design Equation V FA0 X A -rA exit]
    J0 --> L0[Reactor Volume V]
    K0 --> L0
    L0 --> M0[Final Design Specification]:::output

    classDef input fill:#FFD580,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#A0E0A0,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Reaction Order Classification

The diagram below maps the **classification tree** for the concentration dependent term based on order, reversibility, and mechanism type.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    N0[Concentration Dependent Term fCA]:::root --> N1[Order Based Classification]
    N0 --> N2[Reversibility Based Classification]
    N0 --> N3[Mechanism Based Classification]

    N1 --> N1A[Zero Order f equals 1]
    N1 --> N1B[First Order f equals CA]
    N1 --> N1C[Second Order]
    N1C --> N1C1[Same Species f equals CA squared]
    N1C --> N1C2[Different Species f equals CA CB]
    N1 --> N1D[nth Order f equals CA to the n]

    N2 --> N2A[Irreversible f equals CA to the n]
    N2 --> N2B[Reversible f equals CA minus CB over KC]

    N3 --> N3A[Elementary Order equals Molecularity]
    N3 --> N3B[Autocatalytic f equals CA CR]
    N3 --> N3C[Non Elementary Empirical Order]

    classDef root fill:#FF9090,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Schematic Data Flow for Reactor Sizing Using $f(C_A)$

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Stoichiometry] --> P2[Concentration Dependent Term fCA]
    P3[Temperature T] --> P4[Arrhenius Equation k equals A exp -Ea over RT]
    P2 --> P5[Assemble -rA equals k fCA]
    P4 --> P5
    P5 --> P6[Select Reactor Model]
    P6 --> P7[Integrate Design Equation]
    P7 --> P8[Reactor Volume V]
    P8 --> P9[Equipment Specification and Cost]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The block diagrams highlight that the *only path* from stoichiometry to reactor volume passes through the concentration dependent term. Therefore, an error in $f(C_A)$ propagates directly into an error in $V$.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Define the term *order of reaction*. How is it different from *molecularity*?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Order of Reaction (1 mark):** It is the sum of the exponents of the concentration terms in the rate law. For $-r_A = k C_A^{\alpha} C_B^{\beta}$, order $= \alpha + \beta$. It is an empirical quantity.
- **Molecularity (1 mark):** It is the number of molecules colliding simultaneously in an elementary step. It is always an integer (1, 2, or 3) and is a theoretical concept.
- **Key Differences (1 mark):** Order may be fractional/zero/negative; molecularity is always a positive integer. Order applies to overall reaction (elementary or complex); molecularity applies only to elementary steps. They coincide ONLY for elementary reactions.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** For the irreversible reaction $A \rightarrow B$ which is third-order, write the rate equation and give the units of the rate constant $k$.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Rate Equation (2 marks):** $-r_A = k C_A^{3}$. The concentration dependent term is $f(C_A) = C_A^{3}$.
- **Units of $k$ (1 mark):** Using $[-r_A] = \text{kmol} \cdot \text{m}^{-3} \cdot \text{s}^{-1}$ and $[C_A] = \text{kmol} \cdot \text{m}^{-3}$:

$$[k] = \frac{\text{kmol}}{\text{m}^{3} \cdot \text{s}} \cdot \left(\frac{\text{m}^{3}}{\text{kmol}}\right)^{3} = \text{m}^{6} \cdot \text{kmol}^{-2} \cdot \text{s}^{-1}$$

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

**CHOICE OPTION A — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

> **Q.A(a) [7 marks]:** For the elementary reaction $2A + B \rightarrow C$ occurring in the liquid phase, derive the concentration dependent term $f(C_A, C_B)$ in terms of conversion $X_A$. State clearly the assumption regarding the stoichiometric ratio $\Theta_B$.

**Solution (7 marks):**

Step 1 (1 mark): Since the reaction is **elementary**, order = molecularity. The stoichiometric coefficients are $\nu_A = 2$, $\nu_B = 1$. Therefore the rate law is:

$$-r_A = k \, C_A^{2} \, C_B$$

Step 2 (1 mark): Identify the concentration dependent term:

$$f(C_A, C_B) = C_A^{2} \, C_B$$

Step 3 (1 mark): Express concentrations at conversion $X_A$. With $\nu_A = 2$, the rate of disappearance of A is twice that of B:

$$C_A = C_{A0}(1 - X_A), \quad C_B = C_{B0} - \frac{\nu_B}{\nu_A} C_{A0} X_A = C_{B0} - \frac{1}{2} C_{A0} X_A$$

Step 4 (1 mark): Define the stoichiometric ratio: $\Theta_B = \dfrac{C_{B0}}{C_{A0}}$. Then $C_B = C_{A0}(\Theta_B - X_A/2)$.

Step 5 (2 marks): Substitute back into $f$:

$$f(C_A, C_B) = \left[C_{A0}(1 - X_A)\right]^{2} \cdot C_{A0}\left(\Theta_B - \frac{X_A}{2}\right) = C_{A0}^{3}(1 - X_A)^{2}\left(\Theta_B - \frac{X_A}{2}\right)$$

Step 6 (1 mark): The **assumption** is that B is **not in stoichiometric excess** ($\Theta_B < \nu_B/\nu_A = 1/2$), otherwise $C_B$ would become negative before $C_A$ is exhausted.

> **Q.A(b) [7 marks]:** Write the rate equation for the elementary reversible reaction $A + 2B \rightleftharpoons 3C$ and identify the concentration dependent term. Briefly explain the physical meaning of the term $C_C^{3}/K_C$.

**Solution (7 marks):**

Step 1 (2 marks): Since elementary, both directions follow molecularity:

$$-r_A = k_f \, C_A \, C_B^{2} - k_r \, C_C^{3}$$

Step 2 (2 marks): Define $K_C = k_f / k_r$. Eliminate $k_r$:

$$-r_A = k_f \left(C_A \, C_B^{2} - \frac{C_C^{3}}{K_C}\right)$$

Step 3 (2 marks): Concentration dependent term:

$$f(C_A, C_B, C_C) = C_A \, C_B^{2} - \frac{C_C^{3}}{K_C}$$

Step 4 (1 mark): The term $C_C^{3}/K_C$ is the **equilibrium-converted reverse rate**. It represents the *effective reverse driving force* — at equilibrium, this term equals $C_A C_B^{2}$, making $-r_A = 0$.

---

**CHOICE OPTION B — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

> **Q.B(a) [7 marks]:** Explain, with a worked example, how the concentration dependent term $f(C_A)$ is experimentally determined using the **method of initial rates** for the reaction $A + B \rightarrow \text{Products}$.

**Solution (7 marks):**

Step 1 (1 mark): In the method of initial rates, we measure the **initial rate** $-r_{A0}$ for several different initial concentrations $C_{A0}$ (with $C_{B0}$ held constant) and vice versa.

Step 2 (1 mark): For a power-law form: $-r_{A0} = k C_{A0}^{\alpha} C_{B0}^{\beta}$.

Step 3 (1 mark): Take natural logarithm:

$$\ln(-r_{A0}) = \ln k + \alpha \ln C_{A0} + \beta \ln C_{B0}$$

Step 4 (1 mark): Run #1: Fix $C_{B0}$, vary $C_{A0}$. Plot $\ln(-r_{A0})$ vs $\ln C_{A0}$. Slope = $\alpha$ = **order w.r.t. A**.

Step 5 (1 mark): Run #2: Fix $C_{A0}$, vary $C_{B0}$. Plot $\ln(-r_{A0})$ vs $\ln C_{B0}$. Slope = $\beta$ = **order w.r.t. B**.

Step 6 (1 mark): Worked example: Suppose $-r_{A0}$ doubles when $C_{A0}$ is doubled (with $C_{B0}$ fixed): then $\alpha = 1$. If $-r_{A0}$ quadruples when $C_{B0}$ is doubled: then $\beta = 2$. Therefore $f(C_A, C_B) = C_A \, C_B^{2}$.

Step 7 (1 mark): The **concentration dependent term is now fully identified** and can be combined with $k$ (from intercept $\ln k$) to give the full rate law.

> **Q.B(b) [7 marks]:** For the **autocatalytic reaction** $A + R \rightarrow R + 2R$ (where R is a product that catalyzes the reaction), derive the concentration dependent term. Comment on its implication for reactor selection.

**Solution (7 marks):**

Step 1 (1 mark): This is an autocatalytic reaction — R is both a product and a catalyst. Treating the step as elementary:

$$-r_A = k \, C_A \, C_R$$

Step 2 (1 mark): Concentration dependent term:

$$f(C_A, C_R) = C_A \, C_R$$

Step 3 (1 mark): At $t = 0$, $C_{A0}$ is large but $C_{R0}$ is very small (often near zero). Therefore, $-r_A$ starts very small.

Step 4 (1 mark): As the reaction proceeds, $C_A$ decreases but $C_R$ increases. The product $C_A \cdot C_R$ **first increases, reaches a maximum, then decreases**. The maximum rate occurs at an intermediate conversion.

Step 5 (1 mark): **Reactor Selection Implication:**
- A **single CSTR** cannot achieve high conversion in a single pass — autocatalytic reactions perform poorly in simple CSTRs because the feed lacks catalyst R.
- A **PFR** with **recycle** (or a **CSTR train**) is preferred, as some product R is mixed with the feed, immediately accelerating the rate.

Step 6 (1 mark): For ideal batch: $C_A = C_{A0}(1 - X_A)$ and $C_R = C_{R0} + (\nu_R/\nu_A) C_{A0} X_A$ (assuming R is produced). Substituting gives the full $f(C_A, C_R)$ in terms of conversion.

Step 7 (1 mark): The optimal operating conversion in a PFR with recycle must be solved from the design equation — illustrating the engineering relevance of the form $f(C_A, C_R) = C_A C_R$.

---

### 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Forgetting to include products in $f(C_A)$ for reversible reactions** — students often write only the forward concentration term, losing 3–4 marks.
> 2. **Wrong units of $k$** — a single mistake here propagates; verify using $[k] = (\text{conc})^{1-n} \cdot \text{time}^{-1}$.
> 3. **Confusing order with stoichiometric coefficient for non-elementary reactions** — order is empirical, NOT taken from stoichiometry unless the reaction is elementary.
> 4. **Missing the factor $\Theta_B = C_{B0}/C_{A0}$** when expressing $f$ in terms of conversion — this is a frequently tested 2-mark item.
> 5. **Sign errors in reversible rate** — remember: forward minus reverse.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- The rate equation is **two-part**: $-r_A = k \cdot f(C_A, C_B, \ldots)$. The constant $k$ handles temperature; the term $f$ handles composition.
- The concentration dependent term is **always expressible as a power-law** for standard orders: $f(C_A) = C_A^{n}$ for order $n$ in a single reactant.
- For **multiple reactants** in elementary reactions: $f(C_A, C_B) = C_A^{\nu_A} C_B^{\nu_B}$.
- For **reversible reactions**: $f$ contains both forward concentration terms and a backward term divided by $K_C$.
- For **autocatalytic reactions**: $f$ includes a product (catalyst) concentration, e.g., $C_A C_R$.
- **Units of $k$ depend on order** via the relation $[k] = \text{conc}^{1-n} \cdot \text{time}^{-1}$.
- **Order is empirical**; **molecularity is theoretical**. They coincide ONLY for elementary reactions.
- The form of $f(C_A)$ determines the **shape of the conversion-vs-time curve** and the **choice of reactor**.
- **Method of initial rates** and **integral method** are the two experimental techniques to identify $f(C_A)$.
- **Always express $f$ in terms of conversion** when designing PFRs/CSTRs — convert using $C_A = C_{A0}(1 - X_A)$ and stoichiometric relations.
- **For an elementary step, the order with respect to each species equals its stoichiometric coefficient.**
- A **negative order** (e.g., $f = C_A^{-1}$) indicates **product inhibition**.
- **Zero-order** reactions are characteristic of **saturated catalyst surfaces**.
- The term $f(C_A)$ is **separable** from $k$ in isothermal reactor design, but NOT in non-isothermal analysis (where $k$ itself varies with position).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Temperature Dependent Term of a Rate Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT637 - CHEMICAL TECHNOLOGY AND REACTION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Temperature Dependent Term of a Rate Equation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In chemical reaction engineering, the **rate of reaction** is conventionally expressed as a product of two fundamentally distinct factors:

$$-r_A = k(T) \cdot f(C_A, C_B, \dots)$$

where the **concentration-dependent term** $f(C_A, C_B, \dots)$ captures the stoichiometric and mechanistic dependency, while the **temperature-dependent term** $k(T)$ — known as the **rate constant** (or specifically the *specific reaction rate*) — captures the effect of thermal energy on molecular reactivity.
